title: Package boundary: narrow Yazelix public bin surface to yzx
description: Change the packaged Yazelix runtime layout so the public package `bin/` surface is reduced to the intended user-facing command, `yzx`, instead of re-exporting the full bundled runtime toolset into profile-level package merging.
design:
Primary scope:
- `packaging/mk_runtime_tree.nix`
- any packaging helpers or package-shape assumptions directly coupled to the runtime tree layout

Goal:
- Stop exporting bundled runtime tools such as `hx`, `nvim`, `zellij`, `yazi`, `nu`, shells, and helper binaries through the package's public `bin/`.
- Introduce a private runtime tool directory owned by the Yazelix runtime root.
- Keep `bin/yzx` as the canonical public package entrypoint.

Non-goal:
- Do not yet solve every lookup path in bootstrap and runtime code here; that belongs to the bootstrap/runtime child bead.
acceptance_criteria: The packaged Yazelix public `bin/` exports `yzx` and no longer re-exports the bundled runtime toolset into profile-level package merging; bundled tools are still present in a private runtime-owned location for later runtime PATH wiring; package shape changes are minimal and do not reintroduce installer-era wrapper complexity.
close_reason: Completed: packaged Yazelix now exports only bin/yzx and moves bundled runtime tools into a private libexec surface.

title: Narrow packaged runtime bin surface to avoid Home Manager collisions
description:
## Problem

When Yazelix is installed through Home Manager or a profile, the packaged Yazelix output currently exposes many runtime dependency binaries through the package's public `bin/`, not just the intended user-facing `yzx` entrypoint. That creates `buildEnv` collisions whenever the user's profile also contains a different derivation that exports one of the same binary names.

Issue #560 is the first concrete report of this class:

```
two given paths contain a conflicting subpath:
  /nix/store/...-helix-wrapped-.../bin/hx
  /nix/store/...-yazelix/bin/hx
```

But the mechanism is broader than Helix. Any bundled runtime tool that Yazelix re-exports publicly can collide with a separately managed package in the user's profile.

## Root Cause

`packaging/mk_runtime_tree.nix` currently symlinks runtime dependency binaries into the packaged Yazelix public `bin/`. The Home Manager module then adds that full package to `home.packages`, so Yazelix participates in normal profile-level binary merging as if all of those tools were part of its public package surface.

That is a packaging-boundary mistake. The real public package surface is `yzx`. The rest of the runtime toolchain is internal product machinery that Yazelix should use inside its own managed runtime environment, not export as profile-wide binaries by default.

## Important Constraint

Users should not have to change `editor.command`, remove Home Manager-managed Helix/Neovim, or avoid wrapped packages just to install Yazelix cleanly. The fix should narrow the public package surface and keep the managed runtime behavior intact.
design:
Treat this as a package-boundary cleanup, not an editor-specific patch.

Recommended direction:
- Shrink the packaged Yazelix public `bin/` surface to the canonical user-facing entrypoint: `yzx`.
- Move runtime dependency executables into a private runtime tool directory instead of exporting them all through the package's public `bin/`.
- Keep the package/runtime root self-contained: Yazelix-managed shells, `yzx run`, popup/editor flows, doctor checks, tutor helpers, and startup wrappers should resolve runtime tools through the private Yazelix runtime PATH, not through public profile-wide package binaries.
- Update both POSIX bootstrap and Nushell runtime-env construction so the private runtime tool dir is prepended to PATH inside Yazelix-managed execution.
- Keep the editor contract stable. `editor.command = "hx"` / `"nvim"` and existing managed-editor logic should continue to work by name inside the Yazelix runtime env; do not push path-specific exceptions through Helix/Neovim helpers unless required.
- Update package/install validation and docs/specs that currently assume public runtime binaries under package `bin/` beyond `yzx`.

Design consequences to audit explicitly:
- `YAZELIX_NU_BIN` bootstrap resolution currently assumes `bin/nu`; that needs a private-runtime equivalent.
- Package/install smoke tests currently assert the presence of many public runtime binaries; they should be rewritten around the narrowed public surface and the private runtime PATH contract.
- Helper flows such as `yzx tutor hx`, `yzx doctor`, version probes, and managed editor launch must still work under the private runtime PATH without depending on public `bin/hx`.

Delete-first principle:
- Do not keep exporting a large public package `bin/` and then special-case collisions one tool at a time.
- First delete the accidental public ownership of runtime tools.
- Then make the remaining private-runtime contract explicit and well-defended.
acceptance_criteria: The packaged Yazelix public `bin/` exports only the intended user-facing package surface, centered on `yzx`; runtime tools such as `hx`, `nvim`, `zellij`, `yazi`, `nu`, shells, and other bundled helpers no longer collide with separately managed Home Manager/profile packages through public package-path ownership; inside `yzx` commands and Yazelix-managed sessions, those runtime tools still resolve through the private Yazelix runtime PATH; startup/bootstrap, doctor, tutor, popup/editor, and managed-editor flows continue to work without requiring user config changes; validation defends both the narrowed public package surface and the still-working private runtime tool contract.
close_reason: Completed: the packaged public surface was narrowed to yzx, bundled runtime tools moved behind private runtime directories, and validation/docs now defend the non-colliding package boundary.

title: Align yazelix package meta.platforms with documented macOS support
description:
The current tree exposes darwin package/app outputs in `flake.nix`, and the repo docs describe the first-party Yazelix package/runtime story as working on Linux and macOS. But the shared package builder in `packaging/mk_yazelix_package.nix` still hardcodes `meta.platforms = pkgs.lib.platforms.linux`, so `nix profile add github:luccahuguet/yazelix#yazelix` is refused on darwin even though `packages.aarch64-darwin.yazelix` and `packages.x86_64-darwin.yazelix` exist.

This is not just a missing platform string. The same shared package builder is currently being used for two different surfaces with different scope promises:
- the first-party top-level flake package, which the repo currently presents as Linux + macOS
- the draft nixpkgs submission package under `packaging/nixpkgs/`, whose notes still frame the first submission as Linux-first and intentionally not claiming darwin yet

That shared ownership made the narrower nixpkgs-submission metadata leak into the broader first-party flake contract.
design:
Delete-first recommendation: stop making the shared package builder own platform policy.

Do not solve this by blindly adding darwin to the shared `meta.platforms` line in `packaging/mk_yazelix_package.nix`, because that would silently broaden the draft nixpkgs submission contract at the same time. The slimmer and more honest fix is to delete the shared platform-policy ownership and let each wrapper own its own claim:
- `yazelix_package.nix` should claim the supported first-party flake package systems
- `packaging/nixpkgs/yazelix_package.nix` should keep the narrower submission scope if Linux-first is still the intended upstream boundary

An acceptable implementation shape is:
1. remove or parameterize the hardcoded `meta.platforms` from `packaging/mk_yazelix_package.nix` so it is no longer the canonical policy source
2. set the top-level first-party package platforms in `yazelix_package.nix`, preferably matching the explicit four flake systems already exported today rather than claiming a broader unverified universe
3. set the draft nixpkgs-submission package platforms in `packaging/nixpkgs/yazelix_package.nix` to the intended Linux-only submission scope, if that scope is still current
4. update the live package spec/docs so the distinction is explicit instead of accidental drift
5. add a cheap metadata-only validator that would have caught this exact mismatch without requiring darwin builds

Important constraints:
- do not delete darwin flake outputs or narrow the docs unless there is an explicit product decision to retract first-party macOS support
- do not widen the nixpkgs submission scope by accident just because the first-party flake contract is broader
- prefer one small validator over a large matrix of packaging tests; `nix eval` metadata checks are enough here

Rewrite sequencing: do this before the Rust rewrite. This is package-surface truthfulness and contract cleanup, not a Rust-shaped problem.
acceptance_criteria:
1. The top-level first-party flake package no longer reports as unsupported on the darwin systems it explicitly exports today.
2. The draft nixpkgs submission package keeps its intended Linux-first/Linux-only claimed scope unless a separate product decision broadens it.
3. `docs/specs/nixpkgs_package_contract.md` and `packaging/nixpkgs/submission_notes.md` make the first-party-versus-submission scope split explicit.
4. A cheap maintainer validator or equivalent metadata check fails if a future exported flake package system is not actually available according to `meta.platforms`.
notes:
opus 4.6 review:

The Nix packaging fix is clean and correct. The doc/spec changes are clear. But the validator has a critical bug that makes it a no-op — it would not have caught the original bug it was designed to catch.

CRITICAL BUG — validator passes a list instead of a package to lib.meta.availableOn.

The validate_package_platforms function extracts platforms as a list and passes it to availableOn:

  platforms = pkg.meta.platforms or [];
  lib.meta.availableOn platformEntry platforms;

But availableOn expects a derivation/package as the second argument, not a raw list. When given a list, it checks list ? meta.platforms which evaluates to false (lists have no attributes). The negation !false = true makes availableOn always return true regardless of actual platform content.

Proof from nix eval:

  fakePkg = { meta.platforms = ["x86_64-linux"]; };
  linuxOnlyList = ["x86_64-linux"];
  platformEntry = lib.systems.elaborate { system = "x86_64-darwin"; };

  lib.meta.availableOn platformEntry linuxOnlyList;  # → true  (WRONG: darwin not in list)
  lib.meta.availableOn platformEntry fakePkg;         # → false (CORRECT)

The validator would have given a green checkmark on the original linux-only bug. Fix: pass pkg directly to availableOn instead of extracting the platforms list first:

  check = system:
    let
      pkg = flake.packages.${system}.yazelix;
      platformEntry = lib.systems.elaborate { system = system; };
    in
      lib.meta.availableOn platformEntry pkg;

OTHER ISSUES:

2. EXPECTED_FLAKE_SYSTEMS constant is defined at the top of validate_flake_interface.nu but never referenced. The Nix expression hardcodes the same system list as an inline string. Either use the constant to generate the Nix expression or remove the unused constant.

WHAT IS GOOD:

- The Nix packaging architecture is exactly right: metaPlatforms parameter with null default on the shared builder, first-party wrapper claiming the four exported systems explicitly, nixpkgs wrapper claiming Linux-only. Clean delete-first fix that removes shared ownership of platform policy.
- yazelix_package.nix explicitly lists the four systems rather than using a broad lib.platforms set. This is honest and matches the flake outputs.
- packaging/nixpkgs/yazelix_package.nix has a clear comment explaining why its scope is narrower and pointing to the first-party wrapper.
- The spec additions (acceptance cases 8 and 9, the Platform scope subsection) clearly document the scope split.
- submission_notes.md expansion correctly explains the independence of the two surfaces.
- The validator design is right (metadata-only nix eval, no darwin build requirement, graceful skip on eval failure). Only the availableOn call site is wrong.

VERDICT:

The Nix packaging fix and documentation are correct — ship those. Fix the validator before shipping: pass pkg instead of the extracted platforms list to lib.meta.availableOn, and either use or remove the unused EXPECTED_FLAKE_SYSTEMS constant.
close_reason: Completed: first-party flake package platform metadata was split from the narrower nixpkgs submission scope, the validator was fixed to check package availability correctly, commit 0236b08 landed on main, and GitHub issue #562 is now closed.

title: Popup toggle-close still does not refresh Yazi git sidebar

Investigate whether the synced runtime is stale or the current background refresh implementation is not actually executing with the required Yazelix/Zellij context.
close_reason: Fixed: popup toggle-close refresh now works. The implementation was patched to refresh directly on popup close, and the real launch blocker was stale owner-runtime activation: fresh launches were rebuilding ~/.local/share/yazelix from an older profile-owned package until the local repo package was activated and rematerialized.

description: Update the release-facing surfaces around the v14 closeout and the v15 launch. That includes revising the existing v14 GitHub release so it reads as the final historical Classic release, creating the new v15 GitHub release body, and aligning the release-facing docs and note sources that those GitHub releases should reflect.
design: Keep the release story consistent across GitHub releases, CHANGELOG.md, docs/upgrade_notes.toml, and any release-facing history copy. The v14 release should stop implying ongoing support or bug-fix maintenance and should frame the broader dynamic-runtime-management scope as historical. The v15 release should present the current trimmed product honestly: workspace-focused scope, first-party Ghostty, fast popup/menu panes, and the end of the old runtime-manager story. Prefer one coherent note set rather than hand-maintained drift between GitHub and repo docs.
acceptance_criteria: The v14 GitHub release is updated to historical final-release framing, the v15 GitHub release notes exist in a form ready to publish, and the related release-facing note sources in the repo are aligned with those bodies. No public release surface still claims that v14 remains supported or that dynamic runtime management is part of Yazelix scope.
close_reason: Completed: release-facing repo note sources now carry the publish-ready v15 launch body, and the live v14 GitHub release was updated to final historical Classic framing.

title: Ship v15 as the only supported Yazelix line
description: Finalize the public docs, release notes, and version surfaces so Yazelix can ship `v15` as the live supported release and treat v14 as a historical, unsupported line. This is the release-cut planning layer for the README/history cleanup, the GitHub release bodies, the structured upgrade notes, and the actual version bump/tag/publication work.
design: Treat this as one coherent release story, not a set of disconnected edits. Public surfaces should all tell the same truth: Yazelix is now the trimmed workspace-focused product, fast popup/menu and the first-party Ghostty path are part of the core experience, dynamic runtime management was out of scope, v14 is no longer a supported line, and `v15` is the exact release identifier being cut now. Do not tag until the front-door docs, narrative history, changelog/upgrade notes, GitHub releases, and version surfaces stop drifting from each other.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear release-cut task graph for shipping `v15` as the only supported Yazelix line. The graph covers README/history alignment, release-note and GitHub-release alignment, and the exact `v15` bump/tag/publication step, with explicit blockers so the tag is not cut before the public story is coherent.
close_reason: all steps complete

title: Ghostty: restore dead-key IM fallback in the runtime launch path
description: The provided first-party Ghostty path regressed: dead keys no longer work. Yazelix used to ship a Wayland input-method fallback for Ghostty in commit `c82780e` (`fix(ghostty): auto-fallback IM module for dead keys on Wayland`), but that logic disappeared when the old `devenv.nix` terminal wrapper layer was trimmed out. Restore an equivalent fix in the current runtime-owned architecture.
design: Do not revive the old `devenv.nix` wrapper model. Reintroduce the dead-key fix through the current Ghostty ownership seam, most likely the runtime-owned Ghostty launch path in `nushell/scripts/utils/terminal_launcher.nu` or a dedicated runtime wrapper it calls. Preserve the old behavior intent from `c82780e`: when Ghostty is launched on Linux/Wayland with stale or missing IM configuration, fall back to a simple GTK input method so dead keys and compose input work again; when a real `ibus` or `fcitx` daemon is alive, do not blindly override it. Be explicit about host-independence: the old logic used `pgrep`, but the trimmed runtime no longer obviously ships that helper, so the fix may need either a runtime-owned process probe dependency or a different robust detection strategy. Do not perturb macOS or non-Ghostty terminals.
acceptance_criteria: Yazelix restores dead-key behavior for the provided Ghostty launch path on the affected Linux/Wayland setups. The fix lives in the current runtime architecture rather than the deleted `devenv.nix` wrapper layer. Valid running IM-daemon setups are not clobbered unnecessarily, and non-Ghostty or macOS launch paths are unchanged. The launch-env decision is defended by focused tests or another explicit verification path tied to the surviving Ghostty launch seam.
close_reason: Completed: restored the Ghostty dead-key IM fallback through a runtime-owned Linux launch wrapper, added focused wrapper tests, and verified the packaged runtime install path.

title: Use gradual Rust insertion in v15.x and treat v16 as the Rust-forward release
description: Yazelix v15.0 should ship as a trimmed non-Rust release focused on the rebooted product and its UX cleanup. Rust is allowed only as a later v15.x point-release implementation strategy where it clearly pays for itself; it is not part of what defines the initial v15 release. The release-era framing should stay simple: v15.0 is the trimmed reboot, later v15.x may gain selective Rust slices, and v16 is the release where Rust becomes a major visible architectural fact with substantially broader adoption.
design: This is a roadmap and release-framing decision. Consequences: do not present v15.0 as 'the Rust release'; do not make Rust a prerequisite for shipping v15.0; allow incremental Rust slices to land only in later v15.x point releases when they clearly improve the codebase; and shape the Rust planning beads around a gradual insertion path that culminates in a more explicitly Rust-heavy v16. The planning language should separate implementation cadence from release identity.
acceptance_criteria: The decision is recorded durably in Beads; roadmap and Rust beads can now distinguish the initial non-Rust v15.0 release from later v15.x incremental Rust insertion and the later v16 Rust-forward release framing; and future planning no longer conflates 'some Rust lands later' with 'v15 itself is the Rust release.'
close_reason: Decision recorded: v15.x may gain Rust incrementally, while v16 is the Rust-forward release.

title: Treat v15 as a config-surface rebirth and delete config migrations
description: Yazelix v15 should no longer carry config-migration machinery or legacy relocation behavior as part of its normal product contract. The project has been through a real product rebirth, and preserving old config-shape transitions now adds more complexity than value. The live line should converge on one current managed config surface, with explicit breakage if someone brings ancient assumptions back in rather than another layer of migration logic.
design: This is a delete-first product decision. Consequences: remove config_migrations, migration preview/transaction machinery, and startup or doctor flows that speak in migration language; stop treating old config shapes as first-class upgrade targets; keep only the config parsing and validation that defends the current surface; and update docs/specs/beads that still assume migration support is part of the living contract.
acceptance_criteria: The decision is recorded durably in Beads; cleanup beads can now assume config-migration support is to be removed rather than audited for retention; and future roadmap work no longer treats migration machinery as part of the stable v15 product surface.
close_reason: Decision recorded: v15 no longer carries config-migration support as part of the live product contract.

title: Adopt v15 as the sole supported Yazelix line and treat v14 as historical
description: Yazelix v15 is no longer a future branch experiment. The trimmed runtime, explicit update ownership, built-in Ghostty path, and the shared fast transient popup/menu workspace flow are coherent enough that v15 should become the only supported Yazelix line. v14 remains historical release context and migration input, not an actively supported parallel product.
design: This is a product-boundary decision, not just a branch preference. Consequences: stop recommending v14 as the line users should stay on; stop planning for ongoing Classic maintenance as a parallel promise; keep compatibility code only when it directly helps users move onto the current v15 product; and rewrite docs, specs, and roadmap beads that still describe a two-lines strategy. The current v15 identity should center on the trimmed workspace product plus the enhanced, very fast popup/menu-driven workspace UX.
acceptance_criteria: The decision is recorded durably in Beads; the roadmap/docs cleanup beads can now assume v15 is the live line; and stale planning assumptions about preserving or recommending v14 are superseded rather than left ambiguous.
close_reason: Decision recorded: v15 is the sole supported Yazelix line; v14 is historical only.

title: Assess bd feature parity with br and map schema differences
description: Install bd v1.0.0 and produce a concrete feature parity matrix between br and bd commands, flags, and output schemas. Identify gaps where bd lacks a direct equivalent (bv --robot-triage, --robot-plan, --robot-insights, --robot-alerts, --robot-suggest, --robot-diff, --robot-graph; br sync --flush-only/--import-only/--rebuild/--status; br doctor --repair; br lint; br epic; compaction). Map br issue fields (id, title, description, acceptance_criteria, design, notes, status, priority, issue_type, labels, dependencies, external_ref, close_reason, compaction_level) to bd/Dolt schema columns. Document any bd conventions that differ from br (e.g., status values, priority numbering, dependency types). Output a migration feasibility report.
notes:
## Feature Parity Assessment: br vs bd

### br features with direct bd equivalents
| br command | bd equivalent | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| br create | bd create | bd has --acceptance, --design, --notes, --parent, --deps, --external-ref, --ephemeral, --graph |
| br show | bd show | bd has --json, --long, --short, --as-of (time travel!), --watch |
| br update | bd update | bd has --claim, --append-notes, --defer, --due, --estimate, --metadata |
| br close | bd close | bd has --reason, --claim-next, --suggest-next |
| br reopen | bd reopen | Direct equivalent |
| br list | bd list | bd has --status comma-separated, --json, extended filters |
| br dep add/remove/list/tree/cycles | bd dep add/remove/list/tree/cycles | bd dep also has --type with more types: tracks, discovered-from, until, caused-by, validates, relates-to, supersedes |
| br label | bd tag/label | bd has both `tag` and `label` commands |
| br search | bd search | Direct equivalent |
| br lint | bd lint | Direct equivalent |
| br doctor | bd doctor | bd doctor not available in embedded mode yet |
| br count | bd count | Direct equivalent |
| br q | bd q | Direct equivalent |
| br epic | bd epic | bd epic has close-eligible + status |
| br dep | bd dep | bd dep has relate/unrelate for relates_to links |
| br history | bd history | Direct equivalent |
| br sync --flush-only | bd export/import | **Different architecture**: bd uses Dolt for version control, no flush/import cycle needed |
| br sync --import-only | bd import | bd import loads JSONL into Dolt |
| br sync --status | bd info | bd info shows database state |
| br agents | bd setup | bd has Claude/Copilot integration via `bd setup` and `bd prime` |
| br audit | bd audit | bd audit has label subcommand |

### br features with NO direct bd equivalent
| br command | Gap | Workaround |
|---|---|---|
| bv --robot-triage | No graph-aware triage engine | bd prime + bd ready + bd graph --dot + jq scripting |
| bv --robot-plan | No parallel execution planner | bd graph --dot + custom scripting |
| bv --robot-priority | No priority misalignment detection | Not available; manual priority review |
| bv --robot-insights | No PageRank/betweenness/HITS metrics | Not available |
| bv --robot-alerts | No stale/blocker detection | bd stale + bd blocked cover basic cases |
| bv --robot-suggest | No duplicate/cycle suggestions | bd find-duplicates + bd dep cycles cover parts |
| bv --robot-graph | No graph export built-in | bd graph --dot --json covers dependency graphs |
| bv --robot-diff | No diff view | bd diff covers this |
| bv --format toon | No compact agent output format | bd --json + jq covers this |
| br --no-db | Not needed | Dolt is the authoritative store; no separate DB/JSONL sync problem |
| br config | bd config | bd config has yaml/git/db key management |
| br schema | No equivalent | Less needed since Dolt has stable schema |
| br changelog | No direct equivalent | bd history + bd diff can reconstruct |
| br orphans | No equivalent | Not critical |
| br stale | bd stale | Direct equivalent |
| br ready | bd ready | Direct equivalent, bd ready has --explain |
| br blocked | bd blocked | Direct equivalent |
| br defer/undefer | bd defer/undefer | Direct equivalent |
| br rename-prefix | bd rename | bd rename handles individual IDs |

### Schema Compatibility
bd JSONL export schema is nearly identical to br JSONL:
- Same fields: id, title, description/notes, status, priority, issue_type, owner/created_by, labels, dependencies
- bd adds: owner (email), estimate, due, defer, external_ref, metadata, acceptance, design, comment_count, comment threads
- br fields not in bd: compaction_level, original_size, source_repo
- Dependency types: bd supports more (tracks, discovered-from, until, caused-by, validates, supersedes)
- bd uses `thread_id` in comments, br does not

### Critical Findings
1. **DB corruption solved**: Dolt is a production-grade SQL database with cell-level versioning. 50 sequential writes with no corruption in my test. br's fsqlite has 15+ closed corruption issues.
2. **Dotted IDs work**: bd-test-ws-w4h.1.1 resolves correctly in show/update/dep operations. No false cycle detection.
3. **No --robot-triage/--robot-plan/--robot-insights**: This is the biggest gap. bv provides PageRank, betweenness, HITS, eigenvector, k-core, critical path analysis. bd has none of these.
4. **export/import is a migration path**: bd import can read JSONL, making br→bd migration feasible with a schema transformation script.
5. **Embedded Dolt**: No external server needed. Single-writer file lock. Much simpler than br's JSONL+SQLite split.
6. **--prime replaces bv --robot-triage for basic workflow**: bd prime outputs agent-optimized context, but it's not graph-aware.
close_reason: Completed: bd feature parity assessment finished. Key findings: bd has 525 issues imported correctly, dotted IDs work, dependencies preserved, no DB corruption on writes. bv --robot-* features have no bd equivalent but bd ready/bd prime/bd graph cover basic triage needs.

title: Migrate from beads_rust (br) to gastownhall/beads (bd) for stable issue tracking
description:
The current Rust br (beads_rust v0.1.38) has chronic fsqlite DB corruption: writes corrupt pages, dotted-ID lookups fail for show/update while list succeeds, parent-child dep add falsely detects cycles, and the DB must be rebuilt from JSONL after write-heavy sessions. The upstream maintainer has no open issues and no v0.1.39 release, and the fsqlite engine is the root cause across 15+ closed issues (#138, #156, #223, #227, #228, #230, #233, #234, #235).

gastownhall/beads (bd, Go, v1.0.0, 20.6k stars) uses embedded Dolt instead of fsqlite, has proper single-writer locking, native hierarchical ID support, and a stable v1.0 release. The AGENTS.md workflow already references bd-style command patterns (br create, br dep add, br close, bv --robot-triage) which bd shares with compatible semantics.

This epic tracks the full migration: assessment, data export, schema mapping, AGENTS.md rewrite, Home Manager packaging, and validation.
close_reason: Completed: all tracked child beads are closed and the parent scope is done.

title: Make the yzx menu pane a managed toggle
description: The menu pane should behave like the popup pane from the user perspective: pressing the key when missing opens it, pressing from another pane focuses it, and pressing while focused closes it. This should reuse the shared transient-pane state model rather than inventing a second menu-only manager.
acceptance_criteria: 1. `Alt+Shift+M` becomes a toggle with the same state transitions as the popup keybind: missing -> open, present elsewhere -> focus, focused -> close. 2. The menu toggle does not create a visible non-floating helper pane. 3. The menu still runs the existing command palette behavior and post-action handling; command execution semantics do not regress. 4. The implementation reuses the shared transient-pane state/identity model created for popup rather than matching menu panes by ad-hoc strings in a second place. 5. Tests cover duplicate-prevention and focus/close behavior at the contract level.
close_reason: Completed by making Alt+Shift+M a pane-orchestrator-managed toggle with the same missing/focus/close semantics as popup and no visible helper pane.

title: Use one configurable floating geometry for menu and popup
description: Menu and popup currently disagree on size: popup uses `zellij.popup_width_percent` and `zellij.popup_height_percent` from `yazelix.toml`, while the menu keybind hardcodes 70% width/height in KDL. Make the floating pane geometry one shared configured contract so both experiences feel like the same Yazelix transient-pane family.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Remove the hardcoded `x 15%`, `y 15%`, `width 70%`, and `height 70%` menu keybind geometry or move it behind the same runtime config resolution used by popup. 2. Menu and popup use the same width/height values by default and after user config changes. 3. Home Manager/default config/docs stay consistent if the config naming changes; otherwise the existing `zellij.popup_*_percent` fields are explicitly documented as the shared transient-pane geometry for v15. 4. Focused generated-config or command tests prove the menu no longer has stale hardcoded geometry while popup geometry still validates and fails fast on invalid values.
close_reason: Completed by removing the menu hardcoded 70% floating geometry, carrying popup_width_percent and popup_height_percent through the pane-orchestrator plugin config, and using that one geometry contract for both popup and menu.

title: Extract shared transient-pane spec and launcher code
description: After the helperless/toggle control model is proven, extract the shared menu/popup transient-pane contract into one code path instead of keeping parallel `yzx menu --popup`, `yzx popup`, wrapper env, pane naming, cwd, and geometry logic. This should be a real shared seam, not a generic abstraction over behavior that is still undecided.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Introduce one internal transient-pane spec/launcher seam that carries pane kind, pane title, runtime wrapper path, cwd, argv, env, geometry, and close/focus identity. 2. Rewire `yzx menu --popup` and `yzx popup` through that seam without changing their public command behavior. 3. Keep product-specific behavior outside the shared seam: menu action loop stays menu-owned; popup program resolution and sidebar refresh stay popup-owned. 4. Delete any remaining duplicate launcher/env helper code that the shared seam makes redundant. 5. Add or update focused tests that defend behavior rather than implementation trivia.
close_reason: Completed by moving shared transient-pane ownership into the pane orchestrator, routing yzx menu --popup and yzx popup through one open_transient_pane seam, and consolidating popup/menu identity in one transient-pane contract.

title: Prove a helperless shared transient-pane control model for menu and popup
description:
The current parity target is broader than just the Alt+t popup opener. `yzx popup` has toggle semantics but still uses a visible helper pane for the missing-pane open path; `yzx menu` is fast and direct but is not a toggle and hardcodes its floating geometry. Before extracting shared code, prove the Zellij control model that can manage both experiences without losing configured geometry, runtime argv/env, workspace cwd, one-pane-per-tab behavior, and a non-shabby exit story. Exact Zellij `close_on_exit` is not mandatory if the pane can be closed or hidden cleanly without leaving visible dead panes.

Design notes:
- close_on_exit is not strictly required for the helperless transient-pane model. Hiding floating panes or otherwise removing the visible floating pane cleanly is acceptable as long as there are no visible helper panes and no dead/held transient panes left in the user's way.
- The shared transient-pane model should preserve: cwd behavior, argv/env behavior, configured geometry, one transient pane per tab per kind, and the existing yzx popup / yzx menu public identities.
- Toggle behavior should be: missing pane opens, pane present elsewhere focuses, focused pane closes or hides.
- The likely best owner is the Zellij pane orchestrator, because it already owns tab/workspace state and has a stable MessagePlugin alias. If you find a better owner, document why.
- Local Zellij source inspection found that plugin-opened command panes do not expose exact close_on_exit or pane-name controls, so wrappers may still need to rename themselves and/or hide/close the floating pane on exit.
- This bead is only the proof/design bead. Do not implement code changes unless they are strictly necessary to repair Beads.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Inspect the current Zellij plugin API/source and Yazelix popup-runner behavior enough to know whether a plugin-owned helperless opener can preserve close-on-exit, cwd, argv/env, geometry, and permissions. 2. Choose one shared control model for both menu and popup: missing -> open, present elsewhere -> focus, focused -> close. 3. If pure plugin-owned opening is impossible without regressions, document the exact Zellij API blocker and the narrowest fallback that avoids visible helper panes or makes the remaining helper cost invisible and bounded. 4. The design keeps `yzx menu` and `yzx popup` separate public commands, and does not drop popup program overrides, menu action behavior, sidebar refresh after popup close, or workspace-root cwd.
close_reason: Completed: The helperless shared transient-pane control model has been proven and designed. close_on_exit is not strictly required; hiding/closing floating panes cleanly is sufficient. The pane orchestrator is the best owner. Design notes captured. The extraction of shared code is tracked by yazelix-qgj7.4.3.6.

title: Trim popup startup cost by reducing yzx popup re-entry before the real program runs
description: The popup-program path still does more work than the menu path even after the toggle step: `yzx popup` parses config before opening, the floating wrapper re-enters `yzx popup` inside the new pane, and only then does the code `run-external` the actual popup program. Separate what must stay from what is accidental command re-entry, then trim duplicated parsing and startup work without changing the popup command contract.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The popup startup path is measured and explained well enough to separate true requirements from accidental re-entry cost. 2. Duplicated config parsing or command re-entry is removed where possible without breaking cwd/program/geometry behavior. 3. The surviving popup path is observably narrower than the current wrapper-then-re-enter flow.
notes: Implemented: `yzx_popup_program.nu` now runs the resolved popup argv directly instead of importing `yzx/popup.nu`, setting `YAZELIX_POPUP_PANE`, and re-entering `yzx popup`. The public `yzx popup` leaf and the runtime wrapper are now `--wrapped`, so popup child flags are passed through instead of being parsed as Yazelix flags. A focused regression test defends the direct wrapper behavior.
close_reason: Completed by making the runtime popup wrapper execute the resolved argv directly and preserving child flag passthrough.

title: Trim the popup toggle close-refresh seam and remove the fixed helper sleep if possible
description: The `Alt+t` path currently goes through the popup-runner pipe toggle, opens via `popup_open.nu` when no managed popup exists, and on close runs `popup_refresh_active_sidebar_yazi.nu`, which sleeps 150ms before refreshing the sidebar Yazi instance. Re-evaluate who should own popup focus/close/refresh behavior, then delete or justify the helper script and the fixed sleep-based recovery path.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Popup toggle results map to one clear owner for open/focus/close behavior. 2. The close path no longer relies on a standalone sleep-based sidebar refresh helper unless a Zellij limitation makes it unavoidable and clearly documented. 3. The surviving path preserves the managed single-popup contract and sidebar refresh behavior users actually need.
notes: Implemented: `yzx_toggle_popup.nu` no longer shells through `popup_open.nu` or `popup_refresh_active_sidebar_yazi.nu`. The missing-popup path calls `yzx popup` directly, and the closed-popup path calls `refresh_active_sidebar_yazi` directly with no fixed 150ms sleep. The remaining `yzx_toggle_popup.nu` keybind helper is intentionally retained for now because a pure `MessagePlugin` opener would lose the current close-on-exit/workspace-resolution behavior with the Zellij plugin API shape available here.
close_reason: Completed by deleting popup_open and popup_refresh helper wrappers, inlining the close refresh, and removing the fixed sleep.

title: Collapse the transient-pane launcher wrapper chain used by yzx menu and yzx popup
description: The current transient-pane open path crosses `open_floating_runtime_wrapper`, tracked Zellij scripts under `configs/zellij/scripts`, `runtime_helper.nu`, runtime wrapper scripts under `nushell/scripts/zellij_wrappers`, and then the user-facing `yzx menu` or `yzx popup` entrypoint. Re-evaluate which of those layers are actually required by the installed/runtime model, delete the redundant hop(s), and leave one clear owner for opening transient panes.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Both menu-popup and popup-program launch through one surviving launcher seam. 2. Redundant tracked-wrapper/runtime-wrapper pairs are deleted or justified by a hard runtime/install requirement. 3. The surviving seam still works from the packaged runtime and keeps the existing user-facing popup/menu contract.
notes: Implemented: menu and popup floating panes now launch direct runtime scripts under `nushell/scripts/zellij_wrappers` via `open_floating_runtime_script`. The tracked forwarding wrappers `configs/zellij/scripts/yzx_menu_popup.nu` and `configs/zellij/scripts/yzx_popup_program.nu` were deleted, and the `Alt+Shift+M` keybind now points directly at the runtime menu popup wrapper.
close_reason: Completed by collapsing the menu/popup floating launcher to direct runtime scripts and deleting the redundant forwarding wrappers.

title: Investigate popup/menu latency and the extra helper-pane path
description: Trace the popup-program and menu-popup launch paths end to end, identify where the extra helper-pane/process chain appears, and explain why the popup-program path feels slower and less polished than the menu path. The output should be a concrete seam map and a recommended delete-first reduction plan rather than code changes.
notes: Findings: `yzx menu --popup` opens through `open_floating_runtime_wrapper`, a tracked Zellij script, `runtime_helper.nu`, a runtime wrapper, and then re-enters `yzx menu` in popup mode. `Alt+t` is slower because it adds the popup-runner pipe toggle, `popup_open.nu` on missing popup, popup-pane title/marker discovery in the Rust plugin, and a close path that runs `popup_refresh_active_sidebar_yazi.nu` with a fixed `sleep 150ms`. `yzx popup` also parses config before opening and then re-enters `yzx popup` inside the floating pane before finally `run-external`ing the popup program. Follow-on work is split into launcher-chain, toggle/close-refresh, and popup re-entry beads.
close_reason: Investigation completed and split into concrete launcher-chain, close-refresh, and popup re-entry follow-on beads.

title: Trim overlapping high-cost runtime behavior tests after the v15 surface cleanup
description: The runtime and maintainer test buckets are still heavy after the command-surface and ownership cleanup. Audit the largest behavior tests delete-first, remove stale or overlapping cases that no longer defend a distinct surviving contract, and keep only the stronger tests that still protect v15 semantics.
acceptance_criteria: 1. At least one stale or overlapping high-cost runtime or maintainer test path is deleted, merged, or narrowed. 2. The surviving test set still defends the explicit v15 contracts that remain after the trim. 3. Test governance and traceability validators still pass for the surviving suite.
close_reason: Deleted the overlapping low-level legacy-root relocation parser tests from test_yzx_generated_configs.nu and kept the stronger entrypoint-preflight migration tests as the surviving contract owners; syntax and test-governance checks still pass after the trim.

title: Derive the yzx menu command catalog from the real exported yzx surface
description: yzx menu still keeps a handwritten public command catalog even though the command palette spec says the surface should be derived from the real exported yzx tree. Replace the hardcoded catalog with live command discovery plus a small explicit exclusion and metadata override layer so the palette stays in sync as the public surface changes.
close_reason: yzx menu now derives its command catalog from the live exported yzx surface, keeps explicit exclusions for env/run/cwd and maintainer commands, updates the palette-category spec, and adds a regression that proves the catalog tracks live command exports instead of a handwritten list.

title: Delete or relocate maintainer-oriented helpers still shipped under runtime paths
description: The detailed inventory shows that the runtime bucket still carries roughly 1,940 LOC of maintainer-oriented helper logic under shipped runtime paths. Re-evaluate that helper cluster delete-first, but keep yzx dev sync_issues as a surviving maintainer command. The real question is ownership: which helpers still need to exist, which should move fully under maintainer-only ownership, and which wrapper-only seams can disappear without losing functionality.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The surviving maintainer workflows that still matter, including yzx dev sync_issues, are explicitly classified as keep, move, or delete. 2. Wrapper-only helper seams under runtime-owned paths are removed or relocated where that can be done without losing behavior. 3. The maintained command surface and docs/tests agree on the surviving ownership model.
close_reason: Moved the maintainer-only helper cluster out of nushell/scripts/utils into nushell/scripts/maintainer, split runtime upgrade-note reads from maintainer README sync logic, rewired yzx dev plus maintainer validators/tests to the new owners, and deleted an unused dev-bump fixture copy so the ownership cleanup was not just a path shuffle.

title: Delete obvious non-core utility commands from the v15 yzx surface
description: Remove the commands that no longer justify first-class product surface area in v15: yzx gc, yzx refresh, yzx repair, yzx dev sync_terminal_configs, and yzx dev bench. These commands are either generic system maintenance, transitional repair affordances, or maintainer conveniences that inflate the shipped CLI, docs, and tests without strengthening the core workspace product.
design: Delete command entrypoints, menu and catalog references, help and docs text, upgrade-summary guidance, and tests together. Do not keep compatibility aliases by default. If one surviving behavior still matters, move it under the surface that truly owns it instead of preserving a top-level utility command out of habit. This bead should also record which older investigative beads or docs become obsolete once the commands are gone.
acceptance_criteria: The listed utility commands are gone from the public yzx tree, the runtime command catalog, and the docs. Their dedicated tests and stale references are deleted or rewritten against the surviving contract. Any remaining behavior that still matters is rehomed under an explicit owner instead of kept as a standalone utility command.
close_reason: Deleted yzx gc, yzx refresh, yzx repair, yzx dev sync_terminal_configs, and yzx dev bench from the public v15 surface; rewired surviving behavior into owned helpers and updated docs/tests.

title: Delete the installer-owned runtime/current compatibility contract from v15
description: Yazelix v15 still carries the old manual-install compatibility contract built around ~/.local/share/yazelix/runtime/current. The flake installer template, install validators, path docs, and wrapper checks still defend that indirection even though Home Manager and the packaged runtime now use more direct ownership. Delete this compatibility layer if possible, update the installer and validation surface to the slimmer surviving contract, and remove runtime/current wording from docs and tests. The result should be that v15 no longer treats runtime/current as a first-class install boundary.
close_reason: Completed

title: Delete remaining devenv maintainer/test ownership from the v15 repo
description: Yazelix v15 no longer uses devenv as a product or maintainer substrate, but the repo still contains maintainer scripts, fixtures, docs, and tests that read devenv.lock, mention repo-local devenv shells, or fabricate devenv-era checkout state. Remove those remaining seams instead of preserving them as compatibility language. This includes top-level maintainer shell artifacts if they are truly obsolete, the zjstatus update flow, test fixtures that still seed devenv.* files only to mimic old checkout state, and docs that still claim devenv.nix is the maintainer shell. The result should be that repo code, docs, and tests no longer depend on or describe devenv ownership at all.
close_reason: Completed

title: Make the flake package surface primary and demote the installer app
description: Promote the flake package and Home Manager surfaces as the canonical product entrypoints. Ground this in the real code: today `packages.default` still points at the bare runtime package, `apps` only exposes `install`, and the README/install docs still center `#install`. Normal usage should instead center the package output such as `#yazelix`, profile or Home Manager installation, and package-manager updates. `#install` should become legacy/bootstrap-only or disappear. Update docs, flake outputs, smoke checks, and entrypoint assumptions so the product no longer centers a mutable installer-managed runtime by default.
close_reason: Completed

title: Move workspace retarget, reveal, and sidebar-editor targeting truth into the pane orchestrator
description: Continue shrinking cross-language correctness by moving more active workspace targeting truth behind the pane orchestrator. Sidebar identity is one part of the problem, but the broader goal is that current-tab workspace retargeting and reveal behavior stop depending on Nushell-side reconstruction of live session state.
design: Build on the cross-language ownership map and the sidebar-identity cleanup. The pane orchestrator should become the durable owner for hot-path workspace targeting truth, while Nushell remains a command/config layer and Lua stays a thin Yazi adapter. Prefer deleting cache-shaped or reconstruction-shaped correctness paths rather than layering more state synchronizers on top.
acceptance_criteria: Workspace retarget, reveal, and editor-sidebar targeting use a stronger pane-orchestrator-owned source of truth than they do today. Nushell-side reconstruction and cache dependence are materially reduced, and the remaining ownership boundary is explicit.
notes: Pipeline stage 2B on the workspace-truth branch. After sidebar identity is plugin-owned, extend that ownership to workspace retarget/reveal/sidebar-editor targeting so Nushell no longer reconstructs live session truth on the hot path.
close_reason: Completed: pane-orchestrator retarget now returns current-tab editor/sidebar targeting truth, yzx cwd and workspace-retarget integrations consume that single plugin-owned response instead of reconstructing state through separate calls, and the synced wasm carries the stronger retarget contract while Yazi adapter execution stays explicit in Nushell.

title: Prove the workspace UX layer runs without backend ownership
description: Take the boundary discussion from docs/specs into a real executable slice: the surviving workspace UX should run cleanly even when Yazelix is not provisioning or activating the environment. Focus on the tab-local and workspace-local flows that should remain coherent with host-provided tools on PATH, rather than on env/build/install behavior.
design: Treat this as a proof bead, not a speculative new edition. Start from the post-m5rk boundary: backend activation already has a clearer seam, so now prove that cwd/reveal/popup/menu/edit and other workspace UX flows can operate without depending on devenv ownership. Delete any remaining hidden backend assumptions in those flows instead of introducing compatibility shims. The result should make a future Core-style omission mode feel like deleting a layer, not reimplementing one.
acceptance_criteria: There is a concrete validated slice of workspace UX that runs with host-provided tools and without backend ownership. The surviving flows and their limits are explicit. Any remaining backend-coupled workspace behaviors are identified as follow-up seams rather than left implicit.
notes: Pipeline stage 4: the executable proof bead. This should only close after explicit-root cleanup, pane-orchestrator-owned workspace truth, and the honest capability split are in place. Its job is to prove that the surviving workspace UX actually works without backend ownership.
close_reason: Documented the runtime-root-only backend-free workspace slice and added regressions proving popup/menu/reveal workspace flows can run with host-provided tools.

title: Split install, update, and doctor into honest full-vs-core capability tiers
description: Define and implement an honest capability split so a narrower Yazelix mode can say what it does not own. The important part is not branding; it is that install/update/doctor stop implying provisioning or runtime-repair promises when those capabilities are intentionally absent.
design: Use the existing runtime contract and Core-boundary decisions as inputs. Separate workspace/config diagnostics from provisioning/runtime-repair diagnostics, and do the same for install/update surfaces. Avoid fake fallbacks or soft wording that still suggests Yazelix can manage runtime ownership in a narrowed mode. If the right outcome is only a clearer contract plus smaller command behavior, take that path instead of inventing a full product switch.
acceptance_criteria: Install, update, and doctor have an explicit capability split between full Yazelix behavior and a narrowed workspace-only/Core-style mode. The surfaces fail or omit behavior honestly instead of pretending to provision tools or repair runtime ownership. The durable contract is documented clearly enough to guide later Core decisions.
notes: Pipeline stage 3 on the capability-contract branch. Install/update/doctor need an honest full-vs-core split before the repo can claim a backend-free workspace slice without fake fallbacks.
close_reason: Implemented explicit runtime/distribution capability tiers for update and doctor, added regressions, and documented the contract.

title: [Bug]: yzx update runtime fails
description: GitHub #555: yzx update runtime failed while rebuilding generated Yazi runtime configs because bundled plugin directories copied from the store were not writable before placeholder rendering or later recursive cleanup.
close_reason: Completed: fixed on main in 21998b8; bundled Yazi plugin refresh now relaxes copied store permissions before placeholder rendering and recursive managed cleanup, so yzx update runtime succeeds again.

title: Split terminal_configs.nu by shared terminal policy, Ghostty-specific assets, and terminal renderers
description: `nushell/scripts/utils/terminal_configs.nu` is still a mixed-owner production file. It currently bundles shared terminal policy (branding, transparency, user override paths), Ghostty-specific cursor/shader/effect logic, concrete per-terminal renderers, and the top-level generation workflow that copies shader assets and writes generated files. The goal is not to port everything or explode it into helpers; it is to question which of those responsibilities should really share one owner and to delete convenience exports before any larger split.
notes:
2026-04-09 delete-first cut landed:
- Removed backup writes for files under `~/.local/share/yazelix/configs/terminal_emulators/*`.
- Generated terminal configs are Yazelix-owned outputs, so rewriting them in place is the simpler contract; user-owned terminal tweaks still live under `~/.config/yazelix/user_configs/terminal/*`.
- Verification: the bundled terminal generation regression now runs twice and asserts there is no `.yazelix-backup` noise in the generated terminal tree; syntax and traceability validators pass.
close_reason: Completed

title: Split yazi_config_merger.nu by renderers, bundled asset sync, and user-config ownership
description: `nushell/scripts/setup/yazi_config_merger.nu` is still a large mixed-owner production file. It currently owns at least four different concerns at once: user-config reconciliation and legacy-path migration, bundled runtime asset syncing (plugins, flavors, starship config), generated-file rendering (`yazi.toml`, `theme.toml`, `keymap.toml`, `init.lua`), and generic merge policy helpers such as deep TOML merge and keymap append semantics. The goal is not cosmetic file breakup; it is to question whether those requirements belong under one owner at all and then delete or narrow cross-responsibility coupling before any extraction.
notes:
2026-04-09 user-override owner split landed:
- Moved Yazi user override resolution and merge policy into `setup/yazi_user_overrides.nu`.
- `yazi_config_merger.nu` now imports `resolve_yazi_user_file`, `merge_yazi_toml_config`, and `merge_yazi_keymap` instead of owning duplicate user-config reconciliation and merge semantics itself.
- Measured reduction: `yazi_config_merger.nu` is now 374 lines, down from 659 before the current bead started.
- Verification: generated-config canonical tests pass, `validate_syntax.nu` passes, and `validate_default_test_traceability.nu` passes.
close_reason: Renderer coordination now separated from Yazi asset sync and user override ownership

title: Split zellij.nu by pane-orchestrator client, workspace routing, and editor-pane launch ownership
description: The file nushell/scripts/integrations/zellij.nu still mixes several production owners: pane-orchestrator command transport, workspace-root/tab routing, floating-wrapper env assembly, and managed editor pane opening. The goal is not cosmetic file breakup; it is to stop one module from owning both the stable pane client contract and the editor/workspace policy that sits on top of it.
design: Delete-first plan: question which exports are actual external contracts first. Prefer deleting or internalizing wrapper-level helpers such as internal tab-name shaping before extracting modules. The likely stable seams are: pane-orchestrator client transport/response parsing, workspace routing, floating wrapper env assembly, and managed editor pane opening.
acceptance_criteria: 1. zellij.nu stops owning at least one unrelated subsystem family directly. 2. Any surviving exports map to clearer external contracts than the current grab-bag surface. 3. At least one wrapper-level or convenience surface is deleted or internalized rather than merely moved. 4. Workspace and generated-config regressions still defend the surviving behavior.
notes:
2026-04-09 completion notes:
- Split floating wrapper env assembly and popup/menu wrapper launching into `integrations/zellij_runtime_wrappers.nu`.
- `integrations/zellij.nu` now depends on that owner instead of exporting `build_floating_wrapper_env_args`, `get_floating_wrapper_env`, `get_new_editor_pane_launch_env`, and `open_floating_runtime_wrapper` itself.
- Deleted additional wrapper-only convenience surface from `integrations/zellij.nu`: `next_layout_family`, `previous_layout_family`, and `toggle_sidebar_layout`. The small zellij wrapper scripts now call `run_pane_orchestrator_command_raw` directly.
- Verification: popup canonical suite passed, workspace canonical suite passed, `validate_syntax.nu` passed, and `validate_default_test_traceability.nu` passed.
close_reason: Completed

title: Decide whether shell-hook v1-v3 migration support is still required
description: Question whether Yazelix still needs to detect and migrate v1/v2/v3 managed shell-hook sections, or whether support can be narrowed to the current v4 surface plus explicit manual recovery guidance.
design: Start from the requirement, not the implementation. Check what the repo actually promises about backward shell-hook compatibility, what upgrade horizon is still considered supported, and whether old hook generations are still likely enough to justify carrying marker constants, detection branches, and migration code. Prefer deleting unsupported generations entirely over rephrasing the same compatibility burden.
acceptance_criteria: 1. There is an explicit decision on whether shell-hook v1/v2/v3 migration remains supported. 2. If support is retained, the reason is documented clearly. 3. If support is dropped, the code and docs are simplified accordingly and the surviving repair path is explicit.
notes:
2026-04-08 step-1 evidence:
- `config_manager.nu`, `shell_hooks.nu`, and `constants.nu` still carry explicit v1/v2/v3 shell-hook markers and migration branches.
- Repo-local docs/specs currently talk about shell hooks in general, but the audit did not find a clear user-facing promise that v1/v2/v3 hook generations remain supported indefinitely.
- This makes old hook-generation support a legitimate requirement question instead of an assumed permanent contract.
close_reason: Current shell-hook support narrowed to v4 plus manual legacy cleanup

title: Split common.nu by runtime identity, repo-root, and host utility ownership
description: `nushell/scripts/utils/common.nu` currently mixes runtime-root resolution, installed-vs-repo identity, config/state path lookup, repo-root discovery, Nushell binary resolution, runtime-project materialization, build-parallelism policy, and miscellaneous host utilities. At ~528 lines it is not the biggest file in the repo, but it is one of the clearest grab-bag modules in the production control plane.
design: Delete-first plan: do not build a second `common_helpers` junk drawer. First decide which exports are true shared runtime/path ownership, which are maintainer-only repo/build helpers, and which convenience aliases should disappear entirely. Prefer moving code toward the subsystem that already owns the contract, and delete generic aliases such as broad `*_dir` compatibility wrappers where they no longer add real value.
acceptance_criteria: 1. `common.nu` no longer acts as a generic utility dump for unrelated runtime, repo, build, and process helpers. 2. Runtime identity/path helpers are separated from maintainer-only repo/build helpers, or at least one of those unrelated families is removed from `common.nu`. 3. At least one generic alias or convenience export is deleted rather than merely moved. 4. Callers become more explicit about whether they depend on runtime identity, config/state paths, repo-root discovery, or build policy.
notes:
2026-04-08 first delete-first cut landed:
- Deleted the broad runtime alias exports `get_yazelix_dir` and `require_yazelix_dir`.
- Deleted dead exports `require_yazelix_config_dir` and `is_hx_running` after confirming there were no remaining repo callsites.
- Internalized `get_yazelix_runtime_project_dir` and `get_yazelix_repo_root`, which were only used inside `common.nu` itself.
- Updated callers to use the narrower real names `get_yazelix_runtime_dir` and `require_yazelix_runtime_dir` instead of the generic aliases.
- Verification: refresh canonical suite, workspace canonical suite, doctor canonical suite, `validate_syntax.nu`, `validate_default_test_traceability.nu`, and `validate_readme_version.nu` all pass.

2026-04-08 second cut landed:
- Moved build/Nix policy out of `common.nu` into `utils/build_policy.nu`:
  - `get_yazelix_nix_config`
  - `get_max_jobs`
  - `get_max_cores`
  - `describe_build_parallelism`
- Moved runtime-project ownership out of `common.nu` into `utils/runtime_project.nu`:
  - `get_existing_yazelix_runtime_project_dir`
  - `materialize_yazelix_runtime_project_dir`
- Deleted the now-dead runtime-project internals left behind in `common.nu` after that move.
- Updated live callers and the affected refresh/workspace/maintainer test fixtures to the new module boundaries.
- Verification: refresh canonical suite, workspace canonical suite, targeted maintainer runtime-project regression, doctor canonical suite, `validate_syntax.nu`, `validate_default_test_traceability.nu`, and `validate_readme_version.nu` all pass.

2026-04-08 third cut landed:
- Moved writable repo-checkout detection out of `common.nu` into `utils/repo_checkout.nu`.
- `common.nu` no longer owns maintainer checkout resolution; it now stays focused on runtime/config/state identity plus shared path helpers.
- Updated maintainer workflow owners and the defending maintainer regression to import `require_yazelix_repo_root` from `repo_checkout.nu` instead of `common.nu`.
- Measured reduction: `common.nu` dropped to 270 lines after the repo-checkout move.
- Verification: `test_maintainer_repo_root_prefers_checkout_over_installed_runtime_env`, the issue-sync/update maintainer regressions, `validate_syntax.nu`, and `validate_default_test_traceability.nu` all pass.
close_reason: common.nu now focuses on runtime/config/state identity while repo checkout, runtime-project, and build policy ownership live in dedicated modules

title: Separate config_migrations.nu rule registry from planning and apply surfaces
description: `nushell/scripts/utils/config_migrations.nu` is nearly 1k lines and currently mixes canonical migration rule data, per-rule planning logic, plan assembly, preview rendering, rule validation, and apply entrypoints. That makes it hard to reason about what is durable migration metadata versus executable migration behavior, and it muddies the later Rust-shim discussion.
design: Delete-first plan: do not invent a second migration engine or generic framework. First separate what is data contract from what is plan/apply behavior, and delete any avoidable overlap between preview rendering, plan assembly, and per-rule helper logic before extracting modules. Keep one coherent migration engine, but make the registry/metadata owner and the execution owner explicit.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Migration rule metadata has a clearer owner than the full planning/apply engine. 2. Planning, preview rendering, and apply behavior are separated enough that future work can change one without rediscovering the whole file. 3. The refactor does not introduce a second migration engine or duplicate registry. 4. Follow-up Rust or spec work has a narrower seam to target than the current monolith.
notes:
2026-04-08 first ownership cut landed:
- Moved preview/load ownership out of `config_migrations.nu` into `utils/config_migration_preview.nu`:
  - `get_config_migration_plan`
  - `render_config_migration_plan`
  - the release-context formatting helper used only by preview rendering
- Updated `entrypoint_config_migrations.nu` and `yzx/config.nu` to import preview/load behavior from the new module while keeping plan/apply behavior in `config_migrations.nu`.
- Verification: targeted `yzx config migrate` preview/apply regressions, the startup migration-warning regression, `validate_config_migration_rules.nu`, `validate_upgrade_contract.nu`, `validate_syntax.nu`, and `validate_default_test_traceability.nu` all pass.

2026-04-08 second ownership cut landed:
- Moved the canonical rule registry and metadata validator out of `config_migrations.nu` into `utils/config_migration_rules.nu`.
- Updated `config_migrations.nu` to import rule data instead of owning it, keeping the file focused on per-rule planning, plan assembly, and apply behavior.
- Updated `entrypoint_config_migrations.nu`, `yzx/config.nu`, `validate_config_migration_rules.nu`, and `validate_upgrade_contract.nu` to import validation/rule metadata from the new module.
- Result: the migration registry, preview surface, and execution engine now have distinct owners without introducing a second migration engine.
- Verification: targeted `yzx config migrate` preview/apply regressions, the startup migration-warning regression, `validate_config_migration_rules.nu`, `validate_upgrade_contract.nu`, `validate_syntax.nu`, and `validate_default_test_traceability.nu` all pass.
close_reason: Registry, preview, and execution ownership are now separated cleanly

title: Split yazi.nu by sidebar-state, sync, and editor-open ownership
description: `nushell/scripts/integrations/yazi.nu` is a large production module that currently mixes several distinct owners at once: sidebar state discovery, reveal and refresh flows, cwd sync behavior, editor-kind resolution, and file-open routing into managed editors. At roughly 713 lines, it is now large enough that leaving it implicit would undercut the broader Nushell-streamlining goal.
notes:
2026-04-08 creation rationale:
- `integrations/yazi.nu` is currently one of the largest production Nushell modules and was not modeled explicitly enough in the earlier shortlist.
- It mixes at least five concerns: command resolution (`yazi`/`ya`), sidebar state lookup and pane-id mapping, reveal/refresh/sync flows, managed editor cwd sync, and editor-open routing.
- Delete-first direction: do not start with helper extraction. First identify which flows should disappear or move behind narrower contracts, especially any overlap with `integrations/zellij.nu` or future sidebar-ownership work under `yazelix-ewjz.1`.

2026-04-08 refinement after direct code read:
- Current file size is ~713 lines.
- Likely stable survivors are the narrow command-resolution and `ya emit-to` bridge pieces.
- Likely seams to question first are file-backed sidebar identity scanning, managed-editor cwd sync, and editor-open routing mixed into the Yazi integration layer.
- `open_file_with_editor` may belong closer to editor-integration ownership than sidebar-sync ownership.

2026-04-08 first ownership cut landed:
- Moved the managed-editor family out of `integrations/yazi.nu` into `integrations/managed_editor.nu`:
  - `get_managed_editor_kind`
  - `sync_managed_editor_cwd`
  - `resolve_managed_editor_open_strategy`
  - `open_file_with_editor` and its helper openers
- Updated `yzx cwd`, `open_file.nu`, `zoxide_open_in_editor.nu`, and the defending tests to depend on that narrower owner instead of pulling editor policy through `yazi.nu`.
- Result: `integrations/yazi.nu` dropped from 713 lines to 457 lines after the editor-family move.
- Verification: `run_yazi_canonical_tests`, `run_workspace_canonical_tests`, `validate_syntax.nu`, and `validate_default_test_traceability.nu` all pass.

2026-04-08 second ownership cut landed:
- Moved sidebar-state discovery and cache lookup out of `integrations/yazi.nu` into `integrations/yazi_sidebar_state.nu`:
  - sidebar state-path derivation
  - active-session/pane lookup
  - active sidebar state/id/cwd resolution
- Kept `integrations/yazi.nu` focused on Yazi command resolution, bootstrap cwd consumption, and active Yazi actions (`sync`, `refresh`, `reveal`).
- Result: `integrations/yazi.nu` now owns command/config plus action behavior, while `managed_editor.nu` owns editor policy and `yazi_sidebar_state.nu` owns sidebar-state discovery.
- Verification: `run_yazi_canonical_tests`, `run_workspace_canonical_tests`, `validate_syntax.nu`, and `validate_default_test_traceability.nu` all pass.
close_reason: yazi command/action ownership is now separated from managed-editor policy and sidebar-state discovery

title: Split yzx dev.nu by maintainer workflow family
description: `nushell/scripts/yzx/dev.nu` is a large, high-churn production file that currently owns unrelated maintainer flows at once: version/pin syncing, canary setup and execution, vendored asset refresh, Home Manager activation, Rust plugin build helpers, and GitHub/Beads issue sync logic. The problem is not line count alone; it is that one command module is acting as the owner for several different maintainer subsystems.
design: Delete-first plan: do not start by extracting helpers. First identify which maintainer workflows should not share one owner at all: update/canary activation, vendored-runtime sync, plugin build/sync, and GitHub-Beads reconciliation are likely separate seams. Prefer deleting cross-workflow coupling and shared incidental state before moving code. The end state should be a thin `yzx dev` command surface that dispatches to a few focused maintainer modules.
acceptance_criteria: 1. `yzx dev.nu` stops being the single owner of several unrelated maintainer workflows. 2. The resulting split is organized by workflow family, not arbitrary helper extraction. 3. At least one cross-workflow coupling or duplicate utility path is deleted rather than merely moved. 4. Maintainer regressions still defend the surviving command contracts.
notes:
2026-04-08 simplification landed:
- `refresh_home_manager_input_lock` now uses `nix flake update <input> --flake <dir>` instead of the deprecated `nix flake lock --update-input ...` form.
- The recovery message and the maintainer regression were updated to the same surviving command surface.
- Verification: `test_dev_update_home_manager_activation_refreshes_input_and_switches_requested_ref` passes; `validate_default_test_traceability.nu` passes.

2026-04-08 refinement after the hotspot scan:
- `yzx/dev.nu` is ~1194 lines and clearly mixes several maintainer subsystems.
- The visible workflow families are: update/canary activation, vendored asset sync, GitHub-Beads reconciliation, Rust plugin build/sync, and thin test/bench/profile/lint dispatch.
- Delete-first implication: the final UX can stay under one `yzx dev` umbrella, but those implementation owners should stop sharing one module.

2026-04-08 first delete-first cut landed:
- Deleted wrapper-only helpers that were not real owners:
  - `get_dev_repo_root`
  - `get_available_update_canaries`
  - `get_update_activation_modes`
  - `format_activation_command_arg`
- The surviving update workflow now uses `require_yazelix_repo_root` and inline canonical mode/canary lists directly instead of routing through one-file convenience wrappers.
- Verification: `test_dev_update_requires_explicit_activation_for_real_updates`, `test_dev_update_activation_mode_rejects_unknown_values`, `test_dev_update_home_manager_activation_refreshes_input_and_switches_requested_ref`, `validate_syntax.nu`, and `validate_default_test_traceability.nu` all pass.

2026-04-08 update workflow family split landed:
- Moved the whole update/canary activation family out of `yzx/dev.nu` into `utils/dev_update_workflow.nu`.
- `yzx dev update` is now a thin command surface that delegates to that owner.
- Maintainer tests that exercise helper-level update semantics now source `utils/dev_update_workflow.nu` directly instead of relying on `yzx/dev.nu` to own those helpers.
- Measured reduction: `yzx/dev.nu` dropped from 1194 lines to 594 lines after the update-family move.
- Verification: `test_dev_update_requires_explicit_activation_for_real_updates`, `test_dev_update_activation_mode_rejects_unknown_values`, `test_dev_update_home_manager_activation_refreshes_input_and_switches_requested_ref`, `validate_syntax.nu`, and `validate_default_test_traceability.nu` all pass.

2026-04-08 plugin build family split landed:
- Moved pane-orchestrator and popup-plugin build/sync ownership out of `yzx/dev.nu` into `utils/dev_plugin_build.nu`.
- The surviving `yzx dev build_pane_orchestrator` and `yzx dev build_popup_plugin` commands are now thin wrappers over that narrower owner.
- Verification: `use nushell/scripts/utils/dev_plugin_build.nu *`, the maintainer update regressions, `validate_syntax.nu`, and `validate_default_test_traceability.nu` all pass.

2026-04-08 GitHub-Beads sync family split landed:
- Moved GitHub issue lifecycle reconciliation and canonical comment sync out of `yzx/dev.nu` into `utils/dev_issue_sync.nu`.
- The surviving `yzx dev sync_issues` command is now a thin wrapper over that narrower owner.
- Measured reduction after the workflow-family splits: `yzx/dev.nu` is now 178 lines.
- Verification: `test_issue_bead_reconciliation_plan`, `test_issue_bead_comment_plan`, the maintainer update regressions, `validate_syntax.nu`, and `validate_default_test_traceability.nu` all pass.

Current seam after these deletions:
- `yzx/dev.nu` is now close to the intended thin command aggregator shape.
- The remaining likely follow-up is vendored asset/version sync ownership, but that is no longer required to prove the main maintainer workflow split.
close_reason: yzx dev is now a thin maintainer command dispatcher with separate update, plugin-build, and issue-sync owners

title: Separate doctor.nu by check family and shared diagnostic rendering
description: `nushell/scripts/utils/doctor.nu` is one of the largest and most frequently touched production files. It currently mixes Helix checks, config diagnostics, runtime/install ownership checks, desktop-entry checks, launch-profile checks, shell-hook checks, and final result rendering in one place. The goal is not cosmetic file splitting; it is to stop one file from owning several unrelated diagnostic domains at once.
design: Delete-first plan: first identify which doctor checks should stay together because they share one real contract, and which ones are merely co-located out of convenience. Prefer deleting duplicated result-shaping or ownership detection before extracting helpers. A good end state is a thinner doctor entrypoint that composes a few clear diagnostic families: config/managed-surface checks, runtime/install ownership checks, launch-profile/runtime state checks, and tool/editor-specific checks.
acceptance_criteria: 1. `doctor.nu` is no longer the sole owner of several unrelated diagnostic families. 2. Shared result rendering stays thin and explicit instead of growing a helper jungle. 3. At least one mixed-responsibility seam is deleted or narrowed, not just moved. 4. Focused doctor regressions still defend the user-facing output contracts.
notes:
2026-04-08 first deletion inside the bead:
- Deleted the standalone `check_shell_integration` function and removed its call from `run_doctor_checks`.
- Verification: there are no remaining references to the removed function or its old messages; the full doctor canonical suite passes; `validate_default_test_traceability.nu` passes.
- This confirms the step-1 conclusion that generic `which yzx` reporting was duplicate convenience scope, not a required diagnostic family.

2026-04-08 refinement after the hotspot scan:
- `doctor.nu` is ~1436 lines and remains one of the highest-value mixed-owner production files.
- Likely first real seams are: Helix-specific diagnostics, install/runtime ownership checks, config/state checks, and shared result rendering.
- Do not start by extracting desktop-entry or shell-hook helpers in isolation; move code only when it follows one of those real owners.

2026-04-08 install/runtime ownership seam landed:
- Moved the install/runtime artifact family out of `doctor.nu` into `utils/doctor_install_artifacts.nu`.
- That new owner now covers desktop-entry freshness, runtime/current link health, installed `yzx` freshness, and shell-hook freshness.
- Deleted duplicated manual-vs-Home-Manager ownership logic by reusing `utils/install_ownership.nu` for the shared artifact model instead of re-owning it inside doctor.
- Verification: focused desktop-entry/Home-Manager/runtime-asset doctor regressions, the full doctor canonical suite, `validate_syntax.nu`, and `validate_default_test_traceability.nu` all pass.

2026-04-08 Helix diagnostic seam landed:
- Moved the Helix-specific doctor family out of `doctor.nu` into `utils/doctor_helix.nu`.
- That new owner now covers runtime conflict detection, runtime health, managed reveal-integration checks, and the Helix conflict fixer.
- Result: `doctor.nu` now composes Helix, install/runtime, config/state, shared runtime preflight, and final rendering instead of owning all of those implementations directly.
- Measured reduction: `doctor.nu` dropped from 1436 lines to 632 lines after the two family moves.
- Verification: `test_yzx_helix_doctor_contracts.nu`, the full doctor canonical suite, `validate_syntax.nu`, and `validate_default_test_traceability.nu` all pass.
close_reason: Doctor now composes separate Helix and install/runtime diagnostic families

title: Bring the default suite back under the 75s runtime budget
description: The runtime-budget validator no longer hangs, but it now exposes a real default-suite runtime overrun: the profiled default suite takes about 82.37s against the explicit 75.0s cap. The current profile is dominated by the generated-config and doctor lanes, so CI now fails clearly on budget rather than stalling on leaked live Zellij subprocesses.
design: Treat this as a real budget-reduction or re-baselining decision, not a harness bug. Start from the measured profile now emitted by validate_default_test_budget: generated about 25.24s, doctor about 24.70s, core about 13.57s, workspace about 10.64s, with the other lanes much smaller. Prefer deleting avoidable expensive work from default-lane tests, moving slower but lower-signal coverage to the right nondefault lane, or narrowing repeated heavy regeneration paths before touching the cap.
