close_reason: Completed

title: Inventory every test file with lane, contract, score, and verdict
close_reason: Completed

title: Audit and prune the full Yazelix test surface for signal
design: Treat this as an aggressive pruning program, not a test-preservation exercise. First inventory every test file by lane, contract, score, and likely verdict. Then audit the default lane test-by-test using the new strength rubric and remove or demote weak/default-inappropriate cases. After that, audit nondefault lanes file-by-file to decide whether each file still deserves to exist. Finish by applying deletions, demotions, and runner simplifications so the repo is smaller and the suite stays easier to reason about.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Every test file under nushell/scripts/dev/test_*.nu is inventoried with lane, rough contract, and a keep/demote/remove/rewrite verdict. 2. The default lane is reviewed test-by-test and at least one weak, redundant, or mis-laned test is removed, demoted, or rewritten if such a case exists. 3. Nondefault lanes are reviewed file-by-file and dead-weight files or tests are removed or narrowed where justified. 4. The resulting runner/validator surfaces remain coherent and all affected validation lanes pass. 5. The audit leaves behind a concise written inventory or report so future pruning starts from reality instead of memory.
close_reason: Completed

title: Add a scored strength rubric for default-lane tests
design: Add a per-test strength rubric for the default suite so test quality is judged explicitly instead of only through prose. Use a small 0-10 score made from five 0-2 dimensions: failure significance, behavior proximity, refactor resilience, cost efficiency, and uniqueness. Require a nearby '# Strength: N/10' marker for every canonical default-lane test entry and enforce a minimum 7/10 bar in the existing default-suite validator. This should remain a decision aid with mechanical enforcement, not fake precision for every test in the repo: default-lane tests carry the score because that lane is the one whose quality bar matters most in CI.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The test governance spec documents the 0-10 default-test strength rubric and the meaning of the score bands. 2. AGENTS.md tells agents to add a nearby '# Strength: N/10' marker for new canonical default-lane tests and respect the minimum 7/10 bar. 3. validate_default_test_traceability.nu rejects canonical default-lane tests that are missing a score or fall below 7/10. 4. The current default canonical suite is scored under the new rubric and passes the validator. 5. The runtime budget remains a separate guard from the quality score.
close_reason: Completed: added a 0-10 strength rubric for canonical default-lane tests, enforced nearby # Strength: N/10 markers with a minimum 7/10 bar in CI, and scored the current default suite under the new model.

title: Strengthen test governance against weak AI-generated tests
design: Replace the default-suite canonical test-count budget with structural governance that better resists low-value AI-generated tests. Enforce small explicit lane declarations on all test_*.nu files, require nearby justification markers for every canonical default-lane test entry, and forbid new generic _extended overflow files unless they are explicitly grandfathered. Keep the runtime budget as the real speed guard. Update AGENTS and the test governance spec so the repo, not prompt discipline, carries the enforcement.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The canonical test-count budget is removed from the default-suite validator and from the governance spec. 2. CI rejects test files without an allowed # Test lane declaration. 3. CI rejects default-lane canonical tests that lack a nearby # Defends:, # Regression:, or # Invariant: marker. 4. CI rejects new generic _extended overflow files unless they are explicitly grandfathered. 5. The runtime budget validator remains the speed guard for the default suite.
close_reason: Completed: removed the canonical test-count budget, added enforced test-lane declarations and canonical-test justification markers, and blocked new generic _extended overflow files while keeping the runtime budget as the real CI speed guard.

title: Make the Yazelix desktop entry stable across runtime updates
description: The desktop entry should not keep going stale whenever the installed runtime store path changes. Replace the current runtime-targeted desktop-entry contract with a stable launcher path so normal installs and updates do not require rerunning yzx desktop install just to keep the desktop entry working.
design: Treat the current stale-desktop-entry detection as a guardrail, not the final UX. The real contract problem is that the desktop file points at a specific runtime store path, so normal runtime updates can invalidate it. Replace that with a stable launcher surface such as ~/.local/bin/yzx or ~/.local/share/yazelix/runtime/current so desktop launches remain valid across installs and updates. Preserve clear diagnostics, but the goal is to stop needing repeated yzx desktop install repairs in normal use.
acceptance_criteria: A previously installed Yazelix desktop entry continues to work after normal runtime updates without needing yzx desktop install again. The desktop entry uses a stable launcher path instead of a specific runtime store path. Doctor and launch-time checks may still detect genuinely broken installs, but normal runtime churn should not make the desktop entry stale.
close_reason: Completed

title: Consolidate Home Manager flake surface into the top-level flake
description: Reduce packaging-surface duplication by moving the Home Manager flake entrypoint to the top-level Yazelix flake. The goal is one canonical flake surface that exposes both the install/runtime package and `homeManagerModules.default`, with a deliberate migration path for the current `?dir=home_manager` input users.
design: Treat this as flake-surface consolidation, not a packaging side quest. The top-level flake should become the canonical entrypoint for both Yazelix install/runtime outputs and the Home Manager module, with a deliberate compatibility story for current users of the separate ?dir=home_manager path. Prefer deleting duplicate flake surface area over adding another forwarding layer unless a short migration bridge is genuinely necessary.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The top-level flake exposes the canonical Home Manager module surface alongside the existing install/runtime package surface. 2. The repo no longer relies on the separate home_manager flake entrypoint as the primary story. 3. Existing users of the current ?dir=home_manager path have a clear migration note or temporary compatibility path. 4. Docs and examples point to one canonical flake surface after the change.
close_reason: Completed: the top-level flake now exports the canonical Home Manager module surface, the duplicate home_manager subflake and lockfile are deleted, CI validates the root flake interface directly, and docs now migrate users from ?dir=home_manager to the repo root flake.

title: Add launch-profile freshness diagnostics to yzx doctor
description: Teach `yzx doctor` to report whether the current cached Yazelix launch profile is valid, stale, or missing for the active rebuild-relevant config and tracked devenv inputs so users get actionable guidance before launch/reuse confusion sets in.
design: Keep this user-facing and runtime-local. Reuse compute_config_state and the existing refresh-reason logic instead of reimplementing launch-profile validity in doctor. The result should distinguish at least: healthy profile, stale profile because rebuild-relevant config changed, stale profile because tracked devenv inputs changed, and missing verified launch profile. It should suggest the right recovery path such as `yzx refresh` or rerunning without `--reuse`. Do not turn `yzx doctor` into a repo-maintainer parity validator; that stays in the cheap validator lane.
acceptance_criteria: Running `yzx doctor` includes a clear launch-profile health result. When the cached profile matches the current rebuild-relevant config and tracked inputs, doctor reports it as healthy. When the profile is stale or missing, doctor reports the reason using the existing config-state logic and points the user at the right next command. The check relies on the canonical launch-profile contract rather than duplicating its rules ad hoc.
notes:
2026-04-09 implementation:
- Added launch-profile freshness classification to `yzx doctor` by extending the canonical config-state/launch-state contract instead of reimplementing freshness heuristics inside doctor.
- `doctor` now distinguishes healthy cached launch profile, stale due to rebuild-relevant config changes, stale due to tracked runtime/devenv input changes, and missing verified launch-profile evidence.
- Added a strong default-lane regression covering healthy, stale-config, stale-input, and missing-profile states through the real `yzx doctor --verbose` command.
- Hardened the new check so it skips cleanly when the active config cannot yet be parsed into canonical config state; doctor still reports stale/migration-aware config instead of crashing.
close_reason: Completed: yzx doctor now reports launch-profile freshness states via the canonical config-state and launch-state contract.

title: Canonicalize devenv CLI resolution and PATH ordering
description: Yazelix currently conflates the standalone devenv CLI, the project devenv input, and whatever devenv happens to be first on PATH. Fix the contract by introducing one shared host-tool resolver for devenv, using it for internal invocations and version reporting, and repairing Nushell PATH restoration so stale initial PATH entries do not outrank the current shell PATH.
close_reason: Canonicalized remaining maintainer/internal devenv invocations onto the shared resolver and made Nushell PATH restoration explicitly preserve current-shell precedence over stale saved entries.

title: Fix config validation for optional TOML fields
description: Yazelix config validation currently infers the allowed schema from the parsed default TOML surface, which means optional fields that only exist as commented examples in yazelix_default.toml are treated as unknown. Comments should not affect validation in any way. Fix the validator so supported optional fields like helix.runtime_path are accepted based on a real schema/source-of-truth rather than whether the default TOML happens to materialize that key after parsing. The doctor and startup diagnostics should then report real config problems instead of false unknown-field errors for documented optional settings.
close_reason: GitHub issue #499 is closed; tracker synced.

title: Audit rm usage for safety and destructive-command hygiene
description: Audit the codebase for every rm invocation and decide where deletion behavior needs stronger safety checks, clearer scoping, or better diagnostics.
design: Treat this as destructive-command hygiene, not as a mechanical search-and-replace pass. For each rm usage, verify what is being deleted, how paths are resolved, whether the target can escape the intended runtime boundary, and whether errors are visible. The goal is to catch genuinely risky deletion paths without adding unnecessary friction to safe, bounded cleanup.
acceptance_criteria: All rm usage in the repo has been reviewed and any risky or unclear deletion paths have a concrete follow-up fix or direct cleanup. The audit records which rm invocations are already acceptably bounded and which ones need stronger safeguards or diagnostics.
notes: Promote this safety audit. Current repo direction is pre-packaging hardening: reduce LOC, remove dead/redundant code, and audit destructive paths. rm-call hygiene is a high-priority cleanup pass because it can hide unsafe path handling behind routine maintenance code.
close_reason: Completed: bounded managed cleanup now uses a shared root-checked delete helper, preserves lexical symlinked managed paths, and refuses root/outside targets.

title: Move managed Zellij config into user_configs/zellij/config.kdl
description: Make Yazelix-managed Zellij config self-contained under ~/.config/yazelix/user_configs/zellij/config.kdl. In managed mode, stop implicitly reading ~/.config/zellij/config.kdl as the default input surface. Instead, treat native Zellij config as an explicit import/migration source or escape hatch. Keep the generated merged runtime config in ~/.local/share/yazelix/configs/zellij/config.kdl. Update config loading, migration/doctor flows, docs, and verification so the user mental model matches Yazi and the broader user_configs architecture. Include strong behavior tests for managed-path loading, native-config migration/import, and generated runtime output.
close_reason: Completed

title: Move managed Zellij config into user_configs/zellij
description: Make Zellij follow the same ownership model as the rest of managed Yazelix config. In managed mode, the canonical user-editable Zellij input should live under ~/.config/yazelix/user_configs/zellij/ (starting with config.kdl), and Yazelix should generate the merged runtime config into ~/.local/share/yazelix/configs/zellij/. Do not keep ~/.config/zellij/config.kdl as the default managed input path; at most, treat it as an import/migration source or explicit escape hatch. This should optimize for system coherence, least surprise, self-contained flake story, and a single mental model: user edits user_configs/*, Yazelix renders runtime state into ~/.local/share/yazelix/. Include deterministic migration/import behavior, updated docs, and focused regression coverage for path ownership and merge precedence.
close_reason: Completed: managed Zellij config now lives under user_configs/zellij with deterministic legacy relocation, fail-fast duplicate detection, docs updates, and regression coverage.

title: Move user-owned config under user_configs with terminal subdir
description: Move user-owned Yazelix config into a dedicated user_configs boundary. End-state: all Yazelix-managed user inputs live under ~/.config/yazelix/user_configs/ (including yazelix.toml, yazelix_packs.toml, terminal/, yazi/, and zellij/), while generated runtime state lives under ~/.local/share/yazelix/. Keep tracked defaults at the repo/config root. Managed mode should not implicitly read external native tool config from unrelated XDG paths; native tool config should only participate as an explicit import/migration source or escape hatch. Keep the system self-contained, coherent for users, and friendly to a future first-party Yazelix flake. Do not auto-scaffold optional user-owned files by default. Add only one minimal guide file at user_configs/README.md rather than a README in every subfolder.
close_reason: Completed

title: Tighten the test contract further and cut LOC, especially Nushell and tests
description: Investigate how to make the test contract stricter while reducing overall LOC. Focus especially on Nushell code size and the test suite footprint, since a significant chunk of the LOC growth is test code.
notes:
Progress on 2026-03-29:
- Extracted shared managed-config fixture and e2e log helpers into nushell/scripts/dev/test_yzx_helpers.nu.
- Reduced duplicated plumbing across test_yzx_core_commands.nu, test_config_migrate_e2e.nu, test_stale_config_diagnostics_e2e.nu, and test_upgrade_summary_e2e.nu.
- Reduced duplicated doctor/config fixture setup across test_yzx_doctor_commands.nu and test_yzx_generated_configs.nu.
- Fixed stale test setup assumptions that still wrote managed config to legacy root paths instead of user_configs/.
- Landed in commits c62577c and 8f1383e.
Remaining work:
- Continue scanning for honest shared-fixture reductions in remaining high-duplication Nushell tests.
- Keep removing only setup/plumbing duplication; preserve strong behavior coverage and avoid weak existence tests.
close_reason: Tightened the default-suite contract to spec-backed entrypoints only, removed regression-only sidecars, and pruned low-signal tests to cut default-suite time and LOC.

title: Revamp docs/history.md around generated recent releases and narrative eras
description: Rework docs/history.md so it tells the current release story instead of preserving the older branch-era or Classic-maintenance narrative. The document should keep its long-form narrative role, but recent release facts should stay aligned with the structured upgrade notes and with the new decision that v14 is a historical, unsupported line while v15 is the live trimmed release.
design:
Treat this as role clarity plus factual cleanup, not a history wipe. docs/upgrade_notes.toml should remain the canonical recent-release fact source, CHANGELOG.md should remain the concise upgrade-facing surface, and docs/history.md should be the narrative companion. But the narrative itself must now reflect the current product stance clearly: dynamically managed runtimes and the broader Classic runtime-manager scope were out of Yazelix scope, v14 is historical context rather than a maintained fallback line, and v15 is the live workspace-focused release with the slimmer runtime surface, first-party Ghostty path, and fast popup/menu-centered UX.

Do not preserve wording that recommends staying on v14 or frames v14 as a line that will continue receiving support. Keep the Rust-forward framing as a later roadmap, not the identity of the v15 release itself. Prefer a recent-release block sourced from or tightly validated against upgrade notes so the history page cannot drift from the GitHub releases, changelog, and whats_new data.
acceptance_criteria: docs/history.md has a clear long-form role distinct from CHANGELOG.md, and its maintained recent-release narrative matches the current product stance: v14 is historical and unsupported, v15 is the live trimmed release, dynamic runtime management is explicitly out of scope, and the v15-era workspace UX is described accurately. Recent release facts are sourced from or tightly validated against docs/upgrade_notes.toml so the history page does not silently drift from the canonical release surfaces.
close_reason: Completed: docs/history.md now has a distinct narrative role, adds the live v15 release story, and frames v14 as historical and unsupported in line with upgrade notes.

title: Make stale config diagnostics migration-aware across startup and doctor
description: When Yazelix hits a known stale config value or field, surface a precise migration-aware diagnostic at the exact failure boundary instead of collapsing into a generic startup or generation failure. The same classification should also appear in yzx doctor so users can understand what changed and what the safest next action is.
design: Consume the shared migration registry from the migrate work instead of inventing a separate error taxonomy here. Startup must stay read only: no silent rewrites, no hidden fallbacks, and no generic wrap up that hides the real stale config problem. Diagnostics should name the exact config path and stale value, state what changed and in which real Yazelix version or date when known, distinguish safe auto-fixable cases from manual-only cases, and point to the right next step such as yzx config migrate, yzx doctor --fix when explicitly invoked, a one line manual edit, or the changelog surface. Cover startup, refresh, and doctor paths with the same underlying classification and copy contracts, even if the surrounding wrapper command differs.
acceptance_criteria: A stale config such as zellij.widget_tray containing layout fails with a targeted actionable message instead of a generic Failed to generate Zellij configuration wrapper. yzx doctor reports the same migration-aware finding with the same path, rationale, and next steps, and safe cases clearly advertise whether yzx config migrate or yzx doctor --fix can resolve them while startup remains read only. Unknown or unsupported schema problems still fail clearly without pretending to have a migration. Add comprehensive unit tests for finding classification, message formatting, multi-finding aggregation, and safe versus manual guidance. Add end to end scripts with detailed logging that launch or refresh Yazelix from temp homes with stale configs, capture the full failure output, and verify the doctor output for both a known migration and an unknown invalid config case.
notes:
Planning refinements:
- Create a focused spec such as docs/specs/stale_config_diagnostics.md.
- Keep startup read only; explicit repair happens only via dedicated commands.
- Prefer one message-rendering layer so start_yazelix, refresh, and doctor do not drift in terminology or suggested actions.
- Preserve a clear distinction between known migrations, unknown invalid values, missing fields, and non-config runtime failures.
- Verification plan: unit coverage for classified findings and message rendering, plus end to end Nushell scripts that log full launch and refresh stderr or stdout, doctor output, and the exact message differences between known migration cases and unsupported schema errors.
close_reason: Completed: added shared stale-config diagnostics, migration-aware startup and refresh failures, doctor reporting and doctor --fix integration, plus spec-backed unit and e2e coverage for known migrations versus unsupported config problems.

title: Fix missing Yazelix plugin permission popup on fresh Zellij startup
description: On fresh Yazelix launches under Zellij 0.44, the pane orchestrator/popup runner permission popup may never appear even after clearing Zellij runtime/cache/share state. Only zjstatus gets a persisted permission block, while Yazelix plugins remain ungranted until permissions are seeded manually in permissions.kdl.
notes: Added repo-level recovery path on 2026-03-27: `yzx repair zellij-permissions` now seeds the tracked/runtime permission blocks for the pane orchestrator and popup runner into ~/.cache/zellij/permissions.kdl and tells the user to restart Yazelix. This is an explicit recovery/workaround path, not the root-cause fix for the missing popup.
close_reason: Superseded by the startup-handoff hardening and permission recovery work on 2026-03-27. The missing Yazelix plugin permission popup was a downstream symptom of the broken Zellij startup path; commit 5043aeb removed the boot-time plugin IPC and duplicate launch-tree hazard, and commit 142e4c8 added an explicit recovery path for stale permission state.

title: Fix Yazelix launch hanging after Zellij config generation
description: Yazelix sometimes hangs during startup after the welcome prompt and after successfully regenerating the Zellij config. The visible stall point is after the final 'Config will auto-regenerate when source files change' line, before the Zellij UI takes over. This should be tracked separately from the stale zjstatus layout-label bug.
design: Treat this as a startup handoff bug, not a status-bar naming bug. The key boundary is the transition from Yazelix's launch/setup flow into the actual Zellij UI. Investigate whether the hang is in the included terminal path, start_yazelix_inner handoff, wrapper interaction, plugin permission state, or Zellij startup state after config generation. Avoid bundling unrelated fixes. Keep the bug scoped to restoring reliable startup first; layout-label correctness is a separate issue.
acceptance_criteria: Yazelix no longer hangs after printing that Zellij configuration was generated successfully. From a normal launch, the UI reliably transitions into the Zellij session after that point. Add regression coverage or a defended diagnostic contract around the launch handoff, and document the exact failure boundary that was fixed.
notes: March 27 fresh-state evidence: after clearing /tmp/zellij-$UID, /run/user/$UID/zellij, ~/.cache/zellij, ~/.local/share/zellij, and the permission cache, a new launch still recreated the bad state. Zellij restored only the cached zjstatus permission block; neither yazelix_pane_orchestrator.wasm nor yazelix_popup_runner.wasm reached the permission-prompt stage. The launch also spawned two separate Zellij 0.44.0 servers from a clean slate, each with its own launch_sidebar_yazi/set_workspace_root path. This means the missing Yazelix plugin permission popup is a downstream symptom, not the root cause. Current strongest boundary: duplicate server/session creation or earlier Zellij 0.44 startup corruption before Yazelix plugins can finish loading.
close_reason: Completed by the March 27 startup-handoff fixes. Commit 5043aeb (Harden Zellij startup handoff) removed the startup-time sidebar launch path that was implicated in the duplicate-server / pre-UI stall boundary, and commit 142e4c8 added an explicit permission recovery path for already-corrupted Zellij plugin state. The observed hang after successful Zellij config generation is no longer the active failure mode; any future recurrence should come back as a fresh regression with new evidence.

title: Verify whether the LiteLLM PyPI compromise affected Yazelix
description: Audit whether the malicious LiteLLM PyPI releases affected Yazelix directly or through any shipped dependency or package surface.
notes: Assessment: no litellm or LiteLLM references were found in the repo, shipped config, or package declarations. Yazelix does not appear to ship or pin LiteLLM directly in its maintained runtime surface, so this issue is treated as not affected.
close_reason: Not affected: no litellm or LiteLLM references were found in the repo, shipped config, or maintained package declarations, so Yazelix does not appear to ship the compromised PyPI package in its supported runtime surface.

title: Audit large files and identify worthwhile refactors
description: Inspect Yazelix's largest or most complex files and decide which ones deserve real refactoring versus which ones are large for legitimate reasons. The goal is to create a justified shortlist, not to split files reflexively.
design: Use delete-first reasoning rather than assuming every large file is bad. Consider file size, complexity, churn, ownership boundaries, and whether the file is hard to understand or hard to test. Prefer identifying a small number of high-value refactors with concrete boundaries over creating a blanket mandate to break up large files everywhere.
acceptance_criteria: There is a ranked shortlist of large files that are worth refactoring, with concrete reasons and suggested seams. Files that are large but acceptable are explicitly called out as such. Follow-up implementation beads are narrower than this audit umbrella.
notes:
2026-04-09 continued public-surface cleanup:
- Internalized file-local `ascii_art.nu` helpers that only supported the local welcome/screen rendering pipeline.
- Internalized `is_palette_eligible_command` in `yzx/command_palette_catalog.nu`; the real exported surface is `get_palette_menu_items`.
- Verification: screen canonical tests, workspace palette-eligibility regression, syntax validation, and traceability validation pass.

2026-04-09 completion:
- The large-file/refactor audit is now recorded in `docs/streamlining_audit_2026_04.md`.
- The doc names the acceptable large files, the remaining worthwhile seams, and the specific delete-first rationale for stopping the broad umbrella here.
close_reason: Completed

title: README: refresh the project front door after recent architecture and packaging changes
description:
Update the main README so it matches the current v15 Yazelix product instead of older branch-era or Classic-era assumptions. The front page should describe one live supported line, the current workspace-focused scope, the first-party Ghostty path, layouts, and the fast popup-pane and command-menu experience.

This bead also owns the README-side release-surface cleanup: the README should stop implying that v14 remains supported, that dynamic runtime management is still in scope, or that the front page should duplicate release history.
design:
Treat this as a front-door documentation pass, not a changelog dump. The README should explain what Yazelix is today, how the core workspace UX hangs together, and where to go for deeper docs. It should present v15 as the only supported line and explain the surviving shape honestly: workspace entry, layouts, first-party Ghostty, Yazi/Helix/Zellij integration, fast toggleable popup and menu panes, and the trimmed runtime surface behind them.

Do not preserve the old two-lines story, the recommendation to stay on v14, or wording that treats dynamic runtime management as a feature Yazelix still owns. The README should not act like a second changelog; release history belongs in CHANGELOG.md, docs/upgrade_notes.toml, and docs/history.md. It should also avoid overexposing maintainer-only internals on the front page while still linking to architecture docs for readers who want them.
acceptance_criteria: The README presents v15 as the live supported Yazelix line, accurately explains the current feature set and architecture at a front-door level, includes the fast popup/menu and first-party Ghostty story where relevant, and stops implying ongoing v14 support or dynamic runtime-management scope. The README points cleanly to deeper docs instead of duplicating release notes or long historical prose, and the main commands/features referenced there are rechecked against the live v15 surface.
close_reason: Completed: README now presents v15 as the live supported line, moves the tagged-v14 block into historical context, and documents Ghostty, layouts, popup/menu, and the trimmed command surface.

title: Test suite governance: keep every test relevant
description: Define how Yazelix decides whether a test belongs in the suite, which suite it belongs in, and when an existing test should be removed. The goal is to prevent the test surface from growing forever without clear relevance.
design: Start from first principles: every test should defend a real user-visible behavior, a regression that already happened, an integration boundary, a source-of-truth invariant, or an explicitly documented contract. This policy must apply to both new and existing tests. Define the actual Yazelix suite surfaces explicitly: the default yzx dev test path, optional heavier lanes such as sweep or visual integration checks, CI-only checks, and cheap local maintainer hooks like prek. Require a lightweight inventory of the current suites by test file or suite entrypoint, classify each bucket by the kind of contract it protects, and define what happens when a test cannot be tied to a living contract, regression, invariant, or accepted user-visible behavior: remove it, demote it out of the default suite, or explicitly grandfather and justify it. Prefer fewer higher-signal tests over broad shallow coverage.
acceptance_criteria: Yazelix has a written test-policy document or issue design note that defines: 1. the real suite categories and command entrypoints (yzx dev test, optional heavier lanes, CI-only checks, and prek); 2. admission criteria for new tests; 3. retention and pruning rules for existing tests; and 4. when a test belongs in the default suite versus an optional lane. Existing tests are inventoried at least at the suite or file level against that policy, and at least one low-value or redundant test is removed, demoted, or explicitly grandfathered with justification. Future testing work can point to this policy when deciding whether a test is worth adding and where it belongs.
notes: Final shape: public test model simplified again after the aggressive prune. yzx dev test now runs the normal non-sweep automated regression suite with explicit membership: test_yzx_commands.nu, test_yzx_extra_regressions.nu, and test_reuse_mode.nu. The public --extended flag was removed because only a tiny number of extra checks remained and the split no longer earned its UX cost. Internal organization remains: core yzx contracts plus a small extra-regression bundle. The default suite currently covers 20 core checks + 6 extra regressions + 1 reuse-mode regression. yzx dev test --all now means default suite plus sweep + visual. Verified: validate_syntax, yzx dev test, direct core/extra/reuse entrypoints, validate_specs, and validate_readme_version all pass.
close_reason: Completed: added test-suite governance spec, switched to explicit default-suite membership, aggressively pruned low-value tests, and simplified the public yzx dev test model.

title: Repo cleanup pass for legacy and unused artifacts
description: Do a deliberate cleanup pass across the repository to remove legacy, dead, or clearly unused files, commands, docs references, and compatibility paths that no longer serve the current Yazelix runtime model. Keep the pass conservative: prefer deleting things that are demonstrably stale over broad aesthetic churn.
design: Treat this as a conservative pruning pass, not an aesthetic refactor. Target legacy compatibility paths, stale docs references, dead commands, unused helper files, superseded repo-era assumptions, and external-tool artifacts that are no longer part of the intended Yazelix workflow. Every deletion should be justified by one of: no remaining callers, superseded by a newer path, no longer part of the supported public surface, or contradicted by the current architecture/runtime model. Prefer removal over replacement when behavior is no longer needed, and avoid broad style churn or speculative rewrites just to make the repo look cleaner.
acceptance_criteria: The repo leaves the pass with less unjustified surface area: at least one real stale, duplicated, superseded, or unused artifact is removed from the categories touched, any removed public surface is scrubbed from docs/help text, and the default test suite still passes. Leave behind a short inventory of what was removed versus intentionally kept. Net repo LOC should decrease unless a small amount of new code is explicitly justified as necessary to enable a larger cleanup or add a validator that supports future pruning.
notes:
Partial progress already landed in 5253b00, 591edee, 7aef715, and 4857fcb, but the cleanup lane is not exhausted. Keep this bead open as an ongoing high-priority repo-hardening pass for deleting legacy, dead, redundant, or stale artifacts that are still discoverable.

2026-04-09 conservative delete-first cleanup:
- Removed definition-only dead surfaces with no repo/docs/config callers instead of inventing more wrappers or keeping compatibility shims alive.
- Deleted the unused terminal-config compatibility shim `resolve_terminal_config_from_env` from `utils/terminal_launcher.nu`.
- Deleted the unused Helix probe `is_helix_running_test` from `integrations/helix.nu`.
- Deleted the unused sidebar cwd export `get_active_sidebar_cwd` from `integrations/yazi_sidebar_state.nu`.

title: Add a shared migration engine and schema-aware yzx config migrate
description: Add the shared migration engine that knows about supported config removals, renames, moves, and value replacements, and expose it through yzx config migrate. This should preserve user intent whenever the mapping is deterministic, stay separate from blunt reset and replace flows, and always back up the original config before writing.
design: Treat this as the foundation for the whole upgrade UX rather than as a one off command. The migration engine should own structured rules with version and date metadata, path and value matchers, rationale, manual fix text, and whether a change is safe to rewrite automatically. Prefer one canonical Nushell-friendly data shape and API for these rules so startup diagnostics, yzx doctor, yzx config migrate, and the first-run upgrade summary all consume the same metadata. The rule schema should be rich enough for validators to prove that breaking config changes are either covered by a migration rule or explicitly marked as manual only in the upgrade-notes contract. yzx config migrate should default to a non destructive preview, support an explicit apply path with backup, preserve supported user settings, and leave ambiguous or lossy cases untouched but clearly explained. Prefer focused behavior tests over help text checks.
acceptance_criteria: A maintainer or user can run yzx config migrate against stale yazelix.toml files and get a detailed preview of safe rewrites, skipped ambiguous cases, and the exact backup path used on apply. Known migrations such as removed widget tray entries are expressed as explicit rules rather than hard coded one offs, and the same rule metadata can be consumed by diagnostics, validators, and upgrade-summary surfaces. Add comprehensive unit tests for rule matching, ordering, safe versus ambiguous classification, preview rendering, apply behavior, backup creation, and rule metadata completeness. Add end to end scripts with detailed logging that exercise dry run and apply flows in temp config roots, log before and after TOML plus command output, and cover at least one removed value case, one renamed or moved field case, and one ambiguous case that must stay manual.
notes:
Planning refinements:
- Create a focused spec such as docs/specs/config_migration_engine.md.
- Prefer a stable command surface like yzx config migrate [--apply] with an explicit preview-first default.
- Prefer a small migration helper module that exposes rule discovery, rule matching, preview rendering, and apply operations separately so tests can defend each boundary.
- Non-goals: silent startup rewrites, auto-deleting unsupported user settings without explanation, and conflating migrate with config reset.
- Add a cheap validator that checks migration-rule completeness and wire it into the same local and CI contract lane used by the upgrade-notes work.
- Verification plan: canonical unit coverage for rule matching and apply semantics, plus at least one high-signal end to end Nushell script that logs temp HOME setup, input TOML, detected migrations, preview output, backup path, output TOML, and manual-only skips.
close_reason: Completed: added the shared config migration engine, preview-first yzx config migrate command, migration-rule validator, spec, and comprehensive unit + e2e coverage for deterministic and manual-only config migrations.

title: Continue removing unnecessary environment variables
description: Reduce the Yazelix launch/runtime environment contract so only true cross-process handoff variables and explicit override or test escape hatches remain. This lane should keep deleting dead or redundant Yazelix-owned env exports, especially where launch state currently mirrors config into shell env even though consumers can read config or state directly.
design:
This is not a purity contest. The job is to make environment variables stop pretending to be a second source of truth when config, runtime state, or explicit process arguments already exist.

Work from the real seam in the code:
- `nushell/scripts/utils/launch_state.nu`
- `nushell/scripts/utils/environment_bootstrap.nu`
- `nushell/scripts/utils/common.nu`
- `nushell/scripts/integrations/zellij.nu`
- `nushell/scripts/integrations/yazi.nu`
- `nushell/scripts/utils/detect_terminal.nu`
- `nushell/scripts/utils/helix_mode.nu`
- `nushell/scripts/utils/terminal_launcher.nu`
- `nushell/scripts/core/launch_yazelix.nu`
- `nushell/scripts/yzx/desktop.nu`
- `nushell/scripts/utils/constants.nu`

Classify remaining Yazelix-owned env vars into three buckets before deleting anything:
1. Keep:
- true cross-process contract or runtime identity (`YAZELIX_RUNTIME_DIR`, `YAZELIX_NU_BIN`, `IN_YAZELIX_SHELL`, `DEVENV_PROFILE`)
- explicit user or maintainer override/test surfaces (`YAZELIX_CONFIG_DIR`, `YAZELIX_CONFIG_OVERRIDE`, `YAZELIX_LAYOUT_OVERRIDE`, sweep/popup/bootstrap temp vars)
- process-selection state that is genuinely chosen at launch time (`YAZELIX_TERMINAL`, and likely the managed-editor handoff vars until those callers are redesigned)
2. Delete now:
- dead or effectively dead launch exports whose only remaining role is being exported and re-propagated
- current likely first-slice targets from the code are `YAZELIX_DEBUG_MODE`, `YAZELIX_ENABLE_SIDEBAR`, `YAZELIX_WELCOME_STYLE`, plus the stale `YAZELIX_ENV_VARS` constant inventory
3. Evaluate next:
- redundant aliases or config mirrors with live consumers
- current likely candidates are `YAZELIX_DIR` versus `YAZELIX_RUNTIME_DIR`, `YAZELIX_PREFERRED_TERMINAL`, and `YAZELIX_TERMINAL_CONFIG_MODE`

Delete-first rules:
- remove dead exports before touching real overrides
- prefer direct config/state reads for same-process consumers
- do not break real cross-process handoff into wrappers, popup flows, desktop launch, or sweep/test harnesses
- when a variable stays, make sure it stays for a defensible reason rather than habit
acceptance_criteria:
1. The lane produces a clearer keep/delete/evaluate contract for Yazelix-owned environment variables, grounded in the actual launch/runtime call graph.
2. Dead launch exports are removed from launch env generation and wrapper propagation instead of continuing as unused config mirrors.
3. Stale environment inventories such as `YAZELIX_ENV_VARS` stop pretending to be a maintained source of truth if they are no longer used.
4. Redundant aliases or config-mirror vars are only removed when call sites switch to direct config/state reads or a narrower explicit handoff.
5. Changes ship with focused regressions around the affected launch, wrapper, desktop, terminal, or editor flows rather than generic command-list tests.
notes: Code read for this refinement shows that the remaining env-var problem is concentrated in launch-state export and wrapper propagation, not spread evenly across the repo. The most suspicious current exports are `YAZELIX_DEBUG_MODE`, `YAZELIX_ENABLE_SIDEBAR`, and `YAZELIX_WELCOME_STYLE`, which are still added to launch env and Zellij wrapper env but do not appear to drive meaningful runtime behavior anymore. `YAZELIX_DIR` is now mostly a compatibility alias beside `YAZELIX_RUNTIME_DIR`, and `YAZELIX_PREFERRED_TERMINAL` and `YAZELIX_TERMINAL_CONFIG_MODE` look like later cleanup candidates once direct config/state reads are made explicit. Keep this bead focused on shrinking the real runtime contract, not on removing every env var on principle.
close_reason: Completed

title: Harden config and build errors
description: Make Yazelix consistently fail fast with high-signal errors around launch, config validation, rebuilds, refreshes, and other common startup or recovery failures.
design: Treat this as a reliability umbrella, not a single patch. One important slice is already done: the maintainer update flow now uses canary-gated `yzx dev update` behavior so broken lockfile updates stay local instead of propagating. The remaining work is to harden the broader user-facing reliability surface: startup and launcher failures, classification of config versus generated-state versus host-environment problems, and consistent rebuild or refresh failure reporting. Keep this epic as a parent tracker and push concrete work into child issues rather than letting it absorb unrelated error-quality ideas.
acceptance_criteria: The remaining high-risk launch, config, refresh, and rebuild flows surface actionable errors instead of silent or low-signal failures. The child issues under this epic should cover startup error hardening, failure classification, and rebuild or refresh reporting with focused tests and logs for each surface.
close_reason: Completed the reliability slices for hermetic tests, startup/launch hardening, failure classification, refresh/rebuild reporting, and CI/Home Manager coverage

title: Packaging-readiness refactor
description: Decouple the runtime from the repo checkout so Yazelix can be packaged and installed cleanly instead of assuming it lives inside ~/.config/yazelix as a writable source tree.
design: This is the architectural prerequisite behind serious packaging work. Inventory the hardcoded path assumptions, separate shipped assets from user config and generated state, and centralize the runtime path model so core flows work from an installed runtime just as well as from a git checkout. Be explicit about what becomes packaged data, what remains user-owned config, and what Yazelix should generate or cache at runtime.
acceptance_criteria: Core launch, env, refresh, and managed-tool flows no longer require a repo checkout as their runtime root. Add unit tests for path resolution helpers and config/state root derivation, plus end-to-end smoke tests that run the runtime from an installed or relocated path with detailed logs showing resolved asset, config, and state locations.
close_reason: Core packaging-readiness work is complete: split config/runtime roots, routed core launch/config/runtime helpers through the runtime model, removed repo-shaped callers, and added relocated-runtime smoke coverage proving core commands work away from the repo checkout. Remaining FlakeHub evaluation stays open as an orthogonal follow-up.

title: Rollback system
description: Add a real rollback story so users can recover from bad updates or broken generated state without manually reconstructing their environment.
design: The rollback model should match Yazelix's actual failure modes: bad config merges, broken generated assets, failed updates, and rebuild regressions. Define what gets snapshotted, where rollback metadata lives, how users discover the feature, and how safety is preserved when the rollback target spans config, generated state, or environment artifacts. Avoid a fake rollback that only restores one narrow file while leaving the rest of the runtime inconsistent.
acceptance_criteria: Users have a documented rollback path that can restore the supported runtime state after a bad change. Add unit tests for snapshot metadata and retention logic, plus e2e scripts that create a broken state, roll back, and verify the recovered launch path with detailed logs of the stored snapshot and restored targets.
close_reason: No longer needed; Yazelix is sturdy enough without a dedicated rollback system

The contract should state which restored state is supported, which state is intentionally unsupported, how per-window config snapshots interact with restored Zellij sessions, and which diagnostics users/maintainers get when a restored session is incomplete or stale.
design: Reason from first principles: Zellij owns low-level session serialization; Yazelix owns product coherence and diagnostics. Keep restore explicit because users may keep many Yazelix windows open concurrently.
acceptance_criteria: A canonical contract or bead decision defines supported/unsupported restored state, explicit restore entrypoints, config-snapshot/runtime compatibility behavior, and at least one concrete diagnostic/verification path.

Update AGENTS.md so Beads point to contracts, while contracts stay planning-system-independent. Delete instructions that say canonical files must include Bead IDs. Keep only the surviving guidance needed to decide when a durable contract is warranted, where it lives, and how it is defended.

title: Add delete-first canonical contracts validation gate
description: Add a validator that keeps the canonical contracts surface from accumulating planning, research, migration, and Bead-traceability content again.
design: Delete-first scope: remove the old permissive spec validator assumptions before adding new checks. Replace the requirement that every spec names a Bead with the inverse rule for canonical contracts: contracts should not mention Beads unless the contract is specifically about Beads/GitHub/planning architecture. Reject or quarantine planning terms such as Follow-Up Beads, Prototype Outcome, Evaluation, Historical planning note, and broad research diary sections in docs/contracts. Prefer a small denylist plus explicit allowlist over a complicated classifier.
acceptance_criteria: The validator no longer requires Bead traceability for canonical contracts; docs/contracts files are checked for stale docs/specs references, Bead IDs, follow-up bead sections, and planning/evaluation language unless allowlisted; Beads-specific contracts can be exempted explicitly; existing tests are updated to defend the new rule; validation fails clearly when a weak planning file is added to the canonical surface.
close_reason: Completed: validate-contracts now checks docs/contracts for stale docs/specs paths, Bead ids, planning markers, and contract verification hygiene; focused Rust tests defend the new failure modes.

title: Delete-first docs information architecture boundary
description: Define the smallest durable Yazelix documentation architecture after the canonical contracts cleanup. The goal is to delete or demote ambiguous documentation categories before renaming, reorganizing, or adding navigation layers.
design: Delete-first scope: question whether each current docs category deserves to exist as a source of truth. Eliminate duplicate source-of-truth surfaces before moving files. The surviving taxonomy should separate README/front door, user guides, maintainer guides, canonical contracts, architecture maps/inventories, historical archives, and generated/reference data. Prefer fewer top-level concepts over preserving every current folder or document type.
acceptance_criteria: A short docs information-architecture decision exists in the bead notes or a surviving docs architecture file; duplicate categories are identified for deletion or demotion; docs/contracts ownership is distinguished from user guides, maintainer docs, history, and inventories; no new docs category is introduced unless an existing ambiguous category is removed or merged; follow-up implementation work can use the taxonomy without re-litigating the folder model.
close_reason: Completed delete-first docs information architecture boundary in docs/documentation_architecture.md and linked it from the architecture map

title: Make canonical contracts replace bloated specs folder
description: Turn the current docs/specs surface into a canonical Yazelix contracts surface. The folder should contain durable product, runtime, subsystem, integration, and validation contracts only, not research notes, implementation diaries, migration plans, or Bead-driven planning history.
design: Preferred direction: rename docs/specs to docs/contracts unless implementation discovers a stronger existing naming constraint. Contracts should be normative and current: what Yazelix promises, which subsystem owns it, and which checks defend it. Beads should own decisions, investigations, rejected alternatives, implementation sequencing, and closure evidence. Contract files should not mention Bead ids or follow-up beads except for contracts whose subject is Beads/GitHub/planning architecture. Update AGENTS.md, spec-driven workflow docs, inventories, validators, and references so the source-of-truth rule is enforced mechanically where practical.
acceptance_criteria: docs/specs is renamed or replaced by a canonical contracts surface; live contract files are rewritten to remove research/prototype/history/follow-up content; weak planning/evaluation markdown is moved into Bead notes, normal docs, or deleted; canonical contracts no longer reference Bead ids except Beads-specific contracts; AGENTS.md and spec workflow guidance reflect the new Beads-to-contracts direction; validators/tests/docs reference the new path; spec_inventory is replaced with a contracts inventory that contains only canonical contracts; validation passes without stale docs/specs references.
close_reason: Completed: canonical contracts now replace the bloated specs folder with a delete-first docs architecture, workflow, validation gate, and cleaned inventory.

title: Document config immutability and snapshot diagnostics
description: Make the new immutable-config behavior visible and understandable in user-facing docs and diagnostics once the runtime contract lands.
design: Do this after the code path is settled. Avoid adding docs that imply hot-reload semantics unless the implementation explicitly supports them.
acceptance_criteria: README or focused docs explain that config edits apply to the next Yazelix window or yzx restart, not live panes; yzx status or doctor exposes the active snapshot path/source in a concise way; stale snapshot/missing snapshot diagnostics are human-readable; README edits preserve the repo no-final-period style.
close_reason: Completed: status/inspect expose session_config_snapshot diagnostics, bad snapshots report readable errors, and docs explain launch-time config immutability.

title: Evaluate removing persistent_sessions from Yazelix config
description: Persistent Zellij sessions complicate the per-window immutable config snapshot model and are not well-covered by current behavior tests. Evaluate whether Yazelix should remove persistent_sessions/session_name from the supported config surface, keep them behind a clearer contract, or replace them with an explicit advanced launch mode.
design:
Decision: remove `zellij.persistent_sessions` and `zellij.session_name` from the supported Yazelix config surface.

First-principles reasoning:
- Yazelix's core workspace model should let a user open as many Yazelix windows as they want, with each window owning a stable launch-time config snapshot and live session state.
- The new immutable snapshot model says config edits apply to the next window or to the current window through an explicit `yzx restart` transition.
- Persistent Zellij sessions invert that model: later launches attach to one existing named session, fresh path/layout/shell/env intent is ignored, and already-running panes keep the environment/snapshot inherited when the session was first created.
- Keeping persistent sessions would require a separate product contract: snapshot mismatch detection, attach-vs-recreate policy, shared-session restart UX, last-client lifecycle semantics, and tests for all of that.
- That complexity is not justified for the trimmed Yazelix workspace core. Zellij's native session persistence can still exist outside Yazelix for users who intentionally want raw Zellij behavior.

Implementation direction:
- Remove `persistent_sessions` and `session_name` from `yazelix_default.toml`, the main config contract, Home Manager options, onboarding, startup facts, session facts/snapshot facts, status output, generated Zellij rendering inputs, and docs/specs that present persistent sessions as live product behavior.
- Simplify startup handoff to always create a fresh Yazelix session/window path instead of conditionally running `zellij list-sessions` and `zellij attach <session_name>`.
- Keep Zellij close behavior aligned with non-persistent Yazelix semantics; `on_force_close` should not be driven by removed config fields.
- Update the non-persistent window contract into the normal window-session contract. Demote or delete the persistent-window spec from the live spec inventory.
- Migration UX should not silently change a user who explicitly had `persistent_sessions = true`. That case should produce a clear config diagnostic explaining the feature was removed and that the fields must be removed manually. Stale default/false fields can be considered for auto-pruning only if the implementation makes the no-op nature obvious and safe.
- Do not keep legacy config aliases by default. If a removed-field diagnostic exists, it is an error/migration aid, not a compatibility path.

Follow-up implementation should happen before the snapshot handoff is hardened, so the snapshot work does not spend effort preserving a shared-session branch Yazelix intends to delete.
acceptance_criteria: Decision records whether persistent_sessions stays, changes, or is removed; analysis covers multiple Yazelix windows, per-session config snapshots, attach semantics, migration impact, and test requirements before implementation
close_reason: Decision recorded: remove persistent_sessions/session_name from supported Yazelix config rather than preserving a separate shared-session product contract.

title: Add yzx edit cursors sidecar target
description: Add a yzx edit cursors command target so users can open ~/.config/yazelix/user_configs/yazelix_cursors.toml directly after cursor settings moved out of yazelix.toml
design: Follow the existing yzx edit command pattern and route cursors to the active cursor sidecar path. Bootstrap/resolve the active config surface as needed so the sidecar exists before opening. Keep existing yzx edit config behavior unchanged.
acceptance_criteria:
yzx edit cursors opens the active user cursor sidecar path
Existing yzx edit targets keep working
Help or command metadata exposes the cursors target where edit targets are listed
Focused tests cover the target path resolution without launching an interactive editor
notes: Implementation: added cursors as a first-class yzx edit target resolving to user_configs/yazelix_cursors.toml. The target table now bootstraps through active config surface resolution when available, command metadata exposes yzx edit cursors, README and CLI docs mention it, and a public yzx_control regression defends yzx edit cursors --print without launching an editor. Verification: yzx dev rust fmt --check; yzx dev rust check; yzx dev rust test edit_commands; cargo test --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml -p yazelix_core --test yzx_control_public_commands yzx_control_edit_cursors_prints_cursor_sidecar_path; cargo test --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml -p yazelix_core --test yzx_core_command_metadata; yzx_repo_validator validate-rust-test-traceability; git diff --check.
close_reason: Completed: yzx edit cursors now opens or prints the active cursor sidecar.

title: Pretty-print runtime repair summary errors
description:
Make runtime-materialization.repair --summary render concise human-readable failures instead of dumping raw JSON envelopes into activation logs and popup panes

## Context
Home Manager activation and transient repair surfaces use runtime-materialization.repair --summary for human output
Success already prints one clean line, but errors still fall through the global yzx_core JSON envelope writer
The resulting unsupported_config JSON blob is technically useful but ugly and overwhelming for users
design:
Keep the default yzx_core error envelope unchanged for machine-readable callers
Only summary-mode runtime repair should use human stderr formatting
For unsupported_config, show the headline, count, affected fields, and next actions without dumping the full diagnostics object
Avoid hiding details: point users to yzx doctor --verbose for the full report
acceptance_criteria:
runtime-materialization.repair --summary prints a concise human error when repair fails
Non-summary runtime-materialization.repair still emits the JSON error envelope
unsupported_config diagnostics are summarized by path/headline and actionable next steps
Regression coverage defends the summary-mode failure surface
notes: Implementation: runtime-materialization.repair now carries an error output mode. Summary-mode repair failures render a concise human stderr block with reason, blocking issue count, diagnostic headlines, and deduplicated next steps. Non-summary repair failures keep the JSON error envelope. Verification: yzx dev rust fmt --check; yzx dev rust check; focused runtime_materialization_repair_summary_prints_human_config_error; cargo test --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml -p yazelix_core --test yzx_core_config_normalize; yzx_repo_validator validate-rust-test-traceability; git diff --check.
close_reason: Completed: runtime repair summary failures now print a concise human error instead of raw JSON.

title: Cull removal-only absence tests from governed suites
description:
Remove weak tests whose main value is confirming that a deliberately removed implementation detail or artifact is absent, and add a repo guard so this pattern does not keep reappearing

## Context
Example: shipped_default_registry_parses_without_party only verifies that a preset we intentionally removed is not present
This kind of test does not defend user-visible behavior, a real regression, config compatibility, or an integration boundary
It creates maintenance noise and can pass even when the actual behavior users care about is broken
design:
Use the existing governed-test strength model rather than adding a blanket ban on all negative assertions
Keep useful negative tests when they defend a real contract such as rejecting invalid config, preventing unsafe fallback, or blocking a known bug
Remove or rewrite tests that only assert the absence of old names, removed presets, deleted files, or implementation trivia
Add a validator or rubric check that flags removal-only absence tests by name/marker/content and requires a Defends/Regression/Invariant justification that points to behavior, not cleanup history
acceptance_criteria:
Inventory existing governed tests for removal-only absence checks, including shipped_default_registry_parses_without_party
Remove or rewrite each weak absence test so remaining coverage defends active behavior
Add a repo guard that catches new low-value removal-only tests without rejecting meaningful negative-contract tests
Document the distinction in the test-strength guidance or validator failure message
Run the affected test suites and validator checks
close_reason: Completed: removed weak removal-only absence assertions and added a rust traceability guard for cleanup-only absence tests with behavior-backed exceptions.

title: Investigate popup failure from config drift in stale Yazelix sessions
description:
Investigate why popup/menu panes fail hard when an already-running older Yazelix session sees a user config that has changed under a newer Yazelix version

## Context
Observed in an older Yazelix window after updating Yazelix and changing config schema
Opening a yzx_popup pane reported unknown config fields in ~/.config/yazelix/user_configs/yazelix.toml and blocked the popup entirely
Example fields: editor.initial_sidebar_state, terminal.ghostty_trail_duration, zellij.screen_saver_enabled, zellij.screen_saver_idle_seconds, zellij.screen_saver_style
The popup pane should not become unusable just because the user config has fields from a newer or older Yazelix schema, especially when the current session was already running before the update
design:
Separate startup/session-critical config validation from popup/menu rendering requirements
Determine whether popup panes can use already-materialized session facts, a tolerant read path, or a smaller config subset instead of full strict config normalization
Keep strict validation for commands that actually mutate or regenerate runtime state
Avoid silently accepting bad config for normal startup; the goal is stale-session resilience for transient popup/menu panes
acceptance_criteria:
Reproduce or model the stale-session popup failure path
Identify which popup/menu code path performs full config validation and why
Decision or fix defines whether popup panes should tolerate unknown config fields, use a cached session contract, or show a nonblocking warning
Add a behavior-backed regression test or validator case if a fix is implemented
notes:
Rust dependency decision: no new production or dev crates. This is a control-plane/config-read behavior fix using existing in-house config loading and test fixtures. If implementation stays in Nushell or POSIX, the same no-new-dependency decision applies. Packaging impact: none expected.
Implementation: transient-pane facts now use a tiny tolerant TOML subset reader for zellij.popup_program and popup geometry, so unrelated stale/unknown config fields do not block popup/menu pane construction. Popup panes already running in a Yazelix session no longer recompute the full runtime env from strict config before launching their program; they reuse the current process runtime environment. Strict config validation remains on startup/materialization/doctor-style paths. Verification: yzx dev rust fmt --check; yzx dev rust check; yzx dev rust test transient_pane_facts_compute_ignores_unrelated_unknown_fields; yzx dev rust test yzx_control_popup_pane_tolerates_unrelated_unknown_config_fields; cargo test --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml -p yazelix_core --test yzx_core_owned_facts --test yzx_control_workspace_surface; validate-rust-test-traceability; git diff --check.
close_reason: Completed: popup/menu transient facts tolerate unrelated stale config fields, and in-pane popup launch no longer depends on full strict config normalization.

title: Make Ghostty the default packaged runtime
description: Restore Ghostty as the default Yazelix packaged runtime so the cursor-trail identity is present by default, while keeping WezTerm available as an explicit runtime variant.
notes: Implementation: made Ghostty the default packaged runtime across flake/package/Home Manager/runtime helper defaults; changed the default terminal order to ghostty then wezterm; kept WezTerm available as #yazelix_wezterm and runtime_variant = "wezterm"; updated user-facing docs and README generated surface. Verification: yzx dev rust fmt --check; yzx dev rust check; focused Rust tests normalized_configured_terminals_defaults_to_ghostty_then_wezterm and full_launch_materialization_defaults_to_ghostty_then_wezterm; validate-config-surface-contract; validate-flake-interface; validate-readme-version; validate-upgrade-contract; validate-rust-test-traceability; nix derivation show confirmed #runtime writes runtime_variant ghostty and #runtime_wezterm writes wezterm. Note: taplo fmt --check reports existing whole-file TOML formatting drift, so no broad TOML reformat was applied.
close_reason: Completed: Ghostty is now the default packaged runtime and terminal order, with WezTerm retained as the explicit packaged variant.

title: Collapse boids diagonal sprites to cardinal triangles
description: The filled diagonal triangle boids look bad in terminal cells because character height is roughly twice cell width. Keep diagonal movement internally, but render boids using only cardinal directional sprites. Predator should use a small ball body for east/west and a larger ball body for north/south.
design: Keep the existing simulation and 8-way direction classifier if useful, but map visual sprites to cardinal directions. Diagonal visual selection should choose the dominant/readable cardinal axis rather than corner triangles. Prey use ◀ ▶ ▲ ▼. Predator keeps body-plus-tip: east/west use a smaller ball body with horizontal triangles; north/south use a larger ball body with vertical triangles. Update tests to assert no diagonal filled-corner glyphs appear in boids output and that predator horizontal/vertical body sizes differ.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Boids output no longer uses ◤ ◥ ◢ ◣. 2. Prey render with only ◀ ▶ ▲ ▼. 3. Predator east/west use a small ball body. 4. Predator north/south use a larger ball body. 5. Movement remains deterministic and existing boids tests pass.
close_reason: Completed: boids now render only cardinal visual sprites; diagonal movement maps to horizontal cardinal glyphs, corner triangles are removed, and predator uses a small body for east/west plus a larger body for north/south. Tests defend the glyph set, body-size split, frame dimensions, and deterministic motion.

title: Make boids sprites point with filled triangles
description: The dotted V-shaped boids are too abstract. Update the boids sprite vocabulary so creatures visibly point in their movement direction using filled triangle glyphs such as ◀, ▶, ▲, ▼, and filled corner triangles for diagonals, while keeping predator/prey color and size distinctions.
design: Keep the existing 8-way direction classifier and sprite renderer. Replace abstract braille-only prey/predator glyph tables with filled geometric triangle glyphs. Cardinal directions use ◀, ▶, ▲, and ▼; diagonals use ◤, ◥, ◢, and ◣. Preserve the larger predator footprint and existing behavior tests, updating visual tests to assert filled directional glyphs.
acceptance_criteria: 1. East and west boids visibly point with ▶ and ◀ glyphs. 2. North and south boids visibly point with ▲ and ▼ glyphs. 3. Diagonal directions use distinct filled corner triangles. 4. Predator remains visually larger than prey. 5. Boids tests pass.
close_reason: Completed: boids now use filled directional triangle prey glyphs with ◀ ▶ ▲ ▼ and filled corner diagonals, while predator uses a larger red body-plus-tip sprite with a large circle body. Tests defend cardinal and diagonal glyph selection, predator/prey visual size split, and frame dimensions.

title: Make boids render as V-shaped dotted creatures
description: Replace the current arrow-like boids glyphs with V-shaped dotted creatures so the welcome animation reads more organic while preserving predator/prey colors and deterministic rendering.
design: Keep the existing in-house boids simulation and scoped renderer. Add an 8-direction visual direction classifier so diagonals have distinct dotted V silhouettes. Use small V-shaped prey glyphs and a distinct larger/denser predator glyph without adding heavy media assets. Update tests to defend diagonal glyph selection and predator/prey visual identity.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Boids no longer render with arrow glyphs. 2. Boid visual direction supports diagonals. 3. Predator-mode prey keep one shared prey color. 4. Predator glyphs remain visually distinct from prey glyphs. 5. Existing frame dimensions and deterministic animation tests still pass.
close_reason: Completed: boids now render as dotted V-shaped sprites with 8-way direction selection, predator sprites use a larger two-row dotted footprint rendered above prey, arrows are removed, and tests defend diagonal visuals, frame dimensions, predator/prey split, and deterministic motion.

title: Refine boids predator visuals and motion
description: The first behavior-backed boids pass added predator, schools, and flow variants, but the current UX needs a tighter visual and motion follow-up. Predator/prey colors should remain distinct, all prey should share one prey color, the creatures should read as directional creatures rather than plain block cells, flow should be removed because it looks weird, and boids motion should feel faster.
design: Refine the boids renderer around the variants that survive actual product taste. Keep predator and prey visually distinct, but do not assign arbitrary per-prey colors in predator mode. Replace or supplement block glyphs with a directional glyph system, preferably dot-built arrows or another terminal-safe direction cue that makes movement legible without relying on heavy assets. Remove boids_flow from public style resolution, docs, config comments, and random boids-family selection. Increase movement speed in a bounded way that still preserves readable flocking and deterministic tests. Use the existing in-house boids engine; no new crates expected.
acceptance_criteria:
1. boids_predator renders predators and prey with different colors, while all prey share the same prey color.
2. Boids render with a cooler directional creature glyph instead of only plain block cells, with tests defending orientation or glyph selection behavior.
3. boids_flow is removed from public screen/welcome style resolution, docs/config comments, and random boids-family selection.
4. Random boids-family selection is rebalanced across the remaining boids variants.
5. Boids movement is visibly faster while keeping deterministic frame output and bounded terminal cost.
6. Tests defend the predator/prey color contract, flow removal, remaining random selection, and faster movement behavior.
close_reason: Completed: restored the 2s welcome default, removed boids_flow from the public style surface, made boids/predator use dot-arrow directional glyphs with shared prey color and faster motion, rebalanced random boids selection across predator/schools, rendered boids welcome as an unframed broad surface, and trimmed inline welcome padding to avoid terminal autowrap artifacts.

title: Add behavior-backed boids style variants
description: The current boids renderer uses several colors for visual identity, but behavior has no matching classes. Add explicit boids variants so color/style communicates real behavior instead of arbitrary per-index coloring.
design: Implement three boids style variants: boids_predator, boids_schools, and boids_flow. Predator should be ambient predator/prey: mostly prey, 1-3 slow predators, no killing/population changes, predators disturb flock movement without making the welcome stressful. Schools should use 2-3 species where same-species boids align/cohere and all boids separate. Flow should use a single flock with a slow deterministic flow-field/wind bias for wave-like movement. Random selection inside the boids family should choose the three variants with equal chance. Keep the overall random welcome family split separate: Game of Life, Boids family, Mandelbrot.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Public screen/welcome style resolution accepts boids_predator, boids_schools, and boids_flow. 2. boids remains either an alias for the safest default variant or is intentionally kept as the legacy/basic style, with the choice documented. 3. Colors map to behavior classes/species/roles, not arbitrary boid index identity. 4. Random boids-family selection chooses predator, schools, and flow equally. 5. Tests defend each variant's behavioral contract and that random boids selection is balanced. 6. Docs/config comments list the new variants without implying unrelated package or renderer changes.
notes: Dependency decision before implementation: implement boids variants in-house with the existing yazelix_screen boids engine and std only. No production crates, no dev crates. Rejected alternatives: adding animation/physics crates would be disproportionate for deterministic terminal welcome behavior. Packaging impact: none.
close_reason: Completed: boids styles now include behavior-backed flow, predator, and schools variants with tests for variant resolution, motion, and color roles.

title: Make random welcome rotate equally across GOL boids and Mandelbrot
description: Current welcome randomization does not give equal exposure to the three live screen families. Mandelbrot is currently screen-only, while random welcome resolves through the retained Game of Life pool plus existing boids behavior. The desired UX is a fair 1/3 chance for Game of Life, boids, or Mandelbrot when welcome_style = random.
design: Keep explicit welcome styles supported. Change only the random welcome selection contract. Random should choose one family with equal probability: Game of Life, boids, Mandelbrot. For the Game of Life family, either pick a stable/default GOL variant or split the family share across existing GOL variants; document whichever rule is chosen. Use deterministic index/seed plumbing already present in front_door_render tests so tests can assert the rotation without host randomness. Do not make random choose static or logo.
acceptance_criteria: 1. welcome_style = random has a 1/3 family split across Game of Life, boids, and Mandelbrot. 2. Mandelbrot is accepted as an internal welcome style when selected explicitly or by random, while public yzx screen behavior remains unchanged. 3. Config metadata, default config comment, Home Manager option docs, and CLI/docs style lists are updated consistently. 4. Existing explicit styles still work. 5. Tests defend the fair family rotation and exclude static/logo from random.
notes: Dependency decision before implementation: use existing Rust std/in-house renderer logic only. No new production crates, no new dev crates. Random-family selection should reuse the existing deterministic seed/index plumbing in yazelix_screen/front-door tests. Packaging impact: none.
close_reason: Completed: welcome random now rotates evenly across Game of Life, boids variants, and Mandelbrot while yzx screen random remains Game-of-Life-only.

title: Add Ghostty and WezTerm runtime variants without heavy media tools
description: Replace broad runtime feature-matrix ideas with two simple install-time runtime variants: Ghostty for Yazelix identity/cursor shaders and WezTerm for better image/Sixel compatibility. Keep the baseline helper toolset broad enough for normal Yazi/Yazelix use, but do not add heavy media preview dependencies yet.
design: Expose only terminal-centered package variants for now: ghostty and wezterm. Include existing small/medium helpers in both variants: jq, fd, ripgrep, fzf, zoxide, p7zip, poppler, resvg, xclip, wl-clipboard, and add xsel on Linux if it is small enough in nixpkgs closure. Keep ffmpeg and ImageMagick out of both variants. Keep Nerd Fonts user/system-owned. Do not revive v14 arbitrary packs or runtime package mutation. Home Manager and flake package selection own the install closure; yazelix.toml only orders/chooses already-installed terminal behavior.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Flake exposes ghostty and wezterm package variants with clear names and keeps default mapped to the Ghostty variant. 2. Home Manager can select the runtime variant without changing mutable yazelix.toml ownership. 3. Both variants include the existing small preview/navigation/search helpers; Linux variants include xclip, wl-clipboard, and xsel if accepted by closure-size review. 4. Neither variant includes ffmpeg or ImageMagick. 5. Docs explain Ghostty identity/cursor-shader path versus WezTerm image/Sixel compatibility path. 6. yzx doctor/inspect can report the active runtime variant or a follow-up bead is created if that surface is not yet available.
notes: Dependency decision before implementation: packaging-only variant work; no Rust crates expected. Use existing Nix package inputs and in-repo package functions. Rejected alternatives: reviving v14-style mutable packs or adding heavy media dependencies. Packaging impact: flake/Home Manager variant selection and docs only.
close_reason: Completed: packaged runtime now has Ghostty default and WezTerm variants, records runtime_variant, keeps heavy media tools out, and includes xsel for Linux clipboard coverage.

title: Make Mandelbrot screen feel like a real fractal zoom
description: Replace the current Mandelbrot animation feel with a more authentic fractal zoom that uses a proper spatial loop rather than a shallow or fake-looking view interpolation.
design: Investigate and implement a real looping Mandelbrot camera path. The loop should preserve fractal continuity as much as practical in a terminal renderer: pick a visually rich target, use a smooth zoom trajectory, avoid obvious snap-back, and consider loop techniques such as zoom-out/zoom-in palindrome paths, crossfade-free cyclic paths, or known self-similar regions where the start/end relationship feels spatially justified. Keep it in-house/std and bounded for terminal runtime. Add tests for loop periodicity, bounded iteration cost, and structural frame progression, but also include a manual visual acceptance note because the core requirement is perceptual quality.
acceptance_criteria: yzx screen mandelbrot visibly reads as a real fractal zoom rather than color pulsing or fake panning; loop boundaries are not jarring; the selected camera path and rationale are documented in code or notes; CPU cost remains bounded on normal terminal sizes; tests defend deterministic output, loop periodicity, and nontrivial visible structure changes across the path; manual visual test notes are recorded before close.
notes: 2026-04-26 correction: the previous symmetric Seahorse Valley palindrome was rejected as the wrong model. The stricter follow-up bead yazelix-1vqn now owns the true Mandelbrot/minibrot loop contract: monotonic zoom into a baby Mandelbrot, then reset only when the mini copy visually matches the parent.
