design: Be explicit about which files still own deterministic policy and which are already just fixed orchestration. Do not let yzx/dev.nu keep hidden policy by default.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Each remaining maintainer and dev file has a next-action classification. 2. The retained Nu shell floor is defined explicitly. 3. The family target ceiling is reset to the new honest floor.
close_reason: Deletion budget defined and implemented: deleted yzx_test_helpers.nu (231 lines), dev test surface already delegates to Rust yzx_repo_maintainer

title: Shrink maintainer update and yzx dev shell floor
description: Delete more maintainer and dev Nu by narrowing yzx/dev.nu, maintainer/update_workflow.nu, plugin_build.nu, update_yazi_plugins.nu, and adjacent dev helpers to the irreducible external-tool boundary.
design: Keep only fixed git, gh, cargo, bd, and Nix orchestration in Nu when it remains smaller than the Rust alternative. Deterministic planning and report shaping must move into Rust-owned maintainer helpers.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The remaining maintainer and yzx dev floor has an explicit deletion budget. 2. Deterministic update and dev planning moves to Rust wherever it deletes a real Nu owner. 3. The maintainer/dev budget ceiling ratchets downward.
notes: Deleted materialization_dev_helpers.nu (100 lines) and config_normalize_test_helpers.nu (56 lines), inlined into callers. Deleted sync_managed_editor_cwd from managed_editor.nu (49 lines). Deleted dead functions from runtime_paths.nu (~55 lines): get_yazelix_config_dir, get_xdg_config_home, get_yazelix_user_config_dir, get_managed_helix_user_config_path, get_native_helix_config_path, get_generated_helix_config_path. Deleted dead functions from terminal_assets.nu (~12 lines): get_cursor_trail_shaders, get_ghostty_trail_effects, get_ghostty_mode_effects. Deleted dead config-state bridge helpers from yzx_core_bridge.nu (~41 lines): compute_config_state_via_yzx_core, run_yzx_core_from_env_json_command, build_runtime_command_env, build_runtime_command_error_surface, CONFIG_STATE_COMPUTE_COMMAND. Total project LOC now 4884.

title: Implement the typed integration owner cuts and delete wrapper debt
description: Execute the integration deletion budget by moving retained deterministic Yazi, Zellij, and managed-editor planning into Rust and deleting the targeted Nu wrappers and owners.
design: The cut is only honest if Rust becomes the end-to-end owner of the retained deterministic seam and the Nu integration owner materially disappears.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Planned typed integration owner cuts land. 2. Targeted wrapper or integration Nu files are deleted or materially reduced. 3. The integration budget ceiling and inventories are updated.
close_reason: Completed in earlier tranches

title: Define the deletion budget for the remaining integration Nu floor
description: Classify the remaining Yazi, Zellij, managed-editor, and wrapper Nu files into Rust-owner cuts, direct-shell survivors, or delete-now debt.
design: The key question is whether each file owns deterministic state and request shaping or only direct tool execution. Do not protect wrappers just because they are small.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Each surviving integration file is classified with an explicit next action. 2. The typed owner for every retained deterministic seam is named. 3. Any shell survivor has an explicit minimal boundary.
close_reason: Deletion budget defined and implemented: deleted helix.nu (4 lines), all other integration files remain with explicit stop conditions as Yazi plugin entrypoints

title: Collapse Yazi, Zellij, and managed-editor integration Nu into typed Rust owners
description: Delete the remaining integration-side Nu owners around integrations/zellij.nu, yazi.nu, managed_editor.nu, zellij_runtime_wrappers.nu, open_dir_in_pane.nu, zoxide_open_in_editor.nu, and launch_sidebar_yazi.nu.
design: Rust should own typed workspace, sidebar, Yazi, and managed-editor planning. Nushell may survive only where it directly executes external tools or shells into popup or pane boundaries.
acceptance_criteria: 1. A file-level deletion budget exists for the surviving integration owners. 2. Deterministic integration planning moves to Rust typed owners. 3. The integration budget ceiling ratchets downward without reviving wrappers.
notes:
Migrated open_dir_in_pane.nu (50 lines) to yzx_control zellij open-terminal. Migrated zoxide_open_in_editor.nu (40 lines) to yzx_control zellij open-editor. Integration family reduced from ~625 to ~520 lines. Total project LOC: 4794. Remaining integration surfaces: managed_editor.nu (243 lines), zellij.nu (177 lines), yazi.nu (101 lines), launch_sidebar_yazi.nu (14 lines).
2026-04-24 re-audit: the remaining integration Nu floor is now concentrated in managed_editor.nu (243 LOC), zellij.nu (177 LOC), yazi.nu (101 LOC), launch_sidebar_yazi.nu (14 LOC), plus popup/menu wrapper seams. Popup/menu wrappers should stay in Nu unless a future cut deletes the real mode-env, pane rename, and external-process execution boundary end to end instead of adding another Rust bridge. Follow-on children now split the remaining deterministic planning debt by managed-editor, Zellij/sidebar, and Yazi surfaces.
2026-04-24: Completed the remaining integration-owner cut. `yzx_control zellij open-editor` and `open-editor-cwd` now own the Yazi/editor flows, `configs/yazi` entrypoints call the Rust command surface directly, `launch_sidebar_yazi.nu` is reduced to a tiny Yazi exec wrapper, and the deleted Nu owners are `integrations/managed_editor.nu`, `integrations/yazi.nu`, `integrations/zellij.nu`, and `integrations/zoxide_open_in_editor.nu`.
close_reason: Completed

title: Implement the runtime-helper and bridge owner cuts
description: Execute the runtime-helper deletion budget by moving retained deterministic runtime fact and request shaping into Rust and deleting or collapsing the broad bridge owners.
design: This counts only if the broad helper or bridge owner disappears or becomes materially smaller. Do not add another Rust shim above the same Nu bridge.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The planned runtime-helper Rust owner cuts land. 2. The targeted Nu bridge or helper owners are deleted or materially reduced. 3. The Nushell budget and specs are updated to the new floor.
close_reason: Deleted 13 runtime-helper files (719 lines): runtime_env.nu, startup_facts.nu, integration_facts.nu, transient_pane_facts.nu, front_door_runtime.nu, config_report_rendering.nu, doctor_helix.nu, environment_bootstrap.nu, helix_mode.nu, safe_remove.nu, cursor_trail_helpers.nu, config_contract.nu, zjstatus_widget.nu. Inlined thin wrappers into callers. Updated nushell_budget.toml. Runtime helper family now 18 files / 1546 lines.

title: Define the deletion budget for the remaining runtime-helper and bridge floor
description: Name the exact surviving runtime-helper and bridge files, classify them by delete, Rust-own, or irreducible shell helper, and record the post-cut target ceiling for the family.
design: Be explicit about which helpers are truly shell-bound and which only survive because bridge debt was never removed. Keep this lane focused on core runtime helpers, not front-door UX wrappers.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The remaining runtime-helper allowlist is classified file by file. 2. Each proposed Rust owner cut names the Nu owner it will delete. 3. Any surviving shell helper has an explicit stop condition.
close_reason: Deletion budget defined and implemented: deleted runtime_env.nu, startup_facts.nu, integration_facts.nu, transient_pane_facts.nu, front_door_runtime.nu, config_report_rendering.nu, doctor_helix.nu, environment_bootstrap.nu, helix_mode.nu, safe_remove.nu, cursor_trail_helpers.nu, config_contract.nu, zjstatus_widget.nu, yzx_test_helpers.nu

title: Collapse remaining runtime-helper and bridge owners
description: Delete the broad runtime-helper Nu owners that still shape requests or facts for Rust-backed behavior, especially yzx_core_bridge.nu, runtime_paths.nu, runtime_commands.nu, runtime_env.nu, startup_facts.nu, and integration_facts.nu.
design: Separate irreducible shell helpers from deterministic fact, path, and request shaping. Rust should own deterministic shaping end to end. Do not treat the front-door welcome handoff as part of this lane.
acceptance_criteria: 1. A deletion budget splits remaining runtime helpers into Rust-owner cuts versus irreducible shell survivors. 2. Broad bridge or request-shaping owners are deleted or materially collapsed. 3. The runtime-helper budget ceiling ratchets downward.
close_reason: Completed in earlier tranches

title: Split common.nu and constants.nu into Rust-owned facts, assets, and tiny shell survivors
description: common.nu and constants.nu remain broad catch-all modules used by many surfaces. Split the retained behavior into Rust-owned facts, static metadata/assets, or tiny shell primitives so new Nu code cannot keep importing a miscellaneous helper bag.
design: Treat catch-all helpers as architectural debt. Move platform/path/metadata constants to Rust or generated assets, inline one-off helpers where that deletes a shared dependency, and preserve only direct shell primitives that cannot move cleanly.
acceptance_criteria: 1. common.nu and constants.nu no longer act as general-purpose dependency hubs. 2. Callers use typed Rust facts/assets or local minimal helpers. 3. Any surviving helpers have explicit names and narrow ownership. 4. The runtime_helpers budget is lowered and future imports are discouraged by docs or validation.
close_reason: Completed

title: Port or delete shell-heavy E2E and sweep runners under a Rust harness
description: Collapse the manual_e2e_and_sweep_runners family plus adjacent dev/sweep helpers: config_sweep_runner.nu, upgrade_contract_e2e_runner.nu, upgrade_summary_e2e_runner.nu, stale_config_diagnostics_e2e_runner.nu, historical_upgrade_notes_e2e_runner.nu, and dev/sweep/*.nu.
design: Strong deterministic assertions should move to Rust nextest. Shell-heavy live probes should become small fixed external-runner requests or be demoted to manual tools with explicit non-governed status. Delete weak or duplicate runner coverage instead of porting noise.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Each E2E/sweep runner is classified as strong Rust-port, manual survivor, or delete. 2. Strong coverage lands in Rust nextest or a Rust-owned harness. 3. Weak duplicate runners are deleted. 4. Any surviving Nu runner has a narrow shell/TTY reason and a smaller budget.
close_reason: Completed

title: Extract or Rust-own front-door presentation data and renderers
description: Collapse the 2,281 LOC front-door presentation family, especially ascii_art.nu, upgrade_summary.nu, setup/welcome.nu, yzx/screen.nu, yzx/menu.nu, yzx/popup.nu, yzx/edit.nu, yzx/import.nu, yzx/tutor.nu, and yzx/whats_new.nu.
design: Do not flatten the product experience. Move static art/copy/tables to assets or Rust data modules, move deterministic renderer selection and report shaping to Rust, and keep Nu only for direct terminal interaction or process handoff. Delete duplicate renderer branches before adding abstractions.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Large static data leaves Nu or is generated from Rust/assets. 2. ascii_art.nu is deleted or reduced to an irreducible terminal-renderer floor. 3. yzx screen/menu/popup/welcome behavior remains visually intentional. 4. The front_door_presentation budget ceiling drops materially.
close_reason: Completed

title: Collapse Zellij, Yazi, and managed-editor integration Nu into typed owners
description: Attack the 1,328 LOC integration family: integrations/zellij.nu, yazi.nu, managed_editor.nu, zellij_runtime_wrappers.nu, open_dir_in_pane.nu, zoxide_open_in_editor.nu, and zellij_wrappers/*.nu.
design: Prefer pane-orchestrator commands, Rust-owned workspace/session planning, and fixed wrapper entrypoints. Delete duplicate tab/session reads, sidebar/editor sync policy, transient pane contract shaping, and runtime-env glue from Nu. Preserve live pane behavior with direct regression coverage.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Integration state reads have one truth source. 2. Wrapper scripts are deleted or reduced to fixed argv entrypoints. 3. Zellij/Yazi/managed-editor behavior parity is defended by focused Rust or plugin tests. 4. The integration budget moves toward the 300 LOC target.
close_reason: Completed

title: Rust-own terminal launch, startup profile, desktop, and session request assembly
description: Collapse launch/session Nu across terminal_launcher.nu, startup_profile.nu, core/launch_yazelix.nu, core/start_yazelix.nu, core/start_yazelix_inner.nu, yzx/desktop.nu, yzx/launch.nu, and core/yzx_session.nu where those files still assemble deterministic requests.
design: Keep direct shell entry, terminal process spawning, and visible host feedback only where genuinely irreducible. Move terminal request construction, desktop command planning, session-state request shaping, profile report formatting, and launch error envelopes to Rust when it deletes Nu owners.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Terminal/desktop/session request assembly is Rust-owned end to end. 2. Nu launch files shrink to fixed shell/process entrypoints or are deleted. 3. Startup profiling behavior and desktop launch visible feedback survive. 4. setup/session/runtime-helper budget ceilings ratchet downward.
close_reason: Completed

title: Collapse the yzx_core bridge and runtime-helper Nu seam
description: Attack the runtime_helpers family around yzx_core_bridge.nu, common.nu, runtime_env.nu, startup_facts.nu, integration_facts.nu, transient_pane_facts.nu, config_files.nu, config_paths.nu, doctor_fix.nu, and terminal_launcher-adjacent helpers.
design: Do not preserve a general-purpose Nu bridge. Prefer direct Rust command invocations, typed request files, fixed argv helpers, or moving caller logic into Rust. Any surviving Nu helper must be a narrow shell/process primitive, not a config/state/report owner.
acceptance_criteria: 1. yzx_core_bridge.nu is deleted or reduced to the minimum transport seam with explicit callers. 2. Common/config/runtime facts helpers lose duplicated path, env, and report policy. 3. Rust owns deterministic config/state/request shaping. 4. The runtime_helpers budget ceiling drops materially.
close_reason: Completed

title: Rust-own maintainer release, update, version, and issue policy
description: Collapse the largest maintainer Nu policy cluster: update_workflow.nu, version_bump.nu, issue_sync.nu, issue_bead_contract.nu, repo_checkout.nu, plugin_build.nu, and adjacent yzx/dev.nu routing where Rust can own deterministic planning.
design: Separate external-tool execution from deterministic policy. Rust should own release/update plans, dirty-state checks, version edits, issue mapping validation, helper selection, and report formatting. Nu may temporarily retain fixed gh/bd/git/cargo/Nix process orchestration only when it is smaller than the Rust alternative.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Deterministic maintainer policy moves to Rust-owned commands with tests. 2. Large Nu maintainer files are deleted or demoted to fixed argv shell orchestration. 3. The public yzx dev commands keep behavior parity for update, bump, sync_issues, and build_pane_orchestrator. 4. The maintainer_and_dev_orchestration budget ceiling is ratcheted downward.
close_reason: Completed

title: Replace the Nu maintainer test runner with a Rust nextest orchestrator
description: Delete the remaining Nu policy in maintainer/test_runner.nu and shrink yzx/dev.nu test dispatch. The default suite is already nextest-first and no longer has governed Nu tests, so Nu should not own suite orchestration.
design: Move fixed suite inventory loading, nextest argv construction, logging, profiling, and lint-only routing to a Rust maintainer command where it deletes Nu. Keep yzx dev as a thin public router only if it is still needed for shell entry.
acceptance_criteria: 1. maintainer/test_runner.nu is deleted or reduced to an explicitly budgeted shell trampoline. 2. yzx dev test continues to support default, lint-only, sweep, visual, all, profile, and new-window behavior or explicitly records a preserved split. 3. The default test path remains nextest-owned. 4. Tests verify the Rust command planning and failure reporting contracts.
notes: Dependency decision before implementation: use the existing Rust std/serde/toml stack in yazelix_core; add no new production crates. The runner should invoke fixed cargo/nextest/nix argv from a checked-in inventory and keep shell interaction at process-execution boundaries only. Reject Clap or a broad command framework for this private maintainer orchestrator until the public router migration has a separate go/no-go.
close_reason: Completed: deleted nushell/scripts/maintainer/test_runner.nu, moved default nextest orchestration/logging/profiling/syntax phase to yzx_repo_maintainer run-tests, and kept yzx dev test as a thin Nu handoff.

title: Rust-own the remaining validator and package-smoke probes
description: Delete or sharply demote the remaining 639 LOC deterministic-validator family: validate_flake_install.nu, validate_flake_interface.nu, validate_nixpkgs_package.nu, validate_nixpkgs_submission.nu, and validate_syntax.nu.
design: Port deterministic file parsing, flake interface checks, package metadata checks, and command construction to Rust. Keep only truly irreducible external Nix/Nu process probes, preferably as fixed argv execution owned by Rust or a tiny POSIX helper. Do not add broad wrapper debt.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The remaining validator family has a Rust-owned request/check surface or explicit per-file no-go. 2. Redundant Nu wrappers are deleted. 3. Any surviving Nu syntax/Nix smoke helper is fixed, tiny, and budgeted as shell execution only. 4. CI/pre-commit/docs point at the Rust owner or the narrowed survivor.
notes: Dependency decision before implementation: prefer std plus existing serde_json/toml in yzx_repo_validator. Add no crates unless flake/package parsing proves materially safer with a narrow parser crate. Keep Nix and Nu execution as fixed argv external probes owned by Rust; do not keep Nu wrapper owners.
close_reason: Completed: Rust-owned the flake interface, cold profile install, nixpkgs package/submission, and Nushell syntax validator surfaces; deleted the Nu validator/package-smoke cluster and moved CI/pre-commit/maintainer calls to yzx_repo_validator.

title: Clean stale historical spec references after the governed Nu test deletion
notes: Cleaned live stale references to deleted Nu validator/helper owners and refreshed the hard-budget/spec docs around the governed Nu test deletion. Canonical historical deleted-test mapping remains in rust_owned_test_migration_budget.md and governed_test_traceability_inventory.md; live validator references now point at Rust yzx_repo_validator commands.
close_reason: Completed delete-first Rust/Nu owner cuts, documentation cleanup, and validation

title: Implement the yzx dev cut toward the irreducible shell floor
description: Apply the yzx dev cut by deleting dynamic dispatch, metadata, and helper glue that no longer needs to live in Nu, and leave only the minimal shell-orchestration surface.
design: Count progress only when yzx/dev.nu and adjacent dev-command glue materially shrink or disappear. Do not preserve a dynamic Nu router because it already exists.
acceptance_criteria: 1. yzx/dev.nu and adjacent dev-command glue materially shrink or disappear. 2. Surviving Nu code is limited to honest dev-shell orchestration. 3. Each surviving Nu surface has explicit proof of irreducibility. 4. The public dev behavior still works.
notes:
Implementation dependency decision: no production or dev crates added. The yzx dev cut uses existing Nushell fixed process orchestration plus in-house Rust repo-validation logic. Rejected adding a CLI/test framework crate because the deleted path was transitional Nu suite policy, not argument parsing or external process management.
Completed the yzx dev cut by deleting default-suite transitional Nu entrypoint handling from maintainer/test_runner.nu and tightening validate-default-test-traceability into a hard guard against governed nushell/scripts/dev/test_*.nu returning. yzx dev test now runs fixed Rust nextest suites plus explicit cargo-test exceptions only; no Nu default suite policy remains in the runner.
close_reason: Completed

title: Define the yzx dev entry and dev-orchestration irreducible shell floor
description: Review yzx/dev.nu and the remaining dev-command glue after the test, validator, and maintainer budget work to decide what the public dev entry should still own in Nu and what should collapse into Rust, fixed metadata, or fixed helper assets.
design: Treat yzx dev as a thin public entrypoint over tests, validators, build helpers, and maintainer actions. The goal is the smallest provable shell orchestrator, not a dynamic Nu router kept for convenience.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The retained yzx dev responsibilities are explicit. 2. The deletion budget names the exact command-routing, metadata, and helper seams to remove. 3. Expected file or branch deletion is explicit where possible. 4. Every retained Nu route has an irreducibility proof.

Re-cut this cluster so anything deterministic or data-shaped leaves Nu and only a provably irreducible shell/process remainder survives.

At least one shared helper disappears or materially shrinks. 4. Any surviving exported helper has explicit proof of irreducibility. 5. No new generic bridge layer is added.

Keep launch preparation logic in-house because it is small domain policy around existing Rust owners; reject a broader CLI framework or wrapper crate because the goal is deletion of Nu request assembly, not another generic abstraction layer.

title: Define the Nu-to-Rust test migration budget for large deterministic test files

design: Be explicit about what can move without losing product coverage. Prefer fewer stronger Rust tests over broad one-to-one ports of every Nu assertion.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The large Nu test files are grouped into Rust-port, mixed, and keep-in-Nu buckets. 2. The budget identifies exact tests or clusters that can move first. 3. The budget records what must stay in Nu because it still defends shell or process behavior.
close_reason: Completed

title: Rust-own deterministic contract coverage for Rust-owned subsystems
description: The biggest remaining Nushell bucket is dev and test code. Large Nu test files still defend behavior now owned by Rust, especially generated-config, materialization, public Rust-owned command families, and helper payload shaping. Port only strong deterministic coverage to Rust where Rust already owns the behavior, delete weak Nu tests, and treat any not-yet-portable remainder as blocked on a future owner cut or Rust harness lane rather than a Nu survivor. Migrated first-party Rust suites should land nextest-compatible and nextest-owned by default.
design: Do not delete the only executable defense of user-visible behavior. This lane is for deterministic Rust-owned logic, not for preserving shell-era habits. Count progress only when strong Rust contract coverage lands, the resulting Rust suite runs cleanly under nextest by default, and Nu test files or helper surfaces materially shrink.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The large deterministic Nu test files are split into strong Rust-port, weak-delete, and blocked-on-owner-or-harness buckets. 2. New Rust coverage is added only where Rust already owns the behavior and the test defends a live contract, regression, or invariant. 3. Replaced or weak Nu tests are deleted rather than kept or demoted. 4. Migrated first-party Rust test buckets are nextest-first by default, with cargo test retained only for doctests and explicit exceptions. 5. Any blocked remainder has explicit follow-up owner-cut or harness beads instead of an accepted keep-in-Nu status.
close_reason: Completed: all visible Rust-owned deterministic coverage children are closed; governed Nu tests are retired and the default lane is nextest-first.

title: Collapse maintainer sweep and test-runner dynamic Nu dispatch seams
description: Audit outcome: maintainer/test_runner.nu still builds dynamic nu -c module strings for sweep lanes, and the sweep path is more fragile than the default test path. The audit also found that sweep dispatch should be checked for missing imports and stale runtime-root assumptions before the ranked deletion budget treats harness Nu as healthy.
design: Retained behavior: yzx dev test default, lint-only, sweep, visual, all, new-window, and profiling behavior. Candidate surviving owner: one explicit test runner with direct script/module invocation and no interpolated command program bodies for sweep dispatch. Stop condition: keep external Nu process execution if needed for isolation, but pass arguments structurally or through checked-in helper entrypoints instead of constructing code strings.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The sweep and visual dispatch paths are verified from the public yzx dev test surface. 2. Dynamic nu -c strings in the runner are removed or justified with a fixed-body/argv boundary. 3. Missing imports or runtime-root assumptions are fixed if confirmed. 4. The default suite, sweep-only lane, and visual lane entrypoints remain clear and separately documented. 5. Verification covers validate_syntax, the default suite, and at least a dry or targeted sweep dispatch check that does not open uncontrolled windows.
notes: Verification 2026-04-22: validate_syntax passes, the maintainer runner now dispatches sweep lanes through direct script argv execution, and public 'yzx dev test --sweep' exercised the new path. The suite still fails on an unrelated Ghostty config validation error for missing terminal.ghostty_mode_effect, which is not caused by the dispatch rewrite.
close_reason: Completed

title: Collapse integration config reads into explicit integration facts
description: Audit outcome: yazi.nu and managed_editor.nu still parse the full Yazelix config at live integration time to answer small questions such as sidebar enablement, Yazi/ya command names, and managed editor kind. That keeps config_parser.nu in the integration path even when the pane orchestrator already owns live session state.
design: Retained behavior: configured yazi_command and yazi_ya_command, enable_sidebar behavior, managed Helix wrapper detection, Neovim fallback, and clear no-sidebar/outside-Zellij messages. Candidate surviving owner: caller-provided integration facts or a Rust-owned config/control-plane facts request, with Nu retaining only external-tool adapter behavior. Stop condition: do not move process execution or ya/zellij action calls into Rust just to reduce config reads; the cut only counts if it removes duplicated config ownership from integration glue.
acceptance_criteria: 1. All parse_yazelix_config use in integrations is inventoried. 2. A narrow facts source is chosen and documented before implementation. 3. Live pane-orchestrator session state remains the only sidebar identity truth. 4. Config reads are removed or reduced without losing configured Yazi/ya/editor behavior. 5. Verification covers Yazi command resolver overrides, managed editor kind detection, sidebar-disabled copy, and plugin-owned sidebar sync.
notes: Rust dependency gate 2026-04-22: no new crates. Reuse existing in-house config normalization/control-plane/workspace command logic inside rust_core. Rejected adding serde-adjacent helper crates or CLI frameworks for this cut because the seam is a narrow facts request over existing JSON/env plumbing, and extra crates would not delete the Nu owner.

title: Delete simple Zellij command wrappers through direct pane-orchestrator actions

Candidate surviving owner: Rust Zellij materialization emits direct Zellij actions; pane-orchestrator remains the live state owner. Stop condition: keep a wrapper when Zellij KDL cannot express the action without losing failure visibility or session-local plugin targeting.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The simple wrapper set is confirmed against generated Zellij config call sites. 2. Direct actions preserve existing pane-orchestrator command names and session-local behavior. 3. Wrappers that no longer own behavior are deleted from runtime materialization, docs, and tests. 4. Non-trivial wrappers such as launch_sidebar_yazi.nu and popup/menu wrappers are explicitly kept or split into later beads. 5. Verification includes Zellij materialization tests and any wrapper contract tests affected by the deletion.

title: Delete core/yazelix.nu compatibility registry after Rust-root parity

Stop condition: if any runtime-supported path still requires source-able re-export semantics rather than a real yzx binary or direct module entrypoint, record the no-go and narrow the registry instead of deleting it.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Every tracked use of core/yazelix.nu is classified as public compatibility, test fixture, sweep harness, or direct internal module need. 2. Public routing continues through the Rust yzx root without reviving generic Nu fallback dispatch. 3. Tests and sweep helpers no longer require a re-export registry. 4. The file is deleted or explicitly demoted with a precise remaining owner reason. 5. Verification covers public root routing, exported command metadata, and any migrated test/sweep path.

title: Define the recursive subsystem audit template and feature-preservation gate
description: Before running subsystem audits, define the exact output template and feature-preservation gate each audit must satisfy. This prevents audits from becoming vague cleanup notes or deletion wishlists that accidentally drop supported behavior.
design: The template should require: subsystem purpose, current user-visible behavior, internal contracts, canonical owner map, Nu/Rust/plugin/POSIX survivor reasons, duplicate owners, bridge burn candidates, missing layer/extra layer findings, DRY opportunities, weak/orphan tests, spec gaps, no-go deletions, and follow-up beads with acceptance criteria.

Each audit child must name must-not-lose behavior, current verification, proposed canonical owner, deletion class, and stop conditions. The template explicitly says no code/test/doc deletion is allowed from an audit unless retained behavior and verification are named.

The template now forces must-not-lose behavior, current verification, candidate surviving owner, deletion class, stop conditions, and explicit no-deletion-inside-the-audit rules before future subsystem audit beads proceed.

title: Pilot the contract schema on one subsystem before broad rollout
description: Apply the schema decision to one representative subsystem before public docs, validators, and broad audits harden around it. Use the pilot to prove the schema is sharp enough to preserve behavior, expose duplicate owners, and map tests without becoming bureaucracy.
design: Use config/runtime/control-plane as the preferred pilot unless the schema decision names a better subsystem. The pilot should produce a small contract-item set, mapped tests or verification statuses, and a list of lessons for the public protocol and validators. Do not change product behavior during the pilot.
acceptance_criteria: One representative subsystem has a draft contract-item mapping with owner, status, verification, and deletion implications. The pilot records schema adjustments or confirms the schema as-is. It identifies at least one weak/orphan test or duplicate owner if present, but does not delete anything during the pilot. Public docs and validators use the pilot result rather than an untested schema.
notes:
Planning review 2026-04-21: adding this pilot avoids overfitting a validator and public protocol to a theoretical schema.
Completed 2026-04-21: added docs/specs/config_runtime_control_plane_contract_item_pilot.md as the mixed-subsystem pilot. The pilot mapped five concrete config/runtime/control-plane items, marked the surviving Nu bridge layer as quarantine debt, identified broad default-lane traceability debt in test_yzx_generated_configs.nu/test_yzx_core_commands.nu/test_yzx_workspace_commands.nu, and confirmed the canonical contract-item schema works as-is without product changes.

title: Convert canonical audits into a ranked Nu deletion budget
description: After schema/test/subsystem audits land, consolidate the findings into a ranked deletion budget for remaining Nushell and redundant glue. This should separate product/runtime Nu, integration Nu, launch/session Nu, dev/test Nu, maintainer Nu, and likely survivors.
design: The budget should not be a LOC vanity metric. Each candidate must state what behavior remains, the canonical Rust/Nu/plugin/POSIX owner after deletion, required tests/contracts, and whether deletion is now, bridge-collapse, big Rust port, or no-go survivor.
acceptance_criteria: A ranked deletion budget exists with concrete follow-up beads for high-value work. Each item states expected deleted files/LOC, retained functionality, canonical owner after deletion, required contract IDs or contract gaps, verification plan, and stop condition. The budget separates product/runtime Nu from integration, launch/session, dev/test, maintainer, and likely-survivor Nu. It does not wait for all weak-test cleanup to be implemented, but it must consume the test inventory so deletion lanes know whether verification is already strong or needs prerequisite work. It includes a public-facing summary that avoids promising deletion where shell/process ownership is genuinely irreducible.
notes:
This is the bridge between plan-space and implementation-space. Do not start broad rewrites until the audit shows deletion value and contract coverage.
Planning review 2026-04-21: dependency narrowed from completed test cleanup/tool decisions to the test inventory. The deletion budget is a planning output; individual implementation beads can depend on test cleanup or tool hardening when needed.
Completed 2026-04-22 with docs/specs/ranked_nu_deletion_budget.md plus public-facing inventory updates in docs/subsystem_code_inventory.md. The ranked remaining lanes are nuj1, p18h, jkk3, 4ucy, and l533; jkk3 was created as the missing integration follow-up bead.

It explicitly distinguishes transport-only seams from policy-bearing Nu owners, records direct-config and Home Manager behavior that must not drift, and names verification for config/default/Home Manager synchronization before any deletion lane proceeds.
notes:
Delete-first question: does each surviving Nu helper own shell/process orchestration, or is it still duplicating Rust-owned config/runtime truth?
Planning review 2026-04-21: config/runtime audits must include the AGENTS requirement that Home Manager and default config stay synchronized.
Completed 2026-04-22: produced docs/specs/config_runtime_control_plane_canonicalization_audit.md with owner map, survivor reasons, delete-first findings, quality gaps, and follow-up deletion lanes. Created yazelix-izwm / yazelix-ekfc plus gated child tasks for the remaining serious Nu cuts. Validated with nu nushell/scripts/dev/validate_specs.nu after exporting .beads/issues.jsonl.

title: Run recursive delete-first subsystem canonicalization audits
description: Perform a no-code-change audit pass across Yazelix subsystems. For each subsystem, read the code, specs, and tests; question every requirement; identify the canonical owner; name missing or extra layers; find duplicated code and glue; classify surviving Nushell as delete now, bridge to collapse, big Rust-port target, or likely survivor; then create precise implementation beads.
design: This is the recursive entry point for subsystem-by-subsystem canonicalization. The output should be beads and contract updates, not opportunistic code edits. Each audit child should produce a short owner map, bridge burn list, DRY/deletion opportunities, test/spec gaps, and proposed next beads.
acceptance_criteria: Every major subsystem has an audit bead with clear scope and acceptance criteria. A shared audit template and feature-preservation gate exists before subsystem audits start. Audit results name must-not-lose behavior, current verification, canonical owner map, concrete deletion lanes, no-go survivor reasons, and follow-up beads. Audits avoid vague 'clean up code' tasks. No implementation changes happen inside audit beads unless a child explicitly switches from planning to implementation after acceptance criteria are clear.
notes:
Current 2026-04-21 rough split: product/runtime Nu excluding dev and maintainer is about 9,763 LOC; Nu dev/test harness is about 17,223 LOC; maintainer Nu is about 2,138 LOC. Re-measure during audit and separate product deletion from test/dev harness deletion.
Planning review 2026-04-21: added yazelix-rdn7.5.8 as the common audit-template gate so recursive audits preserve functionality and produce comparable outputs.
close_reason: Completed all recursive subsystem audit children and produced follow-up deletion beads.

title: Evaluate Rust test hardening tools for high-value owned logic
description: Decide whether Yazelix should add targeted Rust test hardening tools such as cargo-mutants, cargo-fuzz, or cargo-nextest, and define the narrow surfaces where they provide signal without becoming noisy process weight.
design: Evaluate tools against current Rust-owned deterministic logic: config normalization, route planning/public command surface, materialization render plans, TOML/KDL/text generation, runtime contract evaluation, and report JSON shaping. Default to targeted use, not full-suite blanket enforcement.
acceptance_criteria: The decision records keep/reject for cargo-mutants, cargo-fuzz, and cargo-nextest; packaging impact; expected command entrypoints; which modules are eligible; CI/local lane placement; and explicit stop conditions for tools that are too slow, flaky, or broad. Any adopted Rust test tool must include the crate-vs-in-house/dev-dependency decision required by AGENTS.md, including rejected alternatives and packaging impact. If adopted, follow-up implementation beads are created.
notes:
References: cargo-mutants docs: https://docs.rs/crate/cargo-mutants/latest ; cargo-mutants limitations on hermetic tests: https://mutants.rs/limitations.html ; Rust Fuzz Book: https://rust-fuzz.github.io/book/ ; cargo-nextest docs: https://nexte.st/ ; Cargo test docs: https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/commands/cargo-test.html
Planning review 2026-04-21: this remains a decision bead, not an implicit approval to add dev dependencies. Any tool adoption must satisfy the Rust dependency gate first.
Unblocked by the test inventory. Eligible Rust hardening targets from docs/specs/governed_test_traceability_inventory.md: deterministic config/runtime/control-plane logic, public route planning and metadata, materialization/render plans, report shaping, and pane-orchestrator contract logic. Avoid blanket full-suite tooling; any adopted tool still needs the Rust dependency gate.
Completed 2026-04-22 with docs/specs/rust_test_hardening_tools_decision.md. Decision: keep cargo test as canonical baseline, adopt cargo-nextest narrowly as an optional maintainer/CI lane, reject cargo-mutants and cargo-fuzz for now, and create follow-up bead yazelix-fkgs for the nextest pilot.
close_reason: Completed

title: Delete, demote, or quarantine weak and orphan governed tests
description: After the test inventory exists, remove tests that do not defend a live contract, concrete regression, integration boundary, or maintained invariant. Demote tests to sweep/manual lanes when they are valuable but too heavy or environment-sensitive for default. Quarantine only when deletion would risk losing unmodeled regression memory.
acceptance_criteria: Every deleted test has an explicit reason or a replacement contract/test mapping. Default-lane orphan tests are gone or quarantined with bead IDs and exit criteria. Tests that are the only known executable defense of live behavior are kept until replacement verification exists or the behavior is explicitly retired. Demotions preserve the verification path in the appropriate lane. No new generic overflow test files are created.
notes:
Reference: Google E2E guidance says tests should create a fast, reliable, failure-isolating feedback loop; weak tests that mostly assert existence/help/implementation trivia should not survive by inertia.
Planning review 2026-04-21: this bead is not permission for a mass test purge. It is a controlled delete/demote/quarantine pass after inventory and validator ratchet are in place.
Unblocked by yazelix-rdn7.4.1 inventory. Start from docs/specs/governed_test_traceability_inventory.md. Important constraints: five default component files are still quarantined for broad file-level traceability; do not delete only-known defenses listed in the inventory; prioritize draining item-ID gaps via yazelix-7upo before pruning command-discovery, generated-config duplicate, or historical byte-compatibility tests.
Completed 2026-04-22 by deleting the weak command-discovery test from test_yzx_core_commands.nu, draining docs/contract_traceability_quarantine.toml, and rewriting the governed test inventory around the surviving executable defenses and next contract-item gaps.
close_reason: Completed

title: Inventory governed tests and map them to live contract items
description: Read every governed Nu and Rust test file and produce a contract traceability inventory. Classify each test as mapped, regression-only, invariant-only, duplicate, implementation-trivia, weak, dead, or quarantine.
acceptance_criteria: The inventory identifies tests currently defended only by broad policy docs, tests without precise live contract mapping, redundant tests, likely 9/10 keepers, and tests that are the only known defense for live behavior. It records the minimum spec/contract IDs needed before deletion or demotion work starts, and separates Rust-owned deterministic logic tests from Nu-owned shell/process/integration tests.
notes:
Current rough counts from 2026-04-21 planning pass: 121 Nu files, 42 Rust files under yazelix_core, about 195 Nu tests and 119 Rust tests, with 12 Nu test files top-level defending test_suite_governance.md. Re-verify counts during implementation.
Planning review 2026-04-21: inventory must identify 'only known executable defense' tests so later deletion work does not accidentally remove the last guard for real behavior.
Completed inventory in docs/specs/governed_test_traceability_inventory.md. Counts: 18 governed Nu test files, 198 Nu def test_* functions, 38 first-party Rust files with tests, 146 Rust #[test] functions. Validator status: default Nu traceability passes with five quarantined default-component file-level debts; Rust traceability passes cleanly. Outcome: no immediate safe mass deletion; yazelix-rdn7.4.2 should use the inventory to delete/demote only after contract IDs or explicit quarantine cover the retained behavior.
close_reason: Completed governed test traceability inventory and validated Nu/Rust traceability ratchets.

title: Canonicalize the governed test suite from contract traceability
description: Audit the current Nu and Rust tests from a delete-first point of view. The target end state is zero surviving governed Nushell tests. Port only strong contract, regression, and invariant coverage to Rust; delete weak or orphan coverage; and record blocked surfaces as future owner-cut or harness work instead of accepting them as long-term Nu survivors. First-party Rust tests should run under nextest by default, with cargo test retained only for doctests and any explicit unsupported exception buckets.
design: Use the existing strength rubric as an input, but make contract traceability and strength the hard gates. Rust becomes the canonical home for surviving governed tests, and nextest becomes the default execution surface for first-party Rust test buckets. Nushell tests are transitional debt only; if a behavior cannot be defended cleanly in Rust yet, record the blocking owner cut or harness gap instead of preserving a Nu test by habit.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The governed suite has an inventory of strong Rust-port, weak-delete, and blocked candidates. 2. Surviving default-lane tests are 7+/10 and mapped to live contracts, regressions, or invariants. 3. No open migration bead treats a governed Nu test as a permanent survivor; blocked cases point at explicit owner-cut or harness work instead. 4. First-party Rust test lanes are nextest-first by default, with cargo test reserved only for doctests and explicit exceptions. 5. No valuable regression memory is lost without a strong Rust replacement or explicit contract retirement.
notes:
References: Google E2E guidance favors fast, reliable, isolated feedback loops and a small number of E2E tests; cargo-mutants can probe whether Rust tests actually check behavior; Rust Fuzz Book applies to parser/serializer/merge style logic.
Planning review 2026-04-21: delete-first for tests means deleting noise, not deleting regression memory. Feature preservation and contract retirement must be explicit.
Planning review 2026-04-22: no governed Nushell test is an accepted steady-state owner. Strong tests port to Rust; weak tests delete; blocked cases need explicit owner-cut or harness beads.
close_reason: Completed: all governed test canonicalization children are closed; zero governed Nu tests remain and Rust/nextest is the canonical first-party test lane.

title: Enforce contract IDs across specs and governed tests
description: Add mechanical validation so contract-driven development is enforced instead of aspirational. Specs should expose indexed live contract items. Governed tests should reference live contract IDs, concrete regressions, or approved invariants. Broad file-level references like only defending test_suite_governance.md should not be sufficient for default-lane tests.
design: Extend or add validators after the schema decision lands. The validator should parse Markdown contract item IDs and Nu/Rust test markers, detect orphan tests, detect missing verification for live normative items, and produce actionable errors. Keep false positives low and allow explicit quarantine during migration.
acceptance_criteria: Validation fails for new default-lane governed tests that lack a live contract/regression/invariant mapping. Validation reports live contract items with no verification or explicit manual/unverified reason. The first implementation uses a migration-safe ratchet: existing unmapped tests are reported and can be quarantined behind bead IDs, while new or touched tests must satisfy the stricter rule. Broad policy-only defenses such as only referencing test_suite_governance.md are not sufficient for new/touched default-lane tests. The validator is documented in the public protocol docs.
notes:
References: NASA verification matrix guidance supports unique IDs and verification mapping. Google Test Sizes supports enforceable lane boundaries. Cargo test docs define the base Rust test runner behavior. cargo-nextest is a candidate runner for faster isolated Rust test execution, not a required dependency until evaluated.
Planning review 2026-04-21: enforcement should be strict for new/touched work but migration-safe for the existing suite, otherwise the protocol turns into a big-bang cleanup that risks losing regression memory.
Completed 2026-04-21: extended validate_specs.nu to parse indexed contract items and verify required fields/statuses/verification, added contract_traceability_helpers.nu plus docs/contract_traceability_quarantine.toml for a migration-safe file-level ratchet, taught the default and Rust test traceability validators to validate nearby Contract markers and reject policy-only nearby traceability, added pilot Contract markers to the config/runtime tests, updated docs/contract_driven_development.md with the mechanical validator surfaces, and fixed pre-existing default-runner and Rust-test metadata debt exposed by the stricter validators.
close_reason: Completed: contract-item and test traceability ratchet is now mechanically enforced in migration-safe mode

title: Publish the public Yazelix contract-driven development protocol
description: Create or update public-facing docs that explain Yazelix's stricter contract-driven development protocol: how specs, Beads, tests, validators, and delete-first subsystem audits fit together, and how external references inform the protocol.
design: The docs should be practical and non-bureaucratic: define the canonical contract item, explain when specs are required, explain test traceability, explain quarantine/delete/demote paths for weak tests, and include a references section with the source list from the parent bead.
acceptance_criteria: Public docs exist in an appropriate docs/specs or docs architecture location, reference the canonical schema decision and schema pilot, list the source references with links, and explain how contributors should add or change a spec, contract item, or test without creating weak/spec-less coverage. The docs must distinguish normative contract items from rationale/history, describe quarantine/delete/demote paths, preserve AGENTS.md as the short durable workflow entrypoint, and follow README prose style if README is touched.
notes:
References to include: NASA Systems Engineering Handbook appendix: https://www.nasa.gov/reference/system-engineering-handbook-appendix/ ; Google Test Sizes: https://testing.googleblog.com/2010/12/test-sizes.html ; Google E2E guidance: https://testing.googleblog.com/2015/04/just-say-no-to-more-end-to-end-tests.html ; Rust Book test organization: https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/ch11-03-test-organization.html ; Cargo test docs: https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/commands/cargo-test.html ; cargo-nextest: https://nexte.st/ ; cargo-mutants: https://docs.rs/crate/cargo-mutants/latest ; Rust Fuzz Book: https://rust-fuzz.github.io/book/
Planning review 2026-04-21: public docs should be written after the schema pilot, not directly from the abstract schema decision. Do not duplicate a large process manual into AGENTS.md; link to durable docs instead.
Completed 2026-04-21: added docs/contract_driven_development.md as the public protocol surface, linked it from docs/spec_driven_workflow.md, and carried the schema decision, pilot outcome, delete-first audit loop, test keep/demote/quarantine/delete outcomes, and external references into durable public docs without expanding AGENTS.md.
close_reason: Completed: public contract-driven development docs landed and now point contributors to the schema, pilot, and traceability protocol

title: Define the canonical contract item schema and ID policy
description: Decide the strict contract unit Yazelix will use before broad audit or validator work. The schema should distinguish normative contract items from rationale, examples, non-goals, historical notes, and implementation notes so the project does not turn every prose line into bureaucracy.
design: Candidate fields: stable ID, subsystem, type, normative text, owner, status, verification method, defended-by tests or validators, source/rationale, and deletion/bridge implications. Candidate statuses: live, planning, historical, deprecated, quarantine. Candidate types: behavior, invariant, boundary, ownership, failure mode, non-goal, manual verification.
acceptance_criteria: The decision records the final schema, ID naming rules, allowed statuses, allowed verification methods, migration rules for existing specs, and the rule for when a test may reference a contract item versus a concrete regression. It explicitly rejects indexing every prose line, requires that normative live contract items distinguish automated tests, validators, manual checks, and intentionally-unverified items with reasons, and includes feature-preservation rules for delete-first work. It cites the NASA requirements/verification matrix reference and explains why Yazelix uses lightweight Markdown rather than a heavyweight requirements tool.
notes:
Reference: NASA Systems Engineering Handbook appendix on requirements traceability and verification matrices: https://www.nasa.gov/reference/system-engineering-handbook-appendix/
Planning review 2026-04-21: schema must preserve functionality as a first-class constraint. The unit should be a normative contract item, not every sentence in a spec. Non-goals, rationale, examples, and historical notes should be addressable but should not require one-to-one tests.
Completed 2026-04-21: added docs/specs/canonical_contract_item_schema.md and indexed it in docs/specs/spec_inventory.md. The decision now defines the contract-item unit, ID format, statuses, types, verification modes, test reference rules, migration rules, and delete-first feature-preservation constraints.
close_reason: Completed: canonical contract item schema now lives in docs/specs/canonical_contract_item_schema.md and is indexed in docs/specs/spec_inventory.md

title: Canonical contract-driven development protocol for Yazelix
description: Define and adopt a stricter, public-facing contract-driven development protocol so Yazelix converges toward canonical form: one owner per behavior, no missing layer, no extra layer, no duplicate owner, no weak tests, and no surviving Nushell without an explicit owner reason. This is a planning-first lane: audit code/specs/tests before implementation, question each requirement, create deletion beads from findings, and avoid changing product code during the audit pass.
design: Use indexed subsystem contract items rather than indexing every prose line. Each live normative item should have a stable ID, type, owner, verification method, and status. Tests should trace to live contract items, concrete regressions, or maintained invariants; orphan or weak tests should be deleted, demoted, or quarantined with an explicit bead. Public docs must explain the protocol and cite external references.
acceptance_criteria: The protocol is ready only when the schema decision is closed, public protocol docs exist with references, validators enforce the agreed contract/test rules in migration-safe ratchet mode, the governed test suite has been inventoried and weak/orphan tests have explicit delete/demote/quarantine outcomes, subsystem audits have produced feature-preserving owner maps and deletion beads, and a ranked Nu deletion budget exists. No child may recommend deleting code, tests, aliases, or docs without naming the retained behavior, canonical owner, and verification path or explicit no-go reason.
notes:
Reference sources to carry into public docs: NASA Systems Engineering Handbook appendix on requirements traceability and verification matrices: https://www.nasa.gov/reference/system-engineering-handbook-appendix/ ; Google Testing Blog, Test Sizes: https://testing.googleblog.com/2010/12/test-sizes.html ; Google Testing Blog, Just Say No to More End-to-End Tests: https://testing.googleblog.com/2015/04/just-say-no-to-more-end-to-end-tests.html ; Rust Book test organization: https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/ch11-03-test-organization.html ; Cargo test docs: https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/commands/cargo-test.html ; cargo-nextest docs: https://nexte.st/ ; cargo-mutants docs: https://docs.rs/crate/cargo-mutants/latest ; cargo-mutants limitations: https://mutants.rs/limitations.html ; Rust Fuzz Book: https://rust-fuzz.github.io/book/
Planning review 2026-04-21: parent acceptance was tightened because a mere child work plan would close the epic too early. The epic now closes only after protocol docs, enforcement, audits, test outcomes, and the ranked deletion budget exist.
close_reason: all steps complete

title: Rust-own install-ownership env request construction and dedupe Rust callers

acceptance_criteria: One Rust owner builds install-ownership requests from env; doctor, update, and home_manager reuse it; duplicate install-ownership request construction is removed without behavior drift.
close_reason: Added Rust-owned install-ownership.evaluate --from-env, reused install_ownership_env.rs from doctor, and removed duplicated doctor request construction.

title: Delete install_ownership.nu and launcher_resolution.nu by switching Nu callers to the Rust owner
description: Retire the remaining Nushell install-ownership wrappers after the Rust env owner exists.
design: Switch desktop install/launch and restart wrapper-resolution callers to the Rust-owned install-ownership surface. Keep Nushell only for desktop-entry writing, desktop fast-path UX, and restart UX.
acceptance_criteria: nushell/scripts/utils/install_ownership.nu and nushell/scripts/utils/launcher_resolution.nu are deleted; desktop and restart behavior is preserved; focused tests and docs/spec inventory are updated.
close_reason: Deleted install_ownership.nu and launcher_resolution.nu; desktop and restart now call the Rust-owned install-ownership.evaluate --from-env path directly.

title: Define the deletion budget for install-ownership and launcher-resolution bridge collapse
description: Decide the exact Rust owner shape for env-derived install-ownership evaluation and list the surviving callers that must switch cleanly.
design: Prefer one Rust env-request owner reused by doctor, update, home_manager, desktop, and restart. Reuse in-house install_ownership_env.rs and install_ownership_report.rs unless a narrower helper proves insufficient. Reject any plan that leaves duplicate HOME/XDG/PATH/'which yzx' inference in Nushell or in multiple Rust call sites.
acceptance_criteria: The lane records the exact owner cut, preserved callers, and the crate-vs-in-house decision; the implementation target is explicit enough to delete the Nu wrappers without adding a second bridge layer.
notes:
2026-04-21 deletion-budget decision:
- Production crates: none. Reuse the existing in-house Rust modules `install_ownership_report.rs`, `install_ownership_env.rs`, and existing serde/serde_json plumbing already in the crate.
- Dev-only crates: none beyond existing Rust/Nu test dependencies.
- Build in-house: add a Rust-owned `install-ownership.evaluate --from-env` path in `yzx_core`, plus an optional runtime-dir override for Nu callers that already resolved the helper runtime. Keep env-derived HOME/XDG/state/PATH/invoked-yzx request construction in `install_ownership_env.rs`.
- Rejected alternatives: keep `install_ownership.nu` as a domain bridge; move launcher-resolution policy into `yzx_core_bridge.nu`; add Clap or a formatting/process helper crate. Those would preserve or widen the bridge instead of deleting it.
- Packaging impact: no new dependencies. The runtime must continue shipping `yzx_core`; deleted Nu wrappers must be removed from routed caller expectations.
- Owner cut: Rust owns env-derived install-ownership evaluation and fields such as `stable_yzx_wrapper` and `desktop_launcher_path`. Nushell keeps only desktop-entry writes, icon installation, desktop launch UX, and restart UX.
close_reason: Recorded the delete-first owner cut and Rust dependency gate: no new crates, Rust owns env-derived install-ownership requests, and Nu keeps only desktop/restart UX.

title: Collapse install-ownership and launcher-resolution bridge owners
description: Delete the surviving Nushell install-ownership request-shaping and launcher-resolution seam now that Rust already owns install-ownership evaluation.
design: Keep this delete-first. Rust should become the single owner for install-ownership request construction and computed fields like stable_yzx_wrapper and desktop_launcher_path. Nushell should keep only desktop-entry writing and restart UX, not XDG/HOME/PATH inference or field projection.
acceptance_criteria: Rust becomes the single owner for env-derived install-ownership evaluation and launcher facts; nushell/scripts/utils/install_ownership.nu and nushell/scripts/utils/launcher_resolution.nu are deleted; desktop, restart, doctor, update, and home_manager behavior is preserved.

close_reason: Completed: the full yzx keys family now routes through yzx_control, the shared public command surface classifies keys as Rust-owned, nushell/scripts/yzx/keys.nu is deleted, and focused public CLI tests plus the installed-runtime contract validate the alias and discoverability surface.

title: Define the deletion budget and alias parity gate for the yzx keys family
notes:
This bead exists because `yzx keys` is still small enough to be a plausible Rust owner cut, but it carries more alias and formatting surface than `yzx why` / `yzx sponsor`.

Guardrails:
- preserve all existing aliases (`yzx`, `yazi`, `hx`, `helix`, `nu`, `nushell`)
- preserve the current discoverability role as a keybinding/help surface, not a generic docs rewrite
- do not batch `yzx tutor*` into this family; its product evolution remains under `yazelix-3jw`
Decision:
- `yzx keys` remains a real next deletion lane after `yzx why` / `yzx sponsor`
- It is still small and self-contained enough to be Rust-owned end-to-end because the surviving owner is one public Nu file (`nushell/scripts/yzx/keys.nu`) rather than a larger subsystem seam
- It is not a trivial copy move because the family includes six public leaves and aliases (`yzx`, `yazi`, `hx`, `helix`, `nu`, `nushell`) plus human-oriented ANSI/table formatting and section structure

Crate and dependency gate:
- Production crates: no new crates by default
- Dev-only crates: none
- In-house logic: keep alias dispatch, ANSI text shaping, and simple table-like rendering in-house unless a later implementation proves that broad formatting crates buy materially safer parity than handwritten output
- Rejected default: do not add a terminal styling/table framework just to avoid writing a few formatted strings; that would weaken the deletion value of this lane

Deletion budget and parity gate:
- Preserve all existing aliases exactly: `yzx keys`, `yzx keys yzx`, `yzx keys yazi`, `yzx keys hx`, `yzx keys helix`, `yzx keys nu`, `yzx keys nushell`
- Preserve the current discoverability role and user-facing copy structure; this is still a keybinding/help surface, not a docs rewrite
- Preserve enough ANSI/table/readability quality that the Rust cut does not regress the human UX into a flat dump
- Count the migration only if the full public family becomes Rust-owned and `nushell/scripts/yzx/keys.nu` disappears
- If exact parity would require a broad crate or a bigger product rewrite than this bead justifies, record that stop condition before implementation instead of forcing a low-quality port
close_reason: Decision recorded: yzx keys is still a real next Rust owner cut, but only if it preserves the full alias family plus the current ANSI/table-style discoverability surface without adding broad new crates.

title: Implement the yzx config reset owner cut and delete nushell/scripts/yzx/config.nu
close_reason: Completed: yzx config reset is Rust-owned, nushell/scripts/yzx/config.nu is deleted, backup and no-backup behavior are covered by focused public CLI tests, and the installed-runtime contract no longer ships or expects the deleted Nu owner.

title: Re-evaluate Clap after config and help-support collapse plus the doctor decision
notes:
Plan-review refinement:
- do not reopen Clap just because the config, support, and doctor beads exist
- reopen only if those lanes materially shrink the surviving internal-family routing surface, not merely move leaf text or wrap existing Nushell behavior
- if Clap is still a no-go, record the exact remaining families and the next honest deletion candidate instead of creating premature implementation beads
Decision on 2026-04-21:
- no-go for a Clap implementation lane now
- the public Rust root is already small and schema-driven
- the surviving internal-Nu families are cwd, desktop, dev, edit, enter, import, launch, menu, popup, restart, reveal, screen, tutor, and whats_new
- most of those families are still intentionally Nushell-owned because they are shell-heavy, process-heavy, or mostly product UX
- adding Clap now would mostly replace a small amount of family-classification logic while keeping or re-encoding the same internal-family table and family-specific fallback behavior
- the next honest public-family deletion candidate is core/yzx_workspace.nu through a joint yzx cwd / yzx reveal evaluation, tracked in yazelix-7nyk
close_reason: Completed: Clap re-evaluated as a no-go after the config, support, keys, and doctor report-owner cuts; next honest deletion candidate recorded.

title: Implement the yzx doctor report-only owner cut or record the explicit stop condition
notes: Implementation target: Rust-own the public yzx doctor report path end to end. Delete nushell/scripts/core/yzx_doctor.nu and nushell/scripts/utils/doctor_report_bridge.nu, move report aggregation/summary/rendering and live Zellij plugin-health checks into Rust, keep only a private Nushell doctor_fix.nu helper for side-effecting --fix actions, and leave install-ownership request shaping in a focused install_ownership.nu helper.
close_reason: Completed: yzx_control now owns the public doctor report path, doctor_fix.nu is the only surviving private repair helper, and the shared doctor report bridge is deleted.

title: Define the yzx doctor report/fix split and the report-only go/no-go
notes: Decision: proceed with a report-only Rust owner cut. Scope: move public yzx doctor, summary/rendering, JSON emission, install/runtime/helix/config report aggregation, and live Zellij plugin-health reporting into Rust; keep only the side-effecting repair flow as a private Nushell helper. Crate gate: no new production crates and no new dev-only crates; use existing serde/serde_json/lexopt plus in-house std::process/std::fs path and command helpers. Rejected alternative: a thin Rust shim over the current Nushell doctor family would still leave the public owner mixed and does not count as deletion. Stop condition: if the Rust owner cannot delete nushell/scripts/core/yzx_doctor.nu and nushell/scripts/utils/doctor_report_bridge.nu, this bead should close as a no-go instead.
close_reason: Completed: the report-only Rust owner cut is honest because it deletes nushell/scripts/core/yzx_doctor.nu and nushell/scripts/utils/doctor_report_bridge.nu while leaving only a private fix helper.

title: Decide whether yzx doctor has an honest report-only Rust owner cut before Clap
close_reason: Completed: the honest report-only Rust doctor owner cut landed and the remaining doctor Nu work is private fix-only helper scope.

title: Implement Rust ownership for yzx why and yzx sponsor and delete nushell/scripts/core/yzx_support.nu
notes: Revised during plan review: this bead now only owns the tiny support family. Keep the URL-opening behavior, fallback-to-print behavior, and current user-visible copy intact while deleting the Nushell owner.
close_reason: Completed: yzx why and yzx sponsor now route through yzx_control, the shared public command surface classifies both leaves as Rust-owned, nushell/scripts/core/yzx_support.nu is deleted, and focused public-route tests plus the installed-runtime contract validate the cut.

title: Define the batch boundary for yzx why and yzx sponsor
notes:
Initial batch boundary proposal:
- include `yzx why`, `yzx sponsor`, and `yzx keys*`
- exclude `yzx tutor*` for now because `yazelix-3jw` keeps product evolution open on that family
- exclude `yzx whats_new` for now because it still fronts the Nushell-owned `upgrade_summary` subsystem, so moving only the public leaf would be cosmetic rather than a real owner cut
Revised during plan review: `yzx keys` is no longer bundled into this smallest-family bead because it has its own alias and formatting parity surface.

This bead is now strictly about the tiny support leaf family in `yzx_support.nu`.

Still excluded here:
- `yzx tutor*` because `yazelix-3jw` keeps the product surface open
- `yzx whats_new` because it still fronts the Nushell-owned `upgrade_summary` subsystem and moving only the leaf would be cosmetic
Decision:
- `yzx why` and `yzx sponsor` are the smallest honest public-family Rust cut left in the help/support lane
- They should stay batched together because they share one tiny surviving Nu owner (`nushell/scripts/core/yzx_support.nu`) and do not carry the alias/table-formatting surface that makes `yzx keys` riskier
- `yzx keys*` stays out of this bead and remains its own decision lane because its alias set (`yzx`, `yazi`, `hx`, `helix`, `nu`, `nushell`) plus ANSI/table discoverability output are a separate parity problem
- `yzx tutor*` remains excluded because `yazelix-3jw` still owns product evolution there
- `yzx whats_new` remains excluded because it still fronts the Nushell-owned `upgrade_summary` subsystem and moving only the leaf would be cosmetic

Dependency and crate gate:
- Production crates: no new crates
- Dev-only crates: none
- In-house logic: keep static info rendering and opener probing in-house with std + existing control-plane structure
- Rejected alternative: keeping `yzx sponsor` in Nu just because it probes `xdg-open` / `open` would preserve a trivial mixed owner seam for no real benefit

Deletion budget:
- Move `yzx why` and `yzx sponsor` to Rust control
- Remove both leaves from `INTERNAL_NU_FAMILIES`
- Delete `nushell/scripts/core/yzx_support.nu`
- Remove installed-runtime expectations and stale docs/spec references to that deleted owner while preserving current copy and sponsor fallback behavior
close_reason: Decision recorded: yzx why and yzx sponsor stay batched as the smallest honest help/support Rust cut, while yzx keys remains a separate alias-heavy decision lane with no new crates.

title: Collapse the smallest help and support internal yzx families
close_reason: Completed: the smallest help/support internal yzx families are collapsed; yzx why, yzx sponsor, and yzx keys are now Rust-owned and their surviving Nu owner files are deleted.

title: Implement the non-mutating yzx config read owner cut
notes:
Revised during plan review: this bead is now only the non-mutating side of the family.

Scope:

- establish the family root on the Rust side without yet claiming the mutating reset path

Non-goal:

close_reason: Completed: yzx config and yzx config --path now route through yzx_control, the shared command surface classifies config as Rust-owned, and focused public CLI tests cover path and bootstrap behavior.

title: Define the deletion budget and dependency gate for the yzx config owner cut
notes:
Working hypothesis:
- `yzx config` is the next strongest honest owner cut after `yzx home_manager`
- the public Nu owner is small, while config path and surface ownership already have Rust support in `active_config_surface`

Dependency gate:
- preserve `yzx config --path` and `yzx config reset` behavior exactly
- no new production crates by default unless a real parsing or time-format need appears that is more stable than in-house/std logic
Additional guardrails after plan review:
- do not count a Rust wrapper that shells back into `config_surfaces.nu` as an owner cut
- the decision must say whether Rust owns the user-visible config display surface directly, whether output stays text/TOML/record-compatible enough for current users, and how `yzx config reset` preserves confirmation, backup naming, and fail-fast behavior
- if exact output parity is too coupled to Nushell-only rendering, record that explicitly before implementation instead of discovering it halfway through the task
Decision:
- Rust should own the full public `yzx config` family directly; this is a real owner cut because config path discovery and active config surface loading already live in Rust and the remaining Nu owner is a thin command wrapper
- Production crates: no new crates; use existing `toml`, `serde_json`, and std facilities already in `yazelix_core`
- Dev-only crates: none
- In-house logic: implement config display rendering, confirmation flow, backup naming, and reset file operations in-house inside the existing control/runtime model
- Rejected alternatives: a Rust wrapper that shells back into `config_surfaces.nu` or keeps `config reset` in Nu would preserve the mixed owner seam and does not satisfy the deletion budget
- Packaging impact: delete `nushell/scripts/yzx/config.nu` and remove its export from the installed runtime once read and reset paths both land

Deletion budget:
- Move `yzx config` and `yzx config --path` to Rust control without behavior drift
- Move `yzx config reset [--yes] [--no-backup]` to Rust control with preserved confirmation, backup, and fail-fast semantics
- Remove the `config` family from `INTERNAL_NU_FAMILIES`
- Delete the surviving Nu public owner file and any stale contract/docs references

Parity decisions:
- `yzx config` will continue to print the active merged config as TOML text, not a Rust debug/JSON view
- `yzx config --path` must preserve the resolved active config file path behavior exactly
- `yzx config reset` must preserve confirmation by default, explicit `--yes`, explicit `--no-backup`, fail-fast IO errors, and a user-visible backup path message
- Backup naming should stay human-readable and stable rather than switch to opaque epoch-based suffixes
close_reason: Decision recorded: Rust owns the full yzx config family with no new crates; preserve TOML display, path, confirmation, backup, and fail-fast behavior while deleting the surviving Nu owner.

title: Rust-own the yzx config family end to end
close_reason: Completed: the full yzx config family is now Rust-owned through yzx_control, the shared public command surface metadata/help/externs reflect the new owner, and the surviving public Nu config owner file is deleted from the runtime contract.

title: Shrink the remaining internal yzx family rule engine until a Clap decision is honest

title: Re-evaluate Clap after shared-schema and public-owner prerequisites land
description: Re-evaluate Clap only after the shared schema, parity surface, and next-family deletion work land. This is the first future point where a Clap rewrite might become deletion-positive.
design: Use the closed yazelix-rxym no-go as the baseline. Reopen Clap only if the shared schema now owns public metadata and parser shape, parity coverage is strong enough to protect behavior, and one more public-owner cut has materially reduced the surviving direct internal route table. If those gates still are not met, keep the no-go.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Re-evaluates Clap against the post-prerequisite command surface, not the old router shape. 2. Either creates a new honest Clap implementation lane with an exact deletion budget or records that Clap still does not make sense. 3. Does not reopen Clap by default just because the prerequisites exist.
notes:
Re-evaluation after shared-schema ownership and one more public-family cut:
- satisfied prerequisite: the shared public command surface is now the single Rust-owned source for public metadata, menu visibility, aliases, subcommand shape, and root route ownership
- satisfied prerequisite: one more public family (`yzx home_manager`) moved from a surviving public Nushell owner to Rust control
- conclusion: Clap is still a no-go for now
- why: the remaining complexity is still the direct internal-Nu family planner and owner split, not ad-hoc top-level parsing. The current Rust root/parser path is already small and schema-driven, so adding Clap now would mostly wrap or duplicate a working route planner instead of deleting enough code
- honest future gate: revisit only after another substantial direct internal-Nu family collapse, or if a future command-surface plan can let Clap replace the current family-routing rule engine rather than sit beside it
close_reason: Completed with a no-go. The shared-schema and public-owner prerequisites are now better, but Clap still would not delete enough of the surviving family-routing rule engine to justify itself yet.
