close_reason: Completed: live sidebar/Yazi consumers now read sidebar_yazi from get_active_tab_session_state, the duplicate Nushell sidebar-state bridge file was deleted, the raw get_active_sidebar_yazi pipe command was removed from the pane orchestrator, and Yazi regressions now defend the session-snapshot seam directly.

title: Rust-own install ownership and launcher provenance; delete install_ownership.nu and launcher_resolution.nu as decision owners
description: Move install ownership, stable wrapper selection, desktop launcher provenance, and Home Manager versus manual artifact classification behind one Rust-owned report so install_ownership.nu and launcher_resolution.nu stop being decision owners across doctor, home_manager prepare, and launch-adjacent flows.
design:
Boundary: execute as install-provenance slice, not an expansion of runtime-contract.evaluate. runtime_contract.rs owns launch/preflight (terminal, nixGL); this slice owns install ownership, wrapper provenance, desktop-entry freshness, takeover artifacts, and doctor-facing shadowing findings.

Rust seam: one new yzx_core helper (working name install-ownership.evaluate) returning typed JSON consumed by doctor, desktop/install flows, Home Manager prepare, and yzx restart.

Scope gate (b5u1): prefer dropping legacy managed shell-block upgrade path in this slice; fallback isolate shell-block into tiny Nu-only compatibility; do not port shell-block parsing into Rust unless that path is explicitly retained.

Request inputs explicit (not hidden env): runtime_dir, home_dir, user, xdg_config_home, xdg_data_home, command_search_paths, invoked_yzx_path, redirected_from_stale_yzx_path, optional include_legacy_shell_blocks.

Response covers: install_owner (manual|home-manager), stable_wrapper, desktop launcher path/provenance, desktop entry state (local/profile, Exec, Terminal, shadowing), home_manager prepare artifacts, doctor findings for freshness and wrapper shadowing.

Mutation stays in Nu: mv/backup, shell-block rewrite for prepare --apply, desktop install/uninstall, doctor prose.

Deletion gates: install_ownership.nu no longer decides ownership; launcher_resolution.nu gone or trivial; doctor_install_artifacts.nu stops path/ownership reasoning; legacy shell blocks isolated or removed.

Verification: existing test_yzx_doctor_commands.nu and test_yzx_core_commands.nu; add focused Rust tests for classification, wrapper selection, desktop parsing, shadowing, artifacts.
acceptance_criteria: 1. install_ownership.nu and launcher_resolution.nu materially shrink or disappear as decision owners. 2. Doctor, home_manager prepare, and related flows consume one Rust-owned ownership or provenance report. 3. Legacy shell-block compatibility is removed or explicitly isolated instead of silently carried forward. 4. Tests defend ownership and provenance contracts, not help text.
notes:
Execution sequence: (1) b5u1 boundary decision—this implementation retires legacy shell-block path: remove detection/cleanup from prepare and doctor, align with b5u1 acceptance. (2) Contract in bead/spec. (3) Rust module + yzx_core command. (4) Nu consumer rewrites and deletions. (5) cargo test + nu tests.

Dependency posture: std/in-house + serde; reuse patterns from runtime_contract.rs for path/exec checks.
close_reason: Completed: install-ownership.evaluate in yzx_core; Nushell install_ownership_report.nu bridge; doctor/home_manager/desktop/launcher_resolution/runtime_distribution consume Rust report; removed doctor_install_artifacts.nu and legacy shell-block doctor/prepare paths (b5u1). Integration test install_ownership_evaluate_prints_ok_envelope. Verified: cargo test -p yazelix_core, home_manager prepare tests, validate_specs.

title: Collapse bootstrap and generated-state orchestration to thin Nushell bridges
description: Config normalization, config-state hashing, runtime-env planning, runtime-contract checks, materialization planning, and status summary construction are already Rust-owned, but Nushell still carries too much orchestration and duplicate decision glue in generated_runtime_state.nu, environment_bootstrap.nu, runtime_contract_checker.nu, and nearby bridge helpers. This bead exists to delete that leftover deterministic reasoning so those modules become thin bridges or disappear, instead of letting helper-backed Rust and large Nu orchestration live forever in parallel.
design: Delete-first boundary: keep text renderers, shell process execution, and host integration in Nushell where Rust would not simplify them. Move only the surviving deterministic orchestration and classification that can be centralized behind existing yzx_core/yzx_control seams. A successful outcome materially shrinks utils/bootstrap files rather than adding more helper argv builders and bridge wrappers. Stop if the work would preserve the same Nu owner path behind more JSON plumbing.
acceptance_criteria: 1. generated_runtime_state.nu, environment_bootstrap.nu, runtime_contract_checker.nu, config_parser.nu, or adjacent bridge files lose real shipped logic, not just line reshuffles. 2. The remaining Nushell layer is clearly bridge/render/process glue rather than a second decision owner. 3. Existing startup/generated-state/runtime-contract behavior remains defended by focused tests. 4. The slice yields visible shipped Nushell reduction in large utils/bootstrap files.
notes:
Sequencing note: start with yazelix-ulb2.5.1 (generated-state/materialization orchestration + thin merger owners), then take yazelix-ulb2.5.2 (startup/launch preflight and bootstrap orchestration). This keeps the first cut focused on the biggest delete seam around generated_runtime_state.nu before touching the startup/launch wrappers and runtime_contract_checker.nu.
2026-04-20 residual-owner audit:
- ulb2.5.1 and ulb2.5.2 landed, but a non-trivial Nu bridge layer still survives in the exact family this umbrella owns
- the current high-value residual is the remaining yzx_core/yzx_control bridge/report glue in config_parser.nu, runtime_contract_checker.nu, generated_runtime_state.nu, runtime_env.nu, status_report.nu, config_state.nu, doctor_*_report.nu, install_ownership_report.nu, and the Yazi/Zellij render-plan bridges
- next work under ulb2.5 should target deleting or collapsing those bridge owners instead of adding more helper argv builders or JSON plumbing
close_reason: Completed: child lanes 5.1, 5.2, and 5.3 landed the generated-state/bootstrap/bridge collapse; any new generator deletion work belongs under ulb2.3 siblings.

title: Rust-own doctor/preflight findings and delete large Nushell doctor check owners
description: Move doctor and preflight classification behind one Rust-owned findings engine instead of leaving nushell/scripts/utils/doctor.nu and its helper files as the main owners of runtime/install health reasoning. The goal is to materially shrink shipped Nushell in the doctor/preflight family, especially doctor.nu, doctor_helix.nu, doctor_install_artifacts.nu, and runtime_distribution_capabilities.nu, while keeping human rendering and fix actions in Nushell where shell-side effects still belong.
design: Delete-first boundary: Rust should own the structured findings model reused by yzx doctor, launch preflight, and adjacent install-smoke/reporting paths when the same reasoning already exists in yzx_core or can be consolidated there. Nushell should keep only rendering, severity-to-UX mapping when truly surface-specific, and --fix side effects. Do not land a second helper seam that leaves the same authoritative checks in doctor.nu. Prefer one findings pipeline over more one-off commands. Reuse runtime-contract.evaluate only if the end state deletes real Nushell ownership rather than expanding parallel bridge code.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The main doctor/preflight reasoning no longer lives in doctor.nu and related Nu helpers. 2. doctor.nu, doctor_helix.nu, doctor_install_artifacts.nu, and/or runtime_distribution_capabilities.nu are materially shrunk or partially deleted, not just wrapped. 3. Human-readable doctor output and fix actions still work, but consume Rust-owned structured findings. 4. Tests defend real findings contracts instead of command discovery noise.
notes: Composer 2 prep: do not hand off the whole remaining doctor surface at once. Preferred next bounded child is yazelix-ulb2.4.2 (Helix findings family), because doctor_helix.nu is still a large standalone Nu owner and the fix boundary can remain in Nushell. After that, revisit the remaining doctor.nu surfaces separately: config/reporting, shared runtime preflight/runtime distribution, and zellij plugin health.
close_reason: All bounded doctor/preflight children are complete. Remaining serious Nushell-deletion work has moved to the bridge-layer, materialization/generator, and command-registry/documentation seams tracked elsewhere under ulb2.

title: Trim shared bridge/runtime glue after env/run cut over to Rust
description: After env/run are Rust-owned, audit the shared bridge and runtime-env helpers those commands used to depend on and remove dead or no-longer-justified Nushell glue. The main targets are the env/run-specific portions of environment_bootstrap.nu, runtime_env.nu, core command export/use wiring, and any config_parser.nu bridge code that was only retained for the old public wrappers.
design: Delete-first. Do not leave a large compatibility layer just because it is already there. If a surviving helper is still needed by other Nushell-owned commands, shrink it aggressively and make the remaining ownership explicit. This is the checkpoint that decides whether ulb2.1 actually deleted a meaningful owner seam or merely moved some execution into Rust while leaving the same bridge complexity behind.
acceptance_criteria: The post-cutover audit deletes or materially shrinks the shared Nushell glue that existed only to support public env/run ownership. ulb2.1 should not close until this cleanup confirms a real shipped Nushell reduction beyond the obvious wrapper files.
close_reason: Dropped unused activate_runtime_env from runtime_env.nu; extern bridge appends explicit yzx env/run blocks. Further env/run-only glue in config_parser/environment_bootstrap is still required by other commands (status, parse_yazelix_config, popups).

title: Implement Rust-owned yzx run and delete the public Nushell run owner
description: Move yzx run onto the Rust-owned control-plane path and delete nushell/scripts/yzx/run.nu as the public owner. Preserve child argv passthrough, especially dash-prefixed child args, cwd preservation, and current runtime env activation semantics without keeping wrapper-side parsing in Nushell.
design: Rust should own argv parsing for run, including the no-wrapper-flags behavior after the command boundary, runtime-env activation, cwd handling, and child process launch. Keep the existing high-signal passthrough contract; do not add weak help/discovery tests. The implementation should not shell back into the old run.nu wrapper or reintroduce wrapper-side argument consumption.
acceptance_criteria: yzx run works through the Rust-owned path with current child-argv passthrough semantics preserved. nushell/scripts/yzx/run.nu is deleted or demoted out of public ownership, and the shipped Nushell surface is smaller by the end of the bead.
close_reason: yzx run is parsed and executed in yzx_control with child argv preserved; deleted nushell/scripts/yzx/run.nu. Integration tests invoke yzx_control with a checked-in argv-log fixture.

title: Implement Rust-owned yzx env and delete the public Nushell env owner
description: Move yzx env onto the Rust-owned control-plane path and delete nushell/scripts/yzx/env.nu as the public owner. Preserve current contract behavior such as configured-shell launch, invoking-shell no-shell mode, cwd preservation, and clear failure handling without routing main behavior back through Nushell wrappers.
design: Rust should own argv parsing for env, shell selection logic, cwd preservation, runtime-env construction, and shell/process spawn for this command. Keep only truly minimal shell-integration glue if unavoidable. Do not count this bead complete if Nushell still owns the public env command path or if the implementation simply shells back into the old env.nu wrapper.
acceptance_criteria: yzx env works through the Rust-owned path with the existing behavior contract preserved. nushell/scripts/yzx/env.nu is deleted or demoted out of public ownership, and the shipped Nushell surface is smaller by the end of the bead.
close_reason: Implemented libexec/yzx_control env via yzx_cli.sh dispatch; deleted nushell/scripts/yzx/env.nu and removed its export from yazelix.nu. Config load and runtime env use Rust (active_config_surface + normalize_config + compute_runtime_env) without prepare_environment.

title: Define the Rust public entrypoint and deletion budget for yzx env/run
description: Before implementation, pin down the transition shape that lets yzx env/run stop being public Nushell commands without forcing a broad all-commands Rust CLI rewrite. The output should name the exact entrypoint/dispatcher shape, the exact Nushell modules expected to stop being public owners, and the deletion budget required before the work can count as done.
design: Reject the helper-only shape. The transition must not keep nushell/scripts/yzx/env.nu and nushell/scripts/yzx/run.nu as public parsers behind a Rust shim. Prefer a public dispatcher or dedicated Rust command path that can short-circuit env/run before the Nushell command tree loads. Name what stays out of scope: broad root/help/completion migration, launch/enter/restart, status/doctor, and config/edit/import UX.
acceptance_criteria: There is a written implementation contract for the env/run migration that identifies the Rust entrypoint, the surviving Nushell role if any, the exact public Nushell owner files to delete or demote, and the minimum shipped Nushell LOC reduction expected from the slice.
notes:
2026-04-20 audit findings before implementation:
- Current public owners are nushell/scripts/yzx/env.nu, nushell/scripts/yzx/run.nu, and their export use entries in nushell/scripts/core/yazelix.nu. Closing ulb2.1 must delete or demote those as public parsers, not merely add a Rust helper behind them.
- yzx env contract to preserve: configured-shell default mode uses login shell args, --no-shell uses the invoking shell when available, cwd is preserved, SHELL is set to the launched shell executable, and launch failure prints a clear tip about yzx env --no-shell.
- yzx run contract to preserve: child argv passthrough starts immediately after the command boundary; dash-prefixed child args and child --verbose flags must remain child argv rather than wrapper flags; cwd is preserved.
- Current inefficiency worth deleting: env/run both call prepare_environment, which computes config_state/needs_refresh even though these commands only consume config. The Rust-owned path should not carry that wasted bootstrap work.
- runtime_env.nu is still used by launch, start_yazelix, popup wrappers, and some integration helpers, so env/run cutover alone will not delete that file. ulb2.1.4 exists specifically to force post-cutover bridge cleanup rather than pretending the owner seam is gone once the obvious wrapper files disappear.
- Test policy for this slice: keep the existing high-signal yzx run passthrough regressions; add at most one focused env contract regression if needed; reject help/discovery/parity tests that do not defend real command behavior.
- Preferred transition shape to evaluate in ulb2.1.1: a Rust public dispatcher or direct command path that can short-circuit env/run before the Nushell command tree loads. Reject any plan that still routes main env/run behavior back through the existing Nushell wrappers.
close_reason: Completed: wrote docs/specs/yzx_env_run_rust_owner_transition.md, linked the bead to the spec, pinned the env/run transition shape around yzx_cli.sh -> internal Rust control-plane dispatch, defined the public-owner deletion budget, and validated specs.

title: Only migrate a config generator to Rust if it deletes the full Nushell owner end-to-end
description: Re-evaluate generator migration under a stricter rule: no more renderer-adjacent helper slices that preserve the same Nushell owner with more bridge code. A future generator move is acceptable only if it transfers a whole generated surface end-to-end and materially deletes the corresponding shipped Nushell generator owner.
design: Start with a short audit comparing Yazi vs Zellij as full-owner migration candidates. Prefer the smaller, clearer surface if there is one. Reject partial render-plan follow-ups by default. Good outcomes are either: 1) a concrete full-owner migration plan that can delete the generator file/class, or 2) an explicit decision that the remaining generators should stay Nushell-owned because a full Rust move would not simplify the architecture.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear go/no-go decision for the next generator migration based on owner deletion, shipped Nushell reduction, and architecture clarity. No additive helper-only generator bead should survive this audit. If a generator is chosen, the follow-up target is whole-owner deletion rather than another plan-data seam.
notes:
2026-04-20 priority correction: keep this audit alive, but it is no longer the next serious Rust deletion target. Current tokei numbers show the rewrite is still nearly flat on shipped Nushell, so larger surviving owners in doctor/preflight, bootstrap/orchestration, and public command discovery come first. Revisit generator migration only if those bigger owners are exhausted or a full-file renderer deletion candidate becomes concrete.
2026-04-20 delete-first inventory refresh:
- after the bridge/report family, the biggest remaining product-side Nu deletion opportunity is likely a full-owner materialization/generator move rather than another small CLI slice
- treat Yazi, Zellij, and terminal materialization as serious candidates only if a chosen slice deletes the corresponding Nu owner end-to-end
- do not let the current "renderers stay in Nushell" policy become an untouchable assumption if it blocks the largest real Nushell deletions
2026-04-20 audit outcome: choose Yazi as the first full-owner generator/materialization deletion lane. Evidence: Yazi is the smallest coherent owner family still large enough to matter (about 739 raw Nu lines across yazi_config_merger.nu, yazi_bundled_assets.nu, and yazi_user_overrides.nu), its outputs are bounded, and it does not carry Zellij's larger KDL/plugin/layout/reuse state surface. Defer Zellij until after the first whole-owner cut proves we can actually delete a generator family instead of adding another helper seam. Follow-up implementation bead: yazelix-ulb2.3.1.
close_reason: Completed

title: Delete remaining Nushell status reasoning by moving status report construction behind a Rust-owned seam
description: Move the typed `yzx status` summary owner behind Rust only if that deletes the surviving Nushell reasoning in `nushell/scripts/utils/status_report.nu`. The execution target is the structured `summary` payload already defined by `docs/specs/status_doctor_machine_readable_reports.md`, not a broader doctor or clap rewrite. Composer 2 should implement one narrow Rust-backed status-compute seam and leave Nushell as a thin renderer and dispatch layer only.
design:
Execution-ready boundary:
1. Add one narrow Rust/private-bridge seam for status summary construction (for example `yzx_core status.compute`) that returns the typed `summary` payload consumed by both `yzx status` and `yzx status --json`.
2. Rust must own the current `summary` fields now assembled in `collect_status_report`: `version`, `description`, `config_file`, `runtime_dir`, `logs_dir`, `generated_state_repair_needed`, `generated_state_materialization_status`, `generated_state_materialization_reason`, `default_shell`, `terminals`, `helix_runtime`, `persistent_sessions`, and `session_name`.
3. `versions` stays out of the first Rust seam unless the implementation can move it without broadening scope; keep `--versions` attachment and rendering in Nushell if needed.
4. `render_status_report` may stay Nushell-owned, but `collect_status_report` must be deleted or reduced to a trivial bridge with no remaining summary reasoning.
5. Do not broaden this slice into `yzx doctor`, root help or completion work, clap or public CLI ownership, or text-heavy UX rewrites.
6. Stop rather than land another additive helper if the final diff leaves the same authoritative status reasoning in Nushell.
acceptance_criteria:
Composer-2-ready acceptance:
- `yzx status --json` gets its `summary` payload from a Rust-owned seam instead of Nushell-side report construction.
- A meaningful chunk of `nushell/scripts/utils/status_report.nu` is deleted; surviving Nushell code is limited to thin report rendering and optional `--versions` attachment.
- `nushell/scripts/core/yazelix.nu` keeps only thin dispatch for `yzx status`.
- Existing defended behavior remains true for default human output and JSON output.
- If the implementation cannot name concrete deleted Nushell reasoning by the end, the bead is not done.
notes:
2026-04-20 roadmap note: this slice is now the active deletion-gate candidate for the broader v16 public Rust/clap CLI path. If status cannot move behind a real Rust-owned seam that deletes surviving Nushell reasoning, that weakens the case for any broader clap rewrite and should stop future additive CLI rewrite optimism rather than spawning another helper layer.
2026-04-20 composer-2 prep:
- primary live owner targets: `nushell/scripts/utils/status_report.nu` and the `yzx status` span in `nushell/scripts/core/yazelix.nu`
- current defended checks to keep green: `test_yzx_status_reports_basic_runtime_summary`, `test_yzx_status_json_reports_typed_summary`, and `test_yzx_status_json_reports_materialization_repair_when_artifacts_missing` in `nushell/scripts/dev/test_yzx_core_commands.nu`
- recommended verification for this slice: run those status tests plus any new focused Rust unit tests for the typed status payload
- explicit non-goals for Composer 2: `yzx doctor`, broad clap or public-CLI ownership, prose-table redesign, or porting detached shell behavior into Rust
close_reason: Moved typed yzx status summary to yazelix_core status.compute; Nushell only bridges argv and renders; optional --versions still attached in Nushell.

title: Rust-own yzx env/run and delete the public Nushell control-plane wrapper seam
description: Move the first whole public control-plane family into Rust: yzx env and yzx run. The point is not helper reuse for its own sake; it is deleting the surviving Nushell owner path around command parsing, usage errors, machine-readable exit behavior, and the shared bridge/config helper blob that currently keeps config_parser.nu large.
design: Target a real owner deletion. Rust should become the primary owner for argv parsing, usage/help for the migrated family, control-plane request planning, and machine-readable error behavior. Nushell may keep only the thinnest host-integration or shell-activation adapter that cannot be deleted cleanly. Closing this bead should materially shrink or delete the public wrappers in nushell/scripts/yzx/env.nu and nushell/scripts/yzx/run.nu and remove a meaningful chunk of config_parser.nu bridge glue rather than adding another helper layer.
acceptance_criteria: The public yzx env/run family no longer depends on Nushell wrapper ownership for its main behavior. Shipped Nushell LOC is reduced by deleting or materially shrinking the env/run wrappers and associated bridge code, especially in config_parser.nu. Any surviving Nushell layer is clearly host-shell glue, not a second control-plane owner.
notes:
2026-04-20 audit findings before implementation:
- Current public owners are nushell/scripts/yzx/env.nu, nushell/scripts/yzx/run.nu, and their export use entries in nushell/scripts/core/yazelix.nu. Closing ulb2.1 must delete or demote those as public parsers, not merely add a Rust helper behind them.
- yzx env contract to preserve: configured-shell default mode uses login shell args, --no-shell uses the invoking shell when available, cwd is preserved, SHELL is set to the launched shell executable, and launch failure prints a clear tip about yzx env --no-shell.
- yzx run contract to preserve: child argv passthrough starts immediately after the command boundary; dash-prefixed child args and child --verbose flags must remain child argv rather than wrapper flags; cwd is preserved.
- Current inefficiency worth deleting: env/run both call prepare_environment, which computes config_state/needs_refresh even though these commands only consume config. The Rust-owned path should not carry that wasted bootstrap work.
- runtime_env.nu is still used by launch, start_yazelix, popup wrappers, and some integration helpers, so env/run cutover alone will not delete that file. ulb2.1.4 exists specifically to force post-cutover bridge cleanup rather than pretending the owner seam is gone once the obvious wrapper files disappear.
- Test policy for this slice: keep the existing high-signal yzx run passthrough regressions; add at most one focused env contract regression if needed; reject help/discovery/parity tests that do not defend real command behavior.
- Preferred transition shape to evaluate in ulb2.1.1: a Rust public dispatcher or direct command path that can short-circuit env/run before the Nushell command tree loads. Reject any plan that still routes main env/run behavior back through the existing Nushell wrappers.
2026-04-20 implementation contract recorded in docs/specs/yzx_env_run_rust_owner_transition.md. Decision: keep bin/yzx -> shells/posix/yzx_cli.sh as the public front door for now, but dispatch env/run directly to a dedicated internal Rust control-plane binary before the Nushell command tree loads. Remove public export ownership from core/yazelix.nu and delete/demote nushell/scripts/yzx/env.nu + run.nu rather than keeping them for help/completion.
close_reason: Verified: yzx_cli.sh dispatches env/run to yzx_control; Rust owns parse/exec and config via normalize+runtime-env; yazelix.nu no longer exports env/run modules (removed); extern bridge appends explicit env/run blocks; prepare_environment unused on env/run path. cargo test -p yazelix_core and yzx run passthrough tests pass.

title: Rust rewrite: delete Nushell owners, not just add helpers
description: Reset the Rust rewrite around one hard rule: each implementation slice must reduce shipped Nushell LOC and delete a surviving Nushell owner seam, not merely add helper-backed Rust under the same public/control-plane surface. The goal is clear, safe, performant architecture with fewer language boundaries, less glue, and more authoritative Rust ownership where it actually simplifies the system.
design:
Delete-first constraints:
1. Measure success against shipped Nushell owners, not nushell/scripts/dev test growth.
2. Do not close a Rust slice that leaves the same Nushell owner in place behind new bridge code.
3. Prefer whole-family ownership moves over helper-by-helper partial insertions.
4. Keep only thin rendering or host-integration layers in Nushell where Rust would not simplify the system.
5. Avoid weak parity or governance tests; keep only tests that defend a real runtime contract or regression.

Progress so far:
- ulb2.1 (env/run) is complete
- ulb2.2 (status summary ownership) is complete

Current target order:
- next: doctor/preflight findings ownership in Rust, with large Nushell doctor helpers shrinking to render/fix glue
- next: bootstrap/materialization orchestration cleanup so existing yzx_core ownership deletes bigger utils/bootstrap seams
- next: public command metadata/help/completion ownership and eventual public registry deletion for the v16 Rust CLI path
- later: generator migration only if it can delete a whole Nushell owner end-to-end instead of adding another render-plan seam
acceptance_criteria: There is a concrete child sequence that targets whole Nushell owner deletion, especially in shipped control-plane and generated-config surfaces. The backlog explicitly rejects additive helper slices that increase or preserve shipped Nushell complexity without deleting an owner.
notes:
2026-04-20 aggressive-rewrite reset:
- env/run and status deletion gates are complete, but the net Nushell result is still effectively flat: tokei from c1af5ef^..HEAD is +21 code lines for the whole nushell tree and only -43 code lines when excluding nushell/scripts/dev.
- conclusion: helper-backed leaf moves improved ownership, but did not materially shrink the shipped Nushell mass. Future children under ulb2 must target large surviving owners in nushell/scripts/utils, core, setup, or public command discovery instead of another narrow helper seam.
- new target order after ulb2.1 and ulb2.2: 1) doctor/preflight findings ownership, 2) bootstrap/materialization orchestration cleanup, 3) public command metadata/help/completion ownership, 4) only then revisit generator migration if it can delete a whole owner.
2026-04-20 delete-first inventory refresh:
- runtime-facing shipped Nushell under core/setup/utils/yzx/integrations/zellij_wrappers is still large enough to dominate the product-side rewrite budget
- the remaining Rust bridge layer is still roughly 1.5k lines of shipped Nushell across config_parser.nu, runtime_contract_checker.nu, generated_runtime_state.nu, runtime_env.nu, status_report.nu, config_state.nu, the doctor_*_report files, install_ownership_report.nu, and the Yazi/Zellij render-plan bridges
- the current inventory/spec layer is stale in exactly the wrong direction: multiple docs still reference deleted owners such as nushell/scripts/yzx/env.nu, nushell/scripts/yzx/run.nu, launch_state.nu, and the old config-migration files
- revised serious-deletion order: 1) replace stale inventory/spec-roadmap docs, 2) delete the remaining yzx_core/yzx_control Nu bridge owners, 3) choose a full-owner materialization/generator migration that deletes a major Nu owner, 4) continue public command metadata/help/completion ownership work only after the real product-side delete lanes are current
2026-04-20 follow-up reopening:
- ulb2 was closed after the first serious Rust rewrite wave, but a real product-side owner seam still survives in the runtime materialization lifecycle
- ulb2.5.1 and ulb2.5.3 thinned bridge glue, but the remaining owner is now spread across core/materialization_orchestrator.nu, utils/generated_runtime_state.nu, and utils/config_state.nu rather than one monolithic file
- this follow-up should be tracked as a fresh child lane under ulb2, not forced back into the already-closed thin-bridge tasks
2026-04-20 follow-up reopening: the next serious full-owner materialization lane is terminal plus Helix. The remaining product-side delete budget is no longer the runtime lifecycle owner; it is the generated terminal and Helix owner family around helix_config_merger.nu, terminal_configs.nu, and terminal_renderers.nu. Track that work under yazelix-ulb2.10 so the rewrite order keeps following the biggest surviving Nushell materialization owners.
close_reason: Completed: all child Rust rewrite deletion lanes are closed; the epic is stale-open and already eligible for close.

title: Sidebar and Yazi integration: consume orchestrator-owned tab truth instead of re-deriving it
description: Once the plugin exposes typed tab-local session truth, migrate sidebar/path-sync/Yazi-facing integrations to consume that seam instead of relying on recency-based state selection, ad-hoc pane lookups, or duplicated focus/sidebar inference. This directly targets the cross-tab locality class that already caused sidebar cwd leaks.
design: Treat this as a delete-first migration. Replace non-authoritative shell/Yazi derivations with plugin-owned answers where they overlap. Keep Lua as an adapter and keep config/rendering ownership in Nushell. Use the postmortem in yazelix-f0w as a guardrail against session-global or recency-based shortcuts.
acceptance_criteria: Relevant sidebar and Yazi integration flows use the orchestrator-owned seam for tab-local targeting wherever the plugin can answer authoritatively. The implementation removes or clearly narrows the older duplicated derivation paths, and regression checks defend against cross-tab mis-targeting.
close_reason: Completed: sidebar and Yazi integrations now consume pane-orchestrator session truth via get_active_tab_session_state, the duplicate raw sidebar-state seam was deleted, and shared post-retarget sync behavior is defended by focused regressions.

title: Pane orchestrator: expose a typed tab-local session-state report/query seam
description: Define and implement the first typed plugin-owned read surface for current-tab workspace/session truth: workspace root, managed pane ids/roles, focused owner, and sidebar visibility state. This creates the seam other layers can consume instead of inferring those facts from scattered state files or shell-side heuristics.
design: Keep the surface narrow and contract-first. Prefer one report/query shape that can be consumed by Nushell commands and sidebar/Yazi integrations. Do not drag full CLI ownership into Rust; this is a plugin/session-truth slice only. Reuse existing pane orchestrator ownership rather than inventing a second Rust state store.
acceptance_criteria: The pane orchestrator exposes a documented typed surface for tab-local session truth with focused tests for tab locality and pane-role identity. At least the current tab workspace root, managed editor/sidebar identity, focused pane role, and sidebar open/collapsed state are represented or explicitly scoped out with recorded reasons.
notes:
2026-04-20 Composer-2 prep:
- linked spec docs/specs/pane_orchestrator_tab_local_session_state_seam.md
- choose a new stable pipe command `get_active_tab_session_state` instead of promoting `debug_editor_state`
- v1 payload: schema_version, active_tab_position, workspace {root, source}, managed_panes {editor_pane_id, sidebar_pane_id}, focus_context, layout {active_swap_layout_name, sidebar_collapsed}, sidebar_yazi {yazi_id, cwd}
- current Rust sources to start from: main.rs State maps, panes.rs DebugEditorState/FocusContext, workspace.rs WorkspaceState, sidebar_yazi.rs get_active_sidebar_yazi_state_snapshot, layout.rs is_sidebar_closed
- first Nushell consumers: integrations/zellij.nu workspace-root readers plus sidebar/Yazi callers that still parse debug_editor_state or split sidebar state reads
- crate gate: no new production crates expected; stay on existing serde/serde_json + zellij_tile unless implementation proves otherwise
- doc drift fixed: workspace bootstrap root is plugin initial_cwd, not HOME
close_reason: Implemented get_active_tab_session_state pipe with versioned JSON (schema v1); Nushell read_current_tab_workspace_root and doctor use stable seam; synced wasm; lib unit test for snapshot keys; pane_orchestrator_component.md updated; fixed plugin_build.nu str join quoting.

title: Rust rewrite: make pane orchestrator the source of tab-local session truth
description: Move authoritative tab-local workspace/session truth closer to the Rust pane orchestrator instead of re-deriving it across Nushell and Yazi integration caches. This is the highest-value next Rust rewrite slice after the helper-backed config/render-plan work because it deletes a real multi-runtime correctness seam rather than porting more renderers.
design: Align with docs/specs/cross_language_runtime_ownership.md and docs/specs/rust_migration_matrix.md: keep renderers in Nushell, but move pane identity, focus/sidebar state, and tab-local workspace truth behind a typed orchestrator-owned seam. Lua/Yazi should remain an adapter, not a second owner. The goal is to delete duplicated state derivation outside the plugin, especially where tab-locality bugs can reappear.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear plan and child slices for exposing typed orchestrator-owned tab/session state, migrating Nushell/Yazi consumers to that seam, and deleting duplicate non-authoritative owners. The backlog record makes the owner boundary explicit: pane/session truth in the plugin, renderers and user-facing UX still in Nushell.
notes: 2026-04-20 prioritization update: keep this epic as the correctness-and-complexity lane for pane/session truth, not as the primary Nushell-LOC reduction lane. The new parent for deletion-first Rust ownership and shipped Nushell reduction is yazelix-ulb2. Work here should still prefer deleting duplicate state owners, but it is justified mainly by architecture safety and tab-local correctness rather than by raw control-plane LOC removal.
close_reason: all steps complete

title: Align Zellij render-plan defaults with the config contract and collapse split ownership
description:
The Zellij render-plan slice still encodes duplicated defaults across the Rust helper, the Nushell bridge request builder, and the Zellij generation fingerprint path. The sharpest example is `support_kitty_keyboard_protocol`: the canonical config contract and shipped default say `false`, but the helper-facing fallback path in the new slice still assumes `true`.

Today the normalized-config path usually masks this, so the problem is more contract drift and architecture debt than a fully reproduced startup bug. But it is exactly the kind of split ownership that `yazelix-3qs8` is supposed to delete before the same pattern gets copied into the Yazi render-plan work.
design:
Start with the Rust dependency gate note as usual (likely still in-house/std). Then make one owner responsible for helper-visible defaults and validation semantics instead of re-encoding them in multiple layers.

Implementation target:
- Audit the helper input defaults currently duplicated in: `rust_core/yazelix_core/src/zellij_render_plan.rs`, `nushell/scripts/utils/zellij_render_plan.nu`, and `nushell/scripts/setup/zellij_generation_state.nu`.
- Align `support_kitty_keyboard_protocol` with the canonical config contract/default surface.
- Remove duplicated helper-facing fallbacks where possible; prefer passing normalized values explicitly from Nushell and/or deriving defaults from the canonical contract seam instead of copy-pasting literals.
- Add focused tests for the omitted-field/default path so future helper slices cannot silently drift from `config_metadata/main_config_contract.toml`.
- Capture any remaining unavoidable split ownership in bead notes or a linked spec instead of leaving it implicit.

Delete-first rules:
- Do not just patch the Kitty default and leave the same pattern unexamined everywhere else.
- Do not widen this into Yazi implementation work; the goal is to fix the seam quality before `yazelix-3qs8.2` proceeds.

Non-goals:
- broad Zellij renderer rewrites
- changing user-facing generated config format unless required by contract alignment
acceptance_criteria:
1. The helper-visible default for `support_kitty_keyboard_protocol` matches `config_metadata/main_config_contract.toml` and `yazelix_default.toml`.
2. The Zellij render-plan path no longer relies on silently duplicated fallback literals across Rust/Nushell without an explicit ownership decision.
3. There is at least one focused regression test that would fail if helper defaults drift from the canonical config contract again.
4. `yazelix-3qs8.2` can build on the cleaned seam instead of copying the same split-default pattern.
notes: Created from the post-implementation review of `yazelix-3qs8.1`. This should land before or alongside the early planning for `yazelix-3qs8.2`.
close_reason: Aligned support_kitty_keyboard_protocol defaults with config contract (Rust serde, Nu bridge, generation fingerprint); regression test for omitted JSON field; layout renderer fails on unknown tray token.

title: Restore Rust test traceability for zellij-render-plan tests
description:
Post-implementation review of `yazelix-3qs8.1` found that the new Rust test files for the Zellij render-plan helper violate the repo's governed test metadata contract. `validate_rust_test_traceability.nu` currently fails because `rust_core/yazelix_core/src/zellij_render_plan.rs` and `rust_core/yazelix_core/tests/yzx_core_zellij_render_plan.rs` are missing the required `// Test lane:` declaration, and the integration test file also needs the normal nearby justification/strength markers for each test.

This is a process/CI failure even though the Rust and generated-config behavior tests are green.
design:
Keep this bead narrow and delete-first: do not rewrite the actual helper behavior or expand test scope unless needed for metadata locality.

Implementation target:
- Add the required `// Test lane:` declaration to both governed Rust test files.
- Add nearby `// Defends:`, `// Regression:`, or `// Invariant:` markers plus structured `// Strength:` lines for every governed test in those files.
- Keep the justification text specific to the actual contract these tests cover; do not paste boilerplate.
- Run the Rust traceability validator directly and keep the existing Rust test suites green.

Non-goals:
- changing helper behavior
- broad Rust test reorganization outside the two files above
acceptance_criteria:
1. `nix develop -c nu -c 'source nushell/scripts/dev/validate_rust_test_traceability.nu; main'` passes.
2. The governed tests in `rust_core/yazelix_core/src/zellij_render_plan.rs` and `rust_core/yazelix_core/tests/yzx_core_zellij_render_plan.rs` have valid lane + justification + strength metadata.
3. Relevant Rust tests for `yzx_core` still pass after the metadata-only cleanup.
notes: Review context: created from the post-implementation review of `yazelix-3qs8.1` after reproducing the validator failure locally.
close_reason: Added maintainer-lane test metadata to zellij_render_plan unit and integration tests; validate_rust_test_traceability passes.

title: Delete duplicate Nushell config diagnostics by consuming Rust config.normalize output
description: Yazelix already has a Rust-owned config.normalize core, but startup and doctor still carry duplicate Nushell-side config diagnostic reasoning. This task should make the Rust helper output the canonical machine-readable config findings consumed by the live parser, startup, and doctor paths so Nushell stops re-deriving the same schema and validation decisions.
design: Keep user-facing prose, fix guidance, and final error rendering in Nushell. The Rust helper should remain the typed owner of structured config diagnostics, while Nushell becomes a renderer and wrapper. Target the surviving logic around config_diagnostics.nu, config_schema.nu, config_parser.nu, and doctor consumers rather than broadening into migration machinery or public CLI changes.
acceptance_criteria: Packaged and source-checkout config parsing, startup diagnostics, and doctor consume one canonical Rust-backed config diagnostics source. Nushell keeps presentation, but no longer independently computes the same schema and validation findings in parallel.
notes: Delete-first rule: this bead is not done if Rust adds a second diagnostics path beside the existing Nushell one. By close, startup and doctor should consume one canonical structured diagnostics source or the bead should file explicit follow-up work for each surviving duplicate branch. Keep j498 blocked on this cleanup so migration evaluation does not restart before diagnostics duplication is gone.
close_reason: Completed: doctor and startup now consume Rust-backed config diagnostics from config.normalize, the duplicated Nushell schema-report path is removed, and targeted Rust/doctor/config-parser regressions pass.

title: Extend governed test traceability policy to first-party Rust tests
notes:
Completed in the current worktree:
- Added validate_rust_test_traceability.nu to enforce Rust test lane headers plus nearby justification and structured strength markers for first-party Rust test files under rust_core/ and rust_plugins/.
- Wired the validator into CI, AGENTS.md, and docs/specs/test_suite_governance.md.
- Annotated existing Rust tests in rust_core/yazelix_core and rust_plugins/zellij_pane_orchestrator to satisfy the strict policy immediately.
Verification:
- `nu nushell/scripts/dev/validate_rust_test_traceability.nu`
- `nu nushell/scripts/dev/validate_specs.nu`
- `nu nushell/scripts/dev/validate_default_test_traceability.nu`
- `nix shell nixpkgs#cargo nixpkgs#rustc -c cargo test --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml`
- `nix shell nixpkgs#cargo nixpkgs#rustc -c cargo test --manifest-path rust_plugins/zellij_pane_orchestrator/Cargo.toml --lib`
- `nix shell nixpkgs#nu-lint -c nu-lint nushell/scripts/dev/validate_rust_test_traceability.nu`
- `git diff --check`
close_reason: Completed: Rust tests now follow the same governed traceability and strength protocol as Nushell tests, enforced by CI and a dedicated validator.

title: Delete legacy rebuild_hash string-cache compatibility
notes:
2026-04-19 scope:
- Remove support for pre-2026-02-06 raw-string rebuild_hash cache files.
- Treat malformed or non-structured cache state as missing/stale instead of legacy-compatible.
- Delete the now-useless cached_hash surface and verbose launch print.
- Verify with malformed-cache probe, Rust tests, nu-lint, contract validator, and package build.
close_reason: Completed: raw-string rebuild_hash compatibility removed; malformed cache now behaves like missing state and the cached_hash surface was deleted.

title: Delete the legacy Nushell config parser fallback
description: After yazelix-kt5.2.4, packaged runtimes use libexec/yzx_core for config.normalize, but source checkouts still keep the legacy Nushell parser as an explicit fallback. This bead is the deletion step for that remaining fallback once the maintainer/source-checkout helper story is good enough.
design: First choose the source-checkout helper strategy: checked-in/dev-built yzx_core, explicit maintainer bootstrap, or another low-friction path. Do not make normal source checkout startup run cargo builds implicitly. Once source checkouts can reasonably execute yzx_core, remove parse_yazelix_config_with_nushell_fallback and the legacy contract parsing helpers from nushell/scripts/utils/config_parser.nu. Update tests so source-checkout parsing also exercises the Rust helper instead of preserving the fallback.
acceptance_criteria: Source checkout and packaged runtime config parsing both go through yzx_core config.normalize. The legacy Nushell parser fallback and its dead helper functions are deleted. Missing helper errors remain explicit and actionable. run_generated_config_canonical_tests and nu-lint pass after the deletion.
notes:
Implementation 2026-04-19: deleted the legacy Nushell config parser fallback and all local contract parsing helpers from config_parser.nu. parse_yazelix_config now always resolves a yzx_core helper and calls config.normalize. Packaged runtimes use runtime-local libexec/yzx_core first; source-checkout/maintainer flows use YAZELIX_YZX_CORE_BIN or a prebuilt rust_core/target helper, with clear host-dependency errors when no helper exists. maintainer_shell.nix now exports YAZELIX_YZX_CORE_BIN from the Nix-built rust_core helper before runtime_env parsing.

Verification: focused source/package helper tests; run_generated_config_canonical_tests with yzx_core helper; maintainer shellHook probe via nix develop; nu-lint on changed files; nix build #runtime #yazelix; packaged runtime parser probe; git diff --check.
close_reason: Completed: deleted the legacy Nushell config parser fallback; source and packaged runtimes now require yzx_core config.normalize with explicit maintainer/source helper resolution.

title: Wire live Nushell config parser to Rust config.normalize helper
description: Adopt the packaged yzx_core config.normalize helper from yazelix-kt5.2.1 in the live Nushell config parser path once helper discovery is safe for installed runtimes and source checkouts. This is the activation step, not another parser port.
design: Keep the command surface unchanged. Prefer runtime-local libexec/yzx_core when YAZELIX_RUNTIME_DIR resolves to a packaged runtime. For source checkouts, do not make startup depend on an unbuilt local Rust binary unless there is an explicit maintainer/dev path. Preserve the current Nushell parser as a deliberate compatibility path only where the Rust helper cannot be expected to exist yet, and make that boundary explicit instead of silently masking helper failures in packaged runtimes.
acceptance_criteria: Packaged/runtime Yazelix startup obtains normalized config through yzx_core config.normalize. Source-checkout behavior is explicitly defined and tested. Failures from the Rust helper produce clear config-class errors instead of silent fallback in packaged runtimes. A parity test proves the live parser returns the same normalized default config shape as the Rust helper.
notes:
Implementation 2026-04-19: live parse_yazelix_config now resolves the active runtime and uses runtime-local libexec/yzx_core config.normalize when present. Packaged runtimes without yzx_core now fail fast as host-dependency errors instead of falling back to the legacy Nushell parser. Source checkouts keep an explicit Nushell fallback until the maintainer/source helper story is resolved.

Verification: nu load check; source-checkout parse_yazelix_config against yazelix_default.toml; nix build #runtime plus packaged-runtime parse through libexec/yzx_core; run_generated_config_canonical_tests; nu-lint on changed Nu files; git diff --check.
close_reason: Completed: live config parsing now uses runtime-local yzx_core in packaged runtimes, fails fast when the packaged helper is missing, and preserves only an explicit source-checkout fallback tracked by yazelix-kt5.2.5.

title: Rust first slice: port generated runtime materialization planning and managed writes
description: After normalized config output and config-state hashing exist in Rust, move the generated-runtime materialization plan and managed-write ownership out of generated_runtime_state.nu. Target the decision layer that decides what should be materialized, when warm paths can skip rewrites, how managed state is recorded, and how atomic managed writes are coordinated.
design: Do not force all text-heavy renderers into Rust in this slice. The likely first move is a Rust owner for planning, invalidation, and write orchestration while Yazi and Zellij text assembly may remain Nushell until kt5.5 is decided. Preserve current startup profile/report step names at the boundary so before/after comparisons stay meaningful.
acceptance_criteria: There is a concrete plan or implementation target for Rust-owned generated-runtime materialization planning and managed writes that keeps warm-path skip behavior honest, records applied state deterministically, and preserves comparable startup profile labels before/after. The slice explicitly states which renderer surfaces stay Nushell for now.
notes:
2026-04-19 Rust dependency gate: before coding runtime_materialization plan/apply, record the crate-vs-in-house plan. Atomic writes, path handling, renderer boundaries, and plan data structures should be explicitly assigned to crates versus Yazelix-owned code before implementation starts.
2026-04-19 deletion ledger:
- Temporary seams expected to shrink here: generated_runtime_state.nu planning and managed-write orchestration, adjacent atomic_writes.nu call plumbing where Rust can own the decision/apply boundary, and transition-only parity cases in test_yzx_generated_configs.nu.
- Earliest acceptable survivors are text renderers that kt5.5 has not moved: yazi_config_merger.nu, zellij_config_merger.nu, and terminal_configs.nu.
- Must delete by close of kt5.2.3: either remove a real Nu orchestration seam in generated_runtime_state.nu or file explicit follow-up beads naming each surviving duplicate or shim and why it could not be deleted yet.
- Suspicion rule: if kt5.2.3 lands with new Rust plan/apply code but no net shrink in the surviving Nu orchestration path, treat the bead as incomplete.
2026-04-19 kt5.2.3 dependency decision:
- Production crates: keep the current helper stack only (`serde`, `serde_json`, `toml`, `sha2`, `thiserror`, `lexopt`). No new production crate is justified for runtime-materialization planning or apply verification.
- Dev-only crates: keep the existing Rust test helpers (`tempfile`, `pretty_assertions`, `assert_cmd`) only.
- In-house logic: materialization-plan modeling, expected-artifact resolution, missing-artifact classification, generated-state apply verification, and any bridge JSON shaping specific to Yazelix stay Yazelix-owned.
- Rejected alternatives: no filesystem helper crate, no atomic-write helper crate, no clap, no path-diff/path-absolutize convenience layer. The slice should reuse std fs/path plus existing helper patterns.
- Packaging impact: expected to stay within the current packaged `yzx_core` helper flow with no new Nix dependency surface beyond rebuilding the helper.
2026-04-19 implementation landed:
- Rust helper now owns runtime-materialization planning and apply verification via `runtime-materialization.plan` and `runtime-materialization.apply` in `yzx_core`.
- This slice intentionally kept Yazi and Zellij text rendering in Nushell; renderer ownership was not reopened here.
- Verified with `cargo test`, `nu-lint`, `validate_config_surface_contract.nu`, the canonical generated-config tests, `git diff --check`, `nix build .#runtime .#yazelix`, and a packaged-helper probe against the built runtime.
- The deletion suspicion rule still matters: ownership moved, but `generated_runtime_state.nu` remains too thick because of surviving helper-exec, JSON-envelope, and path-plumbing boilerplate. Follow-up `yazelix-kt5.2.7` tracks deleting that bridge glue before this boundary is considered clean.
close_reason: Completed: Rust now owns runtime-materialization planning and apply verification behind `yzx_core` while Nu keeps renderer ownership for now. The remaining generated_runtime_state.nu bridge boilerplate is tracked separately in `yazelix-kt5.2.7`.

title: Rust first slice: port config-state hashing and invalidation
description: Move the rebuild-hash and invalidation logic from config_state.nu into the Rust core after normalized config output exists. This includes rebuild-required field extraction, current-versus-cached hash computation, runtime hash composition, refresh reasoning, and managed-surface state recording semantics.
design: Treat this as typed deterministic state math, not launch orchestration. Preserve live v15 invalidation behavior for rebuild-required keys and runtime changes. Do not broaden this slice into migration machinery, shellhook code, or text-heavy generator template logic.
acceptance_criteria: There is a concrete plan or implementation target for Rust-owned config-state hashing and invalidation that preserves current refresh/no-refresh outcomes, hash/input boundaries, and machine-readable output for Nushell shims. Parity coverage defends unchanged inputs, runtime-only changes, config-only changes, and ignored non-rebuild keys.
notes:
2026-04-19 Rust dependency gate: before coding config_state.compute, record the crate-vs-in-house plan. Hashing/serialization crates must be justified against deterministic parity needs; state classification and refresh reasoning should default to in-house domain logic unless a crate has a clear narrow payoff.
2026-04-19 crate-vs-in-house decision for config_state.compute:
- Add production crate: sha2 for SHA-256 parity with Nushell `hash sha256`; implement lowercase hex encoding in-house to avoid a separate hex crate.
- Reuse existing production crates: serde/serde_json for bridge envelopes and state JSON; toml for parsing config and contract metadata; thiserror/lexopt for existing helper error and argv seams.
- Build in-house: rebuild-required key extraction, nested TOML projection, config/runtime hash composition, legacy string-cache compatibility, structured cache comparison, refresh-reason classification, and managed-main-surface recording rules.
- Keep out of this slice: Yazi/Zellij rendering, generated file materialization planning, config migration machinery, implicit Cargo builds in startup, and broad launch orchestration.
- Nix impact: yzx_core grows one small hashing dependency but remains packaged into runtime libexec/yzx_core; users still do not need Rust installed.
2026-04-19 implementation outcome:
- Added Rust-owned config_state.compute/config_state.record in yzx_core and rewired config_state.nu to use the helper instead of local Nushell hashing/state-write logic.
- Preserved legacy string-cache compatibility while writing structured {config_hash,runtime_hash} state for managed main configs only.
- Added helper CLI subcommands and Rust integration/unit coverage for compute/record envelopes and refresh semantics.
- Added shared dev helper resolution for source-checkout validators/tests so fake runtime fixtures explicitly use a built yzx_core helper instead of assuming packaged libexec state.
- Verified with: cargo test (Rust unit + integration), validate_config_surface_contract.nu, run_generated_config_canonical_tests, direct source-checkout compute_config_state probe, nix shell nu-lint on changed Nu files, nix build .#runtime .#yazelix, and git diff --check.
close_reason: Completed: config-state hashing and invalidation now route through yzx_core with parity checks and packaged helper coverage.

title: Rust first slice: port yazelix.toml loading, defaults, and schema-backed normalization
description: Implement the first concrete Rust sub-slice for config input handling. Port the live v15 yazelix.toml loading, default application, and schema-backed normalization path now owned by config_parser.nu, config_contract.nu, config_schema.nu, and tightly coupled helpers into a typed Rust core behind the agreed Nushell shim. The goal is to make the normalized config record deterministic and reusable before porting hash/invalidation or runtime materialization logic.
design: Keep config-migration machinery out of this slice. The source of truth remains the canonical config contract plus generated template/Home Manager defaults. Rust should expose machine-readable normalized output that Nushell can consume without changing the current user-facing command surface. Preserve current defaults, enum validation, parser-key shaping, and failure-class boundaries as closely as practical.
acceptance_criteria: There is a concrete plan or implementation target for moving yazelix.toml loading, defaults, and schema-backed normalization into Rust behind the shared shim. Parity fixtures cover the current live config contract and defaults, the slice explicitly excludes deleted migration behavior, and the output is specific enough to feed the later hash/invalidation slice.
notes:
2026-04-19 Rust dependency gate: before coding config.normalize, record the crate-vs-in-house plan. Decide explicitly which TOML/JSON/error/argv/test crates are allowed, what config-domain logic stays in-house, and what Nix packaging or vendoring work the crate set creates.
2026-04-19 crate-vs-in-house decision for config.normalize:
- Production crates: serde/serde_json for bridge envelopes and typed output; toml for parsing yazelix.toml, defaults, and main_config_contract.toml; thiserror for stable helper error classes; lexopt for a small private-helper argv parser.
- Dev-only crates: tempfile for fixture roots, pretty_assertions for readable parity diffs, assert_cmd for helper-binary contract tests once the binary exists.
- Build in-house: schema comparison, enum/range/list validation, parser_key shaping, compact_badge_text, empty_string_to_null, bool_to_string, bridge envelope data model, and Yazelix-specific diagnostics.
- Rejected for this slice: clap because yzx_core is private and not a public CLI; anyhow/miette because error classes must stay explicit and machine-readable; toml_edit because this slice reads/normalizes and does not preserve or rewrite user-authored TOML; schemars/config-rs because the canonical schema is Yazelix's TOML contract metadata.
- Packaging impact: add a repo-local rust_core Cargo workspace and build a native yzx_core helper for the runtime libexec path; users should not need Rust installed. Hashing crates are deferred to kt5.2.2.
2026-04-19 implementation: added rust_core Cargo workspace and private yzx_core helper with config.normalize. The helper reads explicit config/default/contract TOML paths, applies contract defaults, emits deterministic normalized_config JSON with parser_key shaping and current parser transforms, and returns a schema_version=1 success/error envelope. Packaged helper is installed at runtime libexec/yzx_core via packaging/rust_core_helper.nix and mk_runtime_tree.nix. Live Nushell startup is intentionally not switched in this bead; follow-up yazelix-kt5.2.4 owns the adoption boundary so source checkouts do not depend on an unbuilt helper and packaged runtimes do not silently mask helper failures.
2026-04-19 verification: cargo fmt --check, cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings, cargo test, nix build #runtime, nix build #yazelix, runtime-output probe for libexec/yzx_core config.normalize, Rust-vs-Nushell default-config parity diff, validate_specs.nu, git diff --check, and nix flake show --all-systems all passed. During verification the filesystem hit 100%; cleaned only rust_core/target and ran nix store gc to remove unreferenced Nix store paths, freeing about 146 GiB before rerunning the runtime/package probes.
2026-04-19 packaging follow-up: threaded fenixPkgs through the first-party Home Manager module and made rust_core_helper fall back to pkgs.rustPlatform for direct imports such as the nixpkgs draft package. Verified flake runtime/package output, direct packaging/nixpkgs/default.nix build, helper presence in both package paths, and Home Manager module argument evaluation.
2026-04-19 final cleanup: tightened yzx_core error envelopes so unsupported helper commands report the requested command, added an integration test for that contract, and excluded rust_core/target from the Nix clean source filter. Final verification reran cargo fmt --check, clippy -D warnings, cargo test, flake runtime/package builds, direct nixpkgs draft package build with helper presence check, Rust-vs-Nushell default-config parity, validate_specs.nu, git diff --check, Home Manager module argument evaluation, and nix flake show --all-systems.
close_reason: Implemented packaged Rust config.normalize core with parity tests and runtime libexec helper; live Nushell adoption split to yazelix-kt5.2.4.

title: Refactor non-launch runtime shell execution seams
description: Refactor the non-launch runtime/product shell execution seams away from inline quoted script bodies. This covers the smaller production helpers that still shell through brittle `bash -lc` snippets even though they are not the main startup hot path.
design:
Target the current known production/runtime seams:
- `nushell/scripts/utils/launcher_resolution.nu::path_is_symlink`
- `nushell/scripts/yzx/screen.nu::poll_for_screen_keypress`
- `nushell/scripts/setup/welcome.nu::poll_for_welcome_keypress`

Preferred shapes by seam:
- use direct Nushell or direct external argv execution when the logic is structured and does not rely on shell semantics
- otherwise use a dedicated checked-in POSIX helper script or a fixed shell trampoline with argv/env inputs

Do not absorb `terminal_launcher.nu` into this child. That performance-sensitive seam is already owned by `yazelix-4xp1.1`.
acceptance_criteria:
1. The identified runtime/product helpers no longer build inline quoted shell-script bodies for their current behavior.
2. Their replacements are smaller and easier to reason about than the previous shell-string construction.
3. The refactor does not regress the visible behavior of keypress polling or launcher-path resolution.
notes:
These seams are smaller than the launch probe, but they still normalize the wrong pattern if left behind. They are good candidates for a clean follow-up pass once the hot terminal-launch path is handled or in parallel if the write scopes stay disjoint.
2026-04-18 implementation guidance:
- Do not assume every inline shell seam should become Nushell, and do not assume every seam should stay shell.
- Preferred decision rule:
  1. If the behavior does not need POSIX shell semantics, rewrite it into direct Nushell logic or direct argv execution.
  2. If the seam is genuinely a shell boundary \(redirection, backgrounding, shell-entry trampolines, or POSIX runtime hooks\), keep it as shell but move it into a checked-in helper script or a fixed-body trampoline with argv/env inputs.
- Explicit non-goal: replacing inline `bash -lc` with a different large interpolated `bash -lc` string.
- Explicit non-goal: porting true shell-boundary behavior into more complex Nushell just because Nushell is already present.
2026-04-18 prioritization note:
- Promote this child ahead of the broader shell-boundary cleanup pass because it is Rust-rewrite-enabling boundary cleanup, not just repo hygiene.
- Recommended sequencing: complete kt5.1 / kt5.1.1 planning and bridge work, then use hm6n.1 as the small runtime-boundary cleanup pass before the first real Rust implementation slice.
- Keep hm6n.2 lower priority; the runtime/product seams here are the higher-leverage rewrite enabler.
close_reason: Replaced the launcher-resolution symlink shell probe with direct Nushell path-type checks, moved screen and welcome timed keypress polling to a shared Nushell helper, removed the dead welcome poll override seam, and added a regression covering broken Home Manager profile-wrapper symlinks.

title: Productionize the macOS launcher as a real app
description: Productionize the macOS launcher so it behaves like a real owned macOS app rather than a functional MVP. This is the polish and operationalization layer on top of the package-first launcher surface.
design:
Treat this as launcher productization, not initial support.

Likely scope:
- code-signing stance and release ownership
- notarization decision and workflow if Yazelix chooses to own it
- Gatekeeper behavior and user-facing install friction
- launcher packaging polish such as app-bundle metadata and release artifact shape
- stronger release/maintainer validation for the macOS launcher path

This bead should build on a working package-first launcher MVP. It should not be the first place where launcher runtime resolution is invented.

Delete-first guidance:
- do not broaden this into generic macOS parity work
- do not duplicate MVP launcher logic here
- keep the work focused on what turns a working launcher into a polished macOS app surface
acceptance_criteria:
1. Yazelix has an explicit production stance for the macOS launcher: unsigned, signed, or signed plus notarized.
2. The release/install story for the launcher matches that stance and is documented honestly.
3. Gatekeeper-facing behavior and maintainer release steps are explicit instead of accidental.
4. The launcher surface feels like an owned macOS app path rather than an MVP-only utility surface.
notes:
Potential outputs to evaluate:
- signed `.app` bundle
- notarized distribution story
- release automation for launcher artifacts
- explicit unsupported/unsigned path if notarization is deferred

This bead may be operationally heavier than it is code-heavy.
close_reason: Completed: documented the macOS launcher production stance as an unsigned preview with a supported-app promotion gate, tightened preview app metadata, and added focused launch-command coverage for owned bundle metadata.

title: Experimental package-first macOS launcher preview
description: Build a best-effort package-first macOS launcher preview for community testing. Because Yazelix does not currently have maintainer-owned macOS hardware, this bead must not claim a fully supported launcher UX. The goal is to create an explicit opt-in preview that adventurous macOS users can test and report on.
design:
Scope this as an experimental/community launcher path, not a supported MVP.

Must include:
- a package-first launcher artifact or installation path
- correct resolution of the active installed Yazelix runtime
- explicit behavior for both default-profile installs and Home Manager installs when that can be reasoned about from the current contract
- visible, actionable failure behavior when the runtime is missing or stale
- docs that clearly mark the launcher as experimental and maintainer-unverified on macOS hardware
- a community feedback loop: users should know this preview may break and should file issues if it does

Must not include by default:
- support claims that require maintainer macOS verification
- code signing
- notarization
- DMG packaging
- native-grade polish claims

Delete-first guidance:
- do not recreate the deleted clone-era `assets/macos` flow
- build from current package/runtime ownership only
- prefer the smallest launcher artifact that community testers can realistically try
acceptance_criteria:
1. Yazelix has an explicit opt-in package-first macOS launcher preview with an install/uninstall path or equivalent preview flow.
2. The launcher resolves the active installed runtime without clone-era paths or repo-checkout assumptions.
3. Runtime-missing or stale-launcher failures are visible and actionable.
4. Docs clearly label the launcher as experimental/community-tested and not maintainer-validated on macOS hardware.
notes:
Target outcome:
- curious macOS users can intentionally install a Yazelix-owned launcher preview after installing Yazelix
- the launcher reaches the installed runtime reliably enough for community testing
- docs tell the truth: experimental, best-effort, not maintainer-validated on macOS hardware

Likely work areas:
- launcher artifact definition
- `yzx` macOS launcher install/uninstall preview surface or equivalent
- docs/spec updates with explicit experimental wording
- a community-test checklist and bug-report prompt
close_reason: Completed: added explicit yzx desktop macos_preview install/uninstall preview that writes a Yazelix-marked app bundle, resolves the default/Home Manager profile yzx wrapper, surfaces missing/stale launcher failures, and documents the feature as experimental/community-tested rather than supported.

title: Define a package-first macOS launcher surface
description: Yazelix does not ship a supported macOS launcher surface today. `yazelix-0nvb` deliberately removed the stale clone-era `assets/macos` bundle instead of keeping a half-supported path. If Yazelix wants Spotlight/Launchpad/Dock integration later, it should be rebuilt as a package-first surface rather than revived from the deleted clone workflow.
design:
Treat this as new product work, not compatibility cleanup.

Requirements for a first-class macOS launcher surface:
- package-first ownership instead of copying an app bundle from a repo checkout
- launcher resolves the active installed Yazelix runtime correctly
- explicit behavior for default-profile installs and Home Manager installs
- an honest install/uninstall path owned by Yazelix commands or documented package output
- a clear Gatekeeper/signing/notarization stance, or an explicit unsigned-app story
- a defended manual macOS smoke gate

Delete-first guidance:
- do not resurrect the deleted `assets/macos` bundle by patching old paths
- start from the current package/runtime contract and decide what launcher artifact should exist, if any
- if the right answer is not an `.app` bundle, say so explicitly

Sequencing:
- only do this after the macOS support floor is stable
- not required before the Rust rewrite
acceptance_criteria:
1. Yazelix has a concrete package-first decision for whether it ships a macOS launcher artifact.
2. If a launcher artifact exists, it resolves the active installed runtime without clone-era paths like `~/.local/bin/yzx` or repo-checkout assumptions.
3. The install/uninstall ownership model is explicit for both default-profile and Home Manager installs.
4. The docs and manual smoke procedure are aligned with the real launcher behavior on macOS.
notes:
Potential implementation directions to evaluate:
- a generated `.app` bundle built from the installed runtime or flake package output
- a `yzx macos install-launcher` / `uninstall-launcher` surface if Yazelix should own deployment into `/Applications`
- a documented unsigned-app path first, with signing/notarization deferred

Manual acceptance likely needs real macOS hardware:
- install Yazelix
- install the launcher artifact
- launch from Spotlight, Launchpad, Finder, and Dock
- verify it resolves the installed runtime and reaches `yzx desktop launch` or an equivalent owned entrypoint
- verify failures are visible and actionable when the runtime is missing
2026-04-19 decomposition:
- `yazelix-b63b.1` owns the package-first macOS launcher MVP
- `yazelix-b63b.2` owns launcher productization as a real macOS app
- keep `yazelix-b63b` as the umbrella/decision surface instead of one oversized implementation bead
close_reason: Completed: the package-first macOS launcher surface is defined by the closed preview/productization children, with install/uninstall ownership, unsigned preview stance, active profile-wrapper resolution, and actionable failure behavior documented and tested. Verified with macos_preview launcher tests.

title: Define and defend a real first-party macOS support floor
description:
After `yazelix-z5vf`, Yazelix can make the first-party flake package honest and installable on darwin, but that still does not define what macOS support actually means. The current tree contains a mix of signals:

- the top-level docs say Yazelix works on Linux and macOS and that Ghostty is first-party on both
- `packaging/runtime_deps.nix` deliberately uses `pkgs.ghostty-bin` on darwin, so the runtime does own a macOS Ghostty path
- `nushell/scripts/utils/terminal_launcher.nu` already has a macOS-specific Ghostty launch branch, and `test_ghostty_macos_launch_command_omits_linux_specific_flags` defends that narrow command-shape difference
- but the main installation guide and `assets/macos/README.md` still describe a clone-era `Yazelix.app` flow that copies an app bundle from a source checkout into `/Applications/`
- that app bundle launcher still executes `$HOME/.local/bin/yzx` and describes the real code as living in `~/.config/yazelix`, which conflicts with the current package-first v15 runtime contract
- current validators defend the general package/install surface and the first-party flake package metadata split, but they do not define a macOS end-user support floor or prove Linux parity

This bead exists to turn those mixed signals into one explicit contract. The goal is not “make macOS perfect.” The goal is to define a real first-party macOS support floor for the top-level flake/package surface, say what is actually guaranteed, and stop the docs from implying more than the maintained contract proves.
design:
Keep this bead contract-first, narrow, and delete-first. The purpose is to classify the current first-party macOS surface, not to broaden it.

Use one explicit status per surface:
- `supported`: Yazelix is willing to document and defend it now
- `best_effort`: it may work, but Yazelix does not promise it as a stable contract
- `historical_or_out_of_scope`: stale or intentionally unsupported for the current v15 package-first product

The bead should force decisions on these surfaces:
- first-party flake/package installation on macOS
- `yzx --version-short`
- `yzx doctor`
- `yzx launch` on the built-in Ghostty path
- Ghostty shell-integration behavior on macOS
- Spotlight/Launchpad/Dock launcher integration

Constraints and delete-first guidance:
- Keep Ghostty as the first-party macOS terminal assumption. The open question is Yazelix integration quality, not terminal selection.
- Do not silently keep the clone-era `assets/macos` app-bundle story. If launcher integration is not part of the defended floor, the main docs must stop implying it.
- Do not overstate automation. From a Linux maintainer host, current validators can defend packaging/metadata shape and some launch-command shape, but they do not prove a real macOS runtime experience.
- Treat Ghostty shell integration as a separate sub-decision from `yzx launch`. The current runtime wrapper does not provision a macOS resource-discovery story, and Ghostty's official docs say automatic shell integration depends on either the app bundle or a valid resources layout.
- If Spotlight/Launchpad/Dock launcher support is still in scope, do not absorb the full repair here unless the remaining work is tiny. Create a follow-up bead for the launcher contract and implementation seam.

Recommended implementation shape:
1. Add a live spec such as `docs/specs/macos_support_floor.md`
2. In that spec, classify each surface as `supported`, `best_effort`, or `historical_or_out_of_scope`
3. Update `docs/specs/spec_inventory.md` and any branch-level live contract references that should point at the new spec
4. Align `README.md`, `docs/installation.md`, and `assets/macos/README.md` with the spec
5. Add the smallest credible validation story: automated checks where they tell the truth, plus an explicit named macOS manual smoke gate where only a real macOS run can prove the contract

Recommended manual smoke contract for the spec:
1. Install the first-party package on macOS
2. Run `yzx --version-short`
3. Run `yzx doctor --verbose`
4. Run `yzx launch --terminal ghostty`
5. Confirm a Ghostty window opens into Yazelix and note whether Ghostty shell-integration features are actually present
6. If launcher integration is still claimed, launch via Spotlight/Launchpad/Dock and confirm it resolves the installed `yzx`, not a clone-era or `~/.local/bin/yzx` path

Sequencing: do this before later macOS-facing fixes and before the broader Rust rewrite. This is product-surface truthfulness, not Rust work.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Yazelix has a live documented first-party macOS support-floor spec for the top-level flake/package surface, separate from Linux parity claims.
2. That spec classifies at least these surfaces as `supported`, `best_effort`, or `historical_or_out_of_scope`: install, `yzx --version-short`, `yzx doctor`, `yzx launch`, Ghostty shell-integration behavior, and Spotlight/Launchpad/Dock launcher integration.
3. The spec explicitly states that Ghostty is the intended first-party macOS terminal path and does not promise automatic Ghostty shell integration unless the underlying resource-discovery/app-bundle story is actually defended.
4. `README.md`, `docs/installation.md`, and `assets/macos/README.md` no longer imply stronger or broader macOS support than the new support floor actually defends, and they do not keep the stale clone-era app-bundle flow as an implied primary path.
5. The validation story for the support floor is explicit and honest: automated where credible, otherwise a named manual macOS smoke procedure or maintainer gate.
6. If macOS launcher integration remains a supported surface, the remaining implementation work is tracked separately instead of being left as stale clone-era docs.
notes:
opus 4.6 review:

The spec is well-written and the core doc cleanup is correct. Two issues worth fixing before shipping.

ISSUE 1 — assets/macos/README.md body still actively guides users through the out-of-scope app bundle.

The historical note was added at the top (good), but the remaining 100 lines still tell users how to install, configure, troubleshoot, and use the app bundle as if it is current. Key contradictions:
- line 9-11: "Copy the app bundle to your Applications folder" with active install commands
