- line 17-21: "After installation, you can: Search for Yazelix in Spotlight..."
- line 104: "the actual yazelix code lives in ~/.config/yazelix" (the exact stale claim the spec rejects)
- line 105: "Updates to yazelix (via git pull) dont require reinstalling the .app" (clone-era assumption)
- the full Troubleshooting section actively helps users debug the out-of-scope launcher

Per acceptance criterion 4: docs should "no longer imply stronger or broader macOS support than the new support floor actually defends." A disclaimer at the top followed by 100 lines of active how-to instructions still reads as an implied active path. Either trim the body to match the historical status (keep just structure/context, remove active install/use/troubleshoot guidance) or add a clear "the instructions below are preserved for reference only and are not part of the current support floor" marker before the active content.

ISSUE 2 — the app bundle launcher was updated, but the spec declares it out of scope.

The launcher script was changed from hardcoded ~/.local/bin/yzx to PATH-based command -v yzx discovery with an install error message. This is a speculative improvement to a surface the spec just classified as historical_or_out_of_scope. Per delete-first protocol, stale surfaces should be marked, not improved. The change is harmless but contradicts the scope decision: if the launcher is truly out of scope, updating its code suggests it is maintained. Either leave the original launcher as-is with just the historical comment, or accept that you are partly reviving it.

MINOR:
- Missing newline at end of assets/macos/Yazelix.app/Contents/MacOS/yazelix (shows as "No newline at end of file" in git diff).

WHAT IS GOOD:

- The spec itself is excellent. The surface classification table is honest and clear. The three-tier status model (supported/best_effort/historical_or_out_of_scope) makes the contract unambiguous.
- The Ghostty section correctly identifies the resource-discovery gap for shell integration on macOS and does not overclaim. The explicit callout of login-shell behavior as a platform concern is good.
- The manual smoke gate is well-defined with concrete steps.
- The Platform Scope Discipline section correctly cross-references the nixpkgs contract and warns against silent scope widening.
- docs/installation.md macOS section replacement is clean — honest about the current state without being dismissive.
- README.md platform line now links to the spec instead of claiming broad support.
- spec_inventory.md correctly lists the new spec as Live.
- Acceptance cases in the spec match the bead criteria exactly.

NOTE: The spec lists validate_flake_interface.nu as a defender, but from the yazelix-z5vf review, that validator has a bug (passes list instead of pkg to availableOn, always returns true). That is a pre-existing issue from z5vf, not introduced here, but the spec is referencing a broken defender until z5vf is fixed. Since z5vf is a dependency of this bead, the z5vf fix should land first.

VERDICT:

The spec and core doc changes are correct — ship those. Fix the assets/macos/README.md body to match its own historical classification (trim active install/use guidance or add a clear archive marker). Either revert the launcher script update or accept you are partly maintaining the out-of-scope surface. Add trailing newline.
2026-04-19 implementation landed:
- removed the legacy `assets/macos` app-bundle surface instead of keeping it as a half-supported path
- kept the macOS support-floor spec and package-first docs
- created follow-up bead `yazelix-b63b` for any future package-first macOS launcher work
close_reason: Completed: verified the package-first macOS support-floor spec and docs are in place, stale clone-era app-bundle docs are no longer tracked, and future Spotlight/Launchpad/Dock launcher work is explicitly deferred to yazelix-b63b and its children.

title: Avoid redundant runtime config materialization on startup
description:
Recent installed-runtime startup profiles show `inner.materialize_runtime_configs` still costing roughly 500ms on both desktop launch and managed launch. That is large enough to matter, but it sits in a part of the system where it would be easy to overreact with a heavy caching layer that Yazelix would later have to rewrite or defend forever.

The point of this bead is to find out whether there is a slim delete-first win: unconditional rewrites, over-broad invalidation, or repeated work that can be narrowed without making config correctness mysterious.
design:
Treat this as a pre-Rust-rewrite candidate with a hard complexity budget. It is worth evaluating before the rewrite because runtime-config materialization is a live hot path and any slimmer invalidation boundary will help both current users and the later migration matrix. But do not build a generalized cache or migration engine here.

Prefer deleting unnecessary rewrites or narrowing invalidation over introducing fingerprints for every file, wide persistent cache state, or other machinery that mainly shifts complexity around. If profiling shows the only meaningful win requires broad caching/state management, stop and defer the deeper solution to the later Rust runtime layer instead of landing a lot of transitional Nushell code.

Sequencing decision: investigate and potentially land before the broader Rust rewrite only if the solution stays small and clarifies the invalidation contract. Otherwise explicitly defer the deeper optimization into the runtime rewrite lane.
acceptance_criteria:
1. The materialization step is broken down enough to identify which substeps dominate the current ~500ms cost.
2. If a pre-rewrite optimization lands, warm unchanged startup avoids clearly redundant runtime-config work while preserving config correctness.
3. The resulting implementation stays slim; if the only winning shape is broad cache machinery, the bead is deferred rather than forcing that machinery into Nushell.
close_reason: Completed: runtime materialization now reports generated_runtime_state substeps, warm Yazi generation skips unchanged managed file rewrites with stable headers, start_yazelix_inner reuses the computed config state for record_runtime_state, and repo-driven startup profiling now pins YAZELIX_RUNTIME_DIR to the selected source root instead of accidentally measuring the installed runtime. Verified with focused generated-config/profiler regressions, syntax validation, nu-lint, and warm repo-backed profiles that reduced inner.record_runtime_state from the old ~126-133ms range to ~3-5ms and cut inner.materialize_runtime_configs + record_runtime_state to roughly ~425-440ms on unchanged warm runs.

title: Trim the detached terminal spawn/probe startup tax
description:
Latest installed-runtime startup profiles show both desktop launch and managed new-window launch paying roughly one full second inside `terminal_launcher.detached_launch_probe`. This is now one of the clearest user-visible startup taxes in the measured launch path.

The current probe is valuable because it makes early terminal-launch failure visible instead of silently assuming success. But the profiler suggests the current policy is overly coarse for the steady-state success path. The goal is to reduce the fixed success-path tax without regressing the failure visibility that the desktop-launch work just restored.
design:
Treat this as pre-Rust-rewrite work. It is worth fixing before the rewrite because it is both highly user-visible today and a boundary the later Rust launch path must preserve: how Yazelix decides a detached terminal launch is truly alive versus how it reports early death.

Keep this delete-first. First separate actual terminal spawn cost from deliberate probe waiting. Prefer a smaller readiness criterion, a tighter early-death window, or a less blunt success policy over adding a long-lived supervisor, daemon, or large Nushell state machine. If the only robust solution requires substantial new launch orchestration machinery, stop and reframe that larger ownership change under the later Rust launch/bootstrap slice instead of growing transitional Nushell complexity.

Current seam to target:
- `nushell/scripts/utils/terminal_launcher.nu::run_detached_terminal_launch` currently writes a launch log, starts `nohup bash -lc ... &`, sleeps for a fixed `1` second, treats `kill -0 $pid` after that sleep as success, and only then removes the log / returns success.
- That means the measured `terminal_launcher.detached_launch_probe` step currently includes a deliberate one-second success-path wait, not just real terminal spawn time.
- `nushell/scripts/core/launch_yazelix.nu` depends on this helper to decide whether to keep the chosen terminal candidate or fall through to the next candidate.

Constraints:
- preserve early-death visibility and useful stderr tail reporting when a terminal exits immediately
- preserve terminal fallback behavior in `launch_yazelix.nu`
- preserve the startup-profile contract that this remains a first-class `terminal_launcher.detached_launch_probe` step
- do not add terminal-specific readiness machinery unless it is obviously tiny and justified

Likely acceptable solution shapes:
- replace the blunt `sleep 1` with a shorter bounded poll loop that exits early on stable success or early death
- split “early death detection window” from “steady-state success confirmation” more precisely
- keep the launch log only long enough to capture actionable failure output, then delete it on success

Likely non-acceptable solution shapes for this bead:
- a resident supervisor/daemon
- a broad background process manager
- a terminal-specific protocol matrix just to shave this tax
- a solution that hides failures and makes launch feel faster only by becoming less honest

Sequencing decision: do before the broader Rust rewrite, because this defines the launch contract and should not remain as a known one-second tax while waiting for later architectural work.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Startup profiles for both `yzx dev profile --desktop` and `yzx dev profile --launch --terminal <name>` show a materially smaller `terminal_launcher.detached_launch_probe` step on successful launch.
2. Early terminal-launch failure is still surfaced clearly enough that desktop/managed launch errors do not become silent again.
3. The implementation keeps the launch contract understandable and does not add broad new background-management machinery just to save this probe time.
4. Focused regression coverage defends both the faster success path and the preserved early-failure reporting behavior of `run_detached_terminal_launch`.
notes:
2026-04-19 code and profile context:

Measured choke point:
- installed-runtime startup profiles reviewed on 2026-04-18 showed `terminal_launcher.detached_launch_probe` around `1034-1039ms` on both desktop and managed-launch scenarios
- that makes this one of the clearest fixed user-visible startup taxes left in the measured path

Relevant files:
- `nushell/scripts/utils/terminal_launcher.nu`
- `nushell/scripts/core/launch_yazelix.nu`
- `docs/specs/startup_profile_scenarios.md`
- `nushell/scripts/dev/test_yzx_maintainer.nu`

Existing defended contract:
- `docs/specs/startup_profile_scenarios.md` requires detached launch probing to remain visible as `terminal_launcher.detached_launch_probe`
- `test_startup_profile_records_detached_terminal_probe` already proves the step exists as a first-class measured phase
- desktop/managed profile tests already prove profiling waits through `inner.zellij_handoff_ready`

Current testing gap worth addressing in this bead:
- there is currently good coverage that the probe step is recorded, but weak direct coverage for the success-vs-early-failure behavior of `run_detached_terminal_launch` itself
- this bead should preferably add focused behavior coverage for both:
  - a success path that no longer waits the full old one-second tax
  - an early-failure path that still surfaces actionable output

GLM-5.1 instructions:
1. Read before editing:
   - `nushell/scripts/utils/terminal_launcher.nu`
   - `nushell/scripts/core/launch_yazelix.nu`
   - `docs/specs/startup_profile_scenarios.md`
   - `nushell/scripts/dev/test_yzx_maintainer.nu`
2. Start with the delete-first question: which part of the current ~1s cost is deliberate policy rather than required safety?
3. Keep the probe as a single understandable helper. Do not spread launch-state machinery across multiple modules unless the current helper truly cannot carry the simpler policy.
4. Preserve the profiling step name `terminal_launcher.detached_launch_probe`.
5. Add focused regression coverage for both success and failure behavior, not just profile-record presence.
6. Prefer a bounded poll/early-exit shape over a hardcoded fixed sleep.
7. Stop and report back instead of landing a large orchestration rewrite if the only robust fix seems to require a supervisor or heavy terminal-specific logic.

Suggested verification after the patch:
- `yzx run nu nushell/scripts/dev/test_yzx_maintainer.nu`
- `yzx run nu nushell/scripts/dev/validate_specs.nu`
- `yzx run nu nushell/scripts/dev/validate_syntax.nu`
- `nix develop -c nu -c 'use nushell/scripts/yzx/dev.nu *; yzx dev lint_nu'`
- manual comparison with `yzx dev profile --desktop` and `yzx dev profile --launch --terminal ghostty`
close_reason: Completed: trimmed the detached terminal spawn/probe tax by replacing the old one-second-style wait with a bounded early-death probe, preserved actionable early launch failure reporting, added focused maintainer regressions for the fast success path and delayed early failure visibility, and verified the updated runtime manually plus with maintainer tests, syntax validation, and nu-lint.

title: Reroll Ghostty random cursor effects for every Yazelix window
description:
When terminal.ghostty_trail_color, terminal.ghostty_trail_effect, or terminal.ghostty_mode_effect is set to "random", Yazelix currently resolves that random value when generated Ghostty terminal configs are regenerated. Desktop fast-path launches can then reuse the same generated Ghostty config across many windows, so random can look stuck on one palette such as cosmic/purple.

Make Ghostty random cursor settings reroll once per new Yazelix Ghostty window while keeping fixed settings stable and without mutating yazelix.toml.
design: Add a narrow launch-time reroll seam for Yazelix-managed Ghostty configs. Before launching a Ghostty terminal candidate, if terminal.config_mode = "yazelix" and any Ghostty cursor setting is random, regenerate the Ghostty terminal config and matching generated effect shaders from one resolved cursor state. Keep non-Ghostty terminals and fixed Ghostty cursor settings unaffected. Do not trigger broad Yazi/Zellij generated-state repair just to reroll cursor visuals.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Each new Yazelix Ghostty window launch rerolls random Ghostty cursor palette/effects.
2. Generated Ghostty config and generated effect shaders still use the same resolved palette for that launch.
3. Fixed Ghostty cursor settings do not force launch-time rewrites.
4. Desktop fast path and normal launch path share the same behavior.
5. Non-Ghostty terminal launches are unaffected.
close_reason: Completed: desktop fast-path Ghostty launches now reroll random cursor palette/effect settings before each Yazelix window while fixed palettes stay stable; generated config and effect shaders continue sharing one resolved cursor state.

title: Fix Ghostty cursor effects ignoring the selected Yazelix trail palette
description:
Ghostty cursor movement/mode effect shaders are generated with iCurrentCursorColor as their color source. In practice this can resolve to the terminal theme cursor color rather than the Yazelix-selected ghostty_trail_color palette, making cursor effects look like the same purple trail even when the generated config points at a non-purple palette such as reef or ocean.

The generated Ghostty config and generated effect shaders need to share the same resolved palette value, including the single random choice for ghostty_trail_color = "random".
design: Resolve Ghostty cursor color/effect state once during terminal config generation. Render the Ghostty config from that state, and pass the resolved color literal into the generated effect shader builder. Replace both COLOR and TRAIL_COLOR declarations in vendored effect templates so tail/warp/sweep and ripple-style effects use the same Yazelix-selected color. Keep ghostty_trail_color = "none" on the existing theme/current-cursor path.
acceptance_criteria:
1. A fixed palette such as reef generates Ghostty config with the reef cursor color and generated effect shaders with the reef vec4 literal.
2. The movement effects that use TRAIL_COLOR and the mode effects that use COLOR are both covered.
3. ghostty_trail_color = "random" resolves once per generation so config and generated effect shaders cannot pick different colors.
4. Regression tests protect generated Ghostty config and generated shader color output.
close_reason: Fixed: Ghostty generated effect shaders now receive the same resolved Yazelix trail palette literal as the generated config, including single-resolution random color state; regression coverage asserts reef is embedded in both TRAIL_COLOR and COLOR shader styles.

title: make the zellij popup faster
close_reason: Completed already: user confirmed this work was already done and it should be removed from the ready queue.

title: Retire legacy shell-block migration checks once the old installer path is dropped
description: Yazelix now carries transitional cleanup and doctor detection for legacy managed shell blocks written into host startup files by older installer-owned releases. This should not become a permanent compatibility surface.
design: Do not use a time-based TODO. Keep one explicit tracked retirement seam instead. Remove the shell-block takeover cleanup and stale-shell-block doctor warning once Yazelix intentionally drops support for upgrades from installs that wrote managed host-shell blocks, the docs/troubleshooting surface no longer routes users through that migration path, and at least one stable release cycle has passed without needing that bridge.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Decide explicitly that pre-profile/Home Manager installs with managed host-shell blocks are no longer an in-scope upgrade path. 2. Remove the transitional shell-block cleanup and doctor checks, plus any code comments pointing at this seam. 3. Verify the remaining install-ownership and doctor paths still describe only supported update/install contracts.
notes: 2026-04-20 delete-first rewrite follow-up: this is now a direct shipped-Nushell shrink lever for install_ownership.nu. Leaving the legacy shell-block upgrade path open forever forces ulb2.4.1 either to preserve dead compatibility logic in Rust or to keep a large Nu ownership island alive. Treat this as a boundary decision, not distant cleanup.
close_reason: Completed: legacy managed shell-block detection, doctor warnings, and home_manager prepare shell-block archiving removed as part of yazelix-ulb2.4.1; upgrade path no longer tracked in product code.

title: Harden doctor startup checks so desktop-launch regressions do not pass as generic terminal support
description: Doctor currently reports "Configured terminal launch support is available" by checking command availability, not by validating the real desktop or managed-terminal startup contract. That can produce a false green result when desktop launch still fails in practice.
design: Keep the check high-signal. Either probe the real desktop and managed launch preconditions that users depend on, or narrow the doctor wording so it no longer implies end-to-end launch health. Avoid weak tests that only restate command discovery.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Doctor no longer reports a misleading healthy launch state when only terminal binary discovery succeeded. 2. The remaining launch-health signal corresponds to a defended startup contract or is explicitly scoped as command availability only. 3. The output helps users distinguish desktop-entry/runtime issues from simple PATH discovery.
close_reason: Completed

title: Standardize canonical runtime env across Yazelix-managed pane launches
description: Yazelix-managed pane launches do not consistently normalize the canonical runtime/editor environment. The main session and managed editor panes use the shared runtime env contract, but popup/helper paths still rely on ambient inheritance in important places. That breaks tools like `jjui` that run inside a Yazelix popup and then spawn a nested editor for commit messages.
design:
Relevant code seams:
- `nushell/scripts/core/start_yazelix.nu` seeds the main Yazelix session with `with-env (get_runtime_env $config)`
- `nushell/scripts/utils/runtime_env.nu` defines the canonical runtime env, but currently exports `EDITOR` without `VISUAL`
- `nushell/scripts/integrations/zellij_runtime_wrappers.nu` and `nushell/scripts/integrations/zellij.nu` explicitly serialize/inject canonical env for managed editor panes
- `nushell/scripts/zellij_wrappers/yzx_popup_program.nu` now handles `popup_program = ["editor"]` through `run_runtime_argv`, but non-`editor` popup commands still use raw `run-external`
- `rust_plugins/zellij_pane_orchestrator/src/transient.rs` opens transient panes with an empty env map, so popup wrappers depend on whatever env the runtime wrapper/session happened to inherit

Implications:
- Ordinary Yazelix session panes should already inherit the main session env from startup, but helper/transient launches are less explicit and therefore less reliable
- The `jjui` symptom is likely a generic env-contract gap rather than a `jjui`-specific integration bug
- Official `jj` precedence is `JJ_EDITOR` > `ui.editor` > `VISUAL` > `EDITOR`, so adding generic `VISUAL` support is a better fix than exporting `JJ_EDITOR` globally

Likely direction:
- Standardize Yazelix-managed popup/helper launches on the same canonical runtime env contract used elsewhere
- Add `VISUAL` alongside `EDITOR` in the shared runtime env
- Avoid app-specific global exports like `JJ_EDITOR`
- Update env-contract docs if `VISUAL` becomes part of the stable surface
acceptance_criteria: Popup-launched tools receive the canonical Yazelix runtime env including EDITOR and VISUAL; tools launched from popup panes that spawn nested editors honor the configured editor without app-specific global env vars; managed editor panes and ordinary Yazelix session panes keep one consistent editor/runtime contract; regression tests cover popup env normalization and a jj-like nested-editor path
notes:
Implemented canonical popup runtime env propagation with VISUAL and restored explicit missing-popup PATH errors after routing popup commands through run_runtime_argv. Verified with popup and Helix managed-config contract tests. Remaining follow-up before closing: add a more literal jj-like nested-editor regression path if we want the acceptance criteria to be fully explicit.
Refactored yzx_popup_program.nu to resolve config, runtime env, popup argv, and command preflight through one popup launch-context helper. Behavior stayed the same; popup and Helix managed-config contract tests remain green.
2026-04-20 tracker hygiene: moved out of in_progress. The popup runtime-env and VISUAL propagation work was implemented earlier, but there is no active dirty tree for this lane right now. Remaining question before closure: either add a more literal jj-like nested-editor regression path or tighten the acceptance story around the existing popup/env contract tests.
2026-04-21 investigation: the bead design is now stale relative to the code. `nushell/scripts/zellij_wrappers/yzx_popup_program.nu` routes all popup commands through `run_runtime_argv`, so the old split between `popup_program = ["editor"]` and non-editor commands no longer exists. `nushell/scripts/utils/runtime_env.nu` now computes the canonical runtime env through `yzx_core`, and `nushell/scripts/integrations/zellij_runtime_wrappers.nu` carries `EDITOR` and `VISUAL` into managed floating/editor launches. The remaining acceptance question was whether we still needed a literal `jj` integration test. Current generic regressions are sufficient and more durable: `test_runtime_env_includes_visual_equal_to_editor`, `test_popup_program_receives_canonical_env_with_visual`, and `test_get_runtime_env_wraps_helix_with_managed_wrapper` prove the popup/helper env contract that a `jjui`-style nested-editor flow depends on without baking app-specific behavior into the suite.
close_reason: Completed: popup/helper launches now use the canonical runtime env with VISUAL plus EDITOR, and the existing popup/Helix contract regressions are sufficient generic proof for nested-editor flows without an app-specific jj integration test

title: Detect stale host-shell yzx shadowing and make profile wrappers future-proof
close_reason: Completed

title: Make direct update subcommands describe owner scope neutrally
description:
Direct update subcommands currently print wording that can sound like Yazelix has detected the install owner, when it is really just confirming the command path the user invoked. Example: a Home Manager-owned install running `yzx update upstream` prints "Using the default-profile update path for this install" before correctly failing because the active runtime is not in the default Nix profile.

That is technically recoverable, but the first line is misleading for Home Manager users. The message should describe the command scope neutrally and, when ownership is detectable, point directly at the correct owner command before doing unnecessary profile-specific work.
design:
Improve wording without reintroducing a generic auto-updater. Keep the explicit owner commands: `yzx update upstream` for default Nix profile installs and `yzx update home_manager` for Home Manager installs.

For direct subcommands, prefer phrasing like "Requested update path: default Nix profile" rather than "Using ... for this install." If `has_home_manager_managed_install` or runtime/distribution capability detection can cheaply prove the current install is Home Manager-owned, `yzx update upstream` should fail earlier with a specific message: this runtime appears Home Manager-owned; run `yzx update home_manager` from the owning flake and then `home-manager switch`. The symmetric case can stay advisory unless profile ownership is cheaply and reliably detectable.

Do not make `yzx update upstream` silently call the Home Manager updater. The user should choose the owner path explicitly.
acceptance_criteria:
1. A Home Manager-owned install running `yzx update upstream` no longer sees wording implying the default-profile path owns this install.
2. If Home Manager ownership is detectable, the command fails early with an actionable `yzx update home_manager` / `home-manager switch` recovery path.
3. A profile-owned install still gets clear `yzx update upstream` behavior and does not regress exact-profile-entry resolution.
4. Tests cover the misleading Home Manager-owned `yzx update upstream` case and the normal profile-owned update path.
close_reason: Completed: direct update subcommands now describe the requested update path neutrally, and Home Manager-owned installs fail early from `yzx update upstream` with an actionable `yzx update home_manager` plus `home-manager switch` recovery path.

title: Stop packaged runtime entrypoints from owning host shell hooks
description: Package and one-off runtime entrypoints should not rewrite host shell dotfiles like ~/.bashrc or ~/.config/nushell/config.nu. Remove launch-time shell-hook ownership from the normal runtime path instead of widening Home Manager heuristics. Keep generated Yazelix-owned runtime state under ~/.local/share/yazelix, keep profile/Home Manager owned yzx on PATH, and update docs/tests/status surfaces to match the package contract.
acceptance_criteria:
- nix run ...#yazelix -- launch and installed yzx launch do not mutate host shell dotfiles
- normal runtime setup still generates Yazelix-owned derived state under ~/.local/share/yazelix
- yzx remains available through the install owner rather than shell-hook injection
- docs and tests no longer describe first launch as writing host shell hooks
close_reason: Completed

title: Keep pane-orchestrator messaging self-contained per Yazelix session
description:
Alt+m and other pane-orchestrator actions can sometimes materialize a visible broken pane named like yazelix_pane_orchestrator instead of handling the command through the already-loaded background plugin instance.

Live investigation showed that sending a pane-orchestrator message with the same plugin alias but a different runtime_dir configuration can cause Zellij to instantiate a second visible plugin pane. That breaks session isolation and is especially plausible when multiple Yazelix instances with different runtime roots are open or when repo/runtime command surfaces mix.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Pane-orchestrator keybind and CLI transport paths do not create a second plugin instance just because another Yazelix runtime root exists elsewhere
2. Alt+m continues to open a new terminal in the current tab workspace root
3. Existing session-local plugin behavior remains functional without per-message runtime_dir coupling
4. Regression coverage defends the no-per-message-runtime-coupling contract
close_reason: Completed

title: Cold one-off package launch fails before generated Zellij layout materialization
description:
Why this issue exists and what needs to be done:
GitHub issue #558 reports that a cold first-run one-off launch via `nix run github:luccahuguet/yazelix#yazelix -- launch` can create `yazelix.toml`, generate terminal configs, and then fail with a missing generated Zellij layout such as `~/.local/share/yazelix/configs/zellij/layouts/yzx_side.kdl`.

Investigation summary:
- `launch_yazelix.nu` prepares terminal configs and opens a new terminal window
- `start_yazelix.nu` resolves the expected generated layout path and requires that layout before launching the inner startup flow
- the code that actually materializes managed Yazi/Zellij runtime state lives later in `start_yazelix_inner.nu` via `regenerate_runtime_configs`
- on a true first run, startup can therefore demand a generated layout before the normal startup path has had any chance to generate it

Why it matters:
This breaks the documented package-first one-off use path and makes cold first-run behavior inconsistent with the intended product surface.

Acceptance criteria:
- Cold one-off package launch no longer fails just because the generated Zellij layout has not been materialized yet
- The startup ordering and generated-state contract are internally consistent
- The fix preserves clear failure behavior for genuinely broken generated-state cases after the normal materialization path has had a chance to run
close_reason: Completed

Clarify that fresh launches and yzx restart rebuild from the owner runtime, and that local dogfooding requires activating the local repo package before relying on relaunch behavior.

title: Popup: restore Yazi git refresh when lazygit popup toggles closed
description: The managed popup wrapper refreshes the active sidebar Yazi state on a normal popup-program exit, but popup toggle flows can still bypass that refresh path. Investigate re-adding the Yazi git refresh when the managed lazygit popup is toggled closed, if that can be done cleanly.
design: Treat this as a narrow lazygit-popup behavior fix, not a blanket popup refresh policy. Prefer a solution that only refreshes the active sidebar Yazi state when the managed popup being closed is attributable to the lazygit popup path. Avoid broad blind refreshes on every transient-pane toggle if they would add noise, latency, or wrong-side effects for unrelated popup programs. If robust attribution is not practical with the current orchestrator and wrapper contract, document that boundary and close the bead with the rationale instead of adding a brittle heuristic.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear decision and implementation path for restoring sidebar Yazi git refresh on managed lazygit popup toggle-close, or a documented reason why that restoration is not robustly supportable with the current popup contract. Any surviving implementation refreshes the active sidebar Yazi state only for the intended managed lazygit path and does not turn unrelated popup toggles into unconditional sidebar refreshes.
notes:
IMPLEMENTATION COMPLETED 2026-04-14

Kimi k2.5 turbo implementation:

Changes made:
- main.rs: Added TransientPaneKind import, transient_pane_kinds HashMap to State
- transient.rs: 
  - Changed methods to &mut self for HashMap mutation
  - Store pane_id -> kind mapping when opening panes
  - Check kind on toggle-close, only Popup refreshes
  - Added should_refresh_before_close() helper
  - Added refresh_sidebar_yazi_for_transient_close() to open refresh pane
- refresh_yazi_sidebar.nu: New wrapper that refreshes sidebar and auto-exits

How it works:
1. When popup opens, orchestrator stores pane_id -> Popup in HashMap
2. When Alt+t toggles close, checks if pane is Popup kind
3. If Popup, opens small (50x20%) floating refresh pane at 25%,25%
4. Refresh pane runs refresh_yazi_sidebar.nu which:
   - Calls refresh_active_sidebar_yazi
   - Auto-exits immediately (pane closes)
5. Original popup pane closes
6. Menu panes skip refresh (clean attribution)

Visual impact: Brief flash of small refresh pane (acceptable trade-off)

Commit: e09d2cd
close_reason: Implemented: Added pane metadata tracking and conditional refresh. Popup toggle-close now triggers Yazi sidebar refresh before closing. Menu panes correctly skip refresh. Requires wasm rebuild: yzx dev build_pane_orchestrator --sync

title: Make pane orchestrator Rust unit tests hermetic again
description: `cargo test --manifest-path rust_plugins/zellij_pane_orchestrator/Cargo.toml --lib` is currently not hermetic in this repo and can fail on maintainer machines because a transitive OpenSSL-dependent dependency path leaks host/header assumptions into the test lane. This is maintainer debt, not a current release blocker: the live pane orchestrator wasm still rebuilds and syncs cleanly, and higher-level popup/menu behavior remains defended by the existing Nushell and generated-config tests.
design: Keep the current trust model in place until this is fixed: `yzx dev build_pane_orchestrator --sync` plus the higher-level popup/menu contract tests remain the authoritative verification path for live behavior. Then make the Rust unit-test lane hermetic again by fixing the maintainer-shell/toolchain boundary or otherwise eliminating the host OpenSSL mismatch from the supported test path. Prefer a fix that improves the supported maintainer workflow instead of adding more fallback verification paths.
acceptance_criteria: A supported maintainer path can run `cargo test --manifest-path rust_plugins/zellij_pane_orchestrator/Cargo.toml --lib` successfully without depending on host OpenSSL header state. The bead documents the temporary verification policy that keeps trusting `yzx dev build_pane_orchestrator --sync` plus higher-level popup/menu tests until the hermetic Rust test lane is restored. The fix does not weaken the current live-behavior verification story.
close_reason: Fixed by adding pkgs.openssl to maintainer shell and setting OPENSSL_DIR, OPENSSL_LIB_DIR, OPENSSL_INCLUDE_DIR. All 18 pane orchestrator unit tests now pass hermetically in nix develop.

title: Normalize Escape behavior for popup and menu panes
description:
Popup pane and popup menu now both use the shared toggleable transient-pane model. Escape behavior should be normalized across both surfaces instead of letting one surface close/hide while the other behaves differently.

Current context: `nushell/scripts/yzx/menu.nu` still advertises `Enter/Esc: close` and handles `esc` in the menu popup loop. Audit the popup pane path as well, including Zellij keybindings and pane-orchestrator transient-pane behavior, before changing it.

Preferred direction from product discussion: because popup and menu are now toggles, remove popup/menu-specific Escape closing if it is not needed. If Escape remains, it must have exactly the same user-visible behavior on both the popup pane and popup menu.
acceptance_criteria:
1. The popup pane and popup menu have one documented Escape-key contract.
2. Either Escape does nothing on both surfaces, or Escape hides/closes both surfaces through the same transient-pane path.
3. If the toggle path is the canonical close path, remove popup/menu-specific Escape close handling and stale prompt text such as `Enter/Esc: close` or `Esc to cancel`.
4. Update focused tests for the surviving behavior, covering both `yzx popup` and `yzx menu --popup` where practical.
5. Do not reintroduce helper panes or separate popup/menu control paths.
close_reason: Completed: yzx menu no longer advertises or handles Escape as its own close path; popup/menu close semantics stay toggle-owned, with regression coverage in the core yzx suite.

title: Fix doctor desktop launcher validation for stable wrapper installs
description:
`yzx desktop install` correctly prefers the stable installed wrapper (`~/.local/bin/yzx`) when it exists, but `yzx doctor` still validates manual desktop entries against the runtime-pinned `bin/yzx` path. After a fresh local installer run, this creates a false warning: "Yazelix desktop entry does not use the expected launcher path."

Fix the doctor desktop-entry expected-launcher calculation so it uses the same launcher-resolution policy as `yzx desktop install` for manual installs, while preserving Home Manager profile launcher validation.
acceptance_criteria:
1. A manual install with `~/.local/bin/yzx` and a desktop Exec pointing at that wrapper is accepted by `yzx doctor`.
2. Stale runtime-pinned or old store Exec paths are still reported as stale when they do not match the resolved launcher policy.
3. Home Manager profile desktop validation remains unchanged.
close_reason: Completed: doctor now validates manual desktop entries against the same stable launcher resolver used by yzx desktop install, with regression coverage.

title: Delete the remaining br-to-bd migration scaffolding once bd retirement is fully validated
description: The repo still carries leftover migration scaffolding such as scripts/migrate_br_to_bd.py and scattered historical br references. Once the bd workspace is fully validated and br retirement is complete, delete the remaining repo-local migration helper code and trim any non-historical references that are no longer serving an active recovery path.
design: Treat this as a post-validation cleanup gate, not a premature deletion pass. Keep genuinely useful historical narrative where needed, but remove live tooling or workflow references that imply br is still part of the normal maintainer surface.
acceptance_criteria: After gm39 is satisfied, the remaining br-to-bd migration scaffolding is either deleted or explicitly justified; live maintainer workflows no longer reference br except for clearly historical context; and the repo no longer carries dead migration helper code by inertia alone.
close_reason: Completed: removed tracked br-to-bd migration artifacts, switched live maintainer issue sync and CI validation from br to bd, and updated docs/AGENTS references to the bd workflow.

title: Audit superseded v14/devenv-era specs and archive or relabel stale contracts
description: The specs directory still contains many documents that explicitly describe superseded v14-era, devenv-era, installer-primary, migration-preserving, Rust-first-v15, or two-lines-transition semantics. Audit that corpus and make the spec surface honest for the current product direction: v15.0 is the live trimmed non-Rust line, config migrations are being deleted from the live contract, later v15.x may gain selective Rust slices, v16 is the Rust-forward release, and historical contracts should be clearly marked, archived, or linked as superseded references instead of reading like live planning inputs.
design: Do not delete useful project history casually. Prefer a clear active-v15-versus-historical split: keep genuinely useful old specs as explicitly historical references, but stop letting them masquerade as current design contracts or as evidence that Yazelix still supports a parallel Classic line, config-migration safety as a standing feature, or a Rust-first v15.0 release.
acceptance_criteria: There is an explicit inventory of historical versus active specs; superseded v14/devenv-era, migration-preserving, Rust-first-v15, and two-lines-transition contracts are archived, relabeled, or otherwise clearly marked; the docs surface points maintainers toward current v15.0 planning documents first; and future work no longer has to guess whether an old spec is still live.
close_reason: Completed: added a specs inventory, marked migration-era specs historical, updated live v15 specs for no config-migration engine and v15.0 non-Rust framing, and linked the inventory from the spec workflow.

title: README: remove stale v14/devenv-era wording from the v15 front door
description: The README still contains stale wording from the earlier two-lines plan, including language that treats v14/Classic as the recommended line and v15 as a future branch. Refresh the main README so it presents v15.0 as the live trimmed non-Rust Yazelix product, treats v14 as historical context only, explains that config migrations are no longer part of the live contract, and aligns the front door with the trimmed runtime contract, the built-in Ghostty path, and the fast popup/menu-centered workspace UX. Rust should be described as a later v15.x/v16 roadmap item, not as a prerequisite or headline for v15.0.
design: Keep the README as a front door, not a history dump. Preserve useful historical context where it matters, but keep that context explicitly historical. The main README should explain the current product honestly: one live line, one current command/runtime/config contract, one current recommendation to users, and a clear release framing where v15.0 is the trimmed reboot while Rust is a gradual later path toward v16.
acceptance_criteria: The main README presents v15.0 as the live trimmed Yazelix product, treats v14 as historical rather than recommended or maintained, stops implying removed runtime-local ownership, stale command surfaces, or config-migration support, and still points readers to the right deeper docs for history, architecture, and the later Rust roadmap.
close_reason: Completed: refreshed the README front door for the live v15 branch, removed stale v14-recommendation wording, clarified v15.0 as the trimmed non-Rust reboot, and aligned terminal/config migration wording.

title: Re-audit remaining high-cost maintainer test overlap after the latest runtime trims
description: The first maintainer-test trim pass removed a lot, but the current inventory still leaves most of the repo's largest files in the maintainer suite. Revisit the biggest surviving runtime-behavior and generated-config test files after the popup/menu unification, runtime ownership cleanup, and bd migration work, and identify where strong contracts are still defended twice or where old scenario splits no longer buy enough signal.
design: Do not reduce contract strength just to make the suite smaller. Start with a replacement matrix for the biggest surviving files, classify assertions into keep, merge, move, or delete, and only cut coverage when another stronger check already defends the same behavior. Prefer fewer stronger tests over broad overlapping scenario copies.
acceptance_criteria: There is an explicit overlap matrix for the highest-cost surviving maintainer tests; duplicate or stale coverage is either removed or merged into stronger defending checks; the resulting suite keeps the real product contracts defended; and the final lane placement stays honest about what still matters.
notes:
Completed audit and trim:
- Added the qgj7.4.7 overlap matrix to docs/v15_post_trim_test_coverage_matrix.md.
- Consolidated three removed-surface parser tests into test_parse_yazelix_config_rejects_removed_surfaces_without_rewriting while keeping legacy [ascii], removed shell.enable_atuin, and legacy [packs] cases.
- Consolidated two removed enum schema tests into test_config_schema_rejects_removed_enum_values.
- Narrowed the yzx status assertion away from a brittle unwrapped full-path check while preserving the status-summary contract.
- Hardened workspace/Yazi fake zellij fixtures to match pipe command names by argv value instead of brittle $6 position checks.
- Default canonical suite count moved from 92 to 89; the stale count budget was reset to 89 so future growth is guarded again.
Verification: test_yzx_generated_configs.nu, test_yzx_core_commands.nu, test_yzx_commands.nu, validate_default_test_count_budget.nu, validate_default_test_traceability.nu, validate_specs.nu, validate_syntax.nu, and git diff --check passed.
close_reason: Completed test-overlap trim: documented the replacement matrix, consolidated removed-surface and enum rejection coverage, hardened brittle fake Zellij fixtures, restored the default-suite count budget to the post-trim actual count, and verified the default suite and governance validators.

title: Simplify the remaining doctor, front-door, and session-helper hotspots
description: The latest inventory still shows a clear hotspot cluster around ascii_art.nu, doctor.nu, zellij_plugin_paths.nu, and integrations/zellij.nu. Those files are not obviously dead, but they are dense enough that another delete-first pass is likely to find ownership overlap, helper duplication, or branches that survived older product shapes. Audit that cluster as one seam and trim it without losing popup/menu/sidebar/workspace behavior or the current doctor guarantees.
design: Start by classifying each large helper path into keep, drop, and unsure. Prefer deleting duplicate ownership or split-brain logic before refactoring surviving code. Treat popup/menu transient behavior, permission repair, workspace retargeting, and doctor output as defended contracts, not casual collateral damage.
acceptance_criteria: The hotspot cluster has an explicit keep/drop/unsure map; any dead or duplicate helper paths in that cluster are removed; surviving responsibilities are smaller or more clearly separated; and focused regressions still defend doctor, transient-pane, sidebar, and workspace behavior.
notes:
Audit map 2026-04-13:
- Keep: doctor.nu current health/fix output, especially config diagnostics, runtime preflight, desktop freshness, Helix runtime checks, and pane-orchestrator permission/sidebar/editor checks.
- Keep: setup/zellij_plugin_paths.nu stable wasm paths, permission seeding, legacy popup-runner cleanup, and generation-state artifact hashing. These defend live popup/menu/sidebar and plugin permission recovery.
- Keep: integrations/zellij.nu pane-orchestrator pipe transport, popup/menu transient pane open, workspace root resolution, retarget_workspace, active sidebar state, and managed editor launch/open helpers.
- Keep: ascii_art.nu implemented welcome/screen styles: static, logo, boids, game_of_life, and random. These are user-visible front-door surfaces in v15.
- Drop candidates: integrations/zellij.nu set_tab_cwd/set_workspace_for_path/update_tab_workspace are no longer imported anywhere; mandelbrot welcome style is only a reserved enum plus a logo fallback, not a real renderer.
- Unsure / leave for separate bead: deeper ASCII renderer split or pane-orchestrator component reorganization. Those are organization changes, not safe delete-first work inside this bead.
Implemented trim:
- Removed unused zellij workspace setter path: update_tab_workspace, set_tab_cwd, set_workspace_for_path. The surviving retarget_workspace path remains the active workspace contract.
- Removed the placeholder mandelbrot welcome style because it had no renderer and only replayed the logo fallback. Updated default config, Home Manager text, and config contract allowed values.
- Reused remove_runtime_plugins_by_prefix for pane-orchestrator stale runtime wasm cleanup instead of keeping a duplicate scanner in sync_pane_orchestrator_runtime_wasm.
Verification: test_yzx_screen_commands.nu, test_zellij_plugin_contracts.nu, test_yzx_workspace_commands.nu, test_yzx_generated_configs.nu, test_yzx_doctor_commands.nu, validate_config_surface_contract.nu, validate_syntax.nu, and git diff --check all passed.
close_reason: Completed hotspot trim: recorded keep/drop/unsure map, removed unused zellij workspace setters, removed placeholder mandelbrot welcome style, and deduplicated pane-orchestrator stale plugin cleanup; focused tests and syntax checks passed.

title: Delete the config migration and legacy relocation layer from the live v15 line
description: The live v15 line should no longer carry config migration, migration preview, migration transaction, or legacy relocation behavior as part of its product contract. The project has gone through a real rebirth, and preserving old config-shape transitions now adds more confusion and code than value. Delete config_surfaces.nu relocation behavior, config_migrations.nu, config_migration_transactions.nu, config_migration_preview.nu, and any startup or doctor flows that still present migration support as a living feature, while preserving the parsing and validation needed for the current managed config surface.
design: This is no longer an audit-for-retention bead. It is a delete-first removal bead. Fail fast on unsupported old config shapes instead of trying to repair them through migration machinery. Keep only the config parsing, schema, and validation that defend the current v15 surface. Update related docs/specs/tests so they stop describing migration support as part of the live product.
acceptance_criteria: The config migration and legacy relocation layer is removed from the live v15 line; startup and doctor no longer present migration repair as part of the product contract; the surviving config surface fails clearly on unsupported old shapes; and the remaining config code/tests/docs defend only the current managed config contract.
close_reason: Completed: deleted the live v15 config migration/relocation engine, made legacy config surfaces fail fast, and updated diagnostics, upgrade-summary guidance, validators, and tests around the no-migration v15 contract.

title: Organize the pane orchestrator as an internal separately-owned component boundary
description: The pane orchestrator already owns real behavior: transient popup/menu toggles, sidebar focus and visibility, workspace retargeting, and layout-family motions. The repo still spreads that ownership across Rust plugin code, Nushell client wrappers, generated Zellij config, and maintainer build/sync notes in ways that make the subsystem feel more scattered than it is. Tighten the in-repo organization and contract so the wasm/component can be reasoned about as one unit while it still lives in the monorepo.
design: Do not split repos or genericize behavior yet. Work from first principles: if the plugin and its consumer interfaces still change together, a separate repo adds versioning and coordination cost without buying real clarity. Instead, define the hard contract in-tree and organize around it. Likely outputs: a short component doc/README for the pane orchestrator; one explicit catalog of command names, plugin-configuration keys, wrapper/runtime path assumptions, and pane identity assumptions; clearer ownership boundaries between Rust plugin internals, Nushell client transport, generated Zellij keybind/config wiring, and Yazi/sidebar adapters; and deletion of stale cross-layer convenience surfaces where possible. Success means future extraction would be straightforward, but extraction itself is not the goal.
acceptance_criteria: 1. There is one explicit in-repo pane-orchestrator component document or spec covering command surface, plugin configuration keys, runtime and wrapper path assumptions, and pane identity invariants. 2. Source, build, test, and doc ownership are organized so the plugin can be understood as one owned subsystem without hunting across unrelated files. 3. At least one cross-layer or convenience coupling is deleted or internalized rather than merely documented. 4. Popup, menu, sidebar, workspace, and layout-family behavior remain unchanged, and focused regressions still defend the surviving contract.
notes: Implemented pane-orchestrator boundary cleanup: added docs/specs/pane_orchestrator_component.md, registered it in the spec inventory, removed obsolete Rust pipe commands set_workspace_root and set_workspace_root_and_cd_focused_pane, trimmed the workspace session contract to the surviving retarget_workspace command, rebuilt/synced the wasm artifact, and verified popup/menu/sidebar/workspace/layout command coverage through the default suite.
close_reason: Completed: documented the internal pane-orchestrator component boundary, removed stale workspace pipe commands, rebuilt/synced the wasm, and verified the surviving workspace/popup/menu/sidebar/layout contract.

title: Validate migrated bd workspace and retire br
description:
After migration completes:
1. Verify all 521 beads migrated with correct field mappings (spot-check 10+ including nested dotted IDs like yazelix-qgj7.4.3.5).
2. Validate dependency graph integrity: parent-child, blocks, relates_to all preserved.
3. Validate GitHub/Beads contract: external_ref URLs still point to correct luccahuguet/yazelix issues.
4. Run bd doctor and bd graph check on the migrated workspace.
5. Confirm bd show, bd update, bd close, bd dep add all work without corruption for dotted IDs.
6. Delete .beads/beads.db and .beads/beads.db-wal (br artifacts).
7. Remove br from PATH and Nix profile.
8. Update any CI or hooks that reference br.
close_reason: Completed: embedded bd workspace validates through bd list/show/graph/export; 536 issues export cleanly, dotted IDs resolve, tracked br artifacts and live br/bv references are gone, and local .br_recovery SQLite backups were removed. bd doctor is currently not supported in embedded mode, so direct bd operations were used instead.

title: Package bd for Nix/Home Manager and update AGENTS.md workflow
description:
After migration tooling exists and bd feature parity is confirmed:
1. Add bd (gastownhall/beads) to the Nix flake or Home Manager config.
2. Rewrite all AGENTS.md sections that reference br/bv commands to use bd equivalents. Key sections: Beads Workflow, Beads Workflow Integration, Session Protocol, every command example.
3. Remove bv (beads_viewer) entirely — bd has no direct bv equivalent for robot-triage/robot-plan/robot-insights, and those features are not needed. Replace the graph-aware triage workflow with bd ready, bd prime, bd stale, bd blocked, and bd graph.
4. Switch .beads/ from br+fsqlite storage to bd+embeddeddolt storage. This means bd init --force in the repo and then bd import of the migrated JSONL.
5. Update .beads/.gitignore for bd conventions (embeddeddolt/ instead of beads.db/beads.db-wal).
6. Remove br from the Nix/Home Manager config and remove the manually-installed ~/.local/bin/br.
7. Remove beads-viewer from the Nix/Home Manager config since bv is not compatible with bd storage.
close_reason: Completed repo-side migration: AGENTS/docs/CI now use bd, live maintainer issue sync uses bd, the maintainer shell packages pinned Beads v1.0.0 from upstream with the local hash/ICU override, and br/bv references are removed from tracked live surfaces. Personal profile cleanup for old br/bv binaries was intentionally left outside the repo.

title: Write br-to-bd migration export/import tooling
description:
After the schema mapping is done, write a migration script (Nushell or Python) that:
1. Reads the authoritative .beads/issues.jsonl and .beads/config.yaml from the br workspace.
2. Transforms each br issue into bd-compatible create commands or Dolt SQL inserts.
3. Preserves all fields: id prefix migration (yazelix-xxx → bd-xxx or keep yazelix-xxx), title, description, acceptance_criteria, design, notes (concatenated into description or stored as bd comments), status mapping, priority mapping (br P0-P4 → bd priority numbering), issue_type mapping, labels, dependencies (parent-child, blocks, relates_to), external_ref (GitHub issue URLs), close_reason, compaction_level.
4. Handles dependencies in topological order so bd create doesn't fail on forward refs.
5. Verifies the migrated count matches and spot-checks critical beads.

Decide whether to keep the yazelix- prefix or adopt bd- IDs. Keeping the prefix avoids breaking external_refs and GitHub/Beads contract URLs in AGENTS.md.
close_reason: Obsolete after migration: the br-to-bd migration has already completed, so the one-off migration helper and tracked migration artifacts were deleted instead of retained as maintained tooling.

title: Delete residual transient-pane duplication after menu/popup parity
description: After the helperless shared control model, geometry parity, and menu toggle are in place, do the delete-first cleanup pass. Remove stale wrappers, duplicate pane identity helpers, duplicated tests, and stale docs/spec text that describe menu and popup as separate transient-pane mechanisms.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Remove or rewrite leftover wrappers/scripts/tests/docs that only exist because menu and popup used separate transient-pane paths. 2. Keep product-specific menu and popup behavior where it is real, and document why any remaining separation survives. 3. Validate the final code inventory shows less transient-pane duplication than before this series. 4. Run focused popup/menu tests, generated Zellij config tests, syntax validation, and traceability validation.
notes: Follow-up cleanup remains after the shared transient-pane move: the old popup-runner plugin is still shipped/loaded even though popup/menu now route through the pane orchestrator, and docs/spec text such as docs/specs/floating_tui_panes.md still describe the older popup-runner/helper ownership model.
close_reason: Completed

title: Unify the transient-pane infrastructure beneath yzx menu and yzx popup
description: Keep yzx menu and yzx popup as distinct user-facing tools, but re-evaluate the shared transient-pane infrastructure beneath them. The real problem is no longer just duplicated wrappers: the popup-program flow is slower than the menu flow, it still passes through an extra helper-pane path that makes the UX feel shabbier, and ownership of pane open/toggle/close behavior is split across Nushell wrappers, Zellij scripts, and the popup plugin. This bead tracks the effort to define one clearer transient-pane seam without merging the two products.
acceptance_criteria: 1. `yzx menu` and `yzx popup` keep separate user-facing product identities but share one transient-pane contract for geometry, cwd, runtime argv/env, pane identity, and toggle semantics where the behavior overlaps. 2. Both floating experiences use the same configured dimensions instead of hardcoded menu geometry versus configurable popup geometry. 3. Both keybind experiences are managed toggles: open when missing, focus when present elsewhere, close when focused, without duplicate panes. 4. The final design removes visible helper panes from both menu and popup, or records a concrete Zellij API blocker with the narrowest non-regressing fallback. 5. Shared code is extracted only after the helperless/toggle capability model is proven, so we do not harden the current mismatch into a new abstraction.
notes: Reframed target after the first trim: the real remaining UX problem is parity, not just popup cleanup. Current state: popup uses configurable 90% geometry and a Rust popup-runner toggle but still needs a non-floating helper for the missing-pane open path; menu is fast and direct but hardcodes 70% geometry, is not a toggle, and does not share popup state management. Start with the helperless shared transient-pane control model before extracting more Nushell code, because Zellij plugin open semantics and close-on-exit/cwd support determine the correct abstraction boundary.
close_reason: Completed

title: Trim runtime helper and front-door cost after the command-surface cleanup
description: The latest command-surface trim proved that the next meaningful v15 LOC cuts are not in tiny wrapper commands. The detailed inventory now shows the real remaining seams more clearly: maintainer-oriented helpers still shipped under runtime paths, the front-door welcome and discoverability stack, and the very large runtime behavior test bucket that may now contain overlap. This parent bead tracks that next delete-first trim stage.
close_reason: Completed: all tracked child beads are closed and the parent scope is done.

title: Refresh subsystem inventory with trim-oriented runtime and maintainer detail
description: Refresh docs/subsystem_code_inventory.md after the latest v15 trim. Keep the top-level five-family totals current, add a deeper runtime and maintainer breakdown that is actually useful for deletion-first work, and include a direct command-surface view so future trim decisions can see where direct command LOC ends and helper-stack cost begins.
close_reason: Completed: refreshed the subsystem inventory with current totals, exact runtime and maintainer partitions, and a direct command-surface view for trim planning.

title: Evaluate deleting yzx config migrate in favor of startup preflight and doctor --fix
description: The explicit yzx config migrate surface may now be redundant. Startup preflight already catches stale managed config state, and yzx doctor --fix already overlaps the safe deterministic rewrite path. Re-evaluate whether v15 still needs a separate preview and apply command for config migrations, or whether that behavior should collapse into fewer owned surfaces.
design: Do not treat the migration engine and the public migration command as the same thing. v15 may still need migration rules internally while deleting the explicit user-facing preview and apply command. Compare the user value of yzx config migrate against the maintenance cost in docs, tests, guidance text, and duplicated fix flows. If the command survives, it should have a much tighter rationale than because we already have it.
acceptance_criteria: There is a concrete keep, narrow, or delete decision for yzx config migrate. If deleted, startup preflight and doctor or fix become the documented owners and the old command references are removed. If kept, its narrower purpose and non-overlap with doctor or fix are documented and defended by tests.
close_reason: Deleted the public yzx config migrate command while keeping the internal migration engine; startup preflight and yzx doctor --fix now own the user-facing migration path.

title: Decide whether v15 still keeps yzx env as a public shell-entry surface
description: yzx env is small in code but large in product meaning: it still anchors editor terminal integration and non-UI shell entry, yet it also carries startup cost, docs burden, and an open performance complaint. Decide whether v15 still wants this as a first-class public surface, a much narrower integration helper, or not at all.
design: Judge yzx env as a product boundary question, not just a performance bug. If v15 is the narrower workspace product, decide whether editor integration should keep a dedicated shell-entry command, move to a more explicit wrapper or integration story, or disappear. If it stays, narrow its contract and make the performance and ownership story explicit. If it goes, remove the surrounding docs and tests instead of leaving a half-supported shell mode around.
acceptance_criteria: There is a concrete keep, narrow, or delete decision for yzx env, with the supporting docs, tests, and editor-integration guidance aligned to that decision. The existing slow-env complaint is resolved either by removal or by a tighter retained contract with a clearer justification.
close_reason: Kept yzx env as the supported non-UI shell-entry surface for editor and terminal integration; the separate performance complaint remains tracked in yazelix-g3hn.

title: Narrow yzx doctor to a small v15 diagnostic surface and absorb the repair namespace
description: yzx doctor still carries a broad Classic-style health-check shape. Narrow it to the checks that still belong to v15: managed config health, generated runtime state, desktop-launch ownership, and Zellij plugin and workspace health. Fold the standalone yzx repair / yzx repair zellij-permissions story into the doctor-owned fix path or another clearer owner so the public repair namespace disappears.
design: Keep doctor as the explicit troubleshooting command, but make it defend only real v15 contracts. Drop broad environment trivia, redundant drift messaging, and checks that mostly restate implementation details. If Zellij permission seeding still needs a manual fix path, surface it as part of the doctor flow rather than preserving a tiny parallel command tree just for repair wording.
acceptance_criteria: yzx doctor covers only the curated v15 diagnostic surface, and the separate yzx repair namespace is gone or fully subsumed by the surviving doctor or fix contract. The remaining tests and docs describe one clear troubleshooting surface instead of a spread of tiny overlapping recovery commands.
close_reason: Narrowed yzx doctor to the v15 diagnostic surface, removed drift/log trivia, and absorbed plugin-permission repair into doctor --fix.

title: Trim the non-core v15 yzx command surface after the current runtime cleanup
description: The current v15 repo is much cleaner, but the public and shipped command surface still carries several non-core commands and support stacks that look more like Classic-era utility or maintainer affordances than the narrower workspace product. This bead tracks the next delete-first pass across the yzx command tree: remove commands that no longer belong, fold one-off repair flows into stronger owning surfaces, and make the remaining commands smaller and more honest about what v15 is.
design: Treat this as a product-boundary trim, not a random command shuffle. Start by deleting obviously non-core utility commands and redundant namespaces, then narrow the surviving diagnostic and config surfaces, and only then polish the remaining UX. The goal is a smaller public yzx tree with less documentation, test, and support burden, while keeping the user-visible behavior that still belongs to the v15 workspace product.
acceptance_criteria: There is an agreed v15 command-surface trim plan with focused child beads for the obvious deletion candidates, the doctor/config/env decision points, and the remaining status output cleanup. Each child bead names the carried command(s), the owning replacement surface when applicable, and the expected docs/test fallout so later implementation can proceed delete-first instead of reopening scope.
close_reason: Completed the v15 non-core yzx command-surface trim: deleted non-core commands, narrowed doctor, kept env intentionally, removed public config migrate, and structured status output.

title: Refresh architecture map and subsystem LOC inventory for current v15 repo shape
description: Rewrite the maintainer-facing architecture docs so they describe the current v15 repo shape instead of the older broad subsystem framing. Update architecture_map.md to define the real current subsystems and ownership boundaries, then regenerate subsystem_code_inventory.md with a fresh code-only LOC breakdown that matches those subsystem boundaries. The result should make it easy to answer both 'what subsystems does Yazelix have now?' and 'how much does each subsystem weigh?' without relying on stale v14-era buckets or counts.
close_reason: Completed

title: Trim deleted backend and reuse interfaces from workspace command tests
description: The workspace command test fixture still writes deleted devenv-backend, launch-profile, and startup flags into stub modules even though the real startup surface is already narrower. Rewrite the fixture to match the surviving v15 interfaces so the tests stop defending or mentioning deleted backend/reuse semantics.
close_reason: Completed: removed deleted backend/reuse stubs from the workspace-command fixture and trimmed stale DEVENV hostiles from desktop-path probes.

title: Trim stale devenv wording and maintainer-root heuristics from live v15 code
description: Remove stale devenv-era wording and unnecessary maintainer heuristics from live code. Scope: update startup/environment/initializer comments so they describe the current runtime contract, stop requiring devenv-specific repo-root hints where a normal repo-root check is enough, and keep only the maintainer-specific devenv references that are still genuinely required.
close_reason: Completed: removed stale devenv-era wording from live runtime code, simplified repo-root detection away from DEVENV_ROOT, and updated maintainer tests to match the current checkout contract.

title: Trim shell-out indirection from yzx status --versions
description: yzx status --versions still shells out to a second nu process and builds a source string just to run version_info.nu. Delete that indirection and call the version-info helper in-process. Keep the visible output the same while making the status path smaller and easier to verify.
close_reason: Completed: yzx status --versions now calls version_info in-process instead of shelling out to a second nu process.

title: Trim stale lockfile ownership from yzx status --versions and make version_info work on v15 installs
description: The packaged v15 runtime no longer ships devenv.lock, but version_info.nu still treats a runtime-local lockfile as part of the normal status surface and currently crashes when it is absent. Delete that stale ownership from the normal user path. Make yzx status --versions produce a clean runtime-version table on installed v15 runtimes without requiring a runtime lockfile.
close_reason: Completed: yzx status --versions no longer depends on a runtime-local devenv.lock and version_info now reports runtime versions cleanly on v15 installs.

title: Trim deprecated --skip-devenv compatibility from Nix preflight helpers
description: v15 no longer has a normal-user devenv contract, but nix_detector.nu still exports a deprecated --skip-devenv option on check_nix_installation and ensure_nix_available. Remove that dead compatibility surface, simplify the call path, and keep only the real Nix/flakes preflight behavior that still matters for current Yazelix startup and install flows.
close_reason: Completed: removed the dead --skip-devenv option from nix_detector preflight helpers and kept only the real Nix/flakes availability path.

title: Trim devenv GC ownership from yzx gc
description:
v15 no longer ships or owns runtime-local devenv, but yzx gc still resolves a devenv CLI and runs devenv gc before nix-collect-garbage. Delete that stale owner surface, keep yzx gc as a Nix store cleanup command, update tests/docs, and remove devenv_cli.nu if no live users remain.

Acceptance: yzx gc no longer resolves or invokes devenv; yzx gc still reports phase progress, handles du/nix-collect-garbage behavior, and preserves deep/deeper modes; stale devenv-specific gc tests/docs are rewritten or deleted; no live imports of devenv_cli.nu remain.
notes: Implementation: removed the devenv gc phase from yzx gc, deleted devenv_cli.nu after clearing live imports, rewrote gc maintainer tests for the Nix-only contract, and updated CLI/nixpkgs/backend-contract docs.
close_reason: Completed: yzx gc is now Nix-only, stale devenv CLI resolver and GC docs/tests were removed or rewritten, and maintainer GC tests pass.

title: Keep desktop integration explicit by repairing only existing managed manual entries during compatibility install
description:
The compatibility installer currently has a tempting path toward quietly re-owning desktop integration on every run. That muddies the surviving contract, because user-local desktop integration was supposed to stay explicit through `yzx desktop install`, not become a hidden installer side effect again.

Narrow the installer behavior so it only repairs or refreshes an already-existing Yazelix-managed user-local desktop entry when that exact surface exists. If the user never opted into manual desktop integration, the installer should leave desktop integration alone.
design:
Reason from first principles:
- The compatibility installer should not quietly take ownership of optional desktop integration for every user.
- Some users want no desktop entry at all, and `yzx desktop uninstall` should remain a respected choice instead of being reversed by the next installer run.
- The delete-first compromise is narrow: if a Yazelix-managed user-local desktop entry already exists, the installer may repair that exact managed surface; otherwise it should not create one.
- The repair path should ideally reuse the shared stable-launcher resolution seam from `yazelix-4buc.12` instead of inventing a second owner-lookup path.
- Home Manager-managed installs are out of scope for manual-entry repair; the installer should not recreate a user-local desktop entry there.
acceptance_criteria: The compatibility installer no longer creates a user-local desktop entry as a default side effect. If a Yazelix-managed manual desktop entry already exists for a manual/upstream install, the installer may repair or refresh that exact surface to the current expected launcher path. If manual desktop integration is absent, or if the user previously removed it with `yzx desktop uninstall`, it stays absent until the user explicitly runs `yzx desktop install`. Home Manager-managed installs do not get a recreated user-local desktop entry from the installer. Regression coverage defends all three cases.
notes: Completed: compatibility install now refreshes only an existing Yazelix-managed user-local desktop entry and otherwise leaves desktop integration absent; flake install smoke validates the default no-desktop-entry contract.
close_reason: Completed: installer-owned desktop integration is now explicit and repair-only, with flake install smoke coverage.

title: Refuse yzx desktop install in Home Manager-managed installs while preserving manual desktop cleanup paths
description:
Home Manager already owns the profile desktop entry, but `yzx desktop install` can still materialize a user-local launcher that shadows the Home Manager-owned one. That keeps desktop ownership ambiguous and makes it too easy to replace the correct owner with a stale manual surface.

Harden the command surface so Home Manager-managed installs do not quietly create or refresh a user-local Yazelix desktop entry. The surviving contract should point users back to the Home Manager owner and reserve manual desktop install for manual/upstream installs only.
design:
Reason from first principles:
- Desktop integration should have one clear owner. In Home Manager mode that owner is the Home Manager profile, not a user-local compatibility command.
- A warning is too soft here because it still permits writing the wrong owner surface and creating a shadowing problem that users then have to debug later.
- Do not solve this by trying to merge or auto-repair both surfaces at once. The right delete-first move is to narrow the command contract: manual desktop install is for manual/upstream installs; Home Manager owns desktop integration in Home Manager mode.
- Preserve the cleanup path explicitly. Refusing install must not trap users with stale manual entries they can no longer remove.
acceptance_criteria: When Yazelix detects a Home Manager-managed install, yzx desktop install refuses to create or refresh a user-local desktop entry and prints clear owner-aware guidance instead. The guidance points users at the Home Manager-owned desktop surface and the cleanup path for stale manual entries. Home Manager mode must still preserve manual desktop cleanup, including yzx desktop uninstall for removing a stale user-local entry that shadows the Home Manager launcher. Regression coverage defends that Home Manager mode no longer materializes a shadowing manual desktop entry and still permits the cleanup flow.
notes: Completed: yzx desktop install now refuses Home Manager-owned installs while yzx desktop uninstall remains the stale manual-entry cleanup path; default tests cover both.
close_reason: Completed: Home Manager desktop install refusal and manual cleanup preservation are implemented and covered.

title: Add a real Home Manager activation and update smoke lane for launch and restart ownership flows
description:
Home Manager is a first-class supported install owner, but the current regression surface still leans heavily on fixture tests and manual validation. Add one real non-default smoke lane that covers the actual owner-managed update path: Home Manager activation, profile-owned `yzx`, profile desktop entry, update/switch to a newer runtime, fresh launch, and `yzx restart` from an older live session.

Keep this lane high-signal and end-to-end. The point is to catch install-owner transition bugs that narrow fixtures can miss, not to build a second giant matrix.
design:
Reason from first principles:
- Home Manager is not a compatibility edge path; it is one of the two real install owners.
- Narrow fixture tests are still useful, but they do not fully defend owner transitions where profile wrappers, desktop entries, and already-open sessions interact.
- This should be one focused smoke lane, not a broad matrix explosion. Cover the exact surfaces users rely on after `home-manager switch`: profile `yzx`, profile desktop entry, fresh launch, and restart from an older live session.
- Keep it out of the default lane. The goal is to defend a high-value integration seam without turning common maintainer feedback loops into noise.
acceptance_criteria: There is a non-default verification lane that exercises a real Home Manager-owned update path. The lane proves that after a Home Manager activation/update, fresh launch uses the newer runtime, and `yzx restart` from an older live session also transitions onto the newer runtime. The lane also verifies the profile `yzx` path and Home Manager desktop-entry ownership without requiring `~/.local/bin/yzx` or user-local desktop integration, and it proves the flow does not silently depend on a recreated user-local compatibility launcher or desktop entry.
notes: Completed: maintainer lane now covers Home Manager input refresh + home-manager switch command construction and a profile-owned restart smoke test that proves yzx restart uses the Home Manager profile yzx without recreating manual ~/.local/bin/yzx or user-local desktop entries.
close_reason: Completed: non-default maintainer smoke coverage now defends Home Manager activation plus profile-owned restart/desktop ownership without user-local compatibility surfaces.

title: Extract stable external yzx launcher resolution and use it for restart across manual and Home Manager installs
description:
yzx restart currently only treats ~/.local/bin/yzx as the stable relaunch surface. In a pure Home Manager install that path normally does not exist, so restart falls back to yzx launch from the currently running session. After home-manager switch, that can relaunch the old runtime instead of the freshly updated Home Manager-owned one.

Extract one install-owner-aware stable external yzx launcher resolver and use it for restart.
