title: Tighten duplicate refresh-state inspection between prelaunch and in-terminal startup
description: Yazelix no longer appears to do a true double rebuild for the recent desktop and launch paths, but there is still duplicated refresh-state inspection across the prelaunch path and the in-terminal startup path. Both sides reason about needs_refresh and related state before the real interactive session starts. Track the follow-up cleanup so the product keeps one real rebuild boundary while reducing duplicated state inspection and split-brain launch reasoning.
design: Keep this delete-first and narrow. The goal is not to merge every launch helper into one giant orchestrator. The goal is to stop both the external/prelaunch path and the in-terminal startup path from independently inspecting refresh state when only one side should own the authoritative rebuild decision. First map exactly which path should decide whether a rebuild is required and which path should simply consume the already-decided result. Then delete duplicate state checks, notices, and branching where they only restate the same decision. Preserve the current good property: one real rebuild boundary, visible when appropriate, without reintroducing hidden rebuilds or stale-session confusion.
acceptance_criteria: 1. There is one clearly owned rebuild-decision boundary for the affected launch flow, rather than parallel prelaunch and in-terminal inspection of the same refresh state. 2. Remaining startup code consumes an explicit rebuild decision or artifact instead of recomputing the same launch-state judgment ad hoc. 3. The cleanup does not regress the visible-rebuild desktop contract, reuse mode, or terminal-wrapper repair logic. 4. Focused regressions defend that the flow still rebuilds exactly once when needed and does not silently hide rebuild work behind the launcher.
notes: Implemented as a narrow delete-first cleanup: yzx launch and the in-terminal start_yazelix wrapper now parse config directly instead of calling prepare_environment, so they no longer recompute config_state/needs_refresh. launch_yazelix.nu remains the single prelaunch rebuild-decision boundary for this flow, while start_yazelix_inner.nu keeps materializing runtime configs inside the session. Updated the workspace forwarding fixture with a config_parser stub for the lighter launch dependency. Verified: validate_syntax.nu, default yzx suite 90/90, core canonical 17/17, workspace canonical 21/21, validate_default_test_count_budget.nu, validate_default_test_traceability.nu, git diff --check.
close_reason: Completed refresh-state ownership cleanup and verified affected yzx suites.

title: Fix leaked __YAZELIX_RUNTIME_DIR__ placeholder in Zoxide editor launcher
description: Opening a file from the Zoxide editor flow currently fails because the generated command still contains the literal __YAZELIX_RUNTIME_DIR__ placeholder instead of a real runtime path. The failure shows up as a command like 'nu __YAZELIX_RUNTIME_DIR__/nushell/scripts/integrations/zoxide_open_in_editor.nu ...', so the editor pane never opens and the runtime contract is broken.
design: Treat this as a runtime-generation and command-templating bug, not a user-local recovery. Identify where the Zoxide editor command is supposed to resolve the runtime path, remove any duplicate placeholder plumbing that can leave __YAZELIX_RUNTIME_DIR__ unsubstituted, and make the launcher consume one canonical runtime-path source. If substitution is missing, fail fast with a clear runtime-path error instead of emitting a broken command string into the live session.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The Zoxide editor flow never emits a literal __YAZELIX_RUNTIME_DIR__ path in the launched command. 2. Opening a file from the Zoxide/Yazi editor surface works in a normal managed Yazelix session. 3. The runtime path used by this launcher comes from one explicit canonical source instead of ad hoc placeholder substitution. 4. A focused regression defends the seam that previously emitted the broken command.
notes: Resolved by the current generated-Yazi plugin runtime placeholder rendering path. The shipped plugin template still contains __YAZELIX_RUNTIME_DIR__ intentionally, but sync_bundled_yazi_assets renders bundled plugin files into the generated Yazi surface using get_yazelix_runtime_dir. Existing regression test test_generate_merged_yazi_config_renders_runtime_placeholders_in_plugins verifies that zoxide-editor.yazi/main.lua contains the real zoxide_open_in_editor.nu path and no literal placeholder. Verification: generated-config canonical runner passed 28/28.
close_reason: Already fixed and verified: generated zoxide-editor plugin renders a real runtime path and no literal placeholder.

title: Define desktop launch behavior when terminal choice changes across rebuilds
description: Desktop launch now has a real contract question: if the user changes terminal.terminals in yazelix.toml and the newly preferred terminal only becomes launchable after a rebuild, the launcher cannot simultaneously guarantee fast first paint, one-window behavior, and exact-terminal correctness. Capture and decide the intended product behavior instead of letting desktop fast paths guess.
design: Reason from first principles. The competing goals are: fast first paint, one click equals one real session/window, exact adherence to the newly configured terminal, and a maintainable state model. Evaluate at least these options: 1. correctness-first hidden wait, then launch the exact new terminal; 2. one-window visible bootstrap in the last known launchable terminal, with the new terminal taking effect on next launch; 3. two-window/bootstrap-then-final handoff that launches an old or host terminal immediately, rebuilds visibly, then spawns the newly configured terminal and disposes of the bootstrap window. Prefer the simplest contract that is honest, predictable, and maintainable. The bead should end with an explicit decision and follow-on implementation path, not just a list of options.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The bead records an explicit desktop-launch contract for the case where terminal selection changes and the new terminal only becomes available after rebuild. 2. The chosen behavior states whether Yazelix prioritizes fast first paint, exact new-terminal correctness, or a two-stage handoff in this edge case. 3. The decision includes failure behavior and restart or next-launch semantics. 4. The result is specific enough to guide a concrete implementation without re-arguing the product tradeoff.
close_reason: Defined and implemented the desktop launch contract: use a visible one-window bootstrap path when a host-launchable terminal exists, let newly rebuilt managed terminals take effect on the next launch via runtime-owned wrapper reuse, and fall back to the hidden-wait desktop path only when no visible bootstrap terminal is available.

title: Reduce duplicate shellhook/setup chatter during yzx launch rebuilds
close_reason: Completed

title: Define cross-window Yazelix session/update semantics
description: Define the product contract for how multiple Yazelix windows interact with shared runtime state, shared materialized state, and live Zellij sessions.
design:
Yazelix now has a clear runtime model, but its cross-window/session behavior is still under-specified. This epic should define the session contract that later fixes can rely on instead of continuing to infer behavior from launch helpers, Zellij defaults, or stale shell state.

The contract should separate:
- shared durable state: user config, installed/runtime code, generated configs, launch-profile state, rebuild hashes
- live session state: the actual Zellij session and attached clients in each open Yazelix window

The default non-persistent mode should be defined as independent live sessions that happen to consume the same durable state. New launches may use newer shared state than older still-open windows, but open windows must not be silently hot-swapped under the user.

Persistent-session mode should be defined as a different product contract: multiple windows are clients of one named logical session. Path/bootstrap semantics, restart scope, and last-client behavior are different there and must be called out explicitly instead of being treated as edge cases.

This epic should also anchor later session-lifecycle fixes such as detached zero-client Zellij servers burning CPU after windows close. Those bugs should be evaluated against the explicit non-persistent versus persistent contract rather than treated as generic cleanup quirks.
acceptance_criteria:
1. The docs define shared durable runtime/materialized state separately from live window/session state.
2. The docs define the default non-persistent behavior as independent Yazelix sessions/windows rather than implicit reattachment or hot replacement.
3. The docs define persistent-session behavior as multiple clients of one named logical session, with different restart/path/last-client semantics from the default mode.
4. The docs define how `yzx refresh`, `yzx update runtime`, and `yzx restart` relate to already-open windows instead of leaving that behavior implicit.
5. The contract is concrete enough to guide follow-up fixes for detached-session lifecycle bugs, desktop launch, and cross-window update UX.
notes:
Child split:
- `yazelix-1zti`: default non-persistent multi-window semantics
- `yazelix-vxrb`: persistent-session multi-window semantics
- `yazelix-jehj`: refresh/update/restart semantics across open windows

Delivered specs:
- `docs/specs/nonpersistent_window_session_contract.md`
- `docs/specs/persistent_window_session_contract.md`
- `docs/specs/open_window_update_transition_contract.md`

Concrete bug anchor:
- `yazelix-0ol6` captures the observed failure mode where detached zero-client Zellij servers kept burning CPU after windows closed. The fix should be evaluated against this contract rather than patched in isolation.
close_reason: Completed: documented the cross-window session/update contract across non-persistent windows, persistent-session mode, and open-window refresh/update/restart transitions.

title: Spec default multi-window Yazelix behavior for non-persistent sessions
description: Specify the default cross-window behavior when `zellij.persistent_sessions = false`.
design:
This task should pin down the normal Yazelix behavior for users who open more than one window without enabling persistent sessions.

In this mode, each launch should create its own live Yazelix/Zellij session. Windows share durable runtime/materialized state, but they do not share one live session. That means one window may still be running an older activated profile while a newly launched window uses fresher runtime/config state.

This task should also define the expected lifecycle of those independent sessions: closing the last client for a non-persistent window should end that session rather than leaving a long-lived detached server behind. That is the product contract that detached-session bugs should be measured against.
acceptance_criteria:
1. The docs state that non-persistent Yazelix windows are independent live sessions, even when they consume the same config/runtime/materialized state.
2. The docs state that new launches may use newer durable state than older still-open non-persistent windows.
3. The docs state that non-persistent windows are not automatically upgraded or hot-swapped when another window refreshes or updates the runtime.
4. The docs state that closing the last client for a non-persistent Yazelix session should not leave a detached long-lived session behind.
5. The resulting contract is specific enough to support later fixes for `yzx restart`, desktop launch, and detached-session cleanup in non-persistent mode.
notes:
This is the default user-facing behavior because `persistent_sessions = false` is the shipped default.

Spec delivered:
- `docs/specs/nonpersistent_window_session_contract.md`

Current defense:
- `nu nushell/scripts/dev/validate_specs.nu`
- `nu -c 'source nushell/scripts/dev/test_yzx_workspace_commands.nu; test_launch_here_path_uses_requested_directory_for_nonpersistent_sessions'`
close_reason: Completed: documented the default non-persistent multi-window session contract.

title: Spec refresh, runtime update, and restart semantics across open Yazelix windows
description: Specify how shared runtime/materialized-state updates interact with already-open Yazelix windows.
design:
Yazelix now has clearer runtime/materialized-state ownership, but the user-visible meaning of `yzx refresh`, `yzx update runtime`, and `yzx restart` across multiple open windows still needs to be codified.

This task should define the durable-state versus live-session contract:
- `yzx refresh` rebuilds shared materialized state and launch-profile state; it should not silently hot-swap unrelated open windows
- `yzx update runtime` updates the installed runtime on disk; it should affect future launches, not mutate already-open windows in place
- `yzx restart` should be defined as an explicit live-session transition rather than a vague refresh alias

The spec should cover both the normal non-persistent case and the persistent-session exception, where restart scope may be session-wide because multiple windows are clients of one logical session.
acceptance_criteria:
1. The docs state that `yzx refresh` updates shared materialized state but does not hot-replace unrelated already-open windows.
2. The docs state that `yzx update runtime` updates the installed runtime for future launches and does not silently upgrade already-open live sessions in place.
3. The docs state that `yzx restart` is the explicit command that moves a live session onto newer durable state.
4. The docs state how the `yzx restart` contract differs between default non-persistent windows and persistent-session mode.
5. The resulting contract is specific enough to guide future fixes in launch, desktop launch, and session cleanup without re-deriving the UX each time.
notes:
This task is about cross-window semantics, not the internal launch-state resolver mechanics already covered by the `le0s` family.

Spec delivered:
- `docs/specs/open_window_update_transition_contract.md`

Current defense:
- `nu nushell/scripts/dev/validate_specs.nu`
- `nu nushell/scripts/dev/test_yzx_workspace_commands.nu`
close_reason: Completed: documented the open-window refresh, runtime update, and restart transition contract.

title: Spec persistent-session behavior across multiple Yazelix windows
description: Specify how multiple windows behave when `zellij.persistent_sessions = true` and Yazelix reuses one named Zellij session.
design:
Persistent sessions are not just the default behavior with more reuse. They are a different session model: multiple windows become clients of one named logical Zellij session.

This task should define the consequences of that model explicitly:
- once the named session exists, later windows reattach instead of creating fresh live sessions
- `--path` or other fresh-bootstrap intent cannot silently override the already-running session root
- last-client behavior may intentionally differ from non-persistent mode because the point of persistence is to keep the session alive
- restart/update semantics may be session-scoped rather than client-scoped

The goal is to make persistent-session surprises legible and intentional instead of accidental side effects of current Zellij attach behavior.
acceptance_criteria:
1. The docs state that persistent mode means multiple windows attach to one named logical session rather than creating independent sessions.
2. The docs state what happens when a user provides `--path` or similar fresh-start intent after the named persistent session already exists.
3. The docs state whether the last-client lifecycle for persistent sessions intentionally differs from the default non-persistent mode.
4. The docs state that persistent-session restart/update behavior is evaluated at the logical-session scope, not by pretending each attached window is independent.
5. The contract is specific enough that later lifecycle bugs can distinguish an intentional persistent detached session from a default-mode leak.
notes:
This task stays explicit about where persistent behavior intentionally diverges from the default non-persistent contract instead of restating the same rules twice.

Spec delivered:
- `docs/specs/persistent_window_session_contract.md`

Current defense:
- `nu nushell/scripts/dev/validate_specs.nu`
- `nu -c 'source nushell/scripts/dev/test_yzx_workspace_commands.nu; test_launch_here_path_warns_when_existing_persistent_session_ignores_it'`
close_reason: Completed: documented the persistent-session multi-window contract.

title: Deprecate generic get_yazelix_dir and YAZELIX_DIR in favor of explicit root helpers
description: Follow through on the split-root architecture by retiring generic runtime aliases where explicit config, runtime, repo, state, or runtime-reference helpers now exist. Reduce use of get_yazelix_dir and YAZELIX_DIR in code, tests, and docs so helpers stop hiding which ownership domain they actually mean. Keep compatibility only where it remains an intentional user-facing transition surface.
design: Treat this as the final cleanup slice after the explicit helper boundaries are in place. Replace get_yazelix_dir and YAZELIX_DIR usages with config-root, runtime-root, state-root, repo-root, or runtime-reference helpers where the ownership domain is now known. Keep compatibility only at intentional external boundaries, and avoid turning this bead into a search-and-replace churn pass before the sharper helper APIs from the earlier children exist.
acceptance_criteria: Generic get_yazelix_dir and YAZELIX_DIR usage is materially reduced in code, tests, and docs because call sites now use explicit root helpers that match the ownership domain they mean. Remaining compatibility uses are deliberate, documented, and narrow. New code in the affected areas no longer introduces fresh generic root alias usage by default.
notes: Pipeline stage 1 for the current Core-readiness lane. Explicit root ownership and runtime identity must stop leaking through generic helpers before the repo can prove a backend-free workspace slice honestly.
close_reason: Completed: removed the remaining live YAZELIX_DIR runtime-root fallback from shared runtime resolution and the managed Helix wrapper, narrowed docs/tests/helpers to explicit runtime roots, and added wrapper regression coverage for legacy-alias isolation.

title: Make materialized-state persistence use explicit recording helpers
description: Tighten persistent state ownership by replacing generic bootstrap-side mutation helpers with explicitly named recorders for materialized state and launch state. Today mark_config_state_applied and record_launch_state express two different ownership domains but still feel like generic state writes hanging off bootstrap/launch helpers. Refactor that surface so setup/install, launch/refresh, and live session activation each write only the state they actually own.
design: Clarify persistent-state ownership by naming the writes after the ownership domain they represent. The current split between mark_config_state_applied and record_launch_state is already better than before, but the mutation surface still hangs off generic bootstrap and launch helpers. Move toward explicit recorders for materialized state versus launch-state, and keep setup/install, refresh/build, and live launch flows from writing state they do not own.
acceptance_criteria: Persistent state writes are routed through explicitly named helpers that reflect ownership boundaries instead of generic bootstrap-side mutation. Setup or install flows only record the materialized state they own, while launch or refresh flows record launch-state/profile state they own. The resulting API makes it harder for future setup or shell-hook paths to overwrite launch-state accidentally.
close_reason: Completed

title: Decompose resolve_built_profile into explicit profile-resolution layers
description: Break resolve_built_profile into narrower resolvers instead of keeping live-session detection, runtime-project artifacts, recorded launch state, and ambient env fallback inside one smart resolver. The real goal is not arbitrary caller-defined precedence for every call site. It is to separate the two actual policy bundles in the code today: current live-session profile resolution versus runtime-owned reusable profile resolution, so stale maintainer shells stop leaking into external launch and doctor logic.
design: Refactor resolve_built_profile by extracting explicit evidence resolvers for the distinct sources it currently mixes: live launched session activation, runtime-project build artifacts, recorded launch-state, and ambient shell fallback. Then add small policy wrappers for the two real caller families that already exist in code. Current-session flows such as start_yazelix_inner and refresh should prefer live launched-session activation first, then narrower runtime-owned evidence if needed. External/runtime-owned flows such as yzx launch, doctor, and editor launch outside the session should prefer runtime-project build artifacts first, then recorded launch-state, with ambient shell fallback kept only as a narrow compatibility edge. Keep resolve_profile_candidate and shell-script parsing as low-level utilities. It is acceptable to keep resolve_built_profile temporarily as a thin compatibility wrapper, but its precedence must simply delegate to one explicit policy helper rather than remaining the smart policy center.
acceptance_criteria: Profile resolution is decomposed into narrower helpers that each represent one evidence source or one explicit policy bundle. Core callers stop all relying on one monolithic smart resolver for materially different contexts. At minimum, launch and doctor use a runtime-owned reusable-profile policy, while start/refresh use a current-session policy, and editor launch context picks intentionally between them. If a compatibility wrapper remains, it is thin, documented, and no longer the place where most precedence logic hides. Regression coverage defends both key precedence stories: outside a live Yazelix session, runtime-owned evidence beats stale ambient shell state; inside a live Yazelix session, the activated session profile still wins.
close_reason: Completed

title: Split runtime-project lookup from runtime-project materialization
description: Replace ensure_yazelix_runtime_project_dir with a cleaner split between read-only lookup and explicit materialization. The current helper rewrites the symlinked build workspace even when callers only want to inspect build artifacts, which hides write-heavy behavior behind a read-style name. Introduce a read-only get_existing_runtime_project_dir surface and an explicit materialize_runtime_project_dir surface, then move read callers such as launch-state and bootstrap logic onto the non-materializing path unless they really need to regenerate the project tree.
design: This bead is about helper honesty. Split ensure_yazelix_runtime_project_dir into an explicit materializer and a read-only existing-project resolver so callers stop paying write-heavy workspace regeneration when they only want to inspect build artifacts. Classify current callers into writers versus readers first, then move read paths such as launch-state/profile inspection and bootstrap path discovery onto the non-materializing helper unless they truly need to regenerate the symlinked runtime project.
acceptance_criteria: The codebase has separate read-only and materializing runtime-project helpers with names that match their behavior. Read-side callers no longer recreate the runtime project tree as a hidden side effect. The remaining writers are explicit about why they materialize the project workspace, and the repo is easier to reason about because project-workspace writes are no longer hidden behind lookup-style helpers.
close_reason: Completed

title: Fail fast when runtime root resolution cannot find a valid runtime
description: Remove the hidden fallback in get_yazelix_runtime_dir that currently returns the config root when no valid runtime root can be resolved. That fallback keeps config and runtime concerns conflated and violates the runtime-root contract. Replace it with fail-fast runtime resolution, harden the affected callers, and make startup/doctor/launch errors explain that the runtime root is missing or invalid instead of silently treating ~/.config/yazelix as runtime code.
design: Constrain this bead to runtime-root resolution only. Remove the config-root escape hatch from get_yazelix_runtime_dir, preserve explicit YAZELIX_RUNTIME_DIR or legacy YAZELIX_DIR only as runtime aliases, and harden callers that were accidentally surviving on the fallback. Error handling should say that the runtime root is missing or invalid; it should not silently reinterpret ~/.config/yazelix as shipped runtime code. Do not mix this bead with generic alias cleanup beyond what is strictly required for fail-fast behavior.
acceptance_criteria: No canonical runtime-root helper returns the config root as a fallback when runtime resolution fails. Launch, doctor, startup, and other affected user-facing entrypoints fail fast with a clear runtime-root error instead of drifting into config-owned paths. Existing compatibility surfaces for explicit runtime aliases remain intentional and documented rather than being reintroduced as hidden fallback behavior.
close_reason: Completed

title: Define live session activation state as a first-class runtime layer
description: Extend the current runtime/launch specs beyond the three-layer model of dynamic user intent, deterministic runtime code, and materialized/generated state by naming live session activation state explicitly. Clarify how DEVENV_PROFILE, PATH activation, IN_YAZELIX_SHELL, YAZELIX_TERMINAL, and Zellij session markers relate to launch_state.json and the recorded built profile, so stale maintainer shells and live launched Yazelix sessions stop looking like the same thing in the architecture docs and helper contracts.
design: This is the spec-first slice. Extend the runtime/launch contracts and architecture map so they distinguish four layers instead of collapsing the last two: dynamic user intent, deterministic runtime code, materialized/generated state, and live session activation state. Be explicit that launch_state.json and rebuild_hash live in materialized state, while DEVENV_PROFILE, PATH activation, IN_YAZELIX_SHELL, YAZELIX_TERMINAL, and Zellij markers belong to live session activation. Keep this bead focused on naming and ownership, not broad helper refactors.
acceptance_criteria: The runtime-facing specs and architecture notes name live session activation state explicitly and distinguish it from both materialized/generated state and deterministic runtime code. The docs explain why a stale maintainer shell can coexist with a correct launch_state.json, and they clarify which session markers are activation-only rather than persisted runtime truth. The resulting terminology gives later helper refactors a cleaner contract to target instead of keeping activation state implicit.
close_reason: Completed

title: Tighten runtime/launch ownership boundaries after the recent state-ownership fixes
description: Carry the post-hardening runtime/launch architecture tightening lane after the recent fixes to runtime identity, launch-state ownership, desktop launch, refresh/build profile recording, and shell-vs-session profile resolution. The goal is not a rewrite. It is to align specs and code around a cleaner ownership model: dynamic user intent, deterministic runtime code, materialized/generated state, and explicit live session activation state, with narrower helpers and less split-brain between maintainer shells, runtime build artifacts, and recorded launch state.
design: Treat this as a delete-first architecture tightening pass, not a new abstraction project. Keep the current direction: split roots, deterministic runtime code, recorded launch state, and installed-runtime support. Tighten the remaining leaks by making runtime resolution fail fast, separating runtime-project materialization from read-only lookup, decomposing smart profile resolution into explicit layers, clarifying which flows own persistent state writes, and retiring generic root aliases where explicit helpers already exist.
acceptance_criteria: The follow-on child beads land a cleaner runtime/launch model without rewriting the product architecture. The specs name live session activation state explicitly, runtime-root resolution no longer falls back to config-root semantics, runtime-project materialization is not hidden behind read-style helpers, launch/profile resolution uses narrower explicit layers, persistent state writes have clearer ownership, and generic get_yazelix_dir or YAZELIX_DIR usage is reduced to intentional compatibility surfaces.
notes: Deeper review conclusion: the current runtime/launch architecture is directionally sound and does not need a rewrite, but it still needs tightening around runtime root resolution, runtime-project materialization, launch-profile resolution, persistent state ownership, and generic root aliases. The most important finding is that the docs should treat live session activation state as distinct from both materialized/generated state and deterministic runtime code. Follow-on work is now split into child beads le0s.1 through le0s.6 rather than leaving those concerns buried inside one review task.
close_reason: all steps complete

title: Fail fast for yzx dev commands when no writable repo checkout is available
description:
Maintainer commands such as ⚠️  This updates Yazelix maintainer inputs to latest upstream versions.
   The hardened flow updates devenv.lock locally, then runs canary refresh/build checks before finishing.
   Broken updates should stay local and never be pushed.
Continue? [y/N]: Aborted. should not drift into runtime-store paths or fail deep inside devenv 2.0.7 (x86_64-linux) when no writable Yazelix checkout is available. Make the maintainer surface fail fast with a clear repo-root requirement and a short recovery hint whenever it is invoked outside a usable checkout context.
close_reason: Added Alt+1..9 documentation to yzx keys output in keys.nu

title: Speed up desktop-entry launch by bypassing monolithic CLI and core launch imports
description: The desktop launcher path is still paying the full stable-CLI and core-launch bootstrap when opened from the generated desktop entry. Current measurements show `~/.local/bin/yzx desktop launch --help` takes about 2.07s, while importing `nushell/scripts/yzx/desktop.nu` directly takes about 0.025s and `nushell/scripts/core/desktop_launcher.nu` about 0.057s. The generated desktop file currently executes `~/.local/bin/yzx desktop launch`, which falls through the full `core/yazelix.nu` path, and `desktop_launcher.nu` then shells back into `use .../core/yazelix.nu *; yzx launch --home` instead of calling the leaf launch module directly.
design: Treat this as a narrow child of the leaf-dispatcher architecture bead, not a separate launcher subsystem rewrite. The desktop file should keep using the stable `yzx` binary, but the stable CLI should leaf-dispatch the `desktop` command family directly to `nushell/scripts/yzx/desktop.nu`. Inside that family, `yzx desktop launch` should keep using the installed runtime and desktop-launcher entrypoint, but `desktop_launcher.nu` should call `nushell/scripts/yzx/launch.nu` directly instead of re-importing `core/yazelix.nu`. Keep the shared launch semantics, preflight, and terminal dispatch owned by `yzx/launch.nu`; only remove redundant aggregator overhead.
acceptance_criteria:
1. `~/.local/bin/yzx desktop launch --help` no longer pays the full `core/yazelix.nu` import path by default.
2. `desktop_launcher.nu` launches through `yzx/launch.nu` directly rather than routing through `core/yazelix.nu`.
3. The installed desktop entry still works and preserves the current installed-runtime contract.
4. Add at least one focused regression for the lightweight desktop dispatcher path without adding weak implementation-string tests.
5. Measure before/after and keep the local evidence in the bead notes or commit context.
notes: Local evidence before starting: `~/.local/bin/yzx desktop launch --help` ~2.07s, `use nushell/scripts/yzx/desktop.nu *; help 'yzx desktop launch'` ~0.025s, `use nushell/scripts/core/desktop_launcher.nu *; help main` ~0.057s, and `use nushell/scripts/yzx/launch.nu *; help 'yzx launch'` ~0.424s.
close_reason: Completed

title: Refactor the stable CLI into a leaf-command dispatcher for latency-sensitive commands
description: Refactor the stable Yazelix command-entrypoint path so latency-sensitive leaf commands do not pay the full `core/yazelix.nu` import cost by default. Recent performance work already exposed the pattern twice: popup and reveal both became much faster once their hot path stopped importing the monolithic core command suite. This bead turns that from ad hoc fast paths into a cleaner command-dispatch boundary for small interactive commands.
design: Treat this as a narrow pre-Rust architecture cleanup, not a giant CLI rewrite. Keep `core/yazelix.nu` as an aggregator and rich command-development surface, but stop treating it as the default hot path for every small interactive command. The immediate work is to identify which leaf commands are genuinely latency-sensitive, route them through direct module entrypoints by default, and leave slower/heavier commands on the core path until there is evidence they need more. Current measured evidence already justifies this for `reveal`, `popup`, and likely `menu`: full core import is about 2.05s locally, while `yzx/popup.nu` imports around 0.09s, `yzx/menu.nu` around 0.11s, and `integrations/reveal_in_yazi.nu` around 0.06s. The right outcome is a small dispatcher boundary around the stable CLI and runtime wrappers, not a speculative generated registry system yet. This should make the later Rust rewrite cleaner by clarifying command-entrypoint ownership first instead of porting the current monolithic hot path as-is.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The stable CLI and relevant runtime wrappers have an explicit fast path for the small interactive commands that are proven latency-sensitive. 2. At least one remaining hot candidate beyond the already-fixed popup/reveal path is migrated through that cleaner dispatcher boundary and measured before/after. 3. The repo has one authoritative place to decide whether a command uses a leaf entrypoint or the full core command suite, instead of growing ad hoc wrapper exceptions forever. 4. Add focused wrapper-behavior regressions for the new dispatcher paths without adding weak implementation-string tests.
notes: Current local evidence: `use nushell/scripts/core/yazelix.nu *` imports in about 2.05s, `use nushell/scripts/yzx/menu.nu *` in about 0.11s, and the lightweight reveal wrapper import in about 0.06s. `configs/zellij/scripts/yzx_menu_popup.nu` is the clearest remaining hot-path candidate because it still imports `core/yazelix.nu` just to run `yzx menu`.
close_reason: Completed

title: yzx reveal latency is too high
description: Track `yzx reveal` taking around three seconds to reveal the current file in the managed Yazi sidebar. This is a reveal-path performance bug, not a popup bug and not a correctness regression. The work should identify where the cost actually lives: target-path resolution, sidebar state discovery, Yazi `ya emit-to reveal`, pane focus/orchestrator work, or related coordination on the reveal path.
design: Treat this as a measurement-first bug. Reproduce the latency with a concrete reveal action and instrument the reveal path from `yzx reveal` through target resolution, sidebar lookup, Yazi emit, and focus/orchestrator work. Keep the scope on reveal latency only. Prefer deleting unnecessary work or reusing state over adding more coordination layers.
acceptance_criteria: 1. There is a reproducible measurement of reveal latency before and after the fix. 2. The main source of reveal-path cost is identified and documented. 3. `yzx reveal` becomes substantially faster in normal use without regressing correctness or focus behavior. 4. Add one focused regression or benchmark guard at the seam that actually caused the latency.
close_reason: Completed

title: Popup pane open latency is too high
description: Track the managed popup path taking around five seconds to open while closing remains fast. This is a popup-open-path performance bug, not a generic pane-slow issue. The work should identify where the latency actually lives: popup launch orchestration, Zellij pane creation, plugin coordination, shell/env setup, or the launched popup program itself, then remove unnecessary work or cache the expensive step.
design: Treat this as a measurement-first bug. Reproduce the latency with a concrete popup command and instrument the open path from /Alt+t through pane creation to first usable frame. Keep the scope on open latency only; close is already fast and should remain unchanged. Prefer deleting work or reusing existing state over adding more control layers.
acceptance_criteria: 1. There is a reproducible measurement of popup open latency before and after the fix. 2. The main source of open-path cost is identified and documented. 3. The managed popup opens substantially faster in normal use without regressing close/focus/toggle behavior. 4. Add one focused regression or benchmark guard at the seam that actually caused the latency.
close_reason: Popup wrappers now avoid importing the full core command suite on popup open, removing the main measured bootstrap cost.

title: Audit remaining source-vs-installed runtime identity leaks after the launch-path cleanup
description: After the source-shell export cleanup lands, re-audit the remaining runtime identity surfaces. Check launch, doctor, generated shell hooks, popup/wrapper handoff, and maintainer probes for places where an installed runtime path can still shadow a source checkout or vice versa. Delete redundant aliases and document any remaining intentional split between inferred runtime and installed runtime reference.
notes:
2026-04-04 finding: desktop relaunch latency traced to runtime-identity hash churn, not desktop dispatch itself. The installed runtime package symlinks its nushell tree into a Nix -source path, so path-self inference inside common.nu could resolve the source tree even when YAZELIX_RUNTIME_DIR explicitly pointed at the installed runtime. Because compute_config_state hashes the runtime path, that heuristic made combined_hash oscillate between installed-runtime and source-tree identities and forced rebuilds on reopen. The fix direction is to trust an explicit valid YAZELIX_RUNTIME_DIR in installed flows, while keeping repo-shell DEVENV_ROOT as the user-facing source identity and using a separate setup-only runtime root for shellHook bookkeeping.

Deeper follow-on tightening from this audit is now split under yazelix-le0s: le0s.2 for fail-fast runtime root resolution, le0s.3 for explicit runtime-project materialization, le0s.4 for narrower profile resolvers, and le0s.6 for deprecating generic root aliases.
close_reason: Completed: audited remaining runtime-identity surfaces after the source-shell cleanup. Remaining explicit YAZELIX_RUNTIME_DIR exports are intentional installed-runtime, desktop, wrapper, Home Manager activation, or cross-process handoff boundaries rather than ambient source-checkout leaks. Repo-shell documentation now reflects the DEVENV_ROOT plus IN_YAZELIX_SHELL model. Broader generic alias cleanup remains tracked under yazelix-le0s.6.

title: Audit the full GitHub/Beads backlog contract and define which beads deserve public issues
description: Audit the current backlog to ensure every in-scope GitHub issue maps to exactly one bead, lifecycle state is consistent in both directions, canonical automated comments exist where required, and the repo has an explicit policy for which internal beads should or should not become public GitHub issues. The goal is to clean up the contract and publication boundary for the backlog without imposing a blanket one-to-one rule from beads to GitHub.
design: Treat this as backlog-governance work, not as a mass issue-creation task. Start from the existing GitHub/Beads contract in AGENTS.md and the current imported backlog, then classify beads into public-worthy versus internal-planning-only categories. Repair missing GitHub→Beads mappings and lifecycle mismatches first. Only create new GitHub issues for beads that are genuinely user-visible, externally discussable, or worth tracking publicly. Avoid exporting low-level decomposition, dependency scaffolding, or purely internal planning slices just to satisfy symmetry.
acceptance_criteria: There is a documented and applied policy for which beads should have GitHub issues. Every in-scope GitHub issue maps cleanly to exactly one bead with matching open/closed state and the canonical automated comment. The backlog also has a reviewed list of open beads that intentionally remain internal and therefore do not need public GitHub issues.
close_reason: Completed: documented the backlog publication policy, reviewed the intentionally internal open-bead list, synced GitHub lifecycle and canonical Beads comments, and revalidated the GitHub/Beads contract.

title: Define the Rust/Nushell bridge contract and incremental crate layout
description: Define how Rust code will be integrated incrementally into the current Nushell-driven repo so small slices can land after v15.0 during v15.x without forcing a big-bang rewrite or pretending v15.0 itself is the Rust release. Cover the helper-binary versus library boundary, JSON/stdout-stderr contracts, error-classification handoff, repo/workspace crate layout, build/distribution implications, and how Nushell command surfaces should call into Rust while preserving current UX.
design: This is the missing bridge between Rust planning and actually shipping incremental Rust slices in the live v15 repo. The repo already contains Rust Zellij plugins, but those wasm crates do not answer how a future Rust core should coexist with Nushell commands, Nix packaging, and generated-config flows. Prefer a thin typed Rust helper/binary model first, with stable machine-readable output consumed by Nushell shims. Do not jump straight to replacing the whole CLI with clap, and keep the bridge compatible with a longer v15.x insertion path that feeds into v16.
acceptance_criteria: There is a concrete recommendation for the incremental Rust integration strategy: where Rust code lives, how Nushell invokes it, what machine-readable contract is used, how errors/logging are surfaced, and how the build/distribution story stays coherent with the current repo. The result is specific enough that later Rust slices can share one integration pattern across v15.x and into the broader v16 push instead of inventing their own ad hoc shims.
notes:
2026-04-18 bridge note for the first recommended Rust slice:
- This bead is now the concrete prerequisite for the first Rust config/runtime slice.
- Start with a stable helper/CLI surface that Nushell can call for config/runtime-core operations; do not treat the bridge as permission to replace the whole yzx CLI with clap.
- Preserve current startup profile/report step names and semantics at the Nushell boundary so Rust-vs-Nushell before/after comparisons use the same labels and reports.
2026-04-19: Added docs/specs/rust_nushell_bridge_contract.md and registered it in the spec inventory. Decision: use a private runtime-root-resolved Rust helper binary behind Nushell, keep yzx as the public command surface, use strict JSON envelopes and stable error classes, keep startup profile labels at the Nushell boundary, and place helper-oriented Rust under rust_core/ separate from rust_plugins/.
close_reason: Completed: bridge contract added in docs/specs/rust_nushell_bridge_contract.md and registered in spec inventory.

title: Define the Rust migration matrix and first-slice order after boundaries are proven
description: Produce an explicit Rust migration matrix for the current v15 codebase in a form that supports gradual v15.x insertion and a later Rust-forward v16 release. Classify the major surfaces by Rust fitness, parity risk, expected payoff, and whether they should stay in Nushell, move behind a Rust shim, or move fully into a Rust core. Ground the matrix in the actual hot modules carrying product complexity today: config parsing and validation, runtime contract checking, doctor and preflight logic, environment bootstrap, launch/bootstrap state, config generation, and the mixed yzx command surface.
design: This is the gate between 'Rust is safer/faster' sentiment and an implementation sequence that actually improves the live v15 product without confusing release identity. Build directly on the current architecture work: runtime/helper trims, pane-orchestrator cleanup, migration-layer deletion, runtime dependency/preflight contracts, and shared checker work. The output should prefer delete-first simplification before translation. Explicitly identify what should remain Nushell or Nix glue even in a Rust-heavier future, and separate slices that can land quietly during v15.x from slices that are more naturally part of the later v16 push.
acceptance_criteria: There is a written Rust migration matrix covering the main surviving Nushell surfaces with a recommended keep/bridge/rewrite decision for each. The matrix identifies which slices can land during v15.x, which should likely wait for the broader v16 Rust-forward push, and which should likely remain Nushell or Nix. The recommendation is grounded in current hot spots and the live v15 product contract rather than in generic preference for Rust.
notes:
Current sequencing note after the 2026-04-13 product decision: treat this as planning for the live v15 line after the current trim and boundary-hardening work, not as a placeholder for a parallel future branch. Do not use the Rust matrix to bypass the remaining cleanup lanes; it should start only after the key ownership and product-boundary work is stable enough that Rust effort will clarify the system instead of freezing transitional clutter into a new implementation.
2026-04-17 sequencing note: do not treat revival of a broad config-migration engine as a default Rust slice. The cheaper first move is desktop-launch visibility and visible config failure, then only consider a tiny schema-versioned Rust migration adjunct if the pain remains and the product promise justifies permanent machinery.
2026-04-18 first-slice recommendation after the startup/profiling cleanup lane:
- Treat the first Rust slice as the config/runtime core, not launch/bootstrap orchestration, shellhook glue, doctor reasoning, or a broad clap-first CLI rewrite.
- Recommended first-slice order:
  1. yazelix.toml loading, defaults, and schema-backed normalization
  2. config-state hashing / invalidation
  3. generated runtime materialization planning and managed writes
  4. a stable Nushell-callable helper/CLI seam for the slice
  5. preserving the same startup profile/report step names so before/after comparisons stay honest.
- Use this sequence as the default recommendation in the migration matrix unless later boundary work proves a smaller delete-first move.
2026-04-19: Added docs/specs/rust_migration_matrix.md and registered it in the spec inventory. Decision: first Rust implementation sequence is config.normalize via kt5.2.1, config_state.compute via kt5.2.2, then runtime_materialization plan/apply via kt5.2.3. Keep public yzx UX, launch/bootstrap process orchestration, host integration, and most text-heavy renderers in Nushell unless later beads prove a narrower Rust subcore is worth extracting.
close_reason: Completed: Rust migration matrix added in docs/specs/rust_migration_matrix.md and registered in spec inventory.

title: Port the surviving config contract, parsing, and validation core behind a Rust shim after migration deletion
description: Plan and later implement a Rust-backed core for the deterministic config logic that survives after the v15 migration layer is deleted. The target is the current config contract, parsing, schema validation, and state logic, not the old migration machinery. The goal is not to rewrite every config-facing command immediately, but to move the most structured, failure-prone surviving logic into a safer typed core while keeping a thin Nushell compatibility layer during migration.
design: Treat this as a good early Rust slice once the migration matrix and Rust/Nushell bridge strategy are ready and once the config-migration deletion bead has landed. This logic is highly structured, heavily reused, and benefits from strong types, deterministic serialization, and richer tests. Keep the UX and command surface stable by exposing the Rust logic through a narrow helper binary or shim first. Do not mix this with launch orchestration or package-backend work in the same slice, and do not preserve deleted migration behavior just because the old bead used to mention it.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear migration plan for moving the surviving config contract/parsing/validation/state core behind a Rust shim without breaking the current command surface. The slice defines parity fixtures for the live config contract, identifies which Nushell modules become thin wrappers versus being deleted, and is concrete enough to implement incrementally during v15.x or as part of the broader v16 push.
notes:
Primary target modules after migration deletion: config_parser.nu, config_contract.nu, config_schema.nu, config_state.nu, config_diagnostics.nu, and any tightly coupled surviving helpers they require. Explicit non-goal: porting config_migrations.nu or migration transaction machinery, because that layer is being removed from the live product.
2026-04-17 scope note: if Yazelix later revisits config migration, the likely fit is a very small Rust-first adjunct on top of this surviving config core, with explicit schema_version and deterministic structural transforms only. Do not broaden this slice into the historical migration engine without a separate product decision.
2026-04-18 first-slice scope note:
- Treat kt5.2 as the first recommended implementation slice once kt5.1 / kt5.1.1 are ready.
- Immediate scope for that slice:
  1. yazelix.toml loading, defaults, and schema-backed normalization
  2. config-state hashing / invalidation
  3. generated runtime materialization planning and managed writes
- Keep text-heavy Yazi/Zellij template rendering in Nushell unless a narrower structured subcore clearly proves worth extracting; the Rust core should first own typed config/state/materialization decisions and machine-readable outputs.
- Preserve existing startup profile/report step names across the slice so the current profiler stays comparable before/after.
2026-04-19 Rust dependency gate: before implementing this config-core Rust slice, record a crate-vs-in-house plan in the bead or linked spec. The plan must separate production crates, dev-only crates, in-house domain logic, rejected alternatives, and Nix packaging impact.
2026-04-19 kt5.2.1 landed as the packaged Rust config.normalize core and parity seam, not as live startup adoption. Added child yazelix-kt5.2.4 for the explicit Nushell activation boundary; kt5.2.2 and kt5.2.3 can continue using the Rust library/core without waiting for live parser adoption unless their implementation needs startup traffic switched first.
2026-04-19 temporary seam ledger:
- Current temporary seams from kt5.2.1 and kt5.2.2 live in config_parser.nu helper discovery and rendering, config_state.nu bridge wrappers, transition-heavy cases in test_yzx_generated_configs.nu, and CI helper export/build wiring.
- Earliest deletion checkpoint is kt5.2.3. That slice should reassess which planning/state wrappers still need to exist and which parity cases only defended the Nu-to-Rust migration.
- Do not close kt5.2 on additive Rust LOC alone. At least one surviving Nu owner seam in the config/state/materialization path should be deleted or explicitly demoted to a permanent thin bridge.
close_reason: Completed: the config-core Rust slice is finished; config normalization, config-state hashing, runtime materialization planning/apply, live parser adoption, fallback deletion, legacy cache deletion, and bridge-glue cleanup children are all closed.

title: Define the Yazelix Core edition boundary and support story
description: Define whether a no-package-management Yazelix edition should exist, what it would own, and what it would explicitly stop promising. Treat this as a product-boundary bead, not an implementation promise. The likely shape is a Yazelix Core edition that keeps the workspace UX layer, generated config ownership, and selected CLI flows while dropping environment/package provisioning and managed backend guarantees. The output should be explicit enough to say whether Core should be a branch, package, or later mode at all.
design: Reason from the current architecture and command inventory rather than from branding first. Use the backend contract and command-surface audit to determine whether a Core edition is actually coherent. Be explicit about what Core would keep, what it would drop, what failure modes would become user responsibility, and whether it should be a separate package, branch, or not pursued at all. The name can stay provisional until the product boundary is proven; the important part is the support story, not the label.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear recommendation on whether Yazelix Core should exist and, if so, what it would own and explicitly stop promising. The decision is grounded in the backend contract, runtime dependency contract, and command-surface classification rather than generic appetite for a lighter edition. The result is specific enough to guide later implementation or a deliberate no-go decision.
notes:
2026-04-09 completion:
- Added `docs/specs/yazelix_core_boundary.md` with the product-boundary decision.
- Current recommendation: do not ship a separate Yazelix Core edition now; revisit it later only as a narrower same-repo mode after more seam reduction.
close_reason: Completed

title: Evaluate centralizing more shared data into constants.nu and whether it now needs refactoring
description: Audit the current role of nushell/scripts/utils/constants.nu and decide whether more shared data should be centralized there or whether the file has reached the point where further growth would be counterproductive. Scope: inventory existing constants-like data that is still duplicated elsewhere, identify what truly benefits from centralization, and recommend whether constants.nu should stay as-is, absorb more shared data, or be split into smaller focused modules.
design: Treat this as a focused audit, not as a mandate to turn constants.nu into a dumping ground. Identify which duplicated values are genuinely constants-like and shared across multiple real consumers, and which ones should stay near the subsystem that owns them. The output should distinguish between centralizing more data into constants.nu, leaving the file mostly as-is, or splitting it into smaller focused modules if its current role has become too mixed.
acceptance_criteria: There is a concrete recommendation for constants.nu: keep as-is, centralize a specific shortlist of additional shared data, or refactor the file into smaller modules. The recommendation is grounded in actual duplicated data ownership sites, not taste alone. Any suggested next steps leave the repo with clearer ownership of shared values, not more indirection.
notes: Completed the constants audit on v15. Decision: keep constants.nu as a focused shared-values module rather than splitting it or growing it into a subsystem registry. The file is still small (~311 lines), and most remaining lists are subsystem-owned or test fixture data. One concrete centralization was worth doing: production paths that still hard-coded the existing DEFAULT_SHELL/DEFAULT_TERMINAL values now import those constants instead. Verified with validate_syntax, the full yzx default suite, and git diff --check.
close_reason: Completed constants audit and centralized the existing shell/terminal defaults without broadening constants.nu.

title: Fix stale config-root runtime env leaking into Zellij helper scripts
close_reason: Completed: runtime/Nushell resolution now recovers from stale config-root runtime env, and the flake install smoke defends the exact regression path from issue #526.

title: Revert Yazi to default theme
close_reason: Fixed on main in ba937e4 (Yazi default theme restored to upstream default)

title: Fix Ghostty cursor effect shader path mapping
description: Ghostty cursor color changes still apply, but the cursor effect shaders do not render. Investigation shows the generated Ghostty config references effect shader paths like `./shaders/generated_effects/tail.glsl`, while the shader builder currently emits files named `cursor_trail.glsl`, `cursor_ripple_rectangle.glsl`, and similar. That mismatch leaves Ghostty with a flat colored cursor and no trail or mode-change effect.
close_reason: Completed: Ghostty cursor effect shaders were broken by a generated filename mismatch; the builder now emits the user-facing effect filenames that the generated Ghostty config references.

title: Extract flake installer shell block into a maintained script or helper
description:
The `install` app in `flake.nix` still embeds a long shell script inline. That block now carries most of the flake-specific install behavior: runtime symlink setup, seeded config creation, shell hook refresh, generated config rebuilds, and user-facing install messaging.

It works, but it is the largest remaining behavior-heavy chunk in the flake and is harder to review, test, and evolve than the surrounding flake output wiring.
design:
Keep the current install contract and app surface, but move the behavior-heavy shell block out of `flake.nix` into one maintained checked-in script or a small helper derivation that generates the script. Prefer a real script file if possible so install behavior can be reviewed and evolved without editing large inline shell strings.

The goal is not to redesign installation. It is to separate flake output plumbing from installer behavior and make future install-path changes less brittle.
acceptance_criteria: 1. `flake.nix` no longer contains the long inline installer shell block. 2. The install behavior remains the same from the user perspective: `nix run .#install` still installs the runtime, seeds config files, refreshes shell hooks, rebuilds generated configs, and prints the same next-step guidance. 3. Flake install smoke validation still passes after the extraction. 4. The extraction does not reintroduce runtime-path staleness or split install logic across multiple ad hoc shell snippets.
close_reason: Completed: moved the flake installer shell logic into a maintained checked-in script template and kept the nix run install contract unchanged.

title: DRY shared runtime tree assembly across flake and nixpkgs package outputs
description:
The Yazelix packaging layer still duplicates the common runtime tree assembly across `yazelix_runtime_package.nix` and `mk_yazelix_package.nix`. Both build nearly the same runtime content: linked repo directories, top-level config/docs files, `bin/nu`, and the generated `bin/yzx` launcher, then diverge only in the final wrapper and package-shape details.

That duplication is now one of the clearest remaining packaging cleanup targets. It increases maintenance cost and makes authority changes, runtime-file additions, and install-path fixes easier to apply inconsistently.
design:
Keep the current top-level split: one flake/runtime artifact and one nixpkgs-style wrapped package. Do not try to collapse those two products into a single generic builder. Instead, extract one small shared helper for the common runtime tree assembly and let the final package files keep only the wrapper-specific behavior.

The shared helper should own the repeated linked directories/files, the common `bin/nu`, and the common `bin/yzx` launcher surface. Wrapper-specific PATH setup and any final package-shape differences should remain local to the callers. Prefer a narrow helper such as `mk_runtime_tree.nix` over a mini-framework.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The common runtime tree assembly no longer has to be edited in two places. 2. `yazelix_runtime_package.nix` and `mk_yazelix_package.nix` remain separate at the top level but share one maintained implementation for the repeated runtime tree contents. 3. Existing flake install, nixpkgs package, and nixpkgs submission validators still pass after the refactor. 4. The refactor does not reintroduce a second authority for the runtime-owned `devenv` path or weaken the current package/install contract.
close_reason: Completed: extracted shared runtime tree assembly into mk_runtime_tree.nix and kept only wrapper-specific behavior in the package callers.

title: Reproduce and classify pane-frame flicker with the current zjstatus stack
description: Create a concrete behavior matrix for the current pane-frame problem so the fork decision is grounded in observed failure modes rather than memory or vague impressions.
design: Keep this as a focused reproduction and classification pass. Verify the current behavior across the scenarios that matter: pane_frames off or on, single or multiple panes, search or scroll modes, fullscreen, and any obvious transparency interactions. The result should identify which combinations actually flicker or redraw badly.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The current pane-frame behavior is reproduced in a small concrete scenario matrix. 2. The investigation records which combinations are stable, unstable, or visually unacceptable. 3. Later fork or non-fork decisions can point at this matrix instead of anecdotes.
close_reason: Obsolete: pane-frame flicker investigation was superseded by the simpler direct pane_frames path without zjframes or plugin-driven frame toggling.

title: Evaluate the upstream zjframes path against the same pane-frame matrix
description: Spike the upstream zjframes-based alternative under the same scenarios as the baseline reproduction so Yazelix can compare real behavior instead of guessing whether it helps.
design: Do not treat zjframes as automatically good or bad. Evaluate it under the same matrix as the baseline so the comparison is honest. The point is to learn whether the upstream non-fork path actually produces a stable usable pane-frame experience for Yazelix, not just whether it technically toggles frames.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The upstream zjframes path is tested against the same scenario matrix as the current baseline. 2. The repo records whether zjframes materially improves, fails to improve, or worsens the pane-frame experience. 3. The comparison is concrete enough to inform the final fork recommendation.
close_reason: Obsolete: the upstream zjframes evaluation is no longer relevant after adopting direct pane_frames without zjframes.

title: Decide whether a Yazelix-specific zjstatus fork is warranted for pane frames
description: Use the baseline reproduction and the upstream zjframes evaluation to make a concrete recommendation on whether Yazelix should keep pane frames off, rely on upstream alternatives, or pursue a fork.
design: This is the decision bead, not another open-ended investigation. Compare the baseline and zjframes results, explain the user-experience tradeoffs clearly, and recommend either keeping frames off, pursuing an upstream path, or creating a scoped fork follow-up. If a fork is recommended, define the first concrete scope instead of leaving a vague aspiration.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The repo has a concrete recommendation for pane-frame support based on observed evidence, not guesswork. 2. The recommendation explains why non-fork paths are sufficient or insufficient. 3. If a fork is recommended, the next implementation scope is named clearly.
close_reason: Obsolete: the fork-versus-zjframes decision is no longer the active problem after moving to direct pane_frames without zjframes.

title: Investigate whether a Yazelix-specific zjstatus fork is needed for stable pane frames
description: Imported GitHub issue #521.
design: Treat this as a fork-worthiness investigation, not as an immediate implementation commitment. The purpose is to determine whether Yazelix can support real pane frames without severe flicker while keeping the current zjstatus-style top bar, or whether a Yazelix-specific zjstatus fork is the only honest path. Compare the realistic options: keep pane_frames false, prototype upstream zjframes, or fork zjstatus. The outcome should be a concrete recommendation with explicit reasoning and, if a fork is justified, a clearly scoped follow-up rather than a vague aspiration.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The repo has a concrete written recommendation on whether Yazelix should fork zjstatus for stable pane-frame support. 2. The recommendation explains why the non-fork alternatives are sufficient or insufficient. 3. If a fork is recommended, the issue leaves behind a clear next-step scope rather than a vague idea.
notes: Planning refinement: keep this investigation in three explicit phases: reproduce and classify the current flicker behavior, evaluate the upstream zjframes path under the same scenarios, then make a concrete fork-versus-nonfork recommendation. This should avoid a vague all-at-once investigation.
close_reason: Obsolete: the pane-frame fork investigation was superseded by the simpler direct pane_frames path without zjframes.

title: Add a misc pack and place tokei there
description: Imported GitHub issue #519.
close_reason: Completed: the misc pack with tokei is already in the catalog, default packs file, Home Manager module, and docs, and GitHub issue #519 is closed.

title: Split managed config contract tests by subsystem ownership
close_reason: Completed

title: Align runtime-owned devenv with the locked source and replace false skew warning
description:
Yazelix currently has two authorities for `devenv`: the runtime/package outputs ship `pkgs.devenv` from nixpkgs, while `devenv shell` still evaluates against the separately pinned `cachix/devenv` source in `devenv.lock`. That split can drift in either direction and is currently user-visible in `yzx env` as the upstream warning that the CLI is newer than the locked input.

Investigation also showed that the warning itself is not a reliable contract check for Yazelix. In the currently locked upstream `devenv` source, the CLI package version is `2.0.7` while the module-side `src/modules/latest-version` file is still `2.0.6`, so upstream can warn even when the CLI and module tree come from the same locked Git revision. The bead should therefore fix both problems: remove Yazelix's multi-authority packaging seam, and stop relying on upstream's warning semantics as if they were the real source-of-truth check.
design:
Treat this as an authority/alignment cleanup, not as a warning-string tweak. The accepted fix should make `devenv.lock` the authoritative source for the runtime-owned `devenv` CLI, package that same locked source into the flake/runtime/nixpkgs-style outputs, and keep `devenv_cli.nu` preferring the runtime-owned path.

Because upstream `devenv.warnOnNewVersion` currently compares the CLI version to `src/modules/latest-version` rather than to the locked revision identity, Yazelix should not depend on that warning to model its own contract. The likely shape is:
- add a small Nix helper that derives the runtime `devenv` package from the locked `cachix/devenv` source in `devenv.lock`
- use that helper in `yazelix_runtime_package.nix` and `mk_yazelix_package.nix` instead of `pkgs.devenv`
- disable the upstream new-version warning in Yazelix's `devenv.nix`
- add Yazelix-owned validation that the packaged runtime `bin/devenv` comes from the same locked source as the shipped `devenv.lock`, and that `yzx env` no longer surfaces the upstream skew warning

This should also reduce the maintainer command surface instead of preserving a special-case bump path. `yzx dev update` should become the single intentional maintainer flow for updating Yazelix inputs, running canaries, and syncing follow-up artifacts. As part of centralizing that surface, `yzx dev update devenv` should be removed rather than retained as a blessed targeted alias, because it implies there is a separate devenv-specific sync concept beyond the authoritative lockfile. If a raw targeted upstream debug/update flow is still occasionally useful, it should stay an explicit manual repo-maintainer operation, not part of the supported Yazelix command surface.

Keep flake install, `yzx env`, `yzx refresh`, `yzx doctor`, version reporting, maintainer `yzx dev update`, and the local nixpkgs-style package coherent under that single-source contract. Do not solve this by merely updating `devenv.lock` to a quieter state or by only suppressing text without fixing the packaging seam.
acceptance_criteria: A freshly installed or updated Yazelix runtime no longer prints the upstream `devenv ... is newer than devenv input ... in devenv.lock` warning during `yzx env`. The runtime-owned `bin/devenv` in the flake/runtime/package outputs is demonstrably built from the same locked upstream source as the shipped `devenv.lock`, not merely from a matching semver. `yzx env`, `yzx refresh`, `yzx doctor`, flake install smoke, and the nixpkgs-style package smoke remain coherent under the new contract. `yzx dev update` remains the single intentional maintainer path for updating Yazelix inputs, and the targeted `yzx dev update devenv` surface is removed. The repo gains a Yazelix-owned regression check for CLI/lock source parity instead of relying on the upstream warning behavior.
notes:
Investigation details:
- `yazelix_runtime_package.nix` and `mk_yazelix_package.nix` currently wire runtime `devenv` from `pkgs.devenv`
- runtime entrypoints then prefer that runtime-local CLI via `devenv_cli.nu` / `environment_bootstrap.nu`
- maintainer updates currently run `devenv update` via `yzx dev update`, but the current `input_name?` surface still exposes a special `yzx dev update devenv` path that should be deleted if the lockfile becomes the one authority
- the currently locked upstream `cachix/devenv` tree has `Cargo.toml` version `2.0.7` but `src/modules/latest-version` still `2.0.6`
- upstream warning semantics therefore do not reliably express Yazelix's desired single-authority contract
close_reason: Closed as superseded by the v15 runtime trim: live runtime/package code no longer ships or prefers a runtime-local devenv binary, installed-runtime validation now defends that absence, and only historical/docs references remain.

Managed Nushell should source the generated bridge from Yazelix state so subcommand and flag completion return without re-importing the full command implementation or hand-maintaining a duplicate signature file.

title: Fill missing yzx subcommand help descriptions
description: Audit the yzx command surface and add explicit descriptions for user-facing subcommands that currently show blank help entries in Nushell help output. Treat the command surface as a product contract: help text should be present, concise, and useful for every supported subcommand instead of relying on users to guess from the name.
design: Treat this as command-surface contract cleanup, not help-text polishing for its own sake. Audit the real user-facing yzx subcommands that still show blank descriptions, fill them with concise task-level descriptions, and prefer one clear source of truth so help metadata does not drift from the command tree. Avoid verbose marketing copy and avoid testing help-text trivia unless a check defends the maintained command-metadata contract itself.
acceptance_criteria: 1. User-facing yzx subcommands no longer show blank built-in descriptions in Nushell help output. 2. Descriptions stay concise, specific, and aligned with the actual supported command behavior. 3. The implementation does not introduce a second hand-maintained command tree just to improve help text.
notes: Filled blank Nushell help descriptions for exported yzx commands by adding concise comments at the command definitions, not by introducing a second command tree. Added test_yzx_exported_commands_have_help_descriptions to query Nushell's live exported command metadata and fail on blank yzx descriptions. Updated the default-suite count budget and v15 post-trim matrix from 89 to 90 for this deliberate new contract check. Verification: no blank yzx descriptions, run_core_canonical_tests passed 17/17, full test_yzx_commands.nu passed 90/90, validate_default_test_count_budget.nu passed, validate_default_test_traceability.nu passed, validate_syntax.nu passed, and git diff --check passed.
close_reason: Completed: exported yzx commands now have concise help descriptions and the live metadata contract is tested.

title: Load zjstatus from a stable Yazelix plugin path
description: Stop generated Zellij layouts from loading zjstatus via a runtime store path. Copy the runtime-owned zjstatus.wasm into a stable Yazelix user-state plugin path and make generated layouts reference that stable plugin URL so runtime updates do not churn the plugin identity Zellij sees.
close_reason: Implemented stable zjstatus path and permission preservation

title: Preserve and seed zjstatus permission grants across updates
description: Extend the existing zellij permissions.kdl preservation logic to zjstatus so already-granted permissions migrate onto the current stable and tracked zjstatus paths. Keep required permissions explicit and do not mask genuine new-permission prompts.
close_reason: Implemented stable zjstatus path and permission preservation

title: Add Yazelix-managed per-shell user hook surfaces
description: Add explicit Yazelix-owned user hook files for managed shell startup instead of sourcing personal dotfiles in managed panes. Scope includes bash, zsh, fish, and nushell.
design: Preferred design: add optional per-shell hook files under ~/.config/yazelix/user_configs/shells/ (bash.sh, zsh.zsh, fish.fish, nu.nu) and source them at the end of each Yazelix-managed shell startup path. Managed panes and Yazelix env sessions stay self-contained by default; user hooks are an explicit extension surface inside Yazelix ownership, not personal dotfile sourcing. Do not auto-copy or auto-source ~/.bashrc, ~/.zshrc, ~/.config/fish/config.fish, or ~/.config/nushell/* for managed panes. Keep the order strict: runtime env, Yazelix initializers, managed shell config, then optional Yazelix user hook. Nushell should use a managed user hook file in the same spirit as the new managed pane wrapper, not the personal env.nu/config.nu path. Prefer one shared resolver/helper for shell hook file lookup rather than duplicated per-shell path logic.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Managed Yazelix shell startup for bash, zsh, fish, and nu can source an optional Yazelix-owned user hook file from ~/.config/yazelix/user_configs/shells/. 2. Managed panes do not source personal shell dotfiles by default. 3. The feature does not reintroduce the pane-latency problem by importing large personal shell setups implicitly. 4. Missing user hook files are a normal no-op, not an error. 5. Tests defend at least one generated/runtime path and one startup path for the new shell hook contract.
notes: UX reasoning: users still need a place for aliases/functions/prompt tweaks inside Yazelix, but personal shell startup is too broad, slow, and surprising for managed panes. The explicit Yazelix-owned hook surface preserves self-containment while giving advanced users a clear extension point.
close_reason: Completed: managed per-shell user hook surfaces now exist for bash, zsh, fish, and nushell; the remaining stub-scaffolding follow-up stays in yazelix-5yg1.1.

title: Investigate pane interactivity delay before prompt becomes usable
description: Imported GitHub issue #517. Investigate why opening a new pane can leave it unresponsive for a couple of seconds before Starship renders and typing works. Trace the exact pane-open path and identify whether the wait is in Zellij pane creation, shell bootstrap, devenv shell entry, prompt initialization, or another Yazelix-managed hook.
close_reason: Completed: pane interactivity delay fixed by trimming managed Nushell startup, routing new panes through the managed Yazelix Nushell wrapper, and defending the startup contracts with regressions.

title: Fix yzx update runtime output ordering
description: yzx update runtime currently prints captured stdout before stderr from nix run, which makes Nix's build/unpack lines appear after the install app says the runtime is already installed. Preserve the real event order or otherwise render the build phase clearly so the update flow does not look like it creates a second runtime after install already completed.
close_reason: Stream yzx update runtime output directly so build/install phases stay in order

title: Bump the Nushell version Yazelix uses
description: Upgrade the Nushell version Yazelix pins and ships, then update the affected install/runtime/bootstrap surfaces and verification so the newer Nushell version is consistently used across flake installs, runtime-owned tools, and maintainer flows.
design: Treat this as a managed-runtime version bump, not just a single pin edit. Update the authoritative Nushell source Yazelix ships and boots through, then verify the newer version is used consistently across the flake/runtime package, bootstrap wrappers, managed shell entrypoints, doctor/version reporting, and maintainer flows. The change should also make any real bootstrap floor or compatibility caveats explicit instead of leaving them implicit in scattered scripts.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The pinned/shipped Nushell version is intentionally bumped in the authoritative runtime source. 2. Flake installs, runtime-owned wrappers, and managed shell entrypoints all resolve to the new Nushell version coherently. 3. Doctor/version reporting and maintainer update flows remain accurate after the bump. 4. Any new bootstrap caveat or minimum-version expectation is documented explicitly instead of surfacing as a surprise failure.
notes:
2026-04-13 update: tried the Nushell bump path while refreshing runtime pins. Upstream Nushell latest is 0.112.1, published 2026-04-11 at https://github.com/nushell/nushell/releases/tag/0.112.1, but the locked nixos-unstable input now resolves pkgs.nushell.version to 0.111.0. Checked nixpkgs-unstable and nixpkgs master as well; they also still expose 0.111.0.

Implemented the prerequisite pin workflow instead: PINNED_NUSHELL_VERSION is now synced from the locked nixpkgs input alongside PINNED_NIX_VERSION, docs/specs describe that Nu is owned by Nixpkgs, and tests cover the maintainer sync behavior. Leave this bump deferred until Nixpkgs packages 0.112.x or Yazelix deliberately chooses a separate Nushell packaging owner. Do not add a bespoke overlay just to chase upstream Nu without an explicit product decision.
close_reason: Handled by locked-nixpkgs runtime pin workflow; reopen only when nixpkgs ships a newer Nushell that requires repo changes.

title: Add a first-run Helix import notice for managed Helix sessions
close_reason: Completed

title: Make Helix reveal integration self-contained in Yazelix
