Popup identity is no longer implicitly tied to hardcoded wrapper names alone. Regression coverage defends the single-instance managed popup behavior. After those steps, there is enough evidence to decide whether standalone extraction is justified.
notes:
2026-04-26 subsystem review: keep popup inside yazelix_pane_orchestrator for now. The code still depends on Yazelix runtime wrappers and sidebar-refresh hooks, but popup has real external value. New architecture bead yazelix-subsys.4 tracks the capability/API hardening path before any standalone repo or plugin extraction.
2026-04-26 plan-space review: remaining popup work is now tracked by yazelix-subsys.4.1 and yazelix-subsys.4.2. Do not treat the closed child beads as proof that standalone extraction is ready; they completed internal identity/argv/geometry groundwork only.
close_reason: Completed: child beads l968.1.1-l968.1.3 and subsys.4 coverage establish the internal popup identity, argv/cwd/geometry adapter contract, and single-instance regression evidence; no standalone extraction is claimed.

title: Evaluate whether Television belongs in Yazelix
description: Evaluate whether the Television fuzzy finder (tv) adds enough value to Yazelix to justify packaging, integration, or documentation. The question is not whether Television is interesting in general, but whether it improves Yazelix workflows enough to deserve a maintained place in the product.
design: Television is a fast terminal fuzzy finder with channels, shell integration, and editor integrations. Judge it against Yazelix's actual workflow surfaces: Yazi, Helix, popup flows, shell search, and any future picker surfaces. Determine whether Television would be redundant with existing tools, complementary as a generic fuzzy-search surface, or valuable only as an optional pack tool. Prefer a concrete go, no-go, or optional-integration decision over vague enthusiasm for another terminal tool.
acceptance_criteria: There is an explicit decision on whether Television should be ignored, documented, added to a pack, or integrated more deeply into Yazelix. The bead records which user workflows it would improve, what it duplicates, and what the lightest worthwhile adoption shape would be if it is kept.
notes:
Investigation decision, 2026-04-28:
- Reviewed upstream alexpasmantier/television as of 2026-04-28: Television (`tv`) is active, fast, and channel-oriented, with current visible release 0.15.6 from 2026-04-15, shell integration, editor integrations, and Nix availability via `nix run nixpkgs#television`. Source: https://github.com/alexpasmantier/television
- Yazelix already has focused picker surfaces: Yazi for file-tree workflows, Helix/Neovim for editor pickers, zoxide/fzf for workspace jumps, `yzx menu` for commands, and `yzx warp` for project switching into a new tab. Adding Television as default would duplicate fzf/zoxide/Yazi before it owns a clear Yazelix workflow.
- Product fit: Television is interesting as an optional power-user picker or future backend for a generalized picker abstraction, especially channels like files, text, and git-repos. It does not currently justify default packaging, deeper integration, or docs emphasis.
- Decision: no default dependency and no first-party integration now. Revisit only when Yazelix defines a picker abstraction or a package surface where `tv` replaces a concrete existing path better than fzf/zoxide/Yazi. Lightest worthwhile future shape would be opt-in package/docs, not runtime coupling.
- No code change needed for this bead.
close_reason: Recorded no-adopt decision for Television

title: Define Yazelix Nushell tooling strategy
description: Umbrella for deciding how Yazelix should package and govern Nushell-specific maintainer tooling. Scope includes a dedicated Nushell pack, nufmt formatting policy, and nu-lint lint policy. Keep tool availability separate from enforcement: shipping tools in an opt-in pack must not by itself decide canonical formatter status, maintainer workflows, or CI gates.
design: Reason from first principles: Yazelix needs a coherent Nushell-tooling story, not an ad hoc accumulation of commands. There are three separate questions that should not be collapsed into one: 1. what Nushell-specific tools belong in a first-party opt-in pack; 2. whether nufmt becomes the canonical formatter and what rollout it should have; and 3. whether nu-lint is repo-compatible enough to justify any maintained command surface. The parent bead should stay an umbrella for those planning decisions rather than directly implementing a single tool addition.
acceptance_criteria: The subtree under this bead cleanly separates pack composition, formatting policy, and lint policy; each child bead has an explicit boundary and success criteria; and maintainers can make progress on one area without accidentally forcing the others. The parent can close once the dedicated pack direction and the nufmt and nu-lint policy decisions are each resolved.
close_reason: Completed: all three strategy decisions resolved — Nushell pack shipped, nufmt rejected, nu-lint adopted as maintainer command.

title: Evaluate whether Yazelix should drop Foot support because it is not on macOS
description: Evaluate whether Yazelix should stop treating Foot as a meaningful supported terminal option because it is not available on macOS, unlike the other first-class terminal candidates under consideration.
design: Reason from the actual support matrix and product goals rather than from terminal popularity alone. Compare Foot against the terminals Yazelix most plausibly wants to support across platforms, especially macOS-relevant options like WezTerm and Alacritty. The key decision is whether Foot still earns documentation, config generation, testing effort, and maintainer attention despite being Linux-only. If Foot remains supported, the bead should justify that cost explicitly. If not, it should define the de-support path cleanly.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear decision to keep, demote, or drop Foot support. The decision records the platform and maintenance tradeoffs, how Foot compares to WezTerm and Alacritty for Yazelix's goals, and what documentation, config, or support-matrix changes would follow from the chosen direction.
close_reason: Closed on GitHub

title: Evaluate whether sesh adds meaningful value to Yazelix session workflows
description: Evaluate what sesh would actually add to Yazelix compared with the current Zellij and workspace flow, and decide whether it is worth integrating, documenting, or ignoring.
design: Compare sesh against the problems Yazelix already has or may soon have around session discovery, startup, switching, and saved workflows. Do not evaluate it as an abstract cool tool. The question is whether sesh solves a real user problem better than current Yazelix or Zellij surfaces, and if so whether integration should be first-party, optional, or purely documented.
acceptance_criteria: There is a go, no-go, or optional-integration decision on sesh, grounded in concrete session-workflow use cases. The bead records what sesh does better, what it duplicates, and what integration shape would make sense if it is worth adopting.
notes:
Investigation result, 2026-04-28:
- Decision: no first-party sesh integration for now. Sesh is useful, but it is a tmux-centered session manager and project switcher; its strongest current value is tmux session listing/creation, zoxide-backed project switching, configured session startup commands, and fzf-style switching.
- What sesh does better: polished fuzzy session/project selection for tmux users, configured startup commands/windows, and root/session helpers for tmux workflows.
- What it duplicates in Yazelix: zoxide project selection, multiplexer session switching, project-root entry, and startup command orchestration. Yazelix already has Zellij as the workspace layer, per-window config snapshots, pane-orchestrator tab-local state, and a better native next step in yzx warp.
- Integration shape if revisited: optional docs/inspiration only, or an adapter after Yazelix has explicit session save/restore. Do not add sesh to the runtime or make it a dependency before it solves a Zellij-native problem better than Zellij/Yazelix.
- Source checked: https://github.com/joshmedeski/sesh README describes sesh as a CLI for creating/managing tmux sessions quickly using zoxide.
close_reason: Completed evaluation. Decision: no first-party sesh dependency/integration now; revisit only as optional docs or adapter after explicit Yazelix resurrection exists.

title: evaluate splitting some yazelix builting zellij plugins to standalone plugins
description: Imported GitHub issue #485.
close_reason: Superseded by child issues yazelix-l968.1 / #503 and yazelix-l968.2 / #504

title: clone yazi zellij and helix near yazelix so it's easier for AI to read their code
description: Imported GitHub issue #486.
close_reason: Imported closed GitHub issue #486.

title: Use a floating Yazi picker from Helix when the sidebar is disabled
description: Narrower slice of the broader floating Yazi picker feature: provide a picker flow that remains useful when the normal sidebar is disabled, likely using a Zellij floating pane instead of depending on the persistent side surface.
design: Keep this aligned with the broader floating-picker direction instead of creating a second unrelated Helix-Yazi picker implementation. The key question is how the picker should behave when the file-tree sidebar is unavailable: invocation path, floating-pane UX, selected-path return, and cancellation behavior. This should remain compatible with a future general floating picker from Helix.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear design or implementation path for using a floating Yazi picker from Helix when the sidebar is disabled, and it remains aligned with the broader picker feature rather than diverging into a duplicate flow.
close_reason: Closed on GitHub

title: Clarify sidebar vs file tree terminology
description: Clarify Yazelix terminology so `sidebar` remains the generic side-surface term while `file tree` is used for the current Yazi-specific surface where that wording is clearer.
design: Do not perform a literal repo-wide rename from `sidebar` to `filetree`. The current codebase uses `sidebar` as a pane role and workspace contract term, and that should remain the generic abstraction because future side surfaces may not be file trees. The right change is terminology cleanup: preserve `sidebar` for the architectural slot and command surface, while using `file tree` or `Yazi file tree` in user-facing docs or copy when specifically referring to the current Yazi-backed side surface.
acceptance_criteria: The issue is reframed as terminology clarification rather than a mass rename. User-facing wording is clearer about when Yazelix means the generic sidebar surface versus the current Yazi file tree. Internal architectural and plugin-contract language is not renamed in a way that would block future non-Yazi sidebar surfaces.
close_reason: Completed: clarified user-facing wording so sidebar remains the generic managed side-surface term while the current default Yazi-backed surface is described as the Yazi file tree/file-tree sidebar; internal contract names were preserved.

title: Evaluate whether yzx cwd should be removed, narrowed, or kept
description: Evaluate whether the yzx cwd command still deserves to exist as a first-class command surface, and if so what narrower contract it should have.
design: Reason from user-facing intent rather than implementation habit. Determine what real job yzx cwd is doing today: tab naming, workspace retargeting, cwd synchronization, or a compatibility shim for older flows. Then compare that against overlapping surfaces and recent bugs. If the command is confusing or too easy to misuse, prefer narrowing or deleting it with a clear migration story rather than preserving it out of inertia.
acceptance_criteria: There is an explicit decision to keep, narrow, rename, or remove yzx cwd. The bead records the user-facing contract if it survives, or the replacement and migration story if it does not. Any decision accounts for overlaps with newer workspace and tab-cwd flows.
close_reason: Completed: keep yzx cwd as the explicit current-tab workspace-retarget command. The narrowed contract is not "generic navigation" or "open a new project tab"; it is tab-local workspace-root mutation plus managed editor/sidebar sync. Docs and command descriptions were tightened around that contract, while a future warp-style new-tab flow remains a separate feature idea rather than a replacement.

title: Keep Zellij pane cwd aligned with restart directory
description: Imported GitHub issue #500. Restart currently boots the sidebar from the requested directory but was rewriting Zellij's own default cwd to HOME, so native new panes ignored the restart directory.
close_reason: Closed on GitHub

title: Revisit override-layout via owned explicit-run pane creation
description: Capture the future path for returning to Zellij override-layout transitions after the current rollback to layout-family swapping. The experiment showed that override_layout only becomes viable if Yazelix owns the relevant pane-creation surfaces and opens panes with stable explicit run instructions instead of default-shell panes that Zellij normalizes to invoked_with = None. Scope this as design + implementation exploration, not an immediate bugfix. Evaluate which pane creation surfaces Yazelix can realistically own (Alt+m, pane-mode creation, split flows), whether built-in Zellij Run/NewTiledPane/NewStackedPane actions are enough, and what exact contract would let Alt y / Alt [ ] preserve live panes under override_layout without duplicating panes or sidebar state.
design: Treat this as the current explicit-run path back toward override-layout after the earlier rollback. The core question is whether Yazelix can own enough pane-creation surfaces to preserve live panes under override_layout by using stable explicit run instructions instead of anonymous shell panes. This should remain a design and contract bead first, not a promise that override-layout is immediately ready to ship again.
acceptance_criteria: There is a concrete contract for what Yazelix would need to own in order to make override-layout viable again, including pane creation surfaces, run-instruction identity, and layout-transition invariants. If the path is still too costly or constrained, that is captured explicitly instead of leaving override-layout in limbo.

title: Reduce config-surface duplication between Nushell and Nix
description: Reduce unnecessary duplication between the Nushell and Nix config surfaces after the canonical config and launch-profile contract is defined. The goal is not total collapse into one file, but fewer parallel sources of truth and fewer places where the same runtime choice must be expressed twice.
design: This work should follow the canonical config-surface contract rather than guess its own target. First identify which values genuinely need dual representation across Nushell and Nix and which ones are accidental duplication. Then delete redundant surfaces before optimizing the survivors. Prefer one clear source-of-truth plus explicit derivation or validation over two independently maintained copies of the same intent.
acceptance_criteria: There is a concrete duplication-reduction plan grounded in the canonical config and launch-profile contract. The bead records which duplicated fields should be unified, which must remain mirrored with validation, and which existing duplication is acceptable. Follow-up implementation work is narrower than this planning bead.
close_reason: Completed: documented the config metadata centralization plan and classified which duplication should be unified, validated, or kept.

title: Add validator for config surface and launch-profile parity
description: Add a validator that enforces the canonical config-surface and launch-profile contract once that contract is defined, so drift is caught mechanically instead of by maintainer memory.
design: This bead should not invent the contract; it should defend the canonical config-surface and launch-profile ownership model from yazelix-b16x together with the machine-readable main-config contract introduced by yazelix-0oj1.1. Keep the validator high-signal: check the maintained parity and ownership rules that actually matter, and avoid noisy pseudo-invariants that mainly create churn. The right validator may be structural, config-based, or generation-based depending on what the canonical contract ultimately exposes.
acceptance_criteria: There is a validator for the canonical config-surface and launch-profile contract, and it checks the maintained source-of-truth invariants that the contract explicitly requires. The validator is justified by the contract rather than by general anxiety about drift.
close_reason: Completed: added config surface and launch-profile contract validator with CI and pre-commit integration

title: Specify canonical config surface and launch-profile contract
description: Define the canonical config surfaces and launch-profile contract for Yazelix so downstream work can stop guessing which configuration path owns what behavior and how launch entrypoints are supposed to compose.
design: This is a high-leverage contract bead. Clarify the relationship between direct config, Home Manager, generated runtime state, launch profiles, and any environment-specific entrypoints. The goal is not just documentation; it is a stable contract that downstream beads can depend on when reducing duplication or adding validators. Prefer sharp ownership rules and explicit derivation paths over fuzzy precedence.
acceptance_criteria: There is a documented canonical contract for config surfaces and launch profiles that downstream work can reference. The contract states what each surface owns, how launch profiles compose with configuration, and where parity or validation is required. Dependent beads such as duplication reduction and parity validation can proceed without redefining the model.
close_reason: Completed: added the canonical config-surface and launch-profile contract spec and linked it from the architecture map.

title: add yzx import zellij/yazi to import native configs into yazelix-managed overrides
description: Add a short import flow that brings native Zellij and Yazi config files under Yazelix management instead of making users manually copy files into the managed override paths.
design:
Keep the command surface short and product-shaped: use `yzx import zellij` and `yzx import yazi` instead of a generic `yzx cp` command or a 4-word `yzx config import ...` flow. Treat this as an adoption/import path, not a reset path: the source is the native config location and the destination is Yazelix's managed override location.

For Zellij, import only `~/.config/zellij/config.kdl` into `~/.config/yazelix/user_configs/zellij/config.kdl`. For Yazi, import only the override files Yazelix already has a clear contract for: `yazi.toml`, `keymap.toml`, and `init.lua` from `~/.config/yazi/` into `~/.config/yazelix/user_configs/yazi/`. Do not try to import plugin directories, flavors, caches, or other broader state in v1.

Fail fast when the managed destination already exists so users do not silently clobber their Yazelix-managed files. Allow an explicit `--force` path, but back up any overwritten managed file first and keep the messaging clear about what was imported, skipped, backed up, or overwritten.
acceptance_criteria: `yzx import zellij` imports `~/.config/zellij/config.kdl` into `~/.config/yazelix/user_configs/zellij/config.kdl`. `yzx import yazi` imports `yazi.toml`, `keymap.toml`, and `init.lua` from `~/.config/yazi/` into `~/.config/yazelix/user_configs/yazi/`. Missing native source files fail clearly instead of silently succeeding. Existing managed destination files are not overwritten unless `--force` is passed, and the `--force` path writes backups before replacing managed files. The docs point users to the import commands as the supported way to adopt native Zellij or Yazi config into Yazelix-managed overrides. Add focused command-level regression coverage for the happy path and overwrite protection.
close_reason: Completed

design: Treat this as a product and maintenance question, not just a flashy website widget. Clarify the value to users and contributors, the hosting/update burden, and whether the public project graph should be live, periodically generated, or linked out to an existing viewer. Prefer the simplest surface that still gives meaningful roadmap visibility.

If adopted, the bead leaves behind a concrete delivery shape such as embed, generated page, or external link. If rejected, it records why the maintenance cost or product value is not good enough.

title: Investigate how to make session resurrection excellent
description: Investigate what a genuinely strong resurrection story should look like for Yazelix sessions, including Zellij session restore, workspace cwd/state recovery, and any plugin-managed pane context that users would expect to survive restarts.
design: Do not reduce resurrection to merely reopening a Zellij session. Identify what users actually mean by a great restore experience: tabs, cwd, managed panes, sidebar/file-tree state, popup state, editor context, and whether resurrection should be explicit or automatic. Then determine which pieces are already available from Zellij, which belong in Yazelix, and which are too fragile to promise.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear target definition for great resurrection in Yazelix, with supported versus unsupported restored state called out explicitly. The bead leaves behind narrower follow-up beads for the gaps that matter instead of keeping resurrection as a vague aspiration.
notes:
Investigation result, 2026-04-28:
- Great resurrection in Yazelix should be explicit and session-scoped, not automatic by default. Users can keep many Yazelix windows open, so invisible auto-attach/resurrect behavior risks crossing window boundaries.
- Zellij should own low-level process/tab/pane serialization and explicit save/restore primitives: session_serialization, save-session, session-manager listings, and restored commands/cwds.
- Yazelix should own the coherence layer around restored sessions: versioned per-window config snapshot identity, runtime version, tab-local workspace root/source, managed editor/sidebar pane identity, sidebar Yazi identity/cwd when live, layout/sidebar state, and clear diagnostics when restored state is stale or incomplete.
- Supported target state: Zellij tabs/panes/cwds/running commands when Zellij can resurrect them; Yazelix tab workspace root/source; managed pane identity after pane-orchestrator observation; runtime/config snapshot diagnostics; user-triggered explicit save/restore surface.
- Unsupported by default: resurrecting transient popup/menu panes, stale floating panes, editor-internal unsaved buffer state beyond the editor/session tool, and implicit adoption of unrelated existing Zellij sessions.
- Current code already has the important in-session seam: get_active_tab_session_state plus yzx dev inspect_session. The missing piece is a product contract and UX around explicit session save/restore and how restored pane-orchestrator state is rehydrated or diagnosed.
- Created follow-up beads for the remaining narrower work.
close_reason: Completed investigation. Defined explicit, session-scoped resurrection target and split remaining work into yazelix-p7qs and yazelix-l38n.

title: Auto-apply safe config migrations at entrypoints while keeping yzx config migrate
description:
Imported GitHub issue #478.

Yazelix should automatically apply deterministic config migrations when the user enters the product through the normal interactive entrypoints, instead of forcing them to manually run `yzx config migrate --apply` for every stale-but-safe case. This should make startup, launch, restart, and interactive `yzx env` feel self-healing for safe rewrites while preserving explicit control and clear diagnostics for ambiguous cases.

Do not remove `yzx config migrate`. It should remain the canonical preview/apply/debug surface for migrations. The change here is to make the common entrypoints invoke the same safe migration engine automatically when that is clearly safe.
design:
Reason from first principles:
- Deterministic rewrites with backups are product-maintenance work, not user work, so Yazelix should usually handle them automatically when the user is trying to launch or enter the environment.
- Read paths must stay read-only. Do not hide file mutations inside `parse_yazelix_config` or other helpers used by widgets, config generation, or general state inspection. Ordinary reads should not rewrite user config.
- Keep the existing split between safe `auto` migrations and `manual_only` migrations. Automatic entrypoint behavior must only apply the `auto` subset and must leave manual-only items explicit.

Scope:
- Add a migration preflight for interactive entrypoints such as `yzx launch`, `yzx restart`, normal Yazelix startup, and interactive `yzx env`.
- If the migration plan has only deterministic auto rewrites, apply them automatically with backups, then continue into the normal entrypoint flow and print a short high-signal notice.
- If the plan has both auto and manual-only items, apply only the auto rewrites, then continue while printing the remaining manual follow-up clearly.
- If the situation is ambiguous or ownership-sensitive, keep failing fast with the current actionable diagnostics instead of guessing.
- In non-interactive or read-only contexts, do not mutate automatically.

Non-goals:
- Do not remove `yzx config migrate`.
- Do not make `parse_yazelix_config` mutate files.
- Do not auto-apply `manual_only` migrations.
- Do not silently resolve duplicate or conflicting ownership cases that still require user intent.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Interactive entrypoints (`yzx launch`, `yzx restart`, normal Yazelix startup, and interactive `yzx env`) run a migration preflight before normal config consumption.
2. When the migration plan contains only `auto` changes, Yazelix applies them automatically with backup and continues into the entrypoint flow without requiring the user to run `yzx config migrate --apply` manually.
3. When the plan contains both `auto` and `manual_only` items, Yazelix applies only the `auto` subset, surfaces the remaining manual items clearly, and still fails fast when those manual-only items require user intent before startup can continue.
4. When the config state is ambiguous or ownership-sensitive, Yazelix still fails fast with clear guidance instead of auto-mutating.
5. Non-interactive/read-only paths such as widget rendering, general config parsing helpers, and other routine readers do not mutate config files.
6. `yzx config migrate` remains available as the explicit preview/apply/debug surface and still reports the same plan semantics.
7. Add focused regression coverage for: auto-apply on at least one interactive entrypoint, mixed auto/manual plans, non-interactive no-mutation behavior, and continued fail-fast handling for ambiguous ownership conflicts.
close_reason: Completed: interactive entrypoints now auto-apply deterministic config migrations with backups, still fail fast on remaining manual-only follow-up, and keep parse/read paths non-mutating.

title: Evaluate Pretext-inspired text-first welcome screen
description: Use the Pretext site as inspiration for another strong Yazelix welcome-screen direction, focused on text-first composition rather than only boxed logo cards.
design:
Use the Pretext site as inspiration for a more text-forward Yazelix welcome treatment, but test the palette and copy hierarchy step by step instead of composing multiple experimental color systems at once.

Color findings from the shipped logo asset (assets/logo.png): the brand palette is red, green, yellow, blue, and purple. In practice, purple and red should stay out of readable body copy; they work better as wordmark or border accents. The next controlled trials should compare:
- Brand Calm: blue body base with yellow highlights and green only for special emphasis like SSH
- Matrix Clean: green body base with restrained yellow highlights

Preferred first trial: Matrix Clean, because it fits the terminal-native identity and the user explicitly likes that direction. Keep the wordmark multicolor, keep the border single-color, and keep the body text color system simple: one base color plus one accent color. Avoid alternating full-line palettes and avoid more than one or two highlighted terms per line.

This bead is specifically about the text card composition and palette, not about adding another animation engine.
acceptance_criteria: There is a documented, repo-backed color direction for the text-first welcome card that is derived from the shipped logo rather than ad hoc terminal guesses. At least one restrained palette variant is tested end to end on the real welcome card, with width-fit checks, readability checks on the actual background, and a clear keep/change decision before moving on to more experimentation. The implementation should preserve the existing welcome_style system and should not add extra color complexity unless a previous simpler variant has been evaluated and rejected.

title: Add Mandelbrot welcome style
design: Add a Mandelbrot-inspired welcome style using precomputed frames or other very cheap deterministic playback rather than expensive live fractal rendering. The result should feel rich without turning startup into a renderer benchmark.
acceptance_criteria: Selecting welcome_style = mandelbrot renders the Mandelbrot sequence or frame set within the startup budget, preserves readability in narrow layouts, and is defended by tests for asset/frame selection and final-frame fit.
notes:
2026-04-20 tracker hygiene: moved out of in_progress. This welcome-screen child is blocked and there is no active Mandelbrot implementation work in the current tree, so it should not stay claimed until the visual/asset lane is resumed.
2026-04-26 subsystem review: Mandelbrot should wait for the yazelix_screen extension seam so it shares frame playback, fit, and startup-budget checks with other animations.
close_reason: Completed: Mandelbrot is now a live welcome and yzx screen style, wired through yazelix_default.toml, Home Manager metadata, config contract metadata, front-door Rust style resolution, docs, and the welcome-screen canonical contract. The stricter Mandelbrot renderer and screen tests are already landed.

title: Add boids welcome style
design: Add a boids-inspired welcome style that stays cheap enough for startup, runs for a short bounded time, and resolves into a readable final frame. The implementation may be deterministic lightweight live rendering or pre-rendered frame playback, whichever better preserves the startup budget and layout fit.
acceptance_criteria: Selecting welcome_style = boids renders the boids sequence within the startup budget, preserves readability in narrow layouts, and is defended by tests for bounded frame generation and layout fit.
close_reason: Completed

title: Add Game of Life welcome style
design: Add a Game of Life welcome style seeded or shaped so it feels intentional rather than like a generic programmer screensaver. The motion should be short, bounded, and end in a readable final frame. The implementation may be deterministic live generation or pre-rendered playback, whichever best fits startup and width constraints.
acceptance_criteria: Selecting welcome_style = life renders the Life sequence within the startup budget, preserves narrow-layout readability, and is defended by tests for deterministic generation, frame bounds, and final-frame fit.

title: Replace ascii_art_mode with welcome_style selector
design: Use one welcome_style setting instead of separate mode/style knobs. The allowed values should be static, logo, boids, life, mandelbrot, and random. Random must choose only from the animated styles and must never select static. This bead should own the config/schema/default/Home Manager migration from ascii_art_mode to welcome_style, plus the shared style-selection helper used by the welcome renderer. The renderer contract may support either lightweight live frame generation or pre-rendered frame playback behind the same style surface.
acceptance_criteria: Yazelix has one supported welcome_style config surface with values static, logo, boids, life, mandelbrot, and random. Random never resolves to static. Existing config paths and Home Manager are migrated cleanly, and tests defend schema parsing, default generation, Home Manager emission, and the random-pool contract.

It should adapt to narrow, medium, and wide layouts and define the overall visual language the other styles should respect. The implementation may use lightweight live frames or pre-rendered playback, but it must land on a clear final branded frame.
acceptance_criteria: Selecting welcome_style = logo produces a branded welcome sequence that fits narrow layouts, ends in a readable resting frame, and is defended by width-aware layout tests plus startup render checks.

title: Investigate repo-root terminal_overrides regeneration
description: Investigate and fix why an untracked terminal_overrides/ directory is being recreated in the repository root during terminal config generation instead of staying inside the supported Yazelix config or runtime paths.
design: Treat this as a real path-resolution bug, not just local clutter. Identify the exact writer and the exact path-resolution mistake that creates repo-root terminal_overrides/. Then tighten the contract so override scaffolds and generated files can only land in supported locations such as ~/.config/yazelix/terminal_overrides or the runtime share tree. Avoid one-off local cleanup hacks; fix the path derivation at the source and document the intended destination. If tests or dev commands are responsible, make the fixture or runtime root explicit so they cannot accidentally write into the repository.
acceptance_criteria: No command or test path used by Yazelix recreates terminal_overrides/ in the repository root. The real source of the bug is identified and fixed, and the intended destination for override files is documented. Add focused unit or helper tests for the path-resolution function that was wrong. Add an end-to-end regression with detailed logging that runs the reproducing generation flow from the repo root and proves that only the supported config or runtime locations are written.
notes: Already fixed in current generated-config behavior. Repo inspection finds no terminal_overrides writer or directory in the repo root; the remaining references are focused tests. test_generate_all_terminal_configs_keeps_terminal_overrides_opt_in asserts generation does not scaffold user override directories, and test_terminal_override_imports_ignore_yazelix_dir_runtime_root proves terminal override imports stay under HOME/.config/yazelix/user_configs/terminal even when YAZELIX_DIR points at a runtime root. Full default yzx suite passed 90/90 after the current cleanup pass.
close_reason: Already fixed and defended by generated terminal config tests.

title: Add controlled terminal override layers instead of full config ownership modes
description: Replace the coarse terminal.config_mode ownership question with a cleaner terminal-extension contract. Yazelix should keep owning launch and integration-critical terminal behavior, while users get Yazelix-specific per-terminal override files for harmless customization such as theme, fonts, opacity, padding, and similar preferences. Start with ghostty, kitty, and alacritty. Avoid reading the normal terminal config by default. This should simplify the product boundary rather than adding a generic merge engine.
close_reason: Completed: shipped controlled terminal override layers for Ghostty, Kitty, and Alacritty, kept launch-critical behavior under Yazelix ownership, and documented the contract.

title: Investigate touchpad three-finger gestures for Zellij pane navigation
description: Investigate whether Yazelix can support touchpad three-finger gestures or similar gesture-driven input to move between Zellij panes without compromising portability or making the product depend on one desktop stack.
design: Treat this as an input-stack feasibility question spanning terminal, OS gesture system, desktop environment, and Zellij. First determine what signal, if any, can reach Yazelix reliably on the supported platforms. If gesture support requires desktop-specific helpers or fragile remapping layers, capture that explicitly instead of pretending it is a clean cross-platform feature.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear feasibility decision on touchpad gesture support for pane navigation, including which platforms or terminal stacks could support it and what extra components would be required. If the idea is viable, leave behind a narrower implementation bead with explicit platform scope.

title: Remove the need to clone the Yazelix repo under ~/.config/yazelix
description: Eliminate any remaining requirement for end users to keep a full Yazelix git clone under ~/.config/yazelix in order to run the product normally.
design: Treat this as a runtime-boundary and packaging-quality issue. First identify which paths still assume a live repo checkout instead of a packaged or generated runtime layout. Then decide whether the correct fix is packaging changes, relocated runtime assets, generated config changes, or documentation cleanup. Avoid local-host workarounds that keep the clone requirement alive implicitly.
acceptance_criteria: Normal Yazelix usage no longer requires a git clone living under ~/.config/yazelix. Any remaining maintainer-only clone assumptions are explicit and documented. The final behavior works through the supported packaged or relocated runtime paths, not only from a source checkout.
close_reason: Runtime entrypoints and tracked terminal snapshots no longer require a repo clone under ~/.config/yazelix

title: investigate strong flicker when opening a new tab
description:
Imported GitHub issue #468.

### Details

investigate strong flicker when opening a new tab
notes:
Resolved on 2026-03-29:
- Root cause was plugin-driven pane-frame toggling in zjstatus, not tab creation itself.
- Yazelix already enforced `pane_frames false` in the merged Zellij config, while the zjstatus tab template also enabled `hide_frame_for_single_pane "true"`.
- On Zellij 0.44.0 this interaction caused strong flicker/glitching when opening a new tab.
- Fix: remove `hide_frame_for_single_pane` from the managed zjstatus template and from the sweep-test layout fixture so frame ownership stays with the merged Zellij config.
- Verified locally by opening a new tab after the change and by rerunning focused merged-Zellij generation tests.
close_reason: Completed: removed zjstatus frame toggling from the managed template after confirming it was the root cause of the new-tab flicker on Zellij 0.44.0.

title: Ghostty trail glow "none" still renders extreme glow
description:
Imported GitHub issue #466.

### Details

When glow is set to `none`, Yazelix still renders a very large spatial glow instead of disabling the effect.
close_reason: Completed: fixed Ghostty cursor glow mask math and normalized trail glow behavior so none/low/medium/high behave correctly across variants.

title: yzx onboard
description: Guide first-time users through creating the supported Yazelix config set instead of making them hand-edit configuration from scratch. Keep yazelix.toml as the front door, but allow onboarding to emit optional sidecars such as yazelix_packs.toml when that is the supported architecture.
design: Treat yzx onboard as an interactive first-run config generator, not as a generic tutorial surface. The command should ask the user a focused set of high-value questions and then write a sensible configuration outcome based on the answers. The interaction model should feel like a lightweight terminal questionnaire: arrow-key navigation for single-choice prompts, and explicit multi-select prompts where space toggles choices and enter confirms. Keep the scope tight around the settings that matter most for getting productive quickly rather than exposing every config knob up front. Question areas should likely include terminal preference, shell choice, editor choice, whether the sidebar is enabled, whether sessions are persistent, and a small set of useful packs or defaults. The output should stay aligned with the supported config architecture rather than hardcoding everything into yazelix.toml forever; if pack config lives in yazelix_packs.toml, onboarding should generate that file instead of collapsing pack answers back into the main file.
acceptance_criteria: `yzx onboard` can guide a first-time user through creating a valid supported Yazelix configuration with a small number of high-signal prompts. Single-choice prompts support arrow-key selection, and multi-select prompts support toggling choices before confirmation. The generated output is valid, readable, and aligned with the same supported options as the normal config path, including optional sidecars where the architecture calls for them. Add behavior tests for prompt flow and generated-config output, including at least one multi-select case, one interrupted-or-aborted flow, one case that writes the main yazelix.toml only, and one case that writes a pack sidecar when pack answers require it.
notes: 2026-04-26 scope correction during implementation: current trimmed Yazelix does not have a supported yazelix_packs.toml sidecar or pack graph, so yzx onboard must not recreate one. Use current supported main-config fields only; multi-select coverage should target current config such as zellij.widget_tray.
close_reason: Completed: added Rust-owned yzx onboard with interactive single/multi-select prompt state, current main-config generation, overwrite guard, docs, command metadata, and focused tests. Scope intentionally excludes removed packs sidecar.

title: Backfill upgrade notes for historical point releases
description: Retroactively add structured upgrade-note entries and concise changelog coverage for the historical point releases that shipped after Yazelix started doing point releases, so historical exact-version whats_new lookups can work from real note data instead of commit archaeology.
design: Build this on top of the existing upgrade-notes contract instead of inventing a second historical notes format. Prefer exact version entries in docs/upgrade_notes.toml for every supported historical point release, with matching concise sections in CHANGELOG.md. Keep the notes honest: if a release had no migration impact, say so; if migration or manual action cannot be reconstructed confidently, record the uncertainty conservatively rather than guessing. Define the practical historical floor for supported whats_new lookups so the CLI can fail clearly outside that window instead of pretending the archive is complete.
acceptance_criteria: docs/upgrade_notes.toml and CHANGELOG.md contain exact, validator-clean entries for the supported historical point releases in scope, with real dates, short headlines, and accurate upgrade-impact metadata. The historical entries are sufficient for a future exact-version yzx whats_new flow to render notes without scraping git history at runtime. Add focused verification for note integrity and at least one fixture-based or end-to-end check that historical exact-version lookup data can be loaded cleanly once version selection lands.
notes:
Planning refinements:
- Scope this to the era after Yazelix started shipping point releases; do not invent unsupported older history.
- Prefer reconstructing from real tags, commit ranges, release docs, and existing history pages.
- Keep the result narrow and high-signal: concise upgrade-facing notes, not full release essays.
- This bead should pair naturally with a future yzx whats_new <version> enhancement but should still be useful on its own as historical upgrade-note backfill.
close_reason: Completed: backfilled exact upgrade-note entries for the supported v12/v13 point-release floor, repaired the v13.7 structured-note baseline, generalized the upgrade-summary loader to accept explicit versions internally, and added default-suite plus detailed e2e verification for historical exact-version note loading.

title: Show and reopen a first-run upgrade summary after Yazelix version changes
description: Persist the last seen Yazelix version and, on the first relevant interactive run after an upgrade, show a concise summary of what changed, what might break, and what action the user should take. Users must also have a way to reopen that summary later instead of losing it after the first display.
design: Build this on top of the structured upgrade-note contract and shared migration registry rather than inventing another freeform message path. The summary should trigger once per real version change in the right interactive surfaces, stay quiet on repeated launches or non interactive command flows, and highlight breaking changes or migration actions without turning startup into noisy release marketing. Prefer a dedicated reopen command such as yzx whats_new so users can intentionally revisit the current version summary without dumping the full changelog. If the current config is affected by known migrations, the summary should point to the exact docs or command surfaces that help, without attempting hidden rewrites.
acceptance_criteria: After simulating a version bump, the first relevant interactive run shows a concise upgrade summary that is keyed to the actual new version, includes any breaking or migration notes, and does not repeat on the second run for the same version. Users can reopen the same summary on demand through a supported command surface. The feature ignores non interactive flows that should stay quiet, and it does not claim notes for versions that are not represented in the release-note contract. Add comprehensive unit tests for version-state persistence, note selection, and suppression logic, plus end to end scripts with detailed logging that simulate previous and current version state, capture the first run and second run outputs, and verify the manual reopen path.
notes:
Planning refinements:
- Create a focused spec such as docs/specs/first_run_upgrade_summary.md.
- Define the state boundary explicitly: store last-seen version in a Yazelix-managed state location, separate from the user config file.
- Trigger only in relevant interactive entrypoints; keep env-only or scripted flows quiet unless the user explicitly asks for notes.
- Prefer yzx whats_new as the reopen surface, with behavior scoped to the current installed version rather than the whole historical changelog.
- Verification plan: unit coverage for state persistence and suppression logic, plus end to end Nushell scripts that simulate first run, second run, and manual reopen while logging version state files, selected note data, and rendered output.
close_reason: Completed: added shared upgrade-summary state and rendering, a new yzx whats_new reopen command, interactive first-run upgrade summaries, and spec-backed unit/e2e coverage for suppression and migration-aware guidance.

title: Add a root CHANGELOG.md and a structured upgrade-notes contract
description: Add the user facing upgrade-notes surface for Yazelix: a concise root CHANGELOG.md that highlights user visible changes, breaking config changes, and migration notes, with a clean relationship to the deeper docs history. If the first-run summary or diagnostics need structured note data, define that contract here instead of relying on brittle markdown scraping.
design: Treat this as a product contract, not just a markdown file. The system needs one short human facing changelog surface and, if required by the upgrade summary flow, one canonical structured data source that records version, date, highlights, breaking changes, and migration actions. Prefer a simple Nushell-friendly structured file such as docs/upgrade_notes.toml rather than scraping markdown. Keep docs/history.md as the long narrative history, but make CHANGELOG.md the practical upgrade document users can find immediately. Do not invent fake versions; keep YAZELIX_VERSION, changelog entries, and any structured upgrade-note metadata aligned. Link the changelog from the obvious user surfaces and define what maintainers must record whenever a config or behavior change can break upgrades. This bead also owns the upgrade contract validator that should run cheaply in prek for repo consistency and more strictly in CI for diff-aware enforcement on upgrade-sensitive changes.
acceptance_criteria: The repo has a root CHANGELOG.md with a stable format that emphasizes Added, Changed, Fixed, Breaking, and Migration notes for real Yazelix versions, and it is clearly connected to the deeper docs history without duplicating vague prose. A structured upgrade-note source such as docs/upgrade_notes.toml is canonical for machine consumption and the markdown surface is derived from or explicitly aligned with it rather than parsed heuristically. Add a validator such as validate_upgrade_contract.nu and wire it into both .pre-commit-config.yaml and .github/workflows/ci.yml. In prek it must perform the cheap current-version and metadata cross-checks defined in this bead. In CI it must additionally perform diff-aware enforcement for the exact guarded files listed in this bead. The validator must reject current-version note drift, missing required fields, bad upgrade_impact combinations, missing migration coverage, and guarded config-contract changes that land without either matching migration metadata or an explicit acknowledged manual-only or no-user-action note. Leave behind detailed logs for both the passing and failing validator paths.
notes:
Planning refinements:
- Create a focused spec such as docs/specs/upgrade_notes_contract.md.
- Prefer one canonical structured source of upgrade metadata and treat CHANGELOG.md as the human-facing projection of that contract or as an explicitly synchronized sibling, not an unrelated document.
- Record for each version the release date, headline summary, optional highlights, optional breaking section, and explicit migration actions when relevant.
- Keep the enforcement narrow and high-signal: use prek for cheap repo-consistency checks and CI for diff-aware checks against curated upgrade-sensitive surfaces.
- Non-goals: dumping every internal refactor into the changelog, inventing placeholder versions, maintaining two drifting sources of truth, or using noisy diff heuristics that trigger on unrelated edits.

Concrete validator spec:
- Canonical structured source: prefer docs/upgrade_notes.toml.
- Human-facing surface: root CHANGELOG.md.
- Version source of truth: nushell/scripts/utils/constants.nu.
- Migration registry source of truth from yazelix-cr3: prefer nushell/scripts/utils/config_migrations.nu.

Exact initial guarded files for CI diff-aware enforcement:
- nushell/scripts/utils/constants.nu
- yazelix_default.toml
- home_manager/module.nix
- nushell/scripts/utils/config_schema.nu
- nushell/scripts/utils/config_migrations.nu
- docs/upgrade_notes.toml
- CHANGELOG.md

Files intentionally excluded from the first hard guard set to avoid noisy false positives:
- nushell/scripts/utils/config_parser.nu unless a later pass can prove a narrow high-signal rule for parser-only contract drift.

Structured upgrade-note contract for the current version entry:
- version = current YAZELIX_VERSION
- date = real release date
- headline = short summary
- upgrade_impact = one of no_user_action, migration_available, manual_action_required
- acknowledged_guarded_changes = list of guarded file paths intentionally changed for this version
- migration_ids = list of migration rule ids when upgrade_impact = migration_available
- manual_actions = list of explicit user actions when upgrade_impact = manual_action_required

What validate_upgrade_contract.nu should run in prek:
- verify CHANGELOG.md exists
- verify docs/upgrade_notes.toml exists
- verify current YAZELIX_VERSION has an entry in docs/upgrade_notes.toml
- verify current YAZELIX_VERSION appears in CHANGELOG.md
- verify current version entry has non-empty date, headline, upgrade_impact, and acknowledged_guarded_changes fields
- verify upgrade_impact enum is valid
- verify migration_ids are present only when upgrade_impact = migration_available
- verify manual_actions are present only when upgrade_impact = manual_action_required
- verify every migration_id referenced by upgrade notes exists in the migration registry
- verify every migration rule id that is flagged as user-visible for the current version is either referenced by upgrade notes or explicitly marked internal-only in the migration registry schema

What validate_upgrade_contract.nu should additionally run in CI:
- inspect the git diff against the PR base or previous pushed commit
- if nushell/scripts/utils/constants.nu changes YAZELIX_VERSION, fail unless the same diff updates both docs/upgrade_notes.toml and CHANGELOG.md for the new version
- for each changed file in the guarded set {yazelix_default.toml, home_manager/module.nix, nushell/scripts/utils/config_schema.nu, nushell/scripts/utils/config_migrations.nu}, fail unless that exact path is listed in acknowledged_guarded_changes for the current version entry
- if any guarded config surface changed and upgrade_impact = no_user_action, allow it only when the guarded-file acknowledgements are present and both migration_ids and manual_actions are empty
- if upgrade_impact = migration_available, fail unless migration_ids is non-empty and every referenced rule exists
- if upgrade_impact = manual_action_required, fail unless manual_actions is non-empty
- fail if docs/upgrade_notes.toml changed the current version entry but CHANGELOG.md did not change in the same diff
- fail if CHANGELOG.md changed the current version entry but docs/upgrade_notes.toml did not change in the same diff

Preferred repo wiring:
- add validate_upgrade_contract.nu to .pre-commit-config.yaml as a cheap local hook
- add validate_upgrade_contract.nu as an explicit CI step in .github/workflows/ci.yml
- keep validator logs detailed enough to show current version, guarded files touched in the diff, acknowledged_guarded_changes, selected migration ids, and the exact missing requirement when failing.

Verification plan: validator coverage for version alignment and required fields, plus an end to end validation script with detailed logging that shows the current version lookup, rendered markdown entry presence, the guarded files touched in a synthetic diff, and the exact failure when upgrade-note metadata is incomplete or a guarded config surface changed without the required migration coverage.
close_reason: Completed: added the root CHANGELOG, structured upgrade notes, local and CI upgrade-contract validation, and e2e coverage so guarded upgrade-sensitive changes cannot bypass notes or migration metadata.

title: Deliver a superb upgrade UX for config changes and releases
description: Build a coherent upgrade UX for Yazelix config and release changes across startup, doctor, migration commands, changelog surfaces, and first run post-upgrade messaging. Users should never have to diff templates or guess why an update broke their setup.
design: Treat this as one product system, not four unrelated tasks. Use one shared migration knowledge layer for known config changes, one clear human facing upgrade notes surface, and explicit user journeys for failure and recovery: startup failure, doctor diagnosis, safe migrate preview and apply, and first run after an upgrade. Startup must stay read only and fail fast with actionable guidance; explicit commands such as yzx config migrate or yzx doctor --fix may perform safe deterministic repairs with backup. Changes that define supported user visible behavior should leave behind focused specs under docs/specs with traceability to the relevant bead and defended tests. Add a cheap validator lane for this contract: repo-consistency checks should run locally through prek and in CI, while diff-aware enforcement for upgrade-sensitive changes should run in CI so version bumps and config-contract changes cannot quietly bypass migration or release-note obligations.
acceptance_criteria: Yazelix has a coherent upgrade UX: known stale config cases produce precise actionable diagnostics; safe deterministic config migrations can be previewed and applied with backup; maintainers have a stable changelog and upgrade-notes contract for recording breaking changes; and the first interactive run after a real version bump can show a concise once per version summary with a way to reopen it later. Add comprehensive unit coverage for rule logic and state handling, plus high signal end to end scripts with detailed logging for stale config launch failures, doctor flows, migrate preview and apply flows, changelog or release-note validation, and version-bump summary behavior. Add cheap contract validation in the repo validator lane and wire it into both prek and CI so upgrade-sensitive work cannot silently skip the required notes or migration metadata.
close_reason: all steps complete

title: Evaluate replacing the popup-runner plugin with Zellij 0.44 CLI pane control
description:
Post-Zellij-0.44 follow-up: evaluate whether Yazelix can replace the custom popup-runner plugin with pure Zellij CLI control for popup lifecycle management.

Why this exists:
- Zellij 0.44 expanded CLI automation with `zellij action list-panes` (including JSON output) and pane IDs returned from pane/tab creation commands
- Yazelix currently uses a dedicated popup-runner plugin plus a shell toggle wrapper to discover, focus, and close the managed popup pane
- If the new CLI surface is sufficient, Yazelix may be able to remove one Rust/WASM plugin and simplify popup lifecycle logic
design: Treat this as a simplification evaluation first, not an automatic rewrite. Start from the current popup contract: one managed popup identity, toggle=open/focus/close semantics, no duplicates, and active-tab scoping. Validate whether Zellij 0.44 CLI surfaces can cover that contract reliably without plugin state: list panes in the active/current session, identify the managed popup pane by stable metadata, and focus/close it without racing the open path. Preserve the current shell-side `yzx popup` launcher if it remains the most reliable creation path. Success can be either a real plugin removal plan or a documented rejection if active-tab targeting or popup identity still requires plugin state.
acceptance_criteria: The evaluation ends with a concrete decision grounded in verified Zellij 0.44 behavior. Either: 1. Yazelix adopts or prototypes a CLI-only popup lifecycle path that preserves active-tab scoping, toggle semantics, and no-duplicate guarantees, with regression coverage at the popup seam; or 2. the bead records a clear rejection explaining which popup invariants still require the custom plugin. In either case, leave behind a short implementation plan or rejection note rather than vague future intent.
close_reason: Superseded by pane-orchestrator transient-pane ownership; the separate popup-runner plugin was removed instead of being replaced by a CLI-only popup lifecycle path

title: Replace swap-layout step logic with Zellij 0.44 override-layout transitions
description:
Post-Zellij-0.44 follow-up: evaluate replacing Yazelix's current swap-layout stepping model for layout-family changes and sidebar open/close transitions with Zellij's newer `override-layout` capability.

Why this exists:
- the pane orchestrator currently hardcodes layout family names and drives transitions via `next_swap_layout()` / `previous_swap_layout()` plus timing delays
- Zellij 0.44 introduced `override-layout` and the built-in layout manager as first-class layout transition surfaces

Scope:
- map the current Yazelix layout families and sidebar open/closed variants onto an explicit override-layout model
- determine whether sidebar toggle, family cycling, or both can stop depending on swap-step timing
- preserve managed-pane focus behavior and workspace invariants across transitions
- reduce hardcoded layout-name coupling where possible

Success means Yazelix either adopts a cleaner override-layout-based transition path for these flows, or leaves behind a documented reason why the swap-layout model still must remain.
design: Treat this as the broader override-layout evaluation umbrella. Zellij 0.44 made override-layout more realistic, but the real product question is whether Yazelix can replace swap-layout stepping without losing managed-pane invariants. The live-trial notes matter: runtime-file IO inside the plugin sandbox was one blocker, and explicit pane identity may be the deeper blocker. Use this bead to hold the broad transition question, while narrower child beads capture the concrete contracts discovered along the way.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear keep or replace decision for swap-layout stepping versus override-layout transitions, backed by concrete evidence from trials. If override-layout remains the direction, the bead points to the narrower explicit-run and pane-ownership work needed to make it real. If not, it records why swap-layout remains necessary.
notes:
Live trial status on 2026-03-26:
- pointer-driven layout changes still work
- direct keybind actions `toggle_sidebar` and `previous_family`/`next_family` were failing even though layout detection succeeded
- root cause: the pane-orchestrator plugin could not read generated runtime layout/script files under `~/.local/share/yazelix/...` from inside the Zellij plugin sandbox
- implementation change: replace runtime file IO with embedded tracked layout templates and in-memory Yazelix placeholder expansion inside the plugin
- current state: rebuilt and synced locally; awaiting live retest after `yzx restart`
close_reason: Dropped: prior live trial showed override-layout did not preserve Yazelix sidebar/layout behavior reliably; keep swap-layout stepping until a narrower owned-pane/layout contract proves a replacement.

title: Create a GitHub social preview image for Yazelix
description: Create and configure a repository social preview image that represents Yazelix clearly when the repo is shared on GitHub and social platforms.
design: Treat this as a branding and front-door asset, not just a generic screenshot upload. The image should reflect Yazelix's terminal-native identity, remain readable at social-card sizes, and avoid overloading the card with tiny UI details. Decide whether the right asset is an illustrated brand card, a polished product screenshot, or a hybrid, then configure the repo to use the chosen image.
acceptance_criteria: There is a final social preview image asset sized appropriately for GitHub, it matches Yazelix branding well enough for public sharing, and the repository is configured to use it. The bead records the chosen direction so future branding work knows why this asset exists.

title: Add configurable Ghostty cursor trail glow levels
description: Add a first-class config option for the glow around Ghostty cursor trails and related cursor effects. Use 'glow' as the canonical term rather than 'intensity' or 'halo'. The setting should control the spatial aura around the cursor/trail, not the underlying trail color itself. A value of 'none' should keep the trail color/effect but remove the surrounding glow. Proposed levels: none, low, medium, high. Proposed default: medium, matching the current feel closely enough that existing visuals still feel like Yazelix by default.
design: Treat this as a Ghostty shader/config contract first, not a vague visual-preference knob. The setting should only control the outer glow or aura around the cursor/trail, while preserving the underlying trail color/effect. Use 'glow' as the user-facing term. Start with a small discrete enum: none, low, medium, high. The current visual behavior should map roughly to medium so existing Yazelix visuals do not suddenly become more aggressive by default. The implementation should work across the supported Ghostty trail and mode effects, or clearly document any effect families that cannot share the same glow semantics without awkward one-off hacks.
acceptance_criteria: Yazelix exposes a documented Ghostty cursor-trail glow setting with values none, low, medium, high. Setting glow to none keeps the cursor/trail color effect while removing the surrounding spatial glow. Medium is the default and should preserve the current feel closely enough that the default user experience does not regress unexpectedly. Add focused validation for config parsing and generated Ghostty config or shader selection, and document which Ghostty effect families honor the glow setting if any exceptions remain.
close_reason: Completed

title: Fix yzx cwd renaming the wrong Zellij tab
description: Sometimes tab renaming updates the wrong Zellij tab instead of the active tab. In the reported case on March 25, 2026, running yzx cwd in the current tab renamed the tab immediately to the left rather than the current tab. The same wrong-tab rename behavior was also observed when opening from Yazi into the editor. Treat this as a tab-targeting bug in the active-tab and cwd-sync flow: commands and editor-launch flows must resolve and rename only the current Zellij tab, never an adjacent tab or another tab in the session.
design: Start from first principles: yzx cwd should act on the active Zellij tab the user invoked it from, not on whichever tab state file or tab identifier was last touched elsewhere in the session. Verify how the command resolves the current tab identity, how that identity is translated into a tab rename target, and whether stale cross-tab state, pane-to-tab mapping, or tab-index math can redirect the rename one tab to the left. Prefer a fix that uses stable current-tab identifiers rather than inferred position or most-recently-updated state.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear reproduction or at least instrumentation for the wrong-tab rename bug in a multi-tab session. Running yzx cwd from the active tab renames only that active tab, and opening from Yazi into the editor also renames only that same active tab. Neither flow may rename the tab to the left or any other tab in the session. Add focused validation for active-tab resolution and a regression check covering at least two or three tabs with distinct names and cwd values, with logs showing the invoked tab, resolved tab identifier, triggering action, and final renamed tab.
close_reason: Cannot reproduce

title: make the popup receive a size from 1 to 100 (percentage of width, height)
description: Imported GitHub issue #464.
close_reason: Completed: added validated zellij.popup_width_percent and zellij.popup_height_percent config in the 1..100 range, wired yzx popup to use them, synced Home Manager/default config, and added popup regression tests

title: Update Yazelix to the next Zellij major release
description: Update Yazelix to the next Zellij major release and make the supported runtime, generated config, plugins, and documented behavior work cleanly on that release.
close_reason: Completed: upgraded Yazelix to Zellij 0.44, updated the Rust plugins to zellij-tile 0.44, rebuilt and synced the wasm artifacts, refreshed the maintained default-config snapshot, and fixed version reporting so saved status output reflects the current repo-shell runtime.

title: Repo pass after the Zellij major update for simplifications and new opportunities
description: After the Zellij major update lands, do a repository-wide pass looking for simplifications, workaround removals, and new feature opportunities made practical by the newer Zellij surface.
notes:
Audit snapshot after the Zellij 0.44 upgrade:

Ranked follow-up list:
1. `yazelix-tuj` — replace swap-layout stepping/delay logic with an `override-layout` transition model. This is the clearest architectural simplification because the pane orchestrator still hardcodes layout families and depends on `next_swap_layout()` / `previous_swap_layout()` timing.
2. `yazelix-anc` — evaluate replacing the custom popup-runner plugin with Zellij 0.44 CLI pane control (`list-panes --json`, pane IDs from creation commands, targeted actions). This could remove one Rust/WASM plugin if active-tab popup semantics remain reliable without plugin state.
3. `yazelix-6zx` — document and validate modern Zellij sharing flows for Yazelix, now that the built-in share UI and read-only tokens are first-class in the newer release.

No smaller shipped simplification looked clearly worth landing immediately without broadening scope or adding noisy docs that Zellij already covers in-session.
close_reason: Completed

title: Define a supply-chain hardening policy for Yazelix tool surfaces
description: Define a lightweight supply-chain hardening policy for Yazelix so default-installed tool surfaces stay low-risk, optional higher-risk packs stay explicitly scoped, and future incidents have a repeatable triage and communication workflow.
design: Keep this lightweight and policy-first. The goal is not exhaustive auditing of every upstream tool; it is a practical safety model for Yazelix's maintained surfaces. Define the risk tiers for default-installed tools versus opt-in packs, prefer hermetic Nix-managed runtime paths over host package managers, require a small review checklist before enabling new default tools, and define a repeatable incident-response workflow for future supply-chain advisories. The result should help maintainers decide when a tool can be default-enabled, when it must remain opt-in, and how to communicate impact quickly when a dependency incident happens.
acceptance_criteria: Yazelix has a short documented policy describing: 1. what package/tool surfaces Yazelix treats as default, opt-in, or higher-risk; 2. review criteria for enabling new default tools; 3. preferred runtime/update paths and discouraged package-manager paths; and 4. a repeatable incident triage workflow for future supply-chain advisories. The policy should give maintainers enough guidance to classify a new tool addition or a future supply-chain incident without ad-hoc reasoning each time.
close_reason: Completed: docs/contracts/supply_chain_hardening.md now defines the default/opt-in risk split, review checklist for default tool additions, Nix-managed runtime preference, and incident triage workflow. Verified by canonical contract structure and current repo references.

title: Tie the default test suite more explicitly to specs and contracts
description: Strengthen the connection between Yazelix's default automated regression suite and the specs/contracts it is supposed to defend. The goal is not heavy process or one-spec-per-test bureaucracy. The goal is to make the small default suite legible: maintainers should be able to see which supported behavior or contract each default-suite entrypoint exists to defend.
design: Keep this lightweight and default-suite-focused. Treat the public default suite as the strongest candidate for explicit spec/contract traceability. At minimum, map the default suite entrypoints to documented specs or explicitly justified regression-only contracts, add a small validator for that relationship, and avoid expanding the requirement to every helper test or validator in the repo. Prefer file-level traceability over per-function bureaucracy. Cheap validators and sweep/manual lanes can keep lighter ownership rules.
acceptance_criteria: The default automated suite entrypoints are explicitly tied to specs, documented contracts, or a small justified regression-only allowlist. Add a lightweight validator that checks the default-suite traceability relationship. Update the relevant specs and/or nearby comments so a maintainer can tell what the default suite is defending without reverse-engineering the test files. The solution should stay lightweight: no requirement for one spec per test function and no heavy metadata system for the whole repo.
close_reason: Completed: added a lightweight validator tying the default test suite to specs or a tiny justified regression-only allowlist, updated specs/comments to make default-suite ownership legible, and verified the default suite plus validators.

title: Add an optional modern_js pack for Bun, Deno, and future Vite+ workflows
description: Add a dedicated optional modern_js pack instead of widening the default ts pack. The pack owns JS/TS runtime and workflow tooling, while ts stays focused on editor-facing language tooling. Vite+ remains a follow-up once it is packaged in nixpkgs.
design: Keep the boundary explicit: ts is for language servers, linting, and formatting; modern_js is for runtime and workflow tools. Implement modern_js with tools that already exist in nixpkgs and keep the pack small enough to remain an optional add-on. Document the Vite+ gap directly instead of pretending the package exists.
acceptance_criteria: Yazelix ships a documented optional modern_js pack in both yazelix_default.toml and home_manager/module.nix, the ts pack no longer owns runtime tooling that belongs in the new pack, docs explain the split, and tests defend parity across both configuration paths. If Vite+ is not yet packaged in nixpkgs, the docs explicitly say so and the bead still closes with that defended boundary.
notes: Implemented as the optional modern_js pack with bun and deno. bun was removed from the default ts pack so ts stays editor-focused, docs now explain the split, and parity tests cover both ts and modern_js. Vite+ is intentionally deferred until it is packaged in nixpkgs.
close_reason: Completed

title: yzx update <any existing package> for standalone updates
description: Imported GitHub issue #459.
close_reason: Not planned: generic imperative single-package updates do not fit Yazelix's current declarative runtime model. The supported update surfaces remain repo/devenv/nix and maintainer-scoped input updates rather than arbitrary package mutation.

title: Managed popup pane lifecycle: toggle, focus, reuse, no duplicates
description: Move transient popup-pane lifecycle into a dedicated popup runner plus a small shell toggle wrapper so popup actions behave like managed session state rather than disposable launches. The popup key should toggle a single managed popup pane: open it if missing, focus it if present but unfocused, and close it if focused. Repeated presses must not spawn duplicate floating panes.
design: Keep this scoped to one managed popup identity first, backed by the existing configurable popup_program. Use the shell-side yzx popup path for creation because it is the proven reliable floating-pane launcher. Let the popup runner own discovery, focus, close, and reuse semantics so shell glue does not have to infer floating-pane state from raw Zellij output. Preserve the current configurable popup command surface, but make the lifecycle stateful and session-aware without prematurely generalizing to multiple popup types.
acceptance_criteria:
Pressing the popup key opens the configured popup program in one managed floating pane when none exists.
Pressing it again focuses the existing popup if it exists but is not focused, and closes it if it is focused.
Repeated presses do not create duplicate floating panes.
Add regression coverage at the popup-runner/plugin seam for popup discovery and toggle behavior.
Add an end-to-end Yazelix script test that verifies no duplicate popup panes are created across repeated toggles.
close_reason: Implemented managed popup lifecycle with hybrid shell/plugin toggle flow

title: for every comment that is automated or similar prefix it with something like "Automated: <message>"
description:
Imported GitHub issue #458.

could be only for new comments
close_reason: Completed: canonical GitHub issue comments created by the Beads sync flow are now prefixed with Automated:, legacy unprefixed Beads comments are still detected for update, and the maintainer contract test plus AGENTS guidance were updated

title: Investigate whether Yazelix can avoid Zellij plugin permission prompts safely
description: Investigate whether Yazelix can reduce or avoid the recurring Zellij plugin permission popup without undermining the safety model or relying on brittle local hacks.
design: Start from the principle that permission prompts exist for a reason. The task is not to bypass safety blindly; it is to determine whether Yazelix can ship a safer default permission story through stable plugin paths, predictable permission state, or better first-run setup. If Zellij fundamentally requires user approval, prefer an explicit documented flow over unsafe suppression tricks.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear decision on whether Yazelix can safely reduce or avoid Zellij plugin permission prompts. If yes, the bead identifies the legitimate mechanism. If not, it documents the best supported user flow and why forced suppression would be the wrong tradeoff.
close_reason: Superseded by the built-in Yazelix permission-recovery command; separate forced popup suppression is no longer needed as a tracked feature.

title: Fix pane-orchestrator permission limbo after plugin reload
description: Document and fix the failure mode where changing the pane-orchestrator plugin can trigger Zellij's permission popup inside an existing Yazelix window in a broken state that cannot be accepted. After that partial reload, launching Yazelix again can land in a blank-screen limbo because the session/plugin state is inconsistent.
design: Treat this as a plugin-lifecycle and recovery bug, not just a one-off UX glitch. Verify exactly when the permission dialog appears during pane-orchestrator reloads, whether the trigger is plugin hash changes, permission re-requests, stale plugin instances, or Zellij reload semantics, and why the popup becomes unusable inside the managed Yazelix window. The fix should prioritize clean recovery: avoid leaving the session in a blank-screen limbo, fail fast with a recoverable path when permissions are not granted, and make plugin reload/update flows deterministic instead of depending on manual session luck. If the root cause is a Zellij limitation, document the exact safe reload workflow and add guardrails in Yazelix rather than pretending the reload is seamless.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear reproduction path or documented trigger for the permission-limbo state, Yazelix no longer gets stuck in a blank-screen session after the pane-orchestrator plugin reload/update path fails, and there is an explicit recovery path for maintainers/users when plugin permissions are missing or stuck. Add focused validation for the detected failure mode, plus logs or diagnostics that show whether the plugin is loaded, permissioned, and responding after reload attempts.
close_reason: Handled by the restart/recovery workflow and current plugin update guidance; the permission-limbo issue is no longer actionable as a separate task.

title: Remove the Kiro spec file and directory

title: Packaging: route shell and terminal entrypoints through the runtime root

title: Packaging: stamp runtime-root into generated Zellij layouts and wrapper scripts
description: Remove repo-shaped runtime assumptions from generated Zellij layouts and wrapper scripts so the merged Zellij runtime can point at an installed runtime root instead of hardcoding ~/.config/yazelix.
