title: new command: yzx open or yzx o (alias)
description: Imported GitHub issue #455.
close_reason: Not planned: yzx open is too ambiguous as a top-level command, and the closest likely intent already exists as yzx config open. Revisit only if it is rewritten as a narrowly scoped command contract.

title: Pilot the spec-driven workflow on one real Yazelix feature
description: Pick one concrete user-visible feature or bugfix and run it through the proposed spec-driven flow end-to-end: bead, spec, implementation, and regression checks. The goal is to prove the workflow is small enough to use and strong enough to prevent drift before expanding it further.
close_reason: Piloted the spec-driven workflow on shell-opened editors with a concrete spec, user-facing docs, an implementation-side diagnostic clarification, and regression coverage.

title: Define the Yazelix spec template and repository conventions
description: Create the minimal spec format for user-visible Yazelix work. Define where specs live, which changes need one, what sections are required, and how acceptance criteria should be written so specs are precise enough to guide implementation without turning into bureaucracy.
close_reason: Defined the spec-driven workflow, spec location, required sections, acceptance-case rules, and reusable template under docs/spec_driven_workflow.md and docs/specs/template.md

title: Add traceability between specs, beads, and regression tests
description: Make spec-driven development enforceable instead of aspirational. Define a lightweight traceability path from a bead to its spec and from the spec acceptance cases to the tests or checks that defend them. Prefer a small validator or convention check over a heavyweight process engine.
close_reason: Added lightweight traceability between specs, beads, and regression checks through a Traceability section convention plus validate_specs.nu.

title: Define Yazelix subsystem ownership boundaries
description: Define a concrete ownership map for Yazelix subsystems so runtime/source, user config, generated state, workspace/session state, and Linux/desktop integrations stop bleeding into each other through implicit conventions. The output should be an explicit boundary document grounded in the current codebase, not a vague modularity aspiration.
close_reason: Documented the four-subsystem architecture map, ownership rules, and product-layer model in docs/architecture_map.md and linked it from the README

title: Define the workspace/session contract between Nushell and the pane orchestrator
description: Make the workspace/session boundary explicit between Nushell scripts and the Rust pane orchestrator. Define which side owns tab workspace roots, managed-pane identity, sidebar state, tab naming, and cross-tab isolation so future fixes stop rediscovering the same seam through bugs.
close_reason: Documented the current Nushell/plugin/Yazi workspace-session contract, ownership rules, command contract, invariants, and boundary gaps in docs/workspace_session_contract.md

title: teste issue
description: Imported GitHub issue #454.
close_reason: Closed on GitHub

title: Make Yazelix spec driven
description: Imported GitHub issue #451. Define a spec-driven workflow for new Yazelix features so user-visible behavior has an explicit contract before and during implementation.
close_reason: Completed: the spec-driven workflow epic work is done; the remaining Rust rewrite planning work now continues as standalone bead yazelix-kt5 with matching GitHub issue #461.

title: Create a bead-to-issue contract with no lifecycle drift
description: Imported GitHub issue #452. Define and implement the shared-subset contract between GitHub issues and Beads so the mapping and open/closed lifecycle cannot silently drift.
close_reason: Closed on GitHub

title: Make Yazelix modular
description: Imported GitHub issue #450. Create a concrete modularity plan for Yazelix so major subsystems can evolve with clearer boundaries instead of being coupled through ad-hoc shell glue and shared assumptions.
close_reason: Closing the modularity epic: the core modularity framing work has landed, and the remaining follow-up work now continues as standalone beads instead of staying under this parent epic.

title: Adopt minimal prek config for fast maintainer checks
description: Follow-up to the maintainer-pack work: clean up stale prek references that still describe an unrelated tool, add a minimal pre-commit-compatible config at the repo root, and keep the hook set intentionally small and cheap: syntax validation plus README version validation. Document the maintainer usage path without turning prek into the main enforcement layer; CI remains authoritative.
close_reason: Cleaned stale prek references, added a minimal .pre-commit-config.yaml with syntax and README version checks, documented the maintainer usage path, and verified the config with prek run --all-files.

title: Add maintainer pack for local maintainer tooling
description: Imported GitHub issue #453 after deciding that maintainer tooling should be an opt-in pack rather than a special yzx dev mode. Add a top-level maintainer pack for repo maintenance tools that are meaningfully tied to maintaining Yazelix itself, starting with gh, prek, tru, beads-rust, and beads-viewer. Keep runtime behavior unchanged, sync the default config and Home Manager module, document the pack in the collection/docs, and add a focused regression test that verifies the pack stays aligned across configuration paths.
close_reason: Added an opt-in maintainer pack with gh, prek, tru, beads-rust, and beads-viewer; synced the default config, Home Manager module, and collection docs; and added a regression test that keeps the pack aligned across both configuration paths.

title: Add CI check for README version drift
description: Protect the public README version marker from drifting away from YAZELIX_VERSION. Add one focused check that compares the README title/version line against the version constant and run it in CI.
close_reason: Completed: added README version drift validator to CI and taught yzx dev update to auto-sync the README title/version marker from YAZELIX_VERSION

title: Make new tabs inherit the active workspace root
description: New tabs currently inherit a stale bootstrap root, so Alt+m can open panes in the original launch directory even when the current tab visually starts at HOME. Tighten the workspace model so new tabs inherit the active tab's workspace root and tab names follow that root immediately, including HOME-backed tabs.
close_reason: Completed: fresh sidebar tabs now bootstrap the tab workspace root from the actual Yazi launch directory, so Alt+m follows the tab start directory instead of stale launch cwd state

title: Refresh CI actions for Node 24 compatibility
description: The current CI workflow uses DeterminateSystems/nix-installer-action@v21 and DeterminateSystems/magic-nix-cache-action@v13, and GitHub now warns that these actions are still on Node.js 20. Track the follow-up needed to verify newer tags or alternate actions that support Node.js 24 before GitHub flips the default runtime.
design: Use the latest available Determinate action tags, but stop waiting for them to switch runtimes on their own. Force JavaScript actions onto Node.js 24 in CI now so Yazelix discovers compatibility problems before GitHub flips the default globally. Keep FlakeHub disabled in both installer and cache setup because Yazelix does not need it for this workflow.
acceptance_criteria: CI no longer relies on the deprecated Node.js 20 action runtime. The workflow explicitly opts into Node.js 24 for JavaScript actions, keeps Nix installation and cache setup working, and avoids unnecessary FlakeHub auth paths.
notes: At implementation time, the latest available tags were nix-installer-action v21 and magic-nix-cache-action v13, and both still emitted the Node.js 20 deprecation warning. Since there was no newer compatible major tag to upgrade to immediately, the workflow now uses FORCE_JAVASCRIPT_ACTIONS_TO_NODE24=true as the concrete mitigation.
close_reason: Opted CI into Node 24 and disabled unnecessary FlakeHub paths in Determinate actions

title: Align CI with Home Manager smoke checks
description: The main CI workflow currently sets up Nushell and runs the yzx command tests, but the default test suite now includes a Home Manager smoke check that evaluates the home_manager flake via nix eval. CI should install Nix explicitly and keep the supported Home Manager path covered instead of depending on runner luck or environment drift. Consider whether to add a broader home_manager flake check step as dedicated CI coverage after the minimal setup is in place.
design: Install Nix explicitly in the main CI workflow so the default yzx dev test coverage has the tools it now requires. Keep the existing yzx command suite as the primary smoke path, and add a dedicated nix flake check ./home_manager --no-build step for broader Home Manager flake validation without forcing builds.
acceptance_criteria: CI installs Nix before running Yazelix tests. The main yzx command suite continues to run in CI. CI also evaluates the home_manager flake through nix flake check --no-build so Home Manager flake regressions are caught independently of the shell-level smoke tests.
notes: CI now installs Nix explicitly and sets determinate: false on DeterminateSystems/nix-installer-action@v21 so the workflow does not try to authenticate with FlakeHub for features Yazelix is not using. The remaining follow-up is action-runtime maintenance: GitHub warns that the Determinate actions in use still target Node.js 20, so version refresh or replacement is tracked separately.
close_reason: CI now installs Nix, runs the default yzx suite, and checks the Home Manager flake with nix flake check --no-build

title: Fix Home Manager desktop entry evaluation regression
description: The Home Manager module declares the Yazelix desktop entry with an invalid Home Manager option path for StartupWMClass, causing module evaluation to fail before activation. Reproduce with a minimal Home Manager configuration evaluation against home_manager/module.nix. Keep the fix focused on restoring a working supported entrypoint, while leaving the broader question of Home Manager ownership and package-ready path modeling to the existing Home Manager packaging work.
design: Use the Home Manager desktop entry API correctly for desktop-file metadata instead of assuming the nixpkgs makeDesktopItem field names are accepted directly. Add a focused regression check that evaluates a minimal Home Manager configuration and verifies the Yazelix desktop entry exposes StartupWMClass through settings. Keep this as a Nix/Home Manager evaluation test, because the failure is in the Home Manager module contract rather than in the devenv runtime path. Surface it through yzx dev test by default so the supported Home Manager path stays covered in the normal maintainer loop.
acceptance_criteria: A minimal Home Manager evaluation with programs.yazelix.enable = true no longer fails on the Yazelix desktop entry. The desktop entry still carries the Yazelix StartupWMClass metadata. Add a focused regression test that reproduces the evaluation path and fails if the invalid option path comes back.
notes: Implementation direction now landed locally: home_manager/flake.nix exposes a Linux-only desktop_entry_smoke check whose derivation carries passthru.startupWMClass. The regression test resolves the current system and runs nix eval --raw --read-only --no-write-lock-file against that flake check attr instead of embedding a long inline expression. After pinning home_manager/flake.lock and measuring the cached path, the default suite remained fast enough, so the temporary --fast opt-out was removed rather than carrying extra CLI surface area.
close_reason: Closed on GitHub

title: General movement should skip the managed sidebar
description: Adjust general movement/navigation so the managed sidebar is skipped by default. Users should reach the sidebar explicitly through sidebar-focused commands like Ctrl+y, not by incidental workspace movement.
design: After sidebar focus behavior is made correct, consider making general movement treat the sidebar as skippable by default, but only if it falls out naturally from the current Zellij/plugin model. The sidebar should still be reachable through explicit sidebar commands, but ordinary pane movement should prioritize the main workspace panes. If this requires brittle special cases, layout-specific hacks, or substantial new focus state, it is not worth the complexity.
acceptance_criteria: General movement no longer lands on the managed sidebar unless the user uses an explicit sidebar-targeting action, but only if the implementation is straightforward and low-complexity. Add focused tests for the affected movement bindings if implemented; otherwise keep the current explicit-access model and close the issue as not worth the added complexity.
close_reason: Alt+h and Alt+l now use pane-orchestrator walking that skips the managed sidebar when it is closed.

title: Sidebar focus should be explicit, not latent
description: Make the managed sidebar behave like an explicit auxiliary surface rather than a normal focus target. A closed sidebar should not remain focusable, closing it while focused should move focus back to the main workspace, and opening it should not steal focus unless the action explicitly targets the sidebar.
design: Use the managed sidebar as an explicit auxiliary surface, but only if the behavior can be implemented cleanly within the current pane-orchestrator model. Closing it should clear any latent focus on it and return focus to the main workspace; opening it should preserve current focus unless the user explicitly targeted the sidebar. If this requires a large amount of extra focus bookkeeping or layout-specific complexity, it is not worth doing.
acceptance_criteria: When the sidebar is closed it is no longer focusable, and if it was focused the active focus returns to the main workspace/editor pane. Reopening the sidebar does not steal focus by default. Add focused tests for close-while-focused and reopen-without-focus-steal behavior only if the implementation stays small and coherent; otherwise leave the current explicit-focus model in place.
close_reason: Sidebar visibility and focus are now explicit; closed sidebars are not reachable through explicit or incidental movement.

title: Use Yazi as a floating picker from Helix
description: Add a Yazelix-native flow where Helix can open Yazi as a floating picker pane, select a target, and return the chosen path back to the editor. Keep the first version tightly scoped: it should feel fast and integrated inside the existing Zellij workspace rather than spawning an unrelated external file picker.
design: Keep the first slice tightly scoped around a fast file-selection loop from Helix using a floating Yazi surface inside the existing Zellij workspace. Define invocation, selected-path return, cancellation, and what happens when the persistent sidebar is disabled. Avoid letting this turn into a generic command-palette replacement or a second unrelated file-tree system.
acceptance_criteria: From Helix, a user can open a Yazi-based floating picker, choose a path, and return that path cleanly to the editor without breaking the existing workspace flow. The first slice is explicitly scoped and remains compatible with the narrower sidebar-disabled variant tracked separately.

title: Standardize rebuild and refresh failure reporting
description: Make rebuild and refresh failures consistently surface the failing command, a useful stderr tail, and a concrete recovery hint instead of uneven command-specific messages.
design: Unify the way refresh, rebuild, and update helpers report failures. Instead of bespoke command-specific output, preserve the failing command, the most useful stderr tail, and a short recovery hint. Keep the summaries compact, but make the underlying failure reconstructable from logs.
acceptance_criteria: Refresh and rebuild failure paths produce consistent high-signal summaries and detailed logs. Add focused tests for failed rebuild and refresh scenarios, verifying that the command, stderr tail, and recovery hint are all surfaced.
close_reason: Standardized refresh and rebuild failure summaries with focused regressions

title: Launch and startup error hardening
description: Harden Yazelix launch and startup paths so missing runtime roots, missing generated assets, terminal-launch failures, and startup precondition failures fail fast with actionable messages instead of looking hung or broken.
design: Focus on the user-facing startup path first. Distinguish between bad invocation context, missing generated runtime state, missing external tools, and terminal-launch failures. Error messages should say what failed, what command or asset was involved when relevant, and the shortest recovery action.
acceptance_criteria: Common startup failure modes surface clear, consistent errors with recovery hints. Add focused tests for bad runtime roots, missing generated assets, and terminal-launch failure handling, plus logs that preserve the failing command or asset path.
close_reason: Hardened POSIX launchers, desktop launcher runtime checks, and startup regression coverage for missing runtime scripts and launch preconditions

title: Classify config, generated-state, and host-dependency failures
description: Make Yazelix explicitly distinguish bad user config, generated-state drift, and missing host dependencies so failures are classified correctly and recovery guidance matches the actual problem.
design: Build a small failure-classification model instead of ad-hoc messaging. The important boundary is whether the problem comes from the user config, generated Yazelix state, or missing host capabilities. Doctor, launch, and refresh paths should converge on the same classification so users get consistent guidance.
acceptance_criteria: Representative failures are classified correctly as config, generated-state, or host-dependency problems, and the surfaced recovery hint matches the class. Add focused tests for invalid config values, stale generated state, and absent external tools.
close_reason: Added explicit config/generated-state/host-dependency failure classes with focused regressions for each representative case

title: Make yzx command tests hermetic in CI
description: Refactor nushell/scripts/dev/test_yzx_commands.nu so it can run on a clean CI runner without assuming the repo is mounted at ~/.config/yazelix, without assuming generated Yazelix state already exists, and without hard-failing when optional host tools like nix, devenv, zoxide, zellij, yazi, or helix are absent. The suite should provision any config or generated state it needs, use repo-relative entrypoints, and explicitly skip or stub checks whose external toolchain is not available.
close_reason: Completed via hermetic test harness, split suites, and blocking CI gate

title: Remove repo-shaped ~/.config bootstrap from CI and runtime helpers
description: Eliminate the remaining expectation that Yazelix lives at ~/.config/yazelix during tests, CI, and helper scripts. The current CI workflow bootstraps a ~/.config/yazelix symlink only as a temporary compatibility layer. Follow through on the broader packaging/path-model work so command entrypoints, helper scripts, and validation paths resolve from the installed runtime or repo checkout explicitly instead of relying on HOME-relative repo paths.
close_reason: Completed: removed CI symlink bootstrap, resolved runtime helpers/tests via YAZELIX_DIR, and verified nu nushell/scripts/dev/test_yzx_commands.nu passes against a temp fixture without ~/.config bootstrap

title: Make adding custom Zellij layouts straightforward
description: Imported GitHub issue #438 after verifying that custom Zellij layouts are only easy in a narrow no-sidebar case and are otherwise blocked by hardcoded layout generation, hardcoded sidebar-family knowledge in the pane orchestrator, and stale docs.
design: Treat this as a structural customization problem rather than a docs-only paper cut. The supported path for adding layouts should be explicit: layout files must be discoverable or registered through one clear mechanism, sidebar layout families must expose enough metadata for the pane orchestrator to switch and toggle them without hardcoded family names, and unsupported layouts must fail fast with clear errors instead of silently disappearing. The documentation should reflect the actual current filenames and the real extension workflow.
acceptance_criteria: A maintainer can add a supported custom layout without touching multiple unrelated code paths by hand; generated Zellij config picks up the new layout through the documented mechanism; sidebar-aware layouts either work through a documented manifest or naming scheme or fail fast with a clear error; docs describe the current layout files and the actual extension workflow; automated tests cover the supported custom-layout path.
close_reason: Made top-level Zellij layout discovery automatic, documented the supported customization boundary, and added regression coverage for custom layout generation.

title: Add yzx dev bump for version and tag automation
description: Add a maintainer-facing yzx dev bump command that automates the release bump flow instead of doing version edits, commits, and tags by hand.
design: Implement a maintainer-only yzx dev bump command that computes or accepts the next version, updates nushell/scripts/utils/constants.nu so YAZELIX_VERSION matches the intended tag, creates a dedicated version bump commit, and creates the matching git tag. The command should fail fast on a dirty worktree, an existing conflicting tag, or a version mismatch between the file and the requested tag. The flow should be explicit and deterministic so maintainers do not need to remember the manual release steps.
acceptance_criteria: A maintainer can run yzx dev bump with an explicit version and the command updates nushell/scripts/utils/constants.nu to that version; the command creates a dedicated git commit for the version bump with a predictable message; the command creates the matching git tag after the commit succeeds; the command refuses to run when the git worktree is dirty; the command refuses to run when the requested tag already exists or conflicts with repository state; the command verifies that YAZELIX_VERSION matches the tag it creates; the command has automated tests covering the happy path and failure cases.
close_reason: Completed: added yzx dev bump as a clean-worktree maintainer release helper that rotates Unreleased release metadata into a real version, resets the Unreleased placeholder, updates YAZELIX_VERSION, updates the README version marker, creates a dedicated commit, and creates the matching git tag. Automated tests now cover the happy path plus dirty-worktree and existing-tag failures.

title: Document Alt+m cwd regression spiral after refactor
description: Record the regression sequence where Alt+m pane creation was refactored away from the known-good orchestrator path and reintroduced the Zellij cwd bug, leading to panes opening in HOME, stale sidebar-state confusion, and crashing helper-pane experiments.
design: Keep this as a postmortem-style regression bead linked conceptually to yazelix-2ex.1.7.5. The key invariant is that Yazelix must resolve the live sidebar cwd in Nushell and delegate pane creation to the pane orchestrator via open_terminal_in_cwd. Refactors must not replace that path with zellij new-pane/run --cwd helpers, explicit shell launches, or command panes. Preserve the later good fixes from this incident: immediate sidebar-state cache refresh after open-from-Yazi sync, pruning stale sidebar session files, and avoiding launcher cwd poisoning from ~/.config/yazelix.
acceptance_criteria: The bead notes capture the failure chain, the known-good contract, and the forbidden fallback paths. Future work touching Alt+m or pane cwd should be able to use this bead to avoid repeating the regression.
notes: Regression chain on 2026-03-15: a refactor replaced the orchestrator-owned Alt+m pane-open flow from a7b5716/yazelix-2ex.1.7.5 with helper-driven zellij new-pane --cwd and later zellij run --cwd shell launches. Symptoms included panes opening in /home/lucca or ~/.config/yazelix despite correct target resolution, stale sidebar-state files masking the real issue, helper crashes, and a wider session cwd spiral after layout churn. Confirmed root cause remained the upstream Zellij cwd bug already documented in zellij-org/zellij#3068. Final recovery: restore open_workspace_terminal.nu to call run_pane_orchestrator_command_raw "open_terminal_in_cwd" with the resolved sidebar/workspace cwd, keep sidebar-state freshness fixes, and treat the orchestrator path as the only supported Alt+m implementation.
close_reason: Documented the Alt+m regression spiral, the repeated Zellij cwd bug trigger, and the required orchestrator-owned recovery path.

title: Improve Helix support for Ghostty cursor effects
description: Track work needed to improve Ghostty cursor-effect support when using Helix inside Yazelix. Cover which trail effects currently fail or degrade in Helix, what editor/runtime limitations are involved, and what Yazelix-side adaptations or fallbacks could close the gap with Neovim support.
design: Treat this as a capability-gap analysis between Neovim-style cursor effects and the Helix environment Yazelix actually supports. Identify which Ghostty effects fail in Helix, whether the limitation is in Ghostty triggers, Helix state visibility, or Yazelix-side integration, and whether the right answer is parity, graceful degradation, or explicit unsupported status. Avoid promising full parity if the editor/runtime stack cannot expose the right events reliably.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear support decision for Ghostty cursor effects in Helix: which effects work, which do not, why, and what Yazelix-side adaptation or fallback is justified. If there is a viable improvement path, the bead leaves behind narrower implementation work instead of keeping the problem generic.

title: Add Ghostty cursor effects for Zellij pane changes
description: Explore extending the Ghostty cursor-effect system beyond editor mode changes so Yazelix can trigger visual feedback on Zellij pane focus changes, such as ripple/boom effects when switching panes. Cover feasibility, event plumbing, and whether Ghostty shader triggers can be driven cleanly from Zellij/Yazelix without breaking existing cursor behavior.
design: Treat this as an event-plumbing and UX-value question, not just shader enthusiasm. Determine which pane-focus events Yazelix can observe reliably, how those events could trigger Ghostty effects without colliding with editor-mode effects, and whether the result would actually improve navigation clarity rather than becoming distracting visual noise. Prefer a small number of meaningful pane-change effects over an open-ended effect buffet.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear feasibility and UX-value decision for Ghostty cursor effects on Zellij pane changes, including which events can be observed, what effect shapes are worth trying, and whether the feature should move forward, stay experimental, or be dropped.
close_reason: Not planned: this Ghostty cursor-effect idea does not fit the current product direction and is not worth carrying as an exploratory backlog item.

title: Fix skip_welcome_screen=false being ignored during interactive launch
description: The core.skip_welcome_screen setting is currently being ignored for interactive Yazelix launch flows. Users with skip_welcome_screen = false still do not see the welcome UI.
design: Trace so far: the interactive launch path still calls show_welcome from nushell/scripts/core/start_yazelix_inner.nu, so the setting is not being ignored because the UI code was removed. The likely regression is environment leakage: launch wrappers pass --skip-welcome into the devenv/bootstrap layer, which sets YAZELIX_SKIP_WELCOME=true, and show_welcome treats that as test_mode. The most suspicious paths are nushell/scripts/core/start_yazelix.nu, nushell/scripts/utils/environment_bootstrap.nu, and nushell/scripts/yzx/launch.nu. The likely fix is to keep bootstrap welcome suppression for environment setup while clearing or scoping YAZELIX_SKIP_WELCOME before the real interactive inner session starts.
acceptance_criteria: With core.skip_welcome_screen = false, interactive Yazelix launch shows the welcome screen again. With core.skip_welcome_screen = true, the welcome screen is skipped and the info is logged as before. The fix must not regress env-only, one-shot command, or sweep/test flows that intentionally use YAZELIX_SKIP_WELCOME.
close_reason: Cannot reproduce

title: Replace local pi_agent_rust packaging with upstream flake
description:
Track the follow-on work for removing Yazelix's local `pi_agent_rust` derivation once upstream ships a real flake surface. Codex does not need a matching bead because `openai/codex` already exposes `flake.nix` and `flake.lock`.

Scope:
- monitor upstream `pi_agent_rust` flake support
- when a flake exists, replace the local `buildRustPackage` path in `devenv.nix`
- prefer consuming upstream flake outputs instead of carrying custom packaging in Yazelix
- keep the current `pi_rust` user-facing alias stable while changing only the packaging source under the hood

Success means Yazelix no longer needs to maintain a local Nix package definition for `pi_agent_rust`
close_reason: Obsolete after removing pi_rust support from Yazelix

title: support for https://github.com/just-every/code and pi
close_reason: Completed

title: Add yzx keys command for Yazelix bindings and tool discoverability
description: Add a small `yzx keys` surface that shows Yazelix-owned keybindings by default and offers tool-specific discoverability helpers like `yzx keys yazi` and `yzx keys hx`. Keep it intentionally narrow: the default output should focus on the Yazelix layer, while per-tool subcommands explain how to view upstream keybindings inside Yazi or Helix instead of duplicating their full keymaps.
close_reason: Implemented yzx keys with Yazelix defaults plus yazi and hx discoverability helpers

title: Review docs on IDE integration and document both default-terminal modes
description: Review the editor and IDE integration docs and document both recommended terminal-profile setups: one where yzx env is the default integrated terminal and one where it remains an explicit non-default profile.
design: Treat this as a docs-clarity pass grounded in real user confusion. The docs should show both modes cleanly for VS Code or Cursor: a vanilla terminal as the default with an optional Yazelix profile, and a setup where the integrated terminal defaults directly to yzx env. Keep the examples consistent with the current command flow and explain the tradeoff between preserving a raw shell and dropping straight into the Yazelix environment.
acceptance_criteria: The IDE integration docs clearly present both supported VS Code/Cursor terminal configurations and when to choose each one. The JSON examples are current, minimal, and internally consistent. Validate the documented commands or config snippets and leave logs or notes showing which terminal profile becomes default in each example.
close_reason: Completed

title: Investigate Ctrl+y conflict between Yazelix and Yazi picker recommendations
description: Investigate the possible conflict between Yazelix's current keybinding model and Yazi's documented Tips and Tricks recommendation for using Ctrl+y to drive Yazi as a picker from Zellij/Helix.
design: Treat this as an input-model and docs-coherence investigation, not just a keybinding bikeshed. Verify what Yazi's website currently recommends for picker usage from Zellij or Helix, how that overlaps with Yazelix's existing Ctrl+y or related bindings, what escape sequences and ownership layers are involved, and whether Yazelix should preserve the upstream recommendation, intentionally diverge from it, or document a different integrated workflow. The goal is to avoid a confusing mismatch between Yazelix and the mental model users will pick up from Yazi's own docs.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear documented decision about the Ctrl+y flow for Yazi-as-picker usage in Yazelix. If the current mapping is wrong or confusing, update the keybinding/docs accordingly. Add validation or end-to-end coverage for the affected key path and log the emitted sequence, target action, and final documented behavior.
close_reason: Aligned Yazelix keybindings with Ctrl+y => Yazi mental model and removed stale Space-y Helix default

title: Fix pane drift by adding pane pinning mechanics
description: Add pane pinning mechanics so the managed editor pane keeps a stable position instead of drifting around the stack during normal Yazelix workspace activity. The main user-facing goal is to keep the editor anchored as the top pane in the relevant stack so the workspace remains predictable.
design: Treat this as workspace-structure behavior, not just a one-off layout patch. Define what it means for a pane to be pinned, which pane types deserve pinning first, when pinning should be re-applied after layout changes or pane creation, and how this interacts with user-initiated pane moves. The first target is the managed editor pane: it should remain the top pane in its stack unless the user explicitly breaks that contract. Avoid hidden fights against intentional user actions, but prevent routine Yazelix actions from causing gradual pane drift.
acceptance_criteria: The managed editor pane no longer drifts out of its intended top position during normal workspace operations. Add unit tests for the pinning decision logic and pane-order helpers, integration tests for pane-target selection, and end-to-end scripts that exercise pane creation, focus changes, workspace retargeting, and layout transitions with detailed logs of pane ids, stack order, pin state, and any re-anchoring actions.

title: add option of silent refresh on a specific time (like 4am)
description: Imported GitHub issue #389.
close_reason: Too hard and out of scope for Yazelix right now

title: Add eye-watering welcome screen
description: Redesign the default Yazelix welcome screen so it has a visually striking hero treatment without making startup feel heavy or slow. The default experience should feel beautiful, surprising, and recognizably Yazelix instead of just showing plain welcome text.
design: Preferred direction: use an abstract animated visual, gif, or lightweight animation that frames the welcome content rather than obscuring it. The motion should either stay out of the text area or gracefully darken or fade once the reveal is done so the welcome information remains readable. The Yazelix logo should play a central role if possible, ideally through an animation that resolves into the final Yazelix mark. A strong direction would be a logo-based animation that nods to the Yazi, Helix, and Zellij influences already present in the current logo while landing on the final Yazelix icon form.
acceptance_criteria: The default welcome screen has a visually distinctive animation or image path that feels fast, branded, and readable, with a static fallback users can choose explicitly. The final design should work in narrow and split layouts, preserve welcome-text readability, and feel like Yazelix rather than a random screensaver. Add unit tests for animation-selection or layout-decision helpers, end-to-end startup/render validation for normal and narrow layouts, performance measurements for startup impact, and detailed capture logs for whichever animation or asset pipeline is chosen.
notes:
Candidate visual directions: a magician casting colorful ASCII magic; a colored liquid animation, ASCII or non-ASCII depending quality/perf tradeoffs; a Game of Life style simulation; particle-life style motion similar to OfficialCodeNoodles/Particle-Life-Simulation; and continuous-life variants like Lenia or SmoothLife for a more fluid organic feel. Longer-term direction: ship a small curated set of beautiful default welcome animations and choose one at random on each start. Existing context: Yazelix already has a star animation that looks nice, but it degrades badly in vertical splits or lower-width layouts, so any new direction should adapt better to constrained dimensions. Imported overlap from GitHub issue #263: replacing the current welcome art with the Yazelix logo and making the result work well on half screens and narrow layouts is part of this same feature, not a separate track.
2026-04-20 tracker hygiene: moved out of in_progress. This broader welcome-screen feature is still dependency-driven and there is no active implementation in the current tree, so it should remain unclaimed until one of its concrete visual children is actually being worked.

title: yzx tutor
description: Add a tutor surface under `yzx tutor` that helps users learn both the native tools Yazelix ships and the Yazelix-specific workflow layered on top of them.
design: Split the feature in two parts. Part one: thin wrappers for native tutors that already exist, specifically `yzx tutor hx` -> `hx --tutor` and `yzx tutor nu` -> Nushell `tutor`. Part two: Yazelix-specific markdown tutors for `yzx`, `yazi`, and `zellij`. `yzx tutor yzx` should teach the Yazelix mental model and core commands. `yzx tutor yazi` should explain what changes in the Yazelix-managed Yazi experience compared with vanilla Yazi. `yzx tutor zellij` should explain what changes in the Yazelix-managed Zellij experience compared with vanilla Zellij. Prefer opening concise curated markdown guides over building a live demo or interactive checklist system first.
acceptance_criteria: `yzx tutor hx` and `yzx tutor nu` launch the existing upstream tutors. `yzx tutor yzx`, `yzx tutor yazi`, and `yzx tutor zellij` open maintained Yazelix-specific guides. The tutor surface feels coherent: every subcommand either teaches a real upstream tutor or opens curated Yazelix-specific onboarding content. Avoid shallow website-link wrappers with no Yazelix adaptation. Add unit tests for command routing and target resolution, docs-existence checks for the markdown guides, and end-to-end smoke tests that log the resolved tutor path or launched command.
close_reason: Completed: shipped the yzx tutor MVP with a Yazelix overview plus hx/nu upstream tutor wrappers; richer structured tutor flow moved to yazelix-3jw

title: detect first time running yazelix and give a helpful message explaining the zjstatus permission thingy
description: Imported GitHub issue #327.
close_reason: First-run permission guidance is now covered in the welcome flow and docs

title: Allow and document Starship customization
description: Allow users to customize Starship in a supported way and document where the boundary lies between Yazelix-managed prompt behavior and user-owned prompt configuration.
design: This should preserve Yazelix's managed defaults while giving users a real override path. Decide whether the right model is merge-based, file-inclusion-based, or a simpler user hook, then document exactly what is stable and what can drift. Avoid a design where Starship becomes a second uncontrolled config island with no compatibility story.
acceptance_criteria: Users can apply Starship customization without forking core Yazelix files, and the docs explain the supported customization path clearly. Add unit tests for merge or override helpers, integration tests for the chosen generation path, and end-to-end checks that verify the final prompt config is generated as expected, with detailed logs of the merge source and output path.
close_reason: Not planned

title: Expand user config control where the merge cost is worth it
description: This is about giving users more control over generated tool configs without turning Yazelix into a fully generic config engine. The clearest remaining gap is terminal config merging: users should be able to layer more of their own terminal-emulator config on top of Yazelix-managed config when the merge model is safe and maintainable.
design: Treat this as an evaluation-first feature, not a promise to toml-ize every config surface. Start by identifying the low-hanging configuration areas where merging is practical and the maintenance cost is acceptable. The current likely target is terminal config merging. Keep the broader idea of a more fully controlled profile as direction, but do not expand this bead to cover `devenv` config control; that should stay in a separate bead. The bar is whether the extra user control is worth the implementation and support complexity.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear decision and scope for which config surfaces should support richer user merging. If terminal config merging is worth it, Yazelix should support it in a maintainable way. If not, the bead should still produce a documented rationale and narrower control model rather than vague ambition about controlling everything. Any implementation path should include unit tests for merge semantics, end-to-end generation checks for the affected config surfaces, and detailed logs showing the user input, managed defaults, and final merged output.
close_reason: Not planned: first-class Ghostty/WezTerm support should expand through explicit Yazelix-owned options and the existing terminal.config_mode ownership boundary, not through a generic terminal-config merge engine.
