notes:
Implemented on 2026-04-04:
- Added structured startup profiling records under ~/.local/share/yazelix/profiles/startup/ via nushell/scripts/utils/startup_profile.nu
- Upgraded yzx dev profile to drive the real startup path through nushell/scripts/utils/profile.nu instead of shallow helper-only timings
- Instrumented real startup boundaries in start_yazelix.nu, environment_bootstrap.nu, environment.nu, and start_yazelix_inner.nu
- Added a maintainer schema/helper regression plus an end-to-end harness regression in test_yzx_maintainer.nu
- Updated docs in docs/yzx_cli.md and README.md to describe the real report artifact and clear-cache semantics

Measured findings from local runs:
- Warm current-terminal startup was dominated by inner.generate_zellij_config (~2.9-3.3s on this machine)
- Cold/rebuild-heavy startup was dominated by bootstrap.devenv.shell_entry (~16s on this machine)
- Cold path also showed two distinct shellhook process invocations during shell entry; the profiler now surfaces their separate process contexts in the report instead of collapsing them into one ambiguous duplicate block
close_reason: Implemented structured end-to-end startup profiling and documentation

title: Yazi: open the current file or directory in the system file manager
description: Add a Yazelix-managed Yazi action that opens the current file or directory in the host system file manager, on Linux and ideally macOS too.
design: This should start with an investigation because Yazi may already support part of this flow natively. Verify what Yazi already provides, then decide whether Yazelix only needs to surface it through a keybinding or whether Yazelix needs a small wrapper for Linux and macOS. The user-facing goal is simple: from Yazi, hit a keybinding and open the current location in the platform file manager. This is likely a low-hanging fruit and a good candidate to tackle soon once planning cleanup is done.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear supported way from Yazi to open the current file or directory in the system file manager. If Yazi already supports it cleanly, Yazelix documents or binds that flow. If not, Yazelix adds the minimal integration needed for Linux and macOS. Add unit tests for opener selection and platform dispatch helpers, integration tests for keybinding routing, and end-to-end validation that logs the selected path, platform opener invoked, and fallback behavior when the opener is unavailable.
close_reason: Not planned

title: zjstatus: allow a small user-configurable text slot
description: Give users a small piece of personal space in the Yazelix zjstatus bar where they can define a short custom string.
design: Keep this intentionally narrow. The likely placement is to the left of the `YAZELIX` segment. This should not become a full templating or arbitrary string-programming system for the entire status bar. A simple configurable text field in `yazelix.toml` is enough if the result feels stable and readable.
acceptance_criteria: Users can set a short custom string for zjstatus, and it appears in a defined place near the YAZELIX branding without destabilizing the rest of the status bar design. Add unit tests for config parsing and rendering helpers, width-budget or rendering checks, and end-to-end validation that logs the configured value, rendered placement, and truncation or hiding behavior on narrow layouts.
close_reason: Completed

title: Add Tailwind CSS LSP to JS/TS pack
description: Add the Tailwind CSS language server to the JavaScript and TypeScript language-pack story so modern frontend projects have the expected completion and diagnostics out of the box.
design: Scope this to the curated pack model rather than ad-hoc per-user tweaks. Decide whether Tailwind LSP belongs directly in the JS or TS pack or in a narrower frontend or web pack, then wire the package, editor integration, and docs accordingly. Be explicit about how this behaves for projects that do not use Tailwind so the default does not feel like noisy overreach.
acceptance_criteria: Projects that use Tailwind get a working LSP path from the relevant Yazelix pack, and users can understand why it is included. Add unit tests for pack membership and resolver logic, config-generation checks for the chosen editor integration, and an end-to-end smoke test in a minimal frontend fixture with detailed LSP startup logs.
notes: Implemented by adding tailwindcss-language-server to the ts pack in both yazelix_default.toml and home_manager/module.nix, documenting the pack change, and adding a ts-pack parity test. Broader frontend-pack/toolchain questions were split into yazelix-7zs.
close_reason: Completed

title: Add emoji support (status/UI)
description: Add an emoji insertion path inside Yazelix instead of treating emoji support as a vague cosmetic request. The main user value is being able to insert emoji quickly from the terminal workspace, either through a lightweight emoji picker keybinding or by exposing a good emoji CLI in the environment if that gives a cleaner result.
design: Prefer the smallest UX that feels native in Yazelix. First evaluate whether a dependable emoji CLI is enough or whether Yazelix should wire a dedicated keybinding that opens an emoji menu or picker. Keep the feature opt-in or low-friction, avoid disturbing existing keymaps, and make sure the experience works in the managed shell/editor workflow instead of only in isolation.
acceptance_criteria: Users can insert emoji from a Yazelix session without leaving the workspace. The chosen flow is documented and ergonomic enough to use regularly. If a keybinding or menu approach is used, it avoids obvious key conflicts and fails clearly when the underlying emoji tool is unavailable. Add unit tests for picker or command-selection logic, integration tests for the picker or CLI wiring, and an end-to-end script that records the invoked path and resulting insertion behavior with detailed logs.
close_reason: Not planned: low-value compared with other work; any useful residue is better handled as optional docs rather than a first-class Yazelix feature.

title: Add a Helix note-taking section
description: Add a focused documentation section on note taking in Helix for Yazelix users, with special attention to Markdown-heavy workflows, Obsidian-style vaults, and markdown-oxide support.
design: This should not become a generic Helix note-taking essay. Focus on the practical Yazelix angle: how to work on Markdown notes inside the managed editor, what language tooling matters, how markdown-oxide fits in, and what an Obsidian user needs to know when living in a terminal-first workspace instead of a GUI vault app.
acceptance_criteria: The docs give a clear path for a Yazelix user who wants a serious Markdown or Obsidian-adjacent workflow in Helix. The section explains the role of markdown-oxide, shows a realistic note-taking setup, and avoids hand-wavy editor advice. Verify the referenced tooling and commands in the current environment and keep examples and logs concrete enough that regressions in docs examples would be obvious.
close_reason: Not planned

title: Fix Alt-( and Alt-) keybindings in Helix under Zellij
description: Fix the Alt-( and Alt-) Helix keybindings when running under Zellij so these editor shortcuts behave reliably instead of getting lost or mangled by the terminal and Zellij layer.
design: Treat this as an input-stack bug spanning terminal, Zellij, and Helix. First confirm what escape sequences are emitted in the supported terminal set, then decide whether the fix belongs in Zellij keybindings, terminal keyboard protocol settings, Helix mappings, or some narrower compatibility layer. Avoid a fix that only works in one terminal if Yazelix claims broader support.
acceptance_criteria: The affected Alt bindings work in the supported Yazelix terminal stack or the docs state the exact caveat when they cannot. Add unit tests for escape-sequence normalization or mapping helpers where they exist, regression tests or scripted input capture, and end-to-end validation with detailed logs of the emitted sequences and the final behavior inside Helix.
close_reason: Closed after fresh manual verification confirmed Alt-( and Alt-) currently work in the supported Yazelix stack.

title: UX: floating pane option for lazygit/AI TUI
description: Offer an optional floating-pane workflow for transient TUIs such as lazygit or AI terminals when that better fits the workspace than dedicating a persistent split.
design: Keep this focused on the tools that benefit from temporary, attention-stealing use rather than making every pane type floatable by default. Evaluate how floating panes should interact with focus, sizing, closing, cwd inheritance, and the existing managed-pane model so the feature feels intentional instead of bolted on.
acceptance_criteria: Users can launch the supported transient TUIs in a predictable floating-pane flow without breaking normal pane management. Add unit tests for the command and config routing and end-to-end scripts that open, focus, and close the floating TUI path while logging pane ids, cwd, and focus transitions.
close_reason: Shipped configurable managed floating popup workflow for transient TUIs

title: Keep the Git Yazi plugins state from getting stale
description: Keep the Git-related Yazi plugin state and the related Starship Git status from going stale when repo state changes inside the same directory. Prefer narrow event-driven refreshes so users do not need to leave and re-enter a directory just to get current repo information, and keep any added Git styling subtle enough to preserve normal sidebar readability.
design: Treat this as an event-driven freshness contract, not a generic polling problem. First verify which Git-related Yazi surfaces go stale while staying in the same directory, then prefer narrow refresh triggers when Yazelix returns attention to Yazi: sidebar focus regain and popup close back into Yazi are the first-class candidates. Do not add background polling or a global heartbeat unless a clean event-driven refresh primitive proves insufficient. If Yazi exposes a real refresh or file-list invalidation action, use that directly; otherwise stop before adding a fragile keystroke-injection hack. If Git state is tinted in the sidebar, prefer foreground text styling over row-background fills so hover and selection states remain legible.
acceptance_criteria: Git-related Yazi indicators no longer require a directory change to become fresh again after repo state changes inside the same directory. The initial fix is limited to high-signal events: entering the sidebar pane and closing the popup pane back into Yazi. Defend the trigger contract with focused tests where practical, and only proceed if the Yazi-side refresh mechanism stays simple and maintainable. Reject background polling, broad layout or menu triggers, or input-injection hacks unless later evidence proves they are necessary. Any Git tinting should remain a foreground text treatment that coexists cleanly with existing hover and selection visuals.
close_reason: Completed: added event-driven Yazi sidebar refresh on popup close, sidebar focus, initial open, and Yazi navigation, plus distinct Git styling for staged versus unstaged changes and Starship refresh on sidebar open.

title: Fix status bar overflow on narrow layouts
description: Fix status-bar overflow and fit problems so the bar remains usable on narrower screens instead of clipping or breaking layout.
design: Solve this as a responsive-layout problem, not by blindly truncating everything. Audit which widgets or segments dominate width, define a graceful compaction or hiding strategy, and make sure the status surface still communicates the important state at common narrow widths and split sizes.
acceptance_criteria: The status bar fits and remains readable on supported narrow layouts. Add unit tests for width-budget and widget-selection helpers, rendering or layout regression checks, and end-to-end validation in multiple terminal widths with detailed logs of the computed widget set, widths, and any truncation or hiding decisions.
close_reason: Cancelled: this is no longer a product direction Yazelix intends to pursue.

title: Define the behavior for editors opened from the shell instead of from Yazi
description: Decide how Yazelix should treat editors opened directly from the shell instead of through the managed Yazi-to-editor flow, and implement the chosen behavior coherently.
design: This is a workspace-model decision, not just a bug fix. Figure out what state Yazelix can and cannot assume when a user starts Helix or another editor manually from a shell pane: cwd ownership, pane identity, managed editor tracking, sidebar interactions, and open-file routing. The result may be explicit support, explicit non-support, or a narrower managed-handoff model, but it must be intentional.
acceptance_criteria: The behavior of shell-opened editors is explicitly defined and documented: only Yazelix-managed `editor` panes are reused by managed editor flows, while shell-opened editors remain ordinary panes. Add pane-policy unit tests for managed pane detection and focus context, routing regression checks for Yazi open behavior, and user-facing doctor/docs wording that matches the contract.
close_reason: Defined the shell-opened-editor contract, documented it, added Yazi routing regressions, and added plugin pane-policy unit tests for managed editor detection and focus context.

title: Make new panes open on the active Yazi project path
description: Make new Yazelix-managed panes inherit the project path implied by the current Yazi context when that is what the user is clearly working on.
design: The feature should strengthen the workspace model instead of creating hidden cwd magic. Define when a clicked or entered Yazi directory should become the effective workspace root for new panes, how that interacts with the stable tab workspace cwd, and when the user's current shell/editor state should override the file-browser context. Keep the behavior predictable and document the mental model clearly.
acceptance_criteria: New managed panes open on the expected project path when the user is working through Yazi. Add unit tests for workspace-root selection helpers, integration tests for workspace-root resolution, and end-to-end coverage that clicks into directories, opens new panes, and logs the Yazi path, tab workspace root, and resulting pane cwd.
notes: Root cause: Zellij with default_shell=nu did not reliably preserve the requested pane cwd for this path, which matches upstream bug zellij-org/zellij#3068. Final fix: resolve the live sidebar/Yazi cwd in Nushell, then ask the pane orchestrator to open a normal pane directly in that cwd. Rejected approaches: command panes caused the exit prompt UI, and injected cd commands worked but were too ugly.
close_reason: Fixed Alt+m pane opening to resolve the live Yazi/sidebar cwd in Nushell and delegate normal pane creation to the pane orchestrator, avoiding both wrong cwd fallback and command-pane exit UI

title: replace bottom bar with hideable one
description: Replace the current bottom bar with a hideable alternative that keeps the useful workspace status without permanently stealing screen space.
design: This is a layout and information-density problem, not just a cosmetics tweak. Evaluate approaches such as a second zjstatus instance, a hideable bar, or a more context-sensitive status surface, and optimize for narrow layouts, clarity, and discoverability. The result should preserve the important information users rely on while making it easier to reclaim vertical space.
acceptance_criteria: Users can hide or collapse the bottom status surface without losing the important workspace cues permanently. Add unit tests for layout-selection or visibility-state helpers, layout or rendering regression checks for narrow widths, and end-to-end validation of show and hide behavior with detailed logs of the active layout state and visible widgets.
notes: 2026-04-26 subsystem review: hideable bar work should build on yazelix_bar after the static widget registry/zjstatus boundary exists. Do not lose current workspace cues, CPU/RAM widgets, or custom text while reclaiming vertical space.

title: [Bug]: The desktop launcher doesn't work with fish
description: Make the desktop launcher work reliably when the user's default shell is fish instead of depending on shell-specific assumptions that only happen to work for Bash-like setups.
design: Treat the desktop entry as a real supported entrypoint, not a maintainer-only convenience. Verify what environment and shell state the desktop launch path actually has, remove assumptions that leak from interactive shell startup, and make the launcher robust regardless of whether the user prefers fish or another shell. Keep the fix aligned with the broader push away from stale env dependencies.
acceptance_criteria: Launching Yazelix from the desktop entry works for fish users without requiring a manual desktop-file patch. Add unit tests for launcher command-construction helpers where they exist, a launcher smoke test or reproducible script that simulates the desktop-entry environment, and logs that make shell-specific regressions easy to diagnose.
close_reason: Replaced copied desktop asset flow with generated desktop install/uninstall commands, aligned Home Manager on the POSIX launcher, and verified the supported launcher contracts.

title: [Bug]: Starting Yazelix with yzx launch --here --path doesn't work
description: Fix the yzx launch --here --path flow so it opens the intended directory and does not break keybindings or leave the resulting session unresponsive.
design: This is a real multi-symptom bug: wrong startup path, bad session responsiveness, and confusing interactions with user remaps and non-default shells. The fix should trace how the here-path flow sets cwd, initializes the session, and wires the managed plugins so that the path override does not corrupt the normal control layer.
acceptance_criteria: Launching with --here --path opens the requested location and keeps the session responsive. Add unit tests for the command builder and path-handling helpers, integration coverage for the launch argument plumbing, and end-to-end tests that launch into a custom path under at least one non-default shell and capture detailed logs for cwd, plugin readiness, and keybinding responsiveness.
close_reason: Closed after fresh verification showed non-persistent --here --path launches already pass the requested cwd through to Zellij, while existing persistent sessions intentionally reattach and ignore --path with a clear warning.

title: [Bug]: Yazi user configuration not taken into account
description: Honor user-supplied Yazi configuration when generating the merged runtime config instead of silently dropping the user customization path.
design: Decide what the supported merge boundary is for Yazi user config, then implement it consistently. The generated config path should combine Yazelix-managed defaults with the supported user-owned overrides in a way that is predictable and documented, without pretending to merge unsupported pieces silently.
acceptance_criteria: Supported user Yazi configuration is present in the final generated runtime config. Add unit tests for the merge logic and end-to-end validation that inspects the final Yazi config output, with logs showing the user source path, merge inputs, and resulting generated file.
close_reason: Completed

title: Packaging: rework Home Manager integration for the package-ready runtime model
description: Rework the Home Manager module so it matches the package-ready runtime model instead of assuming a repo checkout and repo-owned runtime assets.
design: Define exactly what the Home Manager module should own versus what Yazelix should generate at runtime. The module should set up the install path, stable config hooks, and any package/runtime references cleanly, while avoiding assumptions that only make sense for a live clone under ~/.config/yazelix. Keep option parity with the default config model where that still makes sense, but do not drag repo-first behavior into the packaged world.
acceptance_criteria: The Home Manager path remains first-class under the package-ready runtime model. Add unit tests for option translation and path derivation, evaluation tests for module options and defaults, and end-to-end Home Manager install or activation smoke tests with detailed logs showing where configs, assets, and runtime state land.
notes: The emerging testing model is: runtime behavior stays in the normal Yazelix/devenv lane, while Home Manager module correctness is exercised through Nix/Home Manager evaluation. A flake-native smoke check under home_manager/flake.nix provides a clean bridge: yzx dev test can call nix eval on a focused attr for the default regression path, while broader nix flake check usage can remain a CI or dedicated package-readiness tool. After measuring the cached smoke check, there is no current need for a separate fast-path toggle in yzx dev test.
close_reason: Completed

title: Packaging: add a first-party flake interface around Yazelix
description: Add a thin first-party flake interface around the existing Yazelix runtime so users can consume it through a standard Nix surface without creating a second environment definition.
design: Keep devenv.nix and the runtime model as the source of truth. The flake should expose useful outputs such as a dev shell, launcher or app entrypoints, and module exports where appropriate, but it should stay thin and avoid reimplementing the runtime separately. The point is discoverability and integration, not a forked architecture.
acceptance_criteria: Users can consume Yazelix through a first-party flake surface that wraps the real runtime instead of duplicating it. Add unit tests for any flake-output helper logic, flake evaluation tests, and smoke tests for the exported outputs, with logs that make it obvious which runtime assets and entrypoints are being exercised.
notes:
This bead is now the implementation track for the install decision captured in `yazelix-aa24` and `docs/specs/one_command_install_ux.md`.

Target front door:
  nix run github:luccahuguet/yazelix#install

Keep the flake thin: it should expose installer/launcher surfaces around the existing runtime model, not create a second environment definition.
close_reason: Top-level flake interface and install front door implemented

title: Yazi: remove extra step after zoxide jump
description: Remove the awkward extra step after a Yazi zoxide jump so users can jump to a directory and act on it immediately instead of backing out one level first.
design: Preserve the speed of the current zoxide integration but fix the UX mismatch. After a jump, the user should be able to open the selected target naturally instead of manually navigating back to a parent just to re-select the folder. The design should stay consistent with Yazi semantics and avoid surprising behavior for file versus directory targets.
acceptance_criteria: After a zoxide jump, the next action on the selected target feels direct and natural. Add unit tests for jump-resolution or target-selection helpers, integration tests for the jump-resolution path, and end-to-end Yazi flow coverage with logs showing the resolved target, resulting cursor location, and open action.
close_reason: Completed via direct-open Yazi zoxide flow on Alt+z with managed-editor reuse/new-pane fallback.

title: Zellij keybindings: replace awkward Ctrl+Alt combos with easier workspace bindings
description: Replace the ugliest Ctrl+Alt workspace bindings with easier combinations where possible without wrecking the editor-first feel of Yazelix.
design: Audit the current Zellij-facing bindings, identify the high-frequency awkward ones, and remap only the places where the ergonomic win is real. Favor easier workspace-oriented bindings such as Alt combinations or other low-conflict keys, but keep Helix, Yazi, shells, and terminal-emulator behavior as hard constraints. This should be a surgical ergonomics pass, not a full keybinding philosophy rewrite.
acceptance_criteria: The highest-friction workspace bindings become easier to use without introducing major conflicts. Add unit tests for keymap generation or binding-selection helpers, keymap validation checks where possible, and end-to-end tests in the supported terminal stack that exercise the changed bindings and log the emitted sequences, Zellij actions, and any conflict notes.

title: Website launch at yazelix.com
description: Create a thin but credible public website for Yazelix so the project has a clear front door beyond the repository README.
design: Keep the site focused on what Yazelix is, who it is for, how it differs from a plain editor or shell setup, and where new users should go next. This should complement the repo docs instead of competing with them. Favor maintainability and strong messaging over building a complex web app.
acceptance_criteria: yazelix.com becomes a credible first stop for new users, with clear installation and docs entrypoints and visuals that match the current product. Add unit tests for any generated route or content-selection helpers, end-to-end route and outbound-link validation, and detailed logs for the final validated site structure, screenshots, and key flows.
notes: 2026-04-26 direction update: the website should become the live documentation home, not just a marketing splash page. Keep the first launch thin, but structure it so docs routes can replace GitHub README as the detailed user guide over time.

title: Prototype explicit Yazelix session save and restore UX
description: Prototype the smallest explicit session save/restore surface that fits Yazelix: likely a yzx session family or a Zellij-session-manager handoff that calls Zellij save-session/restore primitives while preserving Yazelix per-window config snapshot expectations.
design: Do not reintroduce persistent_sessions as a config toggle. The first implementation should be explicit, inspectable, and compatible with multiple concurrent Yazelix windows.
acceptance_criteria: There is a working prototype or a documented no-go result for explicit save/restore, including how it handles restored workspace roots, managed panes, and config snapshot/version mismatch diagnostics.

title: Add in-repo standalone yazelix_screen binary and flake output
description: Create the first concrete standalone yazelix_screen surface inside the Yazelix repo. This follows the yazelix-4xt8 decision: keep yzx screen as the current outside-Zellij surface for now, and only add a true standalone surface as an in-repo binary/flake output before considering any separate repo.
design: Package-first and in-repo. Add a small standalone binary that reuses the yazelix_screen crate and exposes the retained screen styles without requiring a Yazelix session, Zellij, or the full yzx command surface. Prefer a flake package/app such as .#yazelix_screen over shell hooks or terminal-specific snippets. Keep config minimal and explicit: terminal size, style selection, keypress exit, resize behavior, and defaults should be owned by this binary rather than borrowed implicitly from user Yazelix config. Do not mutate terminal configs or user files. Do not extract to a separate repository in this slice.
acceptance_criteria: 1. A user can build or run an in-repo standalone yazelix_screen package/app from the flake. 2. The binary renders at least the retained animated styles without requiring Zellij or a Yazelix session. 3. The binary exits cleanly on keypress and preserves terminal state. 4. The package/app boundary is documented as a preview or supported surface with explicit non-goals. 5. Existing yzx screen behavior remains unchanged.
notes: Dependency decision before implementation: no new production or dev crates. Reuse the existing yazelix_screen crate and its existing crossterm dependency for raw mode, key polling, terminal size, and alternate-screen output. Build CLI parsing, style dispatch, and frame loop in-house/std because the standalone surface is small and adding a CLI framework would be unnecessary package surface. Rejected alternatives: depend on yazelix_core front-door command code, because that would pull the standalone binary back into Yazelix session/config semantics; add clap, because the command has one optional style plus help; shell wrapper, because the requested surface is a real binary/flake output. Packaging impact: add one Rust package output and app, not to the default Yazelix runtime closure.
close_reason: Completed: added standalone yazelix_screen Rust binary, .#yazelix_screen flake package/app, and canonical distribution contract. Verified cargo test -p yazelix_screen, cargo fmt --check, nix build .#yazelix_screen, nix run .#yazelix_screen -- --help, and validate-contracts.

title: Add in-repo Ghostty cursor shader export package
description: Add the first supported standalone Ghostty cursor distribution path inside the Yazelix flake/package surface.
design: Package complete generated Ghostty cursor shader files plus example config snippets under a stable share path. Do not mutate user Ghostty configs and do not export Yazelix runtime reroll or yzx cursor sidecar behavior.
acceptance_criteria: A Nix user can install or build the cursor asset export; the installed output contains complete shader files and examples; docs show the custom-shader path; validation confirms exported shader files exist.
close_reason: Completed: added .#ghostty_cursor_shaders as an in-repo flake package that generates standalone Ghostty cursor shader assets from the Yazelix cursor materialization path, ships example custom-shader config, and documents the canonical contract. Verified with nix build .#ghostty_cursor_shaders and validate-contracts.

title: Swap runtime TOML tool from Taplo to Tombi
description: Replace the shipped runtime TOML tool with Tombi after the formatter prototype and support-surface rename gates pass.
design: Scope packaging/runtime_deps.nix, packaging/mk_runtime_tree.nix, packaging/repo_source.nix, docs, package-size notes, managed support file shipping, and validation commands. Do not carry both Taplo and Tombi in the default runtime.
acceptance_criteria: Runtime package exports Tombi instead of Taplo; docs and diagnostics no longer claim Taplo support; package closure delta is recorded; formatter/lint commands are validated against the accepted repo TOML corpus.
close_reason: Completed runtime TOML tooling swap: Tombi is packaged/exported, tombi.toml is the managed config, .taplo.toml is removed, docs/specs updated, and runtime build verified

title: Rename Taplo support to generic TOML tooling support
description: Rename Taplo-specific managed support concepts so Yazelix does not expose Taplo-shaped state names after a future TOML tooling swap.
design: Audit active_config_surface, doctor/onboard diagnostics, fixtures, docs, and runtime-tree support paths. Prefer generic TOML tooling support names while preserving explicit, actionable errors.
acceptance_criteria: Taplo-specific names remain only where the current tool is intentionally referenced; user-facing support paths and diagnostics are ready for a future Tombi swap without stale Taplo wording.
close_reason: Completed generic TOML tooling support rename; concrete runtime tool remains unchanged for the follow-up swap bead

title: Prototype Tombi config and formatting migration
description: Prototype a Tombi config and measure formatter churn across the repo TOML corpus without changing the shipped runtime dependency.
design: Work in a dedicated formatting/prototype bead. Compare Tombi against current repo state and current Taplo output, decide whether to accept a one-time TOML formatting commit, and document unsupported Taplo formatting knobs explicitly.
acceptance_criteria: Temporary or committed prototype clearly reports touched files and diff size; no runtime package dependency changes; recommendation says whether a later package swap is safe.
notes: Prototype completed. Added tombi.toml with a bounded Yazelix-owned TOML corpus: root TOML, config_metadata, docs/upgrade_notes.toml, and user_configs; excluded vendored Yazi flavors and Rust Cargo manifests. Applied Tombi formatting to the accepted corpus. Full default Tombi run would touch 36/49 tracked TOML files; configured corpus avoids unrelated vendor/Cargo churn. Verified tombi format --check, validate-specs, and git diff --check.
close_reason: Completed

title: Evaluate standalone yazelix_screen distribution for terminal users
description:
Evaluate whether yazelix_screen should become usable outside Yazelix and outside Zellij, as a standalone terminal welcome/splash/status experience for any terminal user

## Context
yazelix_screen already has product value as a terminal-native visual surface
The experience may not need to be tied to a Zellij plugin or a full Yazelix session
A standalone path could make the screen useful for Ghostty, WezTerm, Kitty, plain shell startup, demos, and other terminal workflows
design:
Start by separating renderer, runtime host, configuration, and session integration concerns
Evaluate whether standalone use should be a binary, flake output, shell hook, terminal integration snippet, or future separate repo
Keep Zellij integration as one host target rather than the core identity if the boundary is clean
Do not extract until ownership, release cadence, and config compatibility are explicit
acceptance_criteria:
Decision identifies which parts of yazelix_screen are generic terminal UI and which are Yazelix or Zellij specific
Decision compares in-repo standalone binary, flake output, shell integration, and separate repository options
Decision defines a minimal supported standalone contract for terminal users
Decision records whether extraction is worth doing now, later, or not at all
notes:
2026-04-28 decision: keep standalone yazelix_screen distribution as a staged package-first possibility, not a separate extraction now.

Generic terminal UI today:
- rust_core/yazelix_screen owns animation state, frame generation, alternate-screen rendering primitives, terminal-size reads, and ANSI row-addressed output.
- The crate depends only on crossterm, so the renderer itself is not Zellij-specific.

Yazelix-specific surface today:
- The public command is yzx screen, implemented in yazelix_core front-door code.
- yzx screen resolves Yazelix style names, reads session/config facts for Game of Life cell style, and belongs to the Yazelix command surface.
- The supported standalone-ish surface already available is running package-provided yzx screen [STYLE] outside Zellij in any terminal.

Zellij-specific coupling:
- None in yazelix_screen itself.
- Zellij only consumes the style contract indirectly for the pane-orchestrator screen saver config.

Option comparison:
- In-repo binary/flake output: best next step if we want a true standalone preview, because it can reuse the crate while making config defaults explicit.
- Current yzx screen command: good enough as the minimal supported contract for Yazelix package users outside Zellij.
- Shell hook: too invasive for first distribution; it would create startup ownership questions before the binary contract is proven.
- Terminal-specific snippets: poor fit because the screen is an interactive process, not static terminal config.
- Separate repo: premature until an in-repo standalone binary proves config, release, and support boundaries.

Minimal supported contract now: yzx screen [STYLE] from the Yazelix package may be used outside Zellij, enters the alternate screen, exits on keypress, adapts to terminal size, and uses Yazelix style/config semantics. No separate any-terminal package or separate repo is promised yet.
close_reason: Completed: evaluated the renderer/runtime/config/session boundaries. Decision is to keep yzx screen as the current package-provided outside-Zellij surface, avoid a separate repo now, and only consider an in-repo standalone binary/flake output after the config/release contract is explicit.

title: Evaluate standalone Ghostty cursor preset distribution
description:
Evaluate whether Yazelix cursor presets should be packaged for standalone Ghostty users, with an eventual extraction path to a separate repo if the boundary is clean

## Context
The new yazelix_cursors.toml sidecar makes Ghostty cursor themes more product-like and less tied to the full Yazelix runtime
Standalone Ghostty users may benefit from the presets without adopting Yazelix
The evaluation should decide whether this belongs as docs, an installable artifact, a flake output, or a separate repository
design:
Start delete-first: identify what parts are Yazelix-specific and what can be treated as generic Ghostty cursor assets
Prefer a small distribution contract before adding automation
Do not split a repository until maintenance ownership, versioning, and update flow are explicit
acceptance_criteria:
Decision records the target audience and supported install path
Decision compares in-repo export, flake output, and separate repository options
Decision defines which cursor assets/config are generic enough to publish
Decision names the maintenance and release boundary if extraction proceeds
notes: Decision recorded in docs/specs/standalone_ghostty_cursor_distribution.md. Recommendation: expose complete Ghostty cursor shader assets through an in-repo flake/package export first, keep yazelix_cursors.toml/random reroll/Kitty fallback/Yazelix materialization as Yazelix-owned, and defer a separate repository until the export boundary and release ownership prove stable. Follow-ups: yazelix-lo9h.1 and yazelix-lo9h.2.
close_reason: Completed

title: Evaluate migrating TOML tooling from Taplo to Tombi
description: Evaluate whether Yazelix should replace Taplo with Tombi for TOML formatting, validation, editor/LSP guidance, and managed config-support packaging. Upstream check on 2026-04-27: Taplo's GitHub page does not show it as archived, but its latest listed Taplo CLI release is 0.10.0 from 2025-05-23; Tombi presents itself as a TOML formatter/linter/language server and lists latest release v0.9.24 from 2026-04-26.
design: Decision-first scope. Audit every Yazelix Taplo surface before changing dependencies: runtime_deps.nix, mk_runtime_tree exported commands, repo_source.nix, .taplo.toml shipping/sync, active_config_surface managed Taplo support, doctor/onboard messaging, docs, and tests. Compare Tombi CLI/LSP/config semantics against current Taplo behavior, especially formatter output stability on yazelix_default.toml, config_metadata/*.toml, docs/upgrade_notes.toml, and Home Manager examples. Prefer a migration only if Tombi can preserve or improve the maintained contract without broad formatter churn or weaker validation. If migration is chosen, rename managed support concepts deliberately instead of leaving Taplo-named paths in the product surface.
acceptance_criteria: Produce a written recommendation with evidence from upstream status, package availability in the locked nixpkgs input, CLI command parity, formatting diff size, schema/config support, LSP/editor impact, and package/runtime closure impact. Include a no-migration option if Taplo remains acceptable. If migration is recommended, file follow-up implementation beads with exact file scopes, compatibility notes, docs changes, and validation commands. Do not replace Taplo in the same bead.
notes: Decision recorded in docs/specs/toml_tooling_taplo_tombi_evaluation.md. Initial outcome was a staged migration, not a blind dependency swap. Follow-ups yazelix-zz0k.1, yazelix-zz0k.2, and yazelix-zz0k.3 are complete: the bounded Tombi formatter corpus is accepted, managed support naming is generic TOML tooling, the runtime now packages/exports Tombi, tombi.toml is the managed support config, and .taplo.toml is removed from the shipped source.
close_reason: Completed

title: Gate boids spatial hashing behind measured flock size need
description: Avoid adding spatial indexing to boids until the current O(n^2) implementation has a measured user-visible performance problem at the flock sizes Yazelix actually renders.
design: Keep the current simple Vec<Boid> model by default. If flock size or frame budget grows, add a tiny in-house uniform-grid spatial hash scoped to yazelix_screen. Do not add a crate for this unless the in-house implementation becomes nontrivial or risky.
acceptance_criteria: There is a benchmark or frame-budget check showing whether current O(n^2) boids is sufficient; if insufficient, an in-house spatial hash is added with tests for neighbor lookup correctness; if sufficient, the bead is closed with measured evidence and no code added.

title: Clean stale docs that still describe deleted yzx edit/import Nu modules
notes:
Several docs/spec inventory files still mention nushell/scripts/yzx/edit.nu and import.nu as live Nu owners, but those modules are gone and edit/import are Rust-owned. This did not block CI after the contract fix, but the docs are stale and should be cleaned in a follow-up pass.

2026-04-26 note: keep this as a docs-only cleanup bead. The broader Rust canonical-form audit is tracked separately under yazelix-9opk so stale docs do not get mixed with code deletion decisions.
close_reason: Completed

title: Add direct regression coverage for integration-facts.compute
description: yazelix-4xf2 introduced the Rust-owned integration-facts.compute helper and moved yazi.nu and managed_editor.nu onto it, but the current protection is still mostly indirect through broader Yazi and workspace behavior tests. Add one narrow regression lane that defends the helper payload and owner boundary directly.
design: Keep this focused on the surviving owner seam. Cover the helper output shape, configured yazi/ya overrides, sidebar enablement, and managed editor kind resolution without rebuilding another broad integration suite. Prefer deterministic direct helper coverage plus one nearby contract mapping over more wrapper-heavy end-to-end noise.
acceptance_criteria: 1. There is direct regression coverage for integration-facts.compute as a Rust-owned helper surface. 2. The coverage defends the returned facts shape for sidebar enablement, yazi command, ya command, and managed editor kind. 3. The test maps to the live bridge or workspace/editor contract instead of relying only on broad governance markers. 4. The new coverage stays narrow and deterministic rather than duplicating the larger Yazi/workspace suites.
close_reason: Completed

title: Pilot cargo-nextest for deterministic Rust-owned test buckets
description: After the Rust test hardening decision lands, pilot cargo-nextest only on deterministic Rust-owned suites such as config normalization, public route planning/metadata, materialization render plans, and pane-orchestrator contract tests. Do not change runtime packaging or default-suite ownership.
close_reason: Completed

title: Retire or hard-archive docs that cite deleted Nu owners as live examples
description: Audit outcome: several historical docs still mention deleted files such as launch_state.nu, yzx/env.nu, yzx/run.nu, config_migrations.nu, and config_migration_transactions.nu. Some are valid history, but they should not be easy to misread as current planning or live contract guidance.
design: Retained behavior: useful migration history and upgrade-note guarded-change records. Candidate surviving owner: spec_inventory.md plus explicit historical labels, or deletion for docs with no durable value. Stop condition: keep references in upgrade history or archived design notes when deleting them would erase useful release context; rewrite only when current docs present deleted files as current examples.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Every stale deleted-owner reference in docs is classified as history, guarded upgrade history, stale planning, or delete candidate. 2. Current planning docs no longer point at deleted Nu owners as active targets. 3. Historical docs that remain are clearly marked historical or archived. 4. Useless transition docs are deleted or removed from active inventories. 5. validate_specs passes after inventory updates.
close_reason: Completed

title: Delete or demote legacy maintainer wrapper scripts
description: Audit outcome: several dev scripts are now thin compatibility wrappers around canonical maintainer owners or manual-only tooling. Examples include update_zellij_pane_orchestrator.nu, update_zjstatus.nu, record_demo.nu, record_demo_fonts.nu, and packaging-specific nixpkgs validators.
design: Retained behavior: pane-orchestrator wasm build/sync, zjstatus vendoring, demo recording when intentionally kept, and nixpkgs smoke validation. Candidate surviving owner: maintainer/plugin_build.nu, maintainer/update_workflow.nu, public docs, or explicit manual scripts that are not shipped as product runtime contract. Stop condition: do not delete scripts that still provide the only documented command for a maintained release/package workflow; demote to manual docs if deletion would hide an intentional maintainer entrypoint.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Each candidate script is classified as canonical owner, thin wrapper, manual/exploratory, packaging validator, or delete candidate. 2. Thin wrappers are deleted or replaced by direct calls to canonical maintainer modules. 3. Manual-only scripts are documented as manual or moved out of normal runtime expectations. 4. Release, vendoring, wasm sync, and nixpkgs validation workflows keep a supported entrypoint. 5. README or maintainer docs stop presenting deleted wrappers as live product commands.
close_reason: Completed

title: Evaluate initializers and environment for Rust materialization
description: Evaluate whether setup/initializers.nu and setup/environment.nu have a clear deterministic subcore that can be moved to Rust (e.g. a structured shell-hook render plan), or whether their shell-bound integration nature means they should stay Nushell-owned. Only attempt a Rust port if it deletes a real Nushell owner end-to-end without moving shell execution into Rust.
notes:
2026-04-20 audit outcome:
- Reviewed `setup/initializers.nu` and `setup/environment.nu` after the earlier `runtime-env.compute` cut and the landed terminal materialization work
- No further Rust port is recommended in v15.x for this family
- `runtime-env.compute` already captured the only obvious typed decision layer worth moving
- `initializers.nu` remains external-tool initializer generation plus shell-specific text ownership
- `environment.nu` remains shellhook and startup orchestration
- Updated the delete-first roadmap docs so this family is treated as a likely Nushell survivor unless a future full-owner cut can delete the surviving shellhook owner end-to-end
close_reason: Completed: runtime_env.compute already captured the only clear deterministic subcore; initializers.nu and environment.nu stay Nushell-owned in v15.x, and the roadmap now classifies them as likely Nushell survivors rather than an active Rust lane.

title: Extract shared normalize_path_entries and platform name helpers from runtime_contract_checker.nu and doctor_runtime_report.nu
description: runtime_contract_checker.nu and doctor_runtime_report.nu both define near-identical normalize_path_entries and platform-name helpers. Extract these into common.nu or a shared runtime utility so the bridge layer stops duplicating deterministic path-string logic across separate report modules.
close_reason: Extracted normalize_path_entries and get_runtime_platform_name into common.nu, deleting duplicates from runtime_contract_checker.nu (-25), doctor_runtime_report.nu (-25), and terminal_launcher.nu (-10). Net reduction 35 lines. All syntax validation and targeted tests pass.

title: Move startup orchestration out of generated_runtime_state.nu into a dedicated startup module
description: generated_runtime_state.nu currently mixes two concerns: thin bridge transport for runtime-materialization.plan/repair-evaluate/apply, and heavy startup orchestration (regenerate_runtime_configs, repair_generated_runtime_state) that coordinates between Rust plan evaluation and Nushell-side config generators. Split the orchestration into a startup-specific module so generated_runtime_state.nu becomes pure thin transport, deleting the mixed-owner seam.
close_reason: Split generated_runtime_state.nu into pure bridge transport and dedicated orchestration. New core/materialization_orchestrator.nu owns regenerate_runtime_configs, record_current_materialized_state, and repair_generated_runtime_state. generated_runtime_state.nu reduced from 224 to 115 lines (-109). Exported apply_runtime_materialization for cross-module use. Updated all 4 callers: start_yazelix_inner.nu, doctor.nu, update_workflow.nu, test_yzx_generated_configs.nu. All targeted tests pass.

title: Collapse config_parser.nu generic wrapper duplication into one transport seam
description: config_parser.nu exports five near-identical generic wrappers (run_yzx_core_json_command, run_yzx_core_request_json_command, run_yzx_core_command, run_yzx_core_json_command_with_error_surface, run_yzx_core_command_with_error_surface) that differ only in whether they parse JSON, accept a request record, or surface errors. Consolidate these into 2-3 generic exports so the bridge foundation stops carrying repetitive transport boilerplate.
close_reason: Consolidated 5 near-identical yzx_core transport wrappers into 3 canonical exports: run_yzx_core_json_command and run_yzx_core_command now own their full bodies directly, deleting the two _with_error_surface intermediate exports. Also fixed test_yzx_popup_commands.nu fixture to include missing build_record_yzx_core_error_surface and run_yzx_core_request_json_command stubs. Lines reduced from 373 to 356 (-17). All targeted tests pass.

title: Collapse the remaining yzx_core Nushell bridge layer
description: The yzx_core Rust helper owns typed computation for config, state, preflight, materialization, and reports, but a thick Nushell bridge layer still shapes requests, translates errors, and assembles per-command report shims around those Rust owners. This bead tracks collapsing that bridge toward thin transport only.
close_reason: All three children complete: zl3f collapsed config_parser.nu wrappers (-17 lines), fikz split generated_runtime_state.nu into pure bridge transport (-109 lines), a2ex extracted shared helpers into common.nu (-35 lines). Total bridge layer reduction: 161 lines. The remaining bridge files are now thin transport modules.

title: Rust-own the yzx update family via yzx_control and delete the public update block from core/yazelix.nu
description: The public yzx update family is still a sizable owner inside core/yazelix.nu. Move yzx update, yzx update upstream, yzx update home_manager, and yzx update nix behind the existing yzx_control pattern so the public Nushell definitions stop owning this family.
design:
Delete first. Extend `shells/posix/yzx_cli.sh` direct dispatch and `yzx_control` instead of layering another Rust helper behind the same public Nushell commands. Treat this bead as a narrow public-owner deletion slice, not a blanket policy change that every distribution command should move to Rust. The current public owner is concentrated inside `nushell/scripts/core/yazelix.nu`: helper prose/validation functions plus `yzx update`, `yzx update upstream`, `yzx update home_manager`, and `yzx update nix`. The desired end state is that the public update family no longer depends on those Nushell defs for argv parsing, usage/help, or exit behavior. Keep only truly shell-bound or UI-specific behavior outside Rust, and coordinate with CLI metadata work so migrated help/completion remain coherent.

Boundary guidance:
- In scope: public dispatch/parse/exit behavior for `yzx update`, `yzx update upstream`, `yzx update home_manager`, and likely `yzx update nix` if it can move cleanly.
- Keep out of scope: broad clap/root-help rewrite, generated extern ownership, detached terminal execution, renderer/template work, Nix/package policy redesign, maintainer-only update workflows.
- Important tension: `docs/specs/yzx_command_surface_backend_coupling.md` currently treats installed-runtime/distribution maintenance as a family that often stays Nushell-owned. Therefore this bead only makes sense if it deletes a real public owner seam via `yzx_control` and `yzx_cli.sh`; if the result is just a Rust shim over unchanged public Nushell commands, reject that shape.
- If `yzx update nix` turns out to be policy-heavy or too shell/interactive to fit the same cutover cleanly, split it explicitly instead of letting it block `upstream` + `home_manager` ownership deletion.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The public yzx update definitions in core/yazelix.nu disappear or lose public-owner status. 2. yzx_control becomes the public owner for the migrated update family. 3. Help, completion, and exit behavior for the migrated leaves are defended by focused parity checks. 4. The work deletes a real public Nushell owner instead of adding another private helper.
notes:
2026-04-20 Composer handoff notes:
- Current owner inventory in `nushell/scripts/core/yazelix.nu`: `print_update_owner_warning`, `print_update_path_confirmation`, `fail_if_home_manager_owned_upstream_update`, `load_default_profile_elements`, `resolve_active_yazelix_profile_entry`, `require_current_working_flake`, and the four public `yzx update*` defs.
- User contract to preserve from `docs/yzx_cli.md`: bare `yzx update` prints the owner choices; `yzx update upstream` prints and runs `nix profile upgrade --refresh <matching-entry>` only for default-profile installs; `yzx update home_manager` must run from the owning flake dir, runs `nix flake update yazelix`, and prints `home-manager switch` as a manual next step; `yzx update nix` upgrades Determinate Nix via `sudo determinate-nixd upgrade` with `--yes`/`--verbose` behavior preserved.
- Existing defended behavior already lives in `nushell/scripts/dev/test_yzx_core_commands.nu`: exact profile-entry selection for `update upstream`, early failure for Home Manager-owned installs, clear failure when no profile entry matches, and `update home_manager` updating only the current input and printing the manual switch step. Keep those high-signal tests; add at most one focused parity test for `update nix` if its contract stays in scope. Reject help/discovery-only tests.
- Suggested attack plan: (1) compare the env/run `yzx_cli.sh -> yzx_control` cutover with the update-family shape, (2) decide whether `yzx_control` owns both parser and executor for each surviving update leaf or whether some leaf should remain intentionally Nushell-owned, (3) move only the public owner surface first, (4) delete/demote the public update block in `core/yazelix.nu`, (5) keep any surviving Nushell code clearly internal helper glue, not a second public command owner.
- Crate gate: default to no new production crates; prefer existing std + current helper crates only. If argument/help modeling pressure suggests a new crate, record why the dependency buys real owner deletion rather than a thin shim.
- Coordination note: this bead should leave the repo closer to `yazelix-ulb2.7` (public metadata/help/completion ownership), not fight it. Prefer shapes where `yzx_control` can expose stable command/help metadata for the migrated leaves later.
close_reason: Moved yzx update* to yzx_control via yzx_cli.sh; removed public defs from yazelix.nu; added YAZELIX_TEST_PATH_PREPEND for integration tests; cargo test -p yazelix_core and run_core_canonical_tests pass.

title: Demote debug_editor_state to a maintainer-only surface after typed session consumers migrate
description: The stable get_active_tab_session_state seam now exists, but debug_editor_state still survives as a second broad inspection payload. Once live consumers stop depending on it, narrow or demote the debug surface so it no longer acts like an accidental session-truth contract.
design: Do not wrap debug and typed state together behind another abstraction. Keep debug payloads only for maintainer inspection and troubleshooting. Production Nushell and Yazi flows should consume versioned typed payloads or explicit narrow commands.
acceptance_criteria: 1. No shipped live consumer relies on debug_editor_state for tab-local session truth. 2. debug_editor_state is removed, materially reduced, or clearly labeled maintainer-only. 3. Docs reflect the surviving debug versus contract boundary. 4. Tests defend real consumer contracts, not debug payload shape trivia.
notes: Implemented by renaming the pane-orchestrator debug pipe from `debug_editor_state` to `maintainer_debug_editor_state` and renaming the exported Nushell helper to match. No shipped live consumer still depends on the old broad debug payload for tab-local session truth; active Yazi/sidebar/editor flows remain on `get_active_tab_session_state`. Updated workspace/session docs and pane-orchestrator specs to label the surviving debug surface as maintainer-only and to remove stale references to the deleted sidebar read seam.
