title: Choose and narrow the first mixed public family for the Rust yzx root
description: A serious Rust public yzx rewrite still needs one mixed command family beyond env/run where Rust can own the public contract without inheriting fuzzy shell/process ownership. This bead exists to force that choice explicitly instead of pretending the root rewrite can happen in a vacuum. The likely candidates are launch/enter/restart or status/doctor, but the answer must be driven by real owner deletion and exact boundary narrowing, not by which family sounds more important.
design: Compare the remaining mixed families against docs/specs/v16_rust_cli_rewrite_evaluation.md and docs/specs/launch_bootstrap_rust_migration.md. Pick one family only if the public Rust path can delete real Nushell public owners while leaving shell-bound execution, renderers, and prose in the right layer. Good outcomes are: 1) a concrete family-level execution plan with exact Nu modules that disappear or stop being public owners, or 2) an explicit no-go result for a family that is still too shell-bound. Avoid broad hand-wavy 'migrate launch later' planning without a deletion story.
acceptance_criteria: 1. One mixed family is chosen as the first serious public Rust candidate, or explicitly rejected with reasons. 2. The internal boundary and remaining Nushell helper role are named concretely. 3. The exact public Nushell owner modules that disappear or are demoted are listed. 4. The family-level plan is strong enough to satisfy qsb5's go/no-go gate about mixed command families.
notes:
2026-04-20 candidate refresh: yzx update is now a serious mixed-family candidate alongside launch and status or doctor. The public block in core/yazelix.nu is sizable but bounded, and yzx_cli.sh already has the direct yzx_control dispatch pattern from env and run.
2026-04-20 audit outcome:
- Chosen first mixed public family for the Rust yzx root: the already migrated control-plane surface `yzx env`, `yzx run`, and `yzx update*`.
- Why: `shells/posix/yzx_cli.sh` already dispatches that family directly to `yzx_control`; `nushell/scripts/core/yazelix.nu` no longer exports `yzx update*`; and Rust command metadata already owns help, externs, and menu inventory for the family.
- Explicit no-go for the first root cut: `launch` / `enter` / `restart` remain shell-orchestration heavy, and `status` / `doctor` still keep substantial Nushell bridge, rendering, and repair ownership.
- Follow-on scope for `yazelix-qsb5.2`: make Rust the single public root/help/completion owner for `env` / `run` / `update*`, delete the public registry role of `core/yazelix.nu` for that surface, and keep remaining Nu-owned families behind explicit internal helper entrypoints until they have their own deletion story.
close_reason: Completed: chose the first mixed public family for the Rust yzx root as the already migrated control-plane surface (yzx env / yzx run / yzx update*), documented the no-go reasons for launch and status/doctor, and narrowed qsb5.2 to the concrete root-deletion cut.

title: Delete the public Nushell yzx registry and make Rust the single root/help/completion owner
description: The broader Rust yzx rewrite only becomes real once Rust, not core/yazelix.nu plus export-use scanning, owns the public command root. This bead tracks the actual registry deletion step: remove the public export use ../yzx/*.nu tree as the command registry, stop shelling back into use nushell/scripts/core/yazelix.nu *; yzx ..., and make the Rust yzx binary the single parser/help/completion owner for the migrated public surface.
design: Delete-first boundary: a Rust root layered above the unchanged Nushell public tree is not acceptable. Rust may dispatch to internal Nushell helpers for commands intentionally kept Nushell-owned, but the public root/help/completion owner must be singular and Rust-owned. This bead should name the exact Nushell modules that stop being public owners and the command families that remain internal Nu helpers. Detached shell execution, renderers, and explicitly Nushell-owned UX commands stay out of scope unless their owner boundary is separately justified.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The public export-use command registry in nushell/scripts/core/yazelix.nu is deleted or demoted from public-owner status. 2. Rust becomes the single root/help/completion owner for the migrated command surface. 3. The implementation does not shell back into the old public yzx Nu tree for migrated commands. 4. The deleted Nushell public-owner modules and remaining internal Nu helper modules are explicitly named.
notes:
2026-04-20 narrowed starting point from `yazelix-qsb5.3`:
- initial migrated public root surface should be the already Rust-owned control-plane family: `yzx env`, `yzx run`, and `yzx update*`
- success means Rust owns public root parsing/help/completion/dispatch for that family without shelling back into `use nushell/scripts/core/yazelix.nu *; yzx ...`
- leave `launch` / `enter` / `restart`, `status` / `doctor`, and other Nu-owned families on explicit internal helper paths until they get their own deletion budget
2026-04-20 implementation outcome:
- `shells/posix/yzx_cli.sh` is now bootstrap-only and no longer routes command families itself.
- The public root parser and dispatcher moved to `rust_core/yazelix_core/src/bin/yzx.rs`.
- `yzx env`, `yzx run`, and `yzx update*` stay on the Rust-only public path through `yzx_control`; the root no longer falls back to `use nushell/scripts/core/yazelix.nu *; yzx ...` for that surface.
- The surviving Nushell-owned families now sit behind one explicit internal entrypoint: `nushell/scripts/core/yzx_internal_dispatch.nu`.
- Remaining internal Nu helper owners after the cut: `nushell/scripts/core/yazelix.nu` only for `why`, `sponsor`, `cwd`, `reveal`, `status`, `restart`, and `doctor`; plus explicit internal helper modules under `nushell/scripts/yzx/` for launch, enter, desktop, menu, popup, config, edit, keys, tutor, screen, whats_new, home_manager, import, and dev.
- Packaging now ships `libexec/yzx` alongside `yzx_control`, and validators/tests defend that new root seam.
close_reason: Completed: the public yzx root is now Rust-owned via rust_core/yazelix_core/src/bin/yzx.rs, yzx_cli.sh is bootstrap-only, env/run/update stay on the Rust-only public path through yzx_control, and the old generic core/yazelix.nu root fallback was replaced by one explicit internal Nu entrypoint for the remaining Nushell-owned families.

title: Rust-own yzx command metadata and delete extern-scan discovery as a public owner
description: Before a serious public Rust yzx rewrite can happen, Yazelix needs one static Rust-owned source of truth for command metadata, help/completion structure, and migrated-family ownership. Today nushell/scripts/utils/nushell_externs.nu still scans the Nushell command tree and treats generated extern output as part of public command discovery. That keeps the public CLI split alive even after helper-backed Rust moves. This bead exists to replace that discovery model with Rust-owned command metadata so the extern bridge becomes compatibility glue instead of an owner seam.
design: Delete-first boundary: do not add a Rust metadata file while still treating Nushell scope commands or generated extern scans as authoritative. The end state should let the extern bridge render from Rust-owned metadata or shrink to a small compatibility surface. Keep intentionally Nushell-owned commands possible, but they must stop making the Nushell command tree the source of truth for public discovery. This bead should also define or implement the parity harness for help/completion/exit behavior that qsb5 needs.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Public command discovery/help/completion metadata is no longer authored by scanning the Nushell command tree. 2. nushell_externs.nu stops being the authoritative source of public command discovery and shrinks materially or changes role to compatibility-only. 3. A parity harness exists for help/completion/exit behavior for migrated families. 4. The work names the exact public owner seam deleted, not just a new Rust metadata source added in parallel.
close_reason: Completed by e92c63b: yzx_core now owns public command metadata/help/extern rendering, nushell_externs.nu is compatibility-only startup glue instead of authoritative command discovery, and focused Rust/Nu parity checks cover root help, externs, and menu inventory/dispatch.

title: Broader Rust/clap public CLI rewrite after deletion gates
description: Track the eventual broader Rust public `yzx` rewrite as an explicit blocked execution bead instead of an implied future idea. This is a v16-or-later path only if Rust becomes the single public command-tree owner for the control-plane families that matter and deletes real Nushell public-owner seams rather than layering clap over the existing public Nushell tree.
design: Follow docs/specs/v16_rust_cli_rewrite_evaluation.md. The rewrite is only worth doing if it deletes the public Nushell command registry, reduces or removes the generated extern bridge as a public discovery seam, and gives Rust real ownership of parsing/help/completions for migrated families. Do not start this bead as a thin argv shim above the current Nushell tree. Keep pane-orchestrator session truth work separate; it is not part of the public CLI rewrite.
acceptance_criteria: The bead stays blocked until the documented go/no-go gates are materially satisfied. When it becomes actionable, the implementation plan must name the exact Nushell public-owner modules that disappear, the parity harness for help/completion/exit behavior, and the command families that become Rust-owned without carrying the old public Nushell tree forward in parallel.
notes:
2026-04-20 public-CLI rewrite reset:
- ulb2.2 is complete, so the original first deletion gate is cleared.
- this bead remains blocked in practice on three public-owner gates: 1) Rust-owned command metadata/help/completion parity, 2) deletion of Nushell extern-scan discovery as a public owner, and 3) deletion of the public export-use registry in core/yazelix.nu.
- qsb5.1, qsb5.2, and qsb5.3 now track those gates explicitly.
- do not start implementation by layering a Rust root above the unchanged Nushell public tree.
close_reason: Completed as the public-CLI gate tracker: qsb5.1, qsb5.2, and qsb5.3 are all complete, the control-family Rust root cut landed, and any later expansion to additional mixed families should be tracked as a new follow-up bead instead of leaving this gate umbrella open.

title: Audit and delete duplicate pane/focus/sidebar state owners outside the plugin
description: After the new orchestrator seam exists and key consumers move to it, audit remaining non-authoritative owners of pane identity, focus context, sidebar visibility, and tab-local workspace state across Nushell, Yazi glue, and caches. Remove duplicates that no longer need to exist, or explicitly demote them to cache/adapter status.
design: Do not create a second generic state abstraction. The point is to shrink ownership and future bug surface, not to wrap old and new sources in more indirection. Preserve only data that is truly cache, transport, or renderer input; make the pane orchestrator the durable owner for live tab/session truth.
acceptance_criteria: The surviving sources of pane/focus/sidebar/tab-local state are documented and smaller than before. Duplicate or ambiguous owners are removed where possible, and any remaining cache/adapters are labeled as non-authoritative with focused rationale and tests guarding the intended boundary.
notes: Completed by finishing the remaining duplicate-state audit slice: the old `debug_editor_state` inspection payload no longer survives as a public-looking session-truth seam and is now exposed only as maintainer-only `maintainer_debug_editor_state`. The surviving live boundary is the typed pane-orchestrator session snapshot consumed by shipped Yazi/sidebar/editor flows, with docs updated to mark caches/adapters as non-authoritative.
close_reason: Completed: duplicate sidebar/Yazi/debug session-state owners were removed or demoted and the surviving session-truth boundary is documented

title: Move Yazi render-plan decision data into yzx_core while keeping TOML/Lua rendering in Nushell
description: After the Zellij render-plan slice proves the pattern, move the remaining deterministic Yazi render-plan logic into `yzx_core` while keeping TOML/Lua rendering, user override merge, bundled asset syncing, and generated file writes in Nushell. The goal is to delete real Yazi decision logic from Nushell without turning Rust into a second renderer or reviving broad startup optimization machinery.
design:
Start with the Rust dependency gate: record crate-vs-in-house before code changes. Prefer in-house/std unless a narrowly scoped crate clearly improves safety.

Implementation target:
- Add one private helper command that returns a typed Yazi render plan, or extend an immediately adjacent helper only if that keeps the command surface cleaner without mixing unrelated concerns.
- Keep inputs explicit and machine-readable.
- Keep outputs data-only: no prose, no file writes, no TOML/Lua text generation.

Rust should own plan data such as:
- normalized managed plugin set / enabled plugin classification
- deterministic plugin-dependent decisions such as whether git fetcher config is present
- theme/sort render-plan fields that are still re-derived locally before rendering
- any deterministic managed-opener or bundled-asset expectation data that is currently recomputed in Yazi generation helpers

Nushell must continue to own:
- TOML merge with user-managed Yazi config and keymap files
- `init.lua` assembly and runtime-root placeholder rendering
- bundled asset copying/sync and user override discovery
- file writes, comments/headers, and user-facing progress text

Delete-first rules:
- Do not add a Rust plan while leaving equivalent plugin/theme/sort/asset decisions alive in Nushell.
- Keep this bead separate from warm-start optimization experiments; do not absorb deferred optimization bead `yazelix-8h9y` unless the user explicitly retargets it.

Non-goals:
- full Yazi config rendering in Rust
- caching/reuse machinery as a substitute for deleting decision duplication
- rewriting user override merge semantics or bundled asset syncing as part of this slice
acceptance_criteria:
1. One Rust helper returns the canonical Yazi render-plan data consumed by Nushell renderers.
2. Nushell no longer independently computes the same plugin/theme/sort/asset-plan decisions in parallel.
3. TOML/Lua rendering, user override merge, bundled asset syncing, file writes, and user-facing reporting remain Nushell-owned.
4. Verification commands in the notes pass.
notes:
Primary files to inspect first:
- `nushell/scripts/setup/yazi_config_merger.nu`
- `nushell/scripts/setup/yazi_bundled_assets.nu`
- `nushell/scripts/setup/yazi_user_overrides.nu`
- `rust_core/yazelix_core/src/runtime_materialization.rs`
- `rust_core/yazelix_core/src/bin/yzx_core.rs`
- deferred adjacent bead: `yazelix-8h9y`

Concrete seams most worth deleting:
- plugin-set classification and plugin-dependent config branches
- theme/sort render-plan shaping immediately before TOML/Lua rendering
- deterministic managed asset expectation/manifests that are not actually text-rendering work

Suggested verification:
- `nix develop -c cargo test --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml`
- `nix develop -c nu -c 'source nushell/scripts/dev/test_yzx_generated_configs.nu; run_generated_config_canonical_tests'`
- `nix develop -c nu -c 'source nushell/scripts/dev/test_helix_managed_config_contracts.nu; run_helix_managed_config_contract_tests'`
- `nix develop -c nu -c 'source nushell/scripts/dev/validate_specs.nu; main'`
- `git diff --check`
close_reason: Added yazi-render-plan.compute (Rust) + yazi_render_plan.nu bridge; yazi_config_merger consumes plan for theme/sort/git/init load order; unit + integration tests; verification passed.

title: Move Zellij render-plan decision data into yzx_core while keeping KDL rendering in Nushell
description: `zellij_config_merger.nu` and `layout_generator.nu` still mix real decision logic with renderer work. This bead should move the deterministic Zellij render-plan data into `yzx_core` so Nushell becomes a thin consumer for geometry/layout/settings decisions while still owning KDL assembly, semantic block merging, wasm syncing, file writes, and user-facing progress text.
design:
Start with the Rust dependency gate: record crate-vs-in-house before code changes. Default posture should remain in-house/std unless a narrowly scoped crate clearly reduces risk.

Implementation target:
- Add one private helper command that computes a typed Zellij render plan (name it to fit current bridge conventions, e.g. `zellij-render-plan.compute`, unless extending an existing command is truly cleaner).
- Feed it explicit inputs only: normalized config fields and explicit runtime/layout/plugin path inputs as needed.
- Keep the output purely data-oriented. No prose, no file writes, no shell execution.

Rust should own plan data such as:
- default layout family / managed layout choice
- sidebar geometry percentages now computed in `layout_generator.nu`
- validated/normalized widget tray data
- structured dynamic/enforced owned-setting values that are currently re-derived before rendering
- any small plugin/layout metadata records that are deterministic and reused across generated config + layouts

Nushell must continue to own:
- semantic block extraction and merge against user/native Zellij config
- KDL rendering and placeholder substitution
- plugin wasm sync / runtime path wiring
- file writes, generation fingerprints, progress labels, and comments/headers
- any host/runtime probing that is not just typed plan computation

Delete-first rules:
- Remove the duplicated Nushell plan logic you replace; do not add a Rust plan and keep the same Nu computations beside it.
- If any Nu decision branch survives, document exactly why and open a follow-up instead of silently keeping duplicate ownership.

Non-goals:
- porting KDL parsing/rendering wholesale to Rust
- moving startup/launch orchestration into Rust
- changing user-facing generated file format beyond what naturally falls out of the same renderer consuming new plan data
acceptance_criteria:
1. One Rust helper returns the canonical Zellij render-plan data consumed by Nushell renderers.
2. Nushell no longer independently computes the same layout/geometry/widget-tray/owned-setting decisions in parallel.
3. KDL rendering, semantic block merge, plugin syncing, file writes, and user-facing reporting remain Nushell-owned.
4. Verification commands in the notes pass.
notes:
Primary files to inspect first:
- `nushell/scripts/setup/zellij_config_merger.nu`
- `nushell/scripts/utils/layout_generator.nu`
- `nushell/scripts/setup/zellij_generation_state.nu`
- `rust_core/yazelix_core/src/runtime_materialization.rs`
- `rust_core/yazelix_core/src/bin/yzx_core.rs`

Concrete seams most worth deleting:
- `compute_sidebar_layout_percentages`
- layout family / default layout-name choice still derived in Nushell renderers
- validated widget-tray shaping done immediately before placeholder rendering
- owned-setting classification/data shaping that does not require KDL assembly

Suggested verification:
- `nix develop -c cargo test --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml`
- `nix develop -c nu -c 'source nushell/scripts/dev/test_yzx_generated_configs.nu; run_generated_config_canonical_tests'`
- `nix develop -c nu -c 'source nushell/scripts/dev/test_yzx_workspace_commands.nu; run_workspace_canonical_tests'`
- `nix develop -c nu -c 'source nushell/scripts/dev/test_zellij_plugin_contracts.nu; main'`
- `nix develop -c nu -c 'source nushell/scripts/dev/validate_specs.nu; main'`
- `git diff --check`
close_reason: Implemented zellij-render-plan.compute in yzx_core; Nushell merger and layout_generator consume the plan; removed duplicate owned-settings and geometry logic.

title: Continue Rust rewrite through renderer-adjacent render-plan data
description: The next Rust-forward phase should move more deterministic setup and generated-config decision logic into `yzx_core` without porting text renderers, shell/process orchestration, or the public `yzx` UX. After config/state/materialization/runtime-env work, the best remaining v15.x seam is renderer-adjacent plan data for generated Zellij and Yazi surfaces: Rust computes typed render-plan records, Nushell keeps KDL/TOML/Lua assembly, file writes, user-facing comments, and host integration glue.
design:
Keep the current ownership boundary quality at least as strong as the completed v15 Rust slices.

Scope principles:
1. Rust owns structured, deterministic plan data only.
2. Nushell stays the renderer owner for generated Zellij KDL, Yazi TOML/Lua, plugin syncing/copying, semantic block extraction/merge, header comments, and user-facing progress text.
3. Do not revisit launch/bootstrap/process execution as part of this phase; that track is intentionally stopped for v15.x unless a new tiny seam appears.
4. Prefer one helper command family at a time, with parity tests before deleting the Nushell decision logic it replaces.
5. Each implementation bead must start by recording the crate-vs-in-house decision per the Rust dependency gate.

Recommended sequence:
- First: Zellij render-plan data, because it has the clearer concentration of reusable geometry/layout/settings decisions.
- Second: Yazi render-plan data, only after the Zellij shape proves the architecture stays clean.
acceptance_criteria:
1. There is a concrete Rust-forward follow-up sequence after the completed config/state/materialization/runtime-env slices.
2. Each child bead targets a renderer-adjacent deterministic decision seam that can shrink Nushell without weakening architecture.
3. Renderer/template ownership remains Nushell-owned throughout this phase.
4. The phase explicitly avoids regressing into shell/process-orchestration rewrites.
notes:
Suggested primary files for this phase:
- `nushell/scripts/setup/zellij_config_merger.nu`
- `nushell/scripts/utils/layout_generator.nu`
- `nushell/scripts/setup/yazi_config_merger.nu`
- `rust_core/yazelix_core/src/runtime_materialization.rs`
- `docs/specs/rust_migration_matrix.md`
- `docs/specs/launch_bootstrap_rust_migration.md`

Guardrails:
- Reject additive Rust plus unchanged Nushell decision logic for the same plan data.
- Keep renderer/template ownership in Nushell.
- Do not move shell quoting, detached process launching, startup profile wiring, or public command prose into Rust.
- Do not reopen automatic migration machinery or broad CLI rewrite work under this epic.
close_reason: all steps complete

title: Make yzx_core the single owner of managed layout and materialization metadata
description: The first Rust materialization slice moved runtime-materialization planning/apply into yzx_core, but Nushell still re-derives parts of managed layout/materialization metadata across startup, generated-state, and shared runtime checks. This bead makes yzx_core the single decision owner for that metadata so Nushell becomes a thin consumer and renderer-only layer.
design:
Scope boundaries:
- Keep all text/template renderers in Nushell (Yazi, Zellij, terminal, Helix, shell initializers)
- Keep public profile labels and doctor/launch user-facing messaging in Nushell
- Move only structured decision metadata to Rust and consume it from one canonical helper output

Implementation instructions:
1) Inventory and remove duplicated Nushell decision seams:
   - expected layout resolution in runtime/startup checks
   - expected artifact/managed-artifact metadata derivation outside Rust helper output
   - any repeated materialization decision branches that duplicate runtime-materialization.plan data
2) Extend yzx_core output shape only as needed for shared metadata consumption (do not add a second command family unless required)
3) Collapse Nushell callers to consume Rust metadata:
   - generated_runtime_state.nu
   - runtime_contract_checker.nu (where materialization/layout metadata is currently re-derived)
   - startup glue that still computes expected layout/artifact metadata locally
4) Keep renderer invocation in Nushell unchanged; this bead is about decision ownership, not renderer migration

Delete-first enforcement:
- Reject additive Rust + unchanged Nu duplication
- If any Nu decision branch must survive, document exactly why and open explicit follow-up beads
acceptance_criteria:
1) Startup and generated-state paths consume one Rust-owned source for managed layout/materialization metadata
2) Nushell no longer independently computes the same expected-layout or expected-artifact decisions in parallel
3) Renderer/template ownership remains in Nushell and unchanged by this bead
4) Existing startup profile labels remain comparable before/after
5) Verification commands in the bead notes pass
notes:
Concrete targets and constraints:
- Primary files to inspect first:
  - nushell/scripts/utils/generated_runtime_state.nu
  - nushell/scripts/utils/runtime_contract_checker.nu
  - nushell/scripts/core/start_yazelix_inner.nu
  - rust_core/yazelix_core/src/runtime_materialization.rs
- Preserve existing startup profile/report step names
- Do not broaden into launch/process orchestration or renderer ownership changes
- Do not introduce fallback behavior that masks metadata/ownership drift

Verification checklist (minimum):
- Rust helper tests: nix develop -c cargo test --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml
- Generated-config/materialization regressions: nix develop -c nu -c 'source nushell/scripts/dev/test_yzx_generated_configs.nu; run_generated_config_canonical_tests'
- Workspace/startup regression subset touching layout/materialization expectations: nix develop -c nu -c 'source nushell/scripts/dev/test_yzx_workspace_commands.nu; run_workspace_canonical_tests'
- Spec consistency: nix develop -c nu -c 'source nushell/scripts/dev/validate_specs.nu; main'
- git diff --check
close_reason: Startup and doctor now take managed Zellij layout path from runtime-materialization.plan (yzx_core); removed parallel Nushell resolve_expected_layout_path. Exported compute_runtime_materialization_plan for shared consumption. Verification: cargo test, run_generated_config_canonical_tests, run_workspace_canonical_tests, validate_specs, doctor subset.

title: Continue gradual Rust insertion after the first yzx_core slices
description: The first helper-backed Rust slices are done, but Yazelix still has a few remaining Nushell decision seams that are good Rust candidates. This follow-up epic tracks only the next high-value v15.x moves: deleting duplicate config diagnostics, consolidating managed layout and materialization metadata, and extending shared typed classification only where it removes real Nushell logic.
design: Keep the current ownership boundary. Nushell remains the public yzx UX owner, renderer owner, and host-integration surface. The Rust pane orchestrator remains the owner of live workspace truth. New Rust work should stay behind the private yzx_core bridge and target deterministic control-plane logic that is reused across startup, generated-state, doctor, or status flows.
acceptance_criteria: There is a concrete follow-up sequence after the first Rust slices, with child beads for the remaining high-value control-plane seams and explicit guardrails against shell-heavy or text-heavy rewrites.
notes: Stop lines: do not reopen a broad launch/bootstrap rewrite, renderer-port effort, or public CLI rewrite under this epic. The goal is deletion of surviving Nu decision duplication, not additive Rust glue. This epic follows the completed kt5 planning sequence and the completed kt5.2/kt5.3/kt5.4/kt5.5 first slices.
close_reason: all steps complete

title: Split yzx status and doctor into machine-readable core plus Nushell rendering

Intent:

- adding a Rust helper here would mostly wrap the same Nushell check owners instead of deleting a real decision layer
- implementation seam for this bead: build structured status and doctor reports first, keep human rendering in Nushell, and expose JSON output on `yzx status` and `yzx doctor`
- this is the prerequisite narrowing step for any later Rust public-CLI move, not a new v15 helper-port slice

title: Move canonical runtime env planning into yzx_core and collapse runtime_env.nu into a bridge
description: Move the first execution slice under `yazelix-kt5.4` by moving canonical runtime-environment planning behind `yzx_core` and shrinking `runtime_env.nu` to a bridge. This slice covers the shared env computation used by `yzx env`, `yzx run`, popup/helper launches, and startup wrappers. It does not port terminal launch execution or startup orchestration.
design:
- Add a private `runtime-env.compute` helper command under `rust_core/yazelix_core/src/runtime_env.rs`
- Pass explicit runtime root, home/XDG-derived roots, current PATH entries, and normalized config fields that shape the runtime env
- Delete local decision helpers from `nushell/scripts/utils/runtime_env.nu` and keep only the bridge plus `activate_runtime_env` / `run_runtime_argv`
- Leave `shells/posix/runtime_env.sh`, `launch_yazelix.nu`, `terminal_launcher.nu`, `start_yazelix.nu`, and `start_yazelix_inner.nu` as the process/orchestration owners in v15.x
- Coordinate with `yazelix-0dra`: either absorb the remaining explicit nested-editor regression coverage into this slice or keep that bug open until equivalent coverage exists
acceptance_criteria: `runtime_env.nu` no longer owns PATH curation, editor-kind detection, Helix-runtime resolution, or layout-env decision logic locally. A Rust helper returns the canonical runtime env consumed by `yzx env`, `yzx run`, popup/helper launches, and startup wrappers without changing the public command surface. Regression coverage defends `VISUAL == EDITOR`, Helix wrapper and curated PATH behavior, `yzx run` child-argv pass-through, and unchanged launch/startup behavior. `cargo test --manifest-path rust_core/yazelix_core/Cargo.toml` and the targeted Nushell tests for popup, Helix managed-config, core commands, and workspace launch paths pass.
notes:
2026-04-19 start note: implementation claimed. Rust dependency gate for kt5.4.1 stays on the existing helper crate set only: production crates remain serde, serde_json, toml, sha2, thiserror, and lexopt; dev-only crates remain assert_cmd, pretty_assertions, and tempfile. Build in-house/std: PATH normalization and runtime-owned entry filtering, editor-kind detection, managed Helix wrapper selection, Helix runtime override handling, and the runtime-env request/response types. Rejected for this slice: clap, process-management crates, which, shell-escape, async runtimes, and terminal abstraction crates. Packaging impact: keep the helper private under libexec/yzx_core with no new dependency closure changes.
2026-04-19 implementation completion:
- added `runtime-env.compute` to `yzx_core` as a JSON-request helper command and new `rust_core/yazelix_core/src/runtime_env.rs` decision module
- collapsed `nushell/scripts/utils/runtime_env.nu` into a bridge over the helper while keeping `activate_runtime_env` and `run_runtime_argv` as Nushell-owned execution helpers
- kept POSIX/runtime bootstrap and launch/startup orchestration in Nushell/POSIX; no terminal execution logic moved into Rust
- updated popup and Helix contract tests to use the built helper explicitly where fake runtime roots are involved
- validation run: `cargo test --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml`, `nu nushell/scripts/dev/test_helix_managed_config_contracts.nu`, `nu nushell/scripts/dev/test_yzx_popup_commands.nu`, targeted `yzx run` passthrough regressions from `test_yzx_core_commands.nu`, and targeted startup bootstrap env probe from `test_yzx_workspace_commands.nu`
close_reason: Completed: runtime-env.compute now owns canonical runtime env planning behind yzx_core, runtime_env.nu is a bridge, and the targeted Rust/Nushell contract tests pass

title: Delete remaining runtime-materialization bridge glue from generated_runtime_state.nu
description: kt5.2.3 moved generated-runtime materialization planning and apply verification into the Rust yzx_core helper, but the surviving Nushell wrapper in generated_runtime_state.nu still carries a fair amount of helper invocation, JSON-envelope parsing, and path/override plumbing. Delete or centralize that remaining bridge glue so the surviving Nushell layer is materially thinner and more obviously a wrapper rather than a second owner.
design: Prefer deleting or centralizing repeated yzx_core invocation boilerplate across config_state.nu and generated_runtime_state.nu instead of adding more one-off wrapper code. A good outcome is one small shared helper runner plus a slimmer generated_runtime_state.nu that only preserves the public profile step names and calls the Nu renderers that still own text assembly. Do not reopen renderer ownership here; this is boundary cleanup after kt5.2.3, not kt5.5.
acceptance_criteria: There is a materially thinner surviving Nushell wrapper for runtime materialization. generated_runtime_state.nu no longer owns repeated helper-exec/envelope-parsing/path-plumbing boilerplate beyond what is strictly necessary for public profile labels and Nu renderer calls, and the resulting surface is clearly a thin bridge rather than a parallel implementation owner.
notes:
2026-04-19 concrete cleanup targets after kt5.2.3:
- Centralize the repeated `yzx_core` helper invocation and JSON-envelope parsing instead of leaving `compute_runtime_materialization_plan` to assemble and decode the helper call inline.
- Delete or centralize the layout-override and path/argv plumbing that still lives in `generated_runtime_state.nu`; the surviving Nu layer should not own that much bridge setup.
- Keep only the public profile step names and the Nu-owned text renderers in this file. Do not reopen Yazi/Zellij renderer ownership here; kt5.2.7 is boundary cleanup, not kt5.5.
- A good end state is one shared helper runner plus thin `regenerate_runtime_configs` / `record_current_materialized_state` wrappers around `generate_merged_yazi_config`, `generate_merged_zellij_config`, and the Rust helper commands.
2026-04-19 implementation landed:
- Added shared `yzx_core` bridge helpers in `config_parser.nu` for JSON-returning commands and fire-and-forget commands, keeping helper-path lookup, command execution, envelope parsing, and error rendering in one place.
- Moved `get_materialized_state_path` into `common.nu` so `config_state.nu` and `generated_runtime_state.nu` no longer duplicate the rebuild-hash path logic.
- Slimmed `config_state.nu` onto the shared bridge and reduced `generated_runtime_state.nu` to command-arg builders plus the public profile-labeled flow and Nu-owned renderers.
- Verified with `nu-lint`, import/parse smoke for the edited modules, `git diff --check`, and `run_generated_config_canonical_tests` \(all passing\).
- Net cleanup for this bead: 107 insertions, 127 deletions across the touched Nushell bridge files.
close_reason: Completed: the surviving Nushell bridge is now thinner and shared `yzx_core` invocation/envelope handling lives in `config_parser.nu` instead of being duplicated across config_state.nu and generated_runtime_state.nu.

title: Investigate zjstatus-hints
notes:
2026-04-26 subsystem review: evaluate zjstatus-hints as part of the yazelix_bar backend/options surface, not as a separate scattered layout tweak.
Investigation decision, 2026-04-28:
- Reviewed upstream b0o/zjstatus-hints as of 2026-04-28: it is a small Rust Zellij plugin that extends zjstatus with context-aware mode keybinding hints through a named pipe (`pipe_zjstatus_hints`); latest visible release is v0.1.4 from 2025-06-08. Source: https://github.com/b0o/zjstatus-hints
- Current Yazelix bar architecture already centralizes widget rendering through rust_core/yazelix_bar, config_metadata/main_config_contract.toml, and the single vendored zjstatus template. Recent agent/status widgets use zjstatus command slots, not extra wasm plugins.
- Product fit: hints would help onboarding and mode discoverability, but they duplicate part of `yzx keys`, consume scarce status-bar width, and would require a second plugin permission/vendoring/update path plus named-pipe coordination. That is too much surface for a default or immediate optional widget.
- Decision: do not adopt zjstatus-hints now. If hints become a priority, implement them as a first-party `yazelix_bar`/pane-orchestrator status fact or as an explicitly packaged optional `hints` widget with a full vendored-plugin contract, not as an ad-hoc layout tweak.
- No code change needed for this bead.
close_reason: Recorded no-adopt decision for zjstatus-hints

Use --border, --header, --color, --prompt, --pointer styling, and optionally --preview for command help text. Keep the same palette data pipeline (categories, descriptions, formatting) and preserve the popup/non-popup mode distinction.

title: yzx warp: fuzzy project-switch into a new tab, replacing yzx cwd
description:
Replace yzx cwd with yzx warp — a cleaner, simpler command that fuzzy-matches a directory via zoxide and opens a full workspace tab (sidebar + editor) at that location. Optional --kill/-k flag closes the old tab, fully subsuming yzx cwd's retarget-in-place behavior.

Why warp over cwd:
- 'warp' describes the user experience (teleport to project), 'cwd' describes implementation jargon (change working directory)
- Implementation is simpler: yzx warp leverages Zellij new-tab with --cwd and lets the layout + pane orchestrator bootstrap naturally via TabUpdate/PaneUpdate events, instead of the complex retarget choreography yzx cwd uses (tab rename, keystroke injection to sync editor cwd, ya emit-to sidebar sync, error state handling)
- One command with a clear mental model instead of two commands with subtly different semantics
- With -k, warp fully subsumes cwd: create fresh tab at target, close old tab

Scope: implement after the Rust rewrite has progressed.
notes:
External ref: https://github.com/luccahuguet/yazelix/issues/429
Dependency decision, 2026-04-28:
- Production crates: none new. Reuse std::process, std::path, serde_json already in yazelix_core, and existing CoreError/command-surface helpers.
- Dev-only crates: none new. Existing assert_cmd/tempfile tests remain sufficient.
- In-house logic: parse yzx warp args, resolve path-or-zoxide targets, call zellij action new-tab/close-tab-by-id with structured argv, and parse current-tab-info JSON for --kill.
- Rejected alternatives: clap for one public subcommand, shell wrappers around zellij/zoxide, adding sesh or another session manager, and reusing the heavy yzx cwd retarget choreography for the default path.
- Packaging impact: none beyond the existing yzx_control binary; zoxide and fzf are already part of the Yazelix runtime surface.
Implementation result, 2026-04-28:
- Added public yzx warp route and Rust-owned yzx_control warp implementation.
- yzx warp [target] resolves an explicit path or zoxide query, opens a fresh Zellij tab at the target directory, names the tab from the directory basename, and passes ZELLIJ_DEFAULT_LAYOUT when available so the normal Yazelix workspace shape is used.
- yzx warp with no target opens zoxide query -i for interactive project selection; cancellation exits cleanly.
- --kill/-k captures the original stable Zellij tab id with current-tab-info --json, opens the new tab, then closes the original tab by id so it does not accidentally close the new focused tab.
- Dropped yzx cwd from the public yzx command metadata/help/extern surface and updated current docs/contracts to yzx warp. The yzx_control cwd implementation remains as the legacy/internal retarget path used by existing tests and lower-level flows.
- Verification: cargo fmt --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml --all -- --check; cargo test --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml -p yazelix_core; cargo run --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml -p yazelix_maintainer --bin yzx_repo_validator -- validate-contracts; cargo run --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml -p yazelix_core --bin yzx_control -- warp --help.
close_reason: Implemented yzx warp as the public project-switch command, updated docs/contracts/metadata, kept yzx_control cwd as legacy internal retarget support, and verified with focused plus full yazelix_core tests.

title: Refactor dev and test shell execution seams
description: Refactor the dev, maintainer, and test harness shell execution seams away from inline quoted script bodies. These are less user-visible than runtime helpers, but they still spread the same brittle shell-boundary style through the repo and make test/debug code harder to trust.
design:
Target current known non-production seams such as:
- `nushell/scripts/dev/record_demo.nu`
- test harnesses that rely on inline `bash -lc` strings with embedded dynamic commands
- maintainer or validation helpers that shell through quoted program text when direct argv or helper scripts would be clearer

This child should prioritize the highest-signal seams first instead of churning every generated test fixture that happens to contain newline-joined file content. The smell to remove is inline shell-program construction for execution, not ordinary text generation.
acceptance_criteria:
1. The major dev/maintainer/test execution seams using inline quoted shell programs are identified and reduced.
2. Cleanup work distinguishes real shell-execution smells from ordinary text-generation helpers.
3. The remaining execution helpers are easier to review and less dependent on fragile shell quoting.
notes:
Useful heuristic:
- in scope: code that builds a shell program and then executes it via `bash -lc`, `sh -c`, or similar
- out of scope: plain text/config generation that happens to use `str join "\n"`
close_reason: Refactored all major dev/maintainer/test shell execution seams: record_demo.nu now invokes vhs directly via nix shell --command; update_workflow.nu replaced sh -c temp-file wrapper with direct nix profile add using .LAST_EXIT_CODE; test_shell_managed_config_contracts.nu replaced bash -lc test -L with direct test -L; test_yzx_maintainer.nu dropped unnecessary login shell flag; test_yzx_workspace_commands.nu replaced bash -lc env-prefix indirection with direct env -u invocation. All files pass syntax validation.

title: Eliminate inline quoted shell-script execution seams
description:
Yazelix has accumulated several places where Nushell assembles inline quoted shell programs and passes them to `bash -lc`, `sh -c`, or similar entrypoints. The pattern is brittle, hard to review, awkward to test, and easy to make worse when timing or quoting changes. It also spreads shell-policy decisions across Nushell string interpolation instead of keeping them at a clean shell boundary.

This bead exists to drive a repo pass that removes or refactors those seams into cleaner boundaries.
design:
Treat this as a code-shape and boundary-cleanup epic, not a blind search-and-replace.

Delete-first guidance:
- do not replace one awkward inline shell string with a different awkward inline shell string
- prefer checked-in helper scripts or structured argv execution over bigger `bash -lc` blobs
- if a shell trampoline is truly unavoidable, keep the script body fixed and pass dynamic values via argv or env instead of interpolating them into the script text

Known current surface:
- production/runtime helpers such as `launcher_resolution.nu`, `yzx/screen.nu`, and `setup/welcome.nu`
- the detached terminal launch probe in `terminal_launcher.nu` is the hottest production case, but its performance-sensitive cleanup is already owned by `yazelix-4xp1.1` and should not be duplicated here
- dev/maintainer/test seams such as `record_demo.nu` and test harnesses that shell through quoted inline scripts

The point is not to eliminate every shell invocation. The point is to eliminate the inline quoted-script style as a maintained pattern.
acceptance_criteria:
1. The major inline quoted shell-script seams in production/runtime and dev/test paths are tracked explicitly.
2. New refactors replace the pattern with helper scripts, structured argv execution, or fixed-body trampolines with argv/env inputs.
3. `terminal_launcher.nu` cleanup remains coordinated with `yazelix-4xp1.1` instead of being duplicated under a vague repo-wide churn pass.
notes:
Create child beads for the real slices instead of treating this as one giant mechanical refactor.

Expected outcome:
- new code follows the AGENTS shell-boundary rule
- the remaining shell seams become smaller, clearer, and easier to reason about
- the terminal-launch hot path cleanup stays coordinated with `yazelix-4xp1.1`
close_reason: all steps complete

title: Fold extern-bridge startup cost into the Rust/Nushell bridge seam
description:
Recent installed-runtime startup profiles show `shellhook.sync_yzx_extern_bridge` costing roughly 600ms on both desktop launch and managed launch. That is a meaningful startup tax, but this step sits directly on the command-bridge seam that Yazelix already plans to revisit as part of incremental Rust insertion.

This bead exists to keep that cost visible and intentional instead of either forgetting it or papering over it with ad hoc Nushell caching before the bridge contract is decided.
design:
Treat this as a during-the-Rust-rewrite seam, not as default standalone pre-rewrite Nushell work. The right question is not just how to shave time off the current sync step, but whether this bridge work belongs eagerly on startup at all once the Rust/Nushell integration contract is defined.

Use `yazelix-kt5.1.1` as the gating contract work. Only land a pre-rewrite change here if it is obviously tiny and delete-first, such as removing an eager sync that is clearly unnecessary today. Otherwise solve it when the bridge contract answers where generated extern surfaces should live, when they should be refreshed, and how startup should avoid paying this cost unnecessarily.

Sequencing decision: do during the Rust rewrite planning/bridge-slice work, not after. Waiting until after the rewrite would turn a known bridge hot spot into cleanup instead of a design input.
acceptance_criteria:
1. The Rust/Nushell bridge contract explicitly states when extern bridge synchronization happens and why that timing is required.
2. Startup no longer pays the full current eager-sync cost unless that cost is explicitly justified by the preserved contract.
3. User-facing command discovery, completion, and bridge behavior remain correct after the timing/ownership change.
notes:
Implementation notes:
- Added fingerprinted generated-state for the Nushell yzx extern bridge at initializers/nushell/yazelix_extern.fingerprint.json.
- Warm sync now verifies the command-surface fingerprint plus generated extern hash and returns without spawning Nushell when current.
- Refresh failure is non-destructive: an existing generated bridge is kept; only first-run/missing bridge gets an empty sourceable placeholder.
- Updated docs/specs/rust_nushell_bridge_contract.md with Extern Bridge Timing so future Rust bridge work preserves this timing.

Verification:
- nu-lint nushell/scripts/utils/nushell_externs.nu nushell/scripts/dev/test_shell_managed_config_contracts.nu nushell/scripts/setup/environment.nu: 0 violations.
- nu nushell/scripts/dev/test_shell_managed_config_contracts.nu: 11/11 passed.
- nu nushell/scripts/dev/validate_specs.nu: passed.
- nu nushell/scripts/dev/validate_default_test_traceability.nu: passed.
- git diff --check: passed.
- Local direct probe: cold generation about 560ms, warm fingerprinted reuse about 6ms.
close_reason: Completed: extern bridge sync now uses fingerprinted warm reuse, keeps previous generated bridge on refresh failure, and the Rust/Nushell bridge spec documents the timing contract.

title: Evaluate dedicated desktop prelaunch surface after terminal-backed launcher
description: Terminal-backed desktop entries make pre-terminal launch progress and failures visible, but they can briefly show a desktop-environment bootstrap terminal with a different window class before the managed Yazelix terminal appears. After yazelix-c1kw ships, evaluate whether the boring Terminal=true contract is good enough or whether a small dedicated prelaunch wrapper/dialog would reduce flicker and WM-class mismatch without reintroducing invisible failures or fallback launch paths.

title: Expose Ghostty trail duration as a supported yazelix.toml setting
description:
Yazelix already exposes Ghostty cursor palette, trail effect, mode effect, and glow through the main terminal config surface, but it does not expose trail duration as a supported setting. Users who want a shorter or longer Ghostty cursor trail currently need terminal-native overrides or unsupported edits instead of one clear Yazelix-owned knob in yazelix.toml.

Add a first-class trail-duration setting to the main terminal config surface so users can tune the Ghostty cursor trail lifetime through the same supported path as the other Ghostty cursor options.
design:
Keep this narrow and consistent with the existing main-config contract. Prefer a field name that matches the current terminal.ghostty_trail_* family, likely terminal.ghostty_trail_duration. Define one supported numeric range and fail fast on invalid values rather than silently clamping or ignoring them. Update all owned config surfaces together: yazelix_default.toml, config_metadata/main_config_contract.toml, home_manager/module.nix, parser/schema behavior, and the Ghostty config renderer that emits the final terminal config.

Do not turn this into a general Ghostty raw-config escape hatch. The goal is one maintained, documented setting with the same direct-config and Home Manager behavior as the rest of the main Ghostty cursor surface.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Users can set Ghostty trail duration through the main Yazelix config surface without resorting to terminal-native overrides.
2. The setting is present and synchronized across yazelix_default.toml, the canonical config contract, and the Home Manager module.
3. Invalid values fail clearly during config validation instead of being silently ignored.
4. Generated Ghostty config output reflects the configured trail duration, with regression coverage for direct-config and Home Manager paths.
notes:
Dependency decision for Ghostty trail duration: use no new Rust crates. Production and dev-only crate sets stay unchanged. Build validation and rendering in-house because this is a small typed numeric config field wired through the existing contract/schema/materialization helpers. Rejected alternatives: a raw Ghostty config escape hatch or generic numeric parsing crate, both broader than the maintained knob requested. Packaging impact: none beyond normal Rust/core validation.
Implemented terminal.ghostty_trail_duration as a supported duration multiplier with default 1.0 and range 0.25..=4.0. Wired the field through yazelix_default.toml, main_config_contract.toml, Home Manager, config normalization, yzx_core explicit/from-env Ghostty materialization, terminal materialization, launch rerolls, and the Ghostty shader builder. Generated Ghostty config now records the multiplier, color trail shaders scale their tuned DURATION constants, movement effect shaders scale DURATION, and mode-change effects keep their tuned timing. Verification: full yzx_core_config_normalize test file, focused Ghostty materialization/range tests, yzx dev rust fmt --check, yzx dev rust check, validate-config-surface-contract, validate-specs, validate-rust-test-traceability, validate-nushell-syntax, validate-flake-interface, git diff --check.
close_reason: Completed: exposed terminal.ghostty_trail_duration as a validated config/Home Manager setting and wired it into Ghostty shader generation with regression coverage.

title: add to yzx keys the alt <number> motions yazelix has

title: make startup faster
description:
Use this as the umbrella for startup-performance work now that desktop and managed-launch profiling is available from both repo checkouts and installed runtimes.

The latest installed-runtime profiles on 2026-04-18 show three common startup choke points worth tracking explicitly:
- `terminal_launcher.detached_launch_probe` at roughly 1034-1039ms
- `shellhook.sync_yzx_extern_bridge` at roughly 604-618ms
- `inner.materialize_runtime_configs` at roughly 509-513ms

This umbrella should stay broad and outcome-oriented while the concrete work happens in child beads with explicit rewrite sequencing.
design:
Keep this issue as the performance umbrella, not the implementation bead. The current evidence suggests a split rather than one big 'make startup faster' project:

- Do before the broader Rust rewrite: the detached terminal spawn/probe tax, because it is a large live UX hit and defines the launch-success versus early-failure contract the rewrite must preserve.
- Evaluate before the rewrite with a hard simplicity bar: runtime config materialization, because a slim invalidation fix could pay off now and clarify boundaries, but a heavy caching layer would be the wrong trade.
- Do during the Rust rewrite seam work: extern-bridge synchronization, because its ownership/timing should follow the Rust/Nushell bridge contract instead of accumulating transitional Nushell complexity.

Do not create a separate 'after the rewrite' bucket for these three choke points. If they are worth doing, they are either current UX debt that should be trimmed now or design inputs that belong inside the rewrite sequencing itself.
acceptance_criteria:
1. The main measured startup choke points have focused child beads with clear before-versus-during rewrite sequencing.
2. This umbrella remains the GitHub-visible startup-performance tracker while implementation details live in the child beads.
3. Follow-up optimization work is driven by measured startup profiles rather than vague startup-speed complaints.
notes: 2026-04-18 profiling split: child beads yazelix-4xp1.1, yazelix-4xp1.2, and yazelix-4xp1.3 were created from the installed-runtime desktop/managed launch reports. Use those children for concrete implementation and keep this issue as the umbrella and GitHub-facing tracker.
close_reason: Completed: startup-performance umbrella is done; concrete child beads for terminal spawn/probe tax, runtime config materialization, and extern-bridge startup cost are closed.

title: Add minimal Home Manager flake example
close_reason: Completed: added a real minimal Home Manager flake example and linked it from the Home Manager README.

title: Add VISUAL to POSIX bootstrap runtime env
description: Follow-up cleanup: the Nushell canonical runtime env now exports VISUAL alongside EDITOR, but shells/posix/runtime_env.sh still does not seed VISUAL during POSIX bootstrap. That leaves the bootstrap layer slightly behind the runtime env contract even though managed popup and editor paths already normalize it later.
design: Keep this small. Audit whether POSIX bootstrap should export VISUAL with the same resolved value as EDITOR, and only do it if that preserves the trimmed bootstrap boundary without duplicating too much editor-resolution logic. The goal is contract consistency across bootstrap and managed runtime entrypoints, not app-specific behavior.
acceptance_criteria: If POSIX bootstrap is meant to participate in the stable runtime editor contract, shells/posix/runtime_env.sh exports VISUAL consistently with EDITOR; bootstrap and Nushell-managed entrypoints no longer disagree about the base editor env surface; validation covers the intended contract without expanding bootstrap complexity unnecessarily.
close_reason: Not needed: editor env ownership belongs to the Nu-side canonical runtime env, while POSIX bootstrap stays minimal and only bootstraps runtime location, PATH, and YAZELIX_NU_BIN. Reopen only if a concrete pre-Nu bootstrap path actually needs VISUAL.

title: Make direct update commands confirm the chosen owner path instead of re-asking

title: Sweep remaining stale shell-hook wording after runtime setup ownership removal

title: Simplify startup by making inner startup own generated-layout handling
description:
Follow-up cleanup after the cold-start generated-layout fix. The current behavior is correct, but start_yazelix.nu now participates in managed generated-layout repair before inner startup runs.

This should likely be simplified so the outer startup layer only resolves runtime/config, validates the working directory, bootstraps the environment, and hands off. Generated-layout materialization, effective layout resolution, and final layout validation should live in one place.
design:
Preferred direction:
- remove generated-layout ownership from start_yazelix.nu
- let start_yazelix_inner.nu remain the single owner of regenerate_runtime_configs, layout resolution, and require-existing-layout checks
- keep clear failure behavior for custom or unmanaged layout overrides
- preserve the yzx doctor --fix repair surface independently
acceptance_criteria:
1. start_yazelix.nu no longer contains managed generated-layout repair logic
2. start_yazelix_inner.nu is the single owner of generated-layout materialization and validation
3. cold first-run startup still succeeds when only managed generated layouts are missing
4. custom or unmanaged layout overrides still fail clearly when unresolved
5. the regression coverage added for issue #558 remains green
close_reason: Completed

title: Align generated-layout recovery guidance with actual doctor repair behavior
description:
Why this issue exists and what needs to be done:
The missing generated-layout error path currently points users toward `yzx doctor`, but the doctor path does not currently repair missing generated runtime artifacts as part of `yzx doctor --fix`. That makes the recovery guidance weaker than it should be for the issue-558 failure shape.

Investigation summary:
- startup and runtime preflight report missing generated layouts as generated-state problems
- the error guidance points users toward `yzx doctor`
- `generated_runtime_state.nu` already has `repair_generated_runtime_state`, including missing-artifact repair behavior
- `doctor.nu` does not currently invoke that repair path during `yzx doctor --fix`

Acceptance criteria:
- Recovery messaging for missing generated-layout artifacts matches the real supported repair surface
- If `yzx doctor --fix` is intended to repair missing generated runtime artifacts, it actually does so
- Otherwise, the guidance is narrowed so users are not pointed at a repair path that does not exist
close_reason: Completed

title: Add cold package-surface launch smoke coverage for first-run generated state
description:
Why this issue exists and what needs to be done:
The current maintainer validation surface checks the packaged runtime wrapper, `yzx run`, and terminal-config generation, but it does not appear to run a cold first-run `nix run .#yazelix -- launch` style smoke test that exercises generated-state creation all the way through startup. That left the issue-558 ordering bug undetected.

Investigation summary:
- `validate_flake_install.nu` validates package/runtime probes and terminal-config generation
- existing tests defend the missing-layout preflight behavior itself
- there is not yet a focused cold package-surface launch smoke that proves first-run launch can cross the generated-state seam successfully

Acceptance criteria:
- Maintainer validation includes a focused cold-start package-surface launch probe or equivalent high-signal regression coverage
- The coverage defends the real first-run generated-state contract instead of only probing wrapper existence or command discovery
- The coverage would fail on the issue-558 regression shape
close_reason: Completed

title: Clarify v15 'non-Rust reboot' wording in docs and specs
description:
Why this issue exists and what needs to be done:
Current v15 messaging uses phrases like "trimmed non-Rust reboot", but the live workspace layer still depends on Rust in an important way through the Zellij pane orchestrator and related workspace contracts. The intended meaning is that v15 narrowed Rust compared with the broader roadmap, not that Rust was eliminated.

What needs to happen:
Review the main wording surfaces and update them so they communicate the real product boundary: v15 trimmed the product and narrowed Rust usage, while future/selective Rust work remains in scope. Avoid wording that implies Yazelix v15 is Rust-free if that is not true.

Likely surfaces to review:
- README.md
- docs/specs/v15_trimmed_runtime_contract.md
- docs/architecture_map.md
- Any release/history/roadmap text that repeats the same framing

Acceptance criteria:
- User-facing and maintainer-facing wording no longer implies that v15 removed Rust entirely
- The docs describe the actual current state: Rust remains in the workspace orchestration layer, but the product was trimmed and Rust scope was narrowed
- The wording supports the longer-term plan to expand or rewrite pieces in Rust without contradicting current docs
close_reason: Completed: replaced the misleading 'non-Rust reboot' framing with wording that says v15 is a trimmed reboot with narrower Rust scope, while the pane orchestrator and workspace layer still rely on Rust.

title: Remove stale TODO noise from shipped Yazi flavor snapshots
description: Several shipped Yazi flavor snapshot files still contain low-value '# TODO: remove' markers that do not describe a real maintenance action anymore. Clean those comments up so the shipped asset surface reads like an intentional snapshot rather than a half-finished scratch area.
design: This is a tiny cleanup bead, not a style rewrite. Delete obviously stale TODO markers or replace them with a precise upstream-sync note only if the comment still carries real meaning. Do not change flavor behavior just to satisfy the cleanup.
acceptance_criteria: The stale TODO markers in shipped Yazi flavor snapshots are removed or replaced with precise durable comments, and the asset content is otherwise unchanged.
notes: Removed stale '# TODO: remove' marker comments from six shipped Yazi flavor snapshots while leaving all TOML rules and values unchanged. Verification: rg found no remaining TODO markers under configs/yazi/flavors; taplo check --no-auto-config --no-schema passed on the six changed flavor.toml files; git diff --check passed. Full taplo check with repo schema still fails on existing unrelated Yazi schema drift in ashen.yazi.
close_reason: Completed: stale TODO marker comments removed from shipped Yazi flavor snapshots without changing asset behavior.

title: Make yzx status a structured Nushell table instead of hand-printed lines
description: yzx status is worth keeping, but its current line-by-line print output hides structure and makes later trimming harder to see. Rework it to build a clear record and table-oriented output so the surviving v15 status surface is easier to read, cheaper to extend, and more obviously scoped.
design: Prefer building a structured record first and letting Nushell render the main view, with optional richer sections like versions or verbose shell-hook details layered on top. The point is not flashy formatting; it is to make yzx status honest, smaller, and easier to keep aligned with the real surviving runtime contract.
acceptance_criteria: yzx status renders from structured data rather than a pile of manual prints, and the resulting output is clearer without expanding the command scope. Tests cover the surviving structured contract instead of brittle line-order trivia.
close_reason: Reworked yzx status to render from structured rows instead of manual print lines and aligned tests to the surviving table-oriented contract.

title: Document the stale-live-session contract after runtime updates and owner-managed relaunches
description:
Users can update the on-disk Yazelix runtime correctly and still keep an older live Ghostty/Zellij session open. That is a reasonable live-session contract, but it is easy to misread as a broken update when the product does not say it plainly.

Add one concise owner-aware note explaining that already-open Yazelix windows continue running the old runtime until they are explicitly relaunched or restarted. Keep the wording tight and factual in the relevant update and troubleshooting surfaces.
design:
Reason from first principles:
- Updating the runtime on disk and transitioning already-open live sessions are different operations.
- That distinction is normal, but users will read it as a failed update unless the product says so plainly.
- Keep this narrow and factual. Do not add noisy UI; just document the contract in the places where users already think about updates and recovery.
- The wording should stay owner-aware: fresh launch paths use the owner-provided wrapper or desktop surface, while already-open sessions stay on their current live runtime until explicit relaunch or restart.
acceptance_criteria: The relevant update and troubleshooting docs state clearly that already-open Yazelix windows keep running their current live runtime until they are relaunched or restarted. The wording matches the surviving manual-install and Home Manager owner contracts, including the owner-resolved restart behavior from yazelix-4buc.12 and the Home Manager smoke lane from yazelix-4buc.13. The docs must not promise invisible hot-swapping of already-open sessions.
notes: Completed: README, installation docs, troubleshooting docs, yzx CLI docs, and Home Manager README now state that already-open windows keep the current live runtime until relaunch or yzx restart.
close_reason: Completed: stale-live-session update ownership contract is documented across the relevant user-facing surfaces.

title: Delay devenver until v15 Yazelix proves a clean optional runtime and runner boundary
description: Evaluate `devenver` only after the trimmed v15 Yazelix line proves a real need for an optional dynamic runtime plus runner layer. Do not start a second new project at the same time as the v15 trim-first reset and do not genericize the current backend story prematurely.
design:
Reason from first principles:
- If `devenver` is created too early, it will likely just relocate current Yazelix entanglement and call it generic.
- The extraction should happen only after the trimmed v15 line proves the clean boundary between product semantics and optional runtime/runner semantics.
- `devenver` should be thought of as a runtime + runner system, not as a package manager.
- Nix/Home Manager still own install/update. A future `devenver` would own things like dynamic runtime preparation, entry/launch/exec handoff, refresh-like runtime rebuilding if it exists, and related cache/state.
- The output should define extraction criteria, what `devenver` would and would not own, and what evidence would justify extracting it later from the main Yazelix repo rather than leaving it as an internal boundary.
acceptance_criteria: There is an explicit delayed-extraction plan for `devenver`. The result defines when `devenver` should stay an idea, when it becomes an internal boundary, and what conditions would justify extracting it later from the main Yazelix repo after the trimmed v15 line is real.
notes: Completed: README/release direction records that the broader devenv runtime terminal layer may continue only as a separate future project and should not block the v15 trim-first line.
close_reason: Completed: optional devenv/devenver extraction is explicitly delayed until after v15 proves a clean boundary.

title: Classify Yazelix config surfaces by backend and devenv dependence
description: Audit `yazelix.toml` and `yazelix_packs.toml` by ownership and runtime dependence. Classify which settings remain meaningful in a package-runtime-only model, which require backend materialization or `devenv`, which are rebuild-relevant, and which would need to narrow, move, or disappear in a backend-free workspace-only slice. The first output should be a clear matrix and guidance for future simplification work, not an immediate config-file split unless the audit proves that split is actually justified.
notes: This is still useful, but it is no longer the immediate next move now that upstream nixpkgs submission is off the table. Do it after the flake-package-first surface and installer demotion work is concrete enough that the config audit is answering the right product boundary.
close_reason: Completed

title: Re-scope uninstall and desktop integration after runtime_current identity removal
description: Re-scope `yzx uninstall`, manual desktop-install logic, and related ownership assumptions after installer-owned and Home Manager-owned runtime-current identity is removed from the main product model. Keep only the user-local integration surfaces that still make sense for a flake-package-first and Home Manager-first product. Ground this in the real command code: `desktop.nu`, `uninstall.nu`, and `home_manager.nu` currently preserve or clean up paths whose meaning depends on the older installer-managed runtime model.

close_reason: Documented the distinction between distribution trimming and backend trimming
