title: Add Alt+<number> keybindings to yzx keys documentation
description: The yzx keys command should document the Alt+1 through Alt+9 keybindings that Yazelix provides for direct tab navigation. These keybindings already exist in the Zellij config (yazelix_overrides.kdl) but are not shown in the yzx keys output.
notes: Already satisfied by the current keybinding docs: yzx keys reports Alt+1..9 as direct tab navigation, README.md documents Alt+1..9, and docs/keybindings.md documents Alt+1 through Alt+9. No code change needed for this bead.
close_reason: Already documented in yzx keys, README, and keybindings docs.

title: Add local startup profile comparison and baseline tooling
description:
The new structured startup profiler makes local evidence possible, but there is still no first-class way to compare two runs, summarize phase deltas, or keep stable local baselines without relying on noisy hosted CI timing. That limits the practical value of the new reports for ongoing performance work.

Design:
Build on the existing structured JSONL report format rather than adding a separate metrics store. Provide a local-only surface that can diff two startup profile reports or compare a fresh run against a named baseline, highlighting phase deltas and major regressions. Keep this explicitly local and deterministic; do not turn it into a flaky GitHub Actions wall-clock gate.

Acceptance Criteria:
1. There is a user-facing local command or helper to compare two startup profile reports. 2. The comparison output highlights total wall-time delta plus per-step or per-phase deltas. 3. The tooling can work from saved reports under the Yazelix state dir without rerunning startup. 4. Docs explain the intended local workflow and explicitly avoid promising a noisy hosted-CI timing gate.
notes: Implemented local startup profile comparison tooling. Added yzx_control profile compare-reports/save-baseline/compare-baseline, user-facing yzx dev profile compare/save-baseline/compare-baseline wrappers, docs/spec updates, and a Rust regression test for total/per-step deltas. Verified focused Rust test, rust check, fmt check, spec validation, Nu syntax validation, rust test traceability, and command smoke checks against saved JSONL reports.
close_reason: Completed

title: Evaluate a Nix-only, no-devenv, opinionated flake backend for Yazelix Core
description: Evaluate whether a future Yazelix Core edition could use a Nix-only, no-devenv backend centered on an opinionated flake that ships the default Yazelix stack as one fixed bundle. The question is not whether this should replace full Yazelix. The question is whether keeping Nix while dropping devenv yields a simpler, more honest Core backend by shipping the core default tool/runtime set directly: Zellij, Yazi, Helix, Nushell, Starship, Lazygit, relevant default terminal support, runtime assets, and launch/update entrypoints.
design: Treat this as a post-v14 evaluation bead and as a specific Core-backend candidate, not as a new commitment for main. Reuse the backend contract, runtime dependency contract, command-surface audit, and Core-boundary work first. Then evaluate whether a fixed flake-defined default-stack bundle plus app/shell outputs can support the Core surface cleanly without carrying devenv profile semantics. This is intentionally not a full replacement for the current flexible pack-driven model. The evaluation should explicitly compare this shape against the Pixi experiment lane and explain where a Nix-only Core bundle is simpler, more honest, or more constrained.
acceptance_criteria: There is a concrete recommendation on whether a Nix-only, no-devenv, opinionated flake that ships the default Yazelix stack is a good fit for Yazelix Core. The result identifies which current Yazelix guarantees it could preserve, which ones it would intentionally drop, what install/update/run surface it would expose, how it would treat the current default tool set, and whether it is a better Core candidate than a non-Nix backend experiment. The output is specific enough to guide either a later prototype or an explicit no-go decision.
notes: Completed: the v15 branch already answers this evaluation in code and docs. The candidate is not a separate Core experiment anymore; it is the active trimmed branch direction: fixed packaged runtime bundle, no runtime-local devenv, no packs sidecar, built-in Ghostty, and explicit upstream/Home Manager update ownership.
close_reason: Completed: the current v15 branch itself is the evaluated Nix-only, no-devenv, fixed-runtime direction, with explicit update owners and a narrower packaged workspace contract.

title: Port runtime dependency checking and doctor reasoning into a Rust core
description: Once the runtime dependency contract and shared checker are defined in Nushell, evaluate and stage a Rust-backed implementation for the dependency/preflight reasoning that launch and doctor share. Focus on the classification engine and structured results, not on rendering every doctor message in Rust on day one. This is a candidate for gradual v15.x insertion if the slice becomes mature enough, otherwise it can remain part of the larger v16 Rust-forward push.
design: This should follow the contract work, not replace it. First prove the dependency model and user-visible semantics in the current v15 codebase. Then move the checker core into Rust if it materially simplifies correctness, structured diagnostics, and future performance or maintainability. Keep doctor and launch presentation in Nushell initially if that reduces churn.
acceptance_criteria: There is a concrete plan for moving the shared runtime contract checker into Rust, with a clear boundary between structured result generation and user-facing rendering. The plan identifies parity cases, launch-time performance constraints, expected machine-readable output, and how doctor will consume the same results without reintroducing duplicate logic. It also states honestly whether this slice belongs in a v15.x point release or should wait for the broader v16 push.
notes:
Primary target logic lives around doctor.nu and the future shared runtime checker introduced by yazelix-hv2m. This slice should not start until j4qv and hv2m have clarified the contract in the current codebase.
2026-04-19 Rust dependency gate: before any doctor/preflight Rust implementation, record the crate-vs-in-house plan. Structured finding classification should default to Yazelix-owned domain types; add crates only for narrow parsing, serialization, or test value.
2026-04-19 deletion ledger:
- Temporary seams expected in this slice: doctor.nu and launch-time preflight should become formatting and exit-policy consumers, not parallel owners of dependency classification.
- Deletion targets are duplicated reasoning branches in runtime_contract_checker.nu, doctor.nu, doctor_install_artifacts.nu, doctor_helix.nu, runtime_distribution_capabilities.nu, and related launch preflight helpers once Rust findings become the shared source.
- Must delete by close of kt5.3: any duplicated classification path that still computes findings separately in Nushell, or file explicit follow-up beads naming the leftover duplicates.
- Keep prose-heavy rendering in Nushell if useful, but do not keep two reasoning engines.
2026-04-19 concrete first slice for kt5.3:
- Rust target is the shared structured-finding engine behind `runtime_contract_checker.nu`, not the whole `yzx doctor` surface.
- Keep in Nushell for now: doctor rendering, `--fix` actions, desktop-entry and shell-shadowing audits from `doctor_install_artifacts.nu`, Helix-specific diagnostics from `doctor_helix.nu`, runtime distribution tier reporting from `runtime_distribution_capabilities.nu`, and live Zellij session/plugin checks. Those are either prose-heavy, install-surface-specific, or session-local rather than shared launch blockers.
- First Rust command shape should be one batch evaluation per surface \(startup, launch, doctor shared-preflight\), not one helper subprocess per individual check. The launch/startup performance constraint is to avoid turning simple preflight into several extra helper process launches.
- First parity set to port: working-directory validation, runtime script existence, generated-layout existence, terminal availability, and the Linux Ghostty desktop-graphics warning logic that currently lives in `runtime_contract_checker.nu`. Layout-path resolution can stay in Nushell initially and feed the resolved path into Rust.
- Honest v15.x boundary: this slice is reasonable for v15.x if it stays at the shared finding engine plus bridge consumption. Pulling in install-artifact doctoring, Helix runtime analysis, or session-local plugin health should wait for later beads or the broader v16 push.
2026-04-19 kt5.3 dependency decision:
- Production crates: keep the existing helper stack only \(`serde`, `serde_json`, `toml`, `sha2`, `thiserror`, `lexopt`\). No new production crate is justified for runtime/preflight evaluation; PATH scanning, filesystem checks, and OS/env inspection should stay on `std`.
- Dev-only crates: keep the current Rust test helpers only \(`tempfile`, `pretty_assertions`, `assert_cmd`\).
- In-house logic: runtime-check domain types, severity/failure-class modeling, terminal candidate detection for the supported Yazelix terminal list, nixGL wrapper ownership detection, and batch request/response shaping stay Yazelix-owned.
- Rejected alternatives: no `which` crate, no `clap`, no broader system-information crate, no command-runner crate. The contract is narrow enough that extra dependencies would mostly hide simple logic.
- Packaging impact: should stay within the existing packaged `yzx_core` helper, with one new helper command and Nushell bridge updates in launch/startup/doctor call sites.
2026-04-19 landed v15.x shared Rust preflight boundary:
- Added `yzx_core runtime-contract.evaluate` with batch request/response types for startup, launch, and doctor shared-preflight.
- Ported shared structured findings for working-directory validation, runtime-script existence, generated-layout existence, terminal availability/candidate selection, and the Linux Ghostty desktop-graphics warning into `rust_core/yazelix_core/src/runtime_contract.rs`.
- Reworked `runtime_contract_checker.nu` into a Rust-backed bridge while keeping Nushell ownership of rendering, `require_runtime_check`, and doctor/launch exit policy.
- Startup and new-window launch now use one batch preflight call on the main path; direct helper seams like `validate_startup_working_dir`, `validate_launch_working_dir`, and terminal-candidate helpers were restored as thin wrappers so workspace/test contracts stayed stable.
- Tightened runtime-root resolution so repo-sourced code prefers the checkout over ambient installed-runtime pointers from `/nix/store` or `~/.local/share/yazelix/runtime/...`, while explicit synthetic runtime roots still win for tests and real runtime surfaces.
- Updated `docs/specs/runtime_dependency_preflight_contract.md` to record the v15.x Rust/Nushell boundary and helper verification surface.
Verification:
- `nix shell nixpkgs#nu-lint -c nu-lint nushell/scripts/utils/common.nu nushell/scripts/utils/config_parser.nu nushell/scripts/utils/runtime_contract_checker.nu nushell/scripts/utils/doctor.nu nushell/scripts/core/start_yazelix.nu nushell/scripts/core/launch_yazelix.nu nushell/scripts/dev/test_yzx_doctor_commands.nu nushell/scripts/dev/test_shell_managed_config_contracts.nu`
- `YAZELIX_YZX_CORE_BIN=/home/lucca/pjs/yazelix/rust_core/target/debug/yzx_core nu -c "source nushell/scripts/dev/test_yzx_workspace_commands.nu; run_workspace_canonical_tests"`
- `YAZELIX_YZX_CORE_BIN=/home/lucca/pjs/yazelix/rust_core/target/debug/yzx_core nu -c "source nushell/scripts/dev/test_yzx_doctor_commands.nu; run_doctor_canonical_tests"`
- `nu -c "source nushell/scripts/dev/test_shell_managed_config_contracts.nu; run_shell_managed_config_contract_tests"`
- `nix shell nixpkgs#cargo nixpkgs#rustc -c cargo test --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml`
- `nu -c "source nushell/scripts/dev/validate_specs.nu; main"`
- `git diff --check`
close_reason: Completed: shared runtime preflight reasoning now runs through the Rust yzx_core batch helper, with Nushell kept as the rendering and fix-policy surface.

title: Make welcome launch prompt use any key instead of Enter
description: The welcome screen currently says 'Press Enter to launch Zellij and start your session...' and waits for a full line input. Change it to a true single-key continue prompt so the text and behavior match and startup feels snappier.
close_reason: Changed welcome prompt to a true any-key continue

title: Clarify and verify the zsh/fish managed-shell availability boundary
description: Record and evaluate the current shell boundary: Yazelix-managed zsh/fish hook surfaces exist and work in managed shells, but zsh and fish are not currently shipped in the installed runtime bin/ and are instead provided through the managed devenv environment. Decide whether that is the intended product contract or whether extra shells should eventually become runtime-owned too.
design: Keep this as a low-priority clarification bead, not a pressure to runtime-own zsh or fish. The goal is to make the boundary explicit: Yazelix-managed hook surfaces exist for zsh and fish, but the shells themselves currently come from the managed devenv environment rather than the installed runtime bin. Verify the real behavior, record the intended support boundary, and only add tests or docs that defend that boundary clearly.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The repo has a clear documented statement of where zsh and fish are expected to come from in managed Yazelix sessions today. 2. The support boundary between managed hook surfaces and runtime-owned shell binaries is verified instead of guessed. 3. Any added validation or docs defend that actual boundary without turning this into pressure to runtime-ship more shells immediately.
close_reason: Obsolete: the repo now runtime-ships fish and zsh as part of the fixed v15 shell set, and the current docs plus install validation already describe and defend that boundary.

title: Scaffold visible stubs for Yazelix-managed user config surfaces
description: Create lightweight empty/commented stubs for relevant Yazelix-managed user config surfaces so users can discover where customizations belong without reading docs first.
design: Scope this to lightweight, self-explanatory scaffolding for Yazelix-owned user config surfaces under ~/.config/yazelix/user_configs/. Prefer creating stubs automatically when a surface becomes relevant or is first managed, not a giant first-run dump of empty files. For shells, likely create ~/.config/yazelix/user_configs/shells/ plus small commented stubs. For other managed surfaces, favor one-line comment stubs or empty directories only when they materially improve discoverability. These are user-owned runtime files under ~/.config/yazelix, not tracked repo files and not personal dotfiles outside Yazelix ownership.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Relevant Yazelix-managed user config surfaces are auto-created under ~/.config/yazelix/user_configs/ when they become relevant. 2. Scaffolding stays lightweight and does not create a noisy pile of unused files by default. 3. Stub files clearly say they are Yazelix-managed extension points. 4. No personal config files outside ~/.config/yazelix/ are created or modified by this feature. 5. The implementation does not add tracked repo-state pollution for these user-owned stubs.

title: Audit update devenv and related version-sync flows
description: Verify yzx update devenv and the surrounding version-sync behavior against the current runtime contract. Confirm the command updates to the Yazelix-pinned devenv surface and does not leave version reporting, doctor output, or runtime entrypoints in a contradictory state.
close_reason: Audit completed: yzx update devenv and yzx dev update canaries were verified; canary syntax and temp config writability bugs were fixed.

title: Evaluate Ghostty package source policy
description: Evaluate which Ghostty package source Yazelix should prefer for its managed/default terminal path once the current integration bugs are fixed.
design: Compare the three realistic Ghostty package sources separately instead of treating 'Ghostty' as one blob: nixpkgs Ghostty on Linux, the upstream Ghostty flake, and system Ghostty / Homebrew on macOS. Judge them on stability, platform coverage, integration burden, update cadence, and whether they improve or complicate Yazelix's managed launch path. Do not let this policy work blur into the current Ghostty runtime/shader bug fixes.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear Ghostty package-source policy for Yazelix covering nixpkgs Ghostty, the upstream Ghostty flake, and system/Homebrew Ghostty on macOS. The chosen default(s) and any experimental/override path are explicit, with reasoning tied to platform support and maintenance cost.
notes: Decision on 2026-04-01: Yazelix should not switch its default managed Ghostty source from nixpkgs to the upstream Ghostty flake. The upstream flake is explicitly the tip/dev path, not the regular-user package path, and it carries the same non-NixOS OpenGL caveat. Recommended policy shape: macOS should continue to use system/Homebrew Ghostty; NixOS can continue to treat nixpkgs Ghostty as the normal managed package path; non-NixOS Linux should stop assuming nixpkgs Ghostty is a friction-free default and should likely prefer another default terminal or a system Ghostty path unless the user explicitly opts into the Nix-managed Ghostty path and its GPU/driver caveats.
close_reason: Completed

title: Clarify pinned bootstrap-tool onboarding responsibilities
description:
Problem:
Yazelix now pins and manages some bootstrap-adjacent tools, but the onboarding story is still fuzzy about what the installer should install directly versus what it should merely assume or validate.

Design:
Define the onboarding contract for pinned bootstrap tools such as devenv, and clarify what is in scope for the installer versus what must already exist on the host.

Acceptance Criteria:
- The onboarding contract clearly states which bootstrap tools Yazelix installs or refreshes itself.
- The docs/specs distinguish installer-managed bootstrap tools from host prerequisites.
- The result does not blur into unrelated release/update automation work.
close_reason: Completed

title: Reconcile docs around the current flake install story
description:
Problem:
Some docs still describe flake as removed or otherwise contradict the new `nix run ...#install` front door.

Design:
Audit and fix the remaining contradictory docs so the current install story is internally consistent.

Acceptance Criteria:
- Primary docs no longer contradict the existence of the flake install surface.
- User-facing installation guidance is internally consistent.
close_reason: Reconciled README and troubleshooting docs with the current flake install story

title: Decide desktop entry installation responsibility for the flake installer
description:
Problem:
The flake installer materializes runtime state, but desktop-entry installation is not yet an explicit part of the contract.

Design:
Decide whether the installer should install/update desktop-entry assets directly or whether that remains a separate setup responsibility.

Acceptance Criteria:
- Desktop-entry responsibility is explicitly decided.
- The decision is reflected in docs or implementation follow-up scope.
close_reason: Decided desktop-entry installation stays explicit via yzx desktop install and updated specs/docs accordingly

title: Improve yzx run argument pass-through for external commands
description: Improve yzx run so argument pass-through to external commands is predictable and does not silently reshape argv in surprising ways.
design: Treat this as a command-surface contract issue. Clarify exactly what yzx run should pass through, where Yazelix-specific parsing should stop, and how quoting, separators, and variadic arguments should behave for external commands. Avoid compatibility hacks that preserve ambiguous behavior; the end state should be a clear argv contract that maintainers can test directly.
acceptance_criteria: There is a defined argv pass-through contract for yzx run, including representative examples of external command invocation. The bead leaves behind either an implementation path or a documented decision on what ambiguous legacy behavior will not be preserved.
close_reason: Completed with wrapped argv passthrough contract and focused regression coverage

title: Remove tracked .vscode repo state
description: Remove the tracked .vscode/settings.json Kiro-local preference, ignore .vscode/, and keep editor-local repo settings out of git by default.
close_reason: Resolved earlier: .vscode is ignored and no .vscode files are tracked.

title: Evaluate Lenia welcome style later
design: Defer Lenia until the welcome-style system exists and the cost/readability tradeoffs are proven. Prefer a pre-rendered or precomputed frame-sequence evaluation first; only consider live Lenia later if it proves clearly worth the complexity. This bead is for evaluating whether Lenia should join the animated pool later, not for forcing a live simulation into startup prematurely.
acceptance_criteria: The evaluation documents whether Lenia should be live, precomputed, or rejected for startup use, and only proceeds after the shared welcome_style selector exists.

title: Add optional yazelix_packs.toml sidecar config
description: Add an optional ~/.config/yazelix/yazelix_packs.toml sidecar so dense pack configuration can move out of yazelix.toml without removing any existing pack capability or turning packs into a folder forest.
design: Treat this as a configuration-clarity feature, not a pack redesign. Keep yazelix.toml as the front door and add yazelix_packs.toml as an optional structured sidecar for denser pack enablement and pack-specific settings. Preserve all current pack features; the sidecar must not remove any capability currently expressible in yazelix.toml. Define one sharp ownership rule so users are never left wondering which file wins: either the sidecar owns dense pack config while yazelix.toml keeps only simple top-level references, or Yazelix fails fast when the same pack keys are declared in both places. Prefer explicit duplicate-source errors over silent precedence surprises. Keep this limited to packs; do not turn it into a generic many-file config explosion. Because packs are supported through both direct config and Home Manager today, the sidecar design must preserve a coherent story for both paths.
acceptance_criteria: Yazelix supports an optional yazelix_packs.toml without regressing existing pack behavior from yazelix.toml. The final design clearly documents what pack data belongs in each file, how duplicate or conflicting definitions are handled, and how the feature works for both direct config users and Home Manager users. Add focused unit tests for sidecar discovery, parse or merge behavior, duplicate-source errors, and pack-resolution behavior. Add end-to-end tests with detailed logs for main-file-only pack config, sidecar-only pack config, a conflicting dual-definition case that fails clearly, a direct-config path, a Home Manager path, and a representative generated-config run showing the resolved pack set and emitted tooling or config output.
close_reason: Completed

title: Add data-driven Ghostty cursor builder
description: Add a constrained, data-driven Ghostty cursor builder that lets users choose a supported cursor family and supply 2-3 colors in ~/.config/yazelix/yazelix_cursors.toml while preserving all existing named presets. Generate runtime shader output from structured Yazelix-owned config rather than exposing raw GLSL or terminal-native shader editing.
design: Refined direction: this is not just a one-off custom color escape hatch. The goal is to migrate Yazelix's useful Ghostty cursor preset library and cursor behavior settings into a data-driven sidecar so presets and cursor effects can be edited without bloating yazelix_default.toml. Once this lands, yazelix.toml should lose every Ghostty cursor setting; cursor selection, enabled cursor list, global effects, duration, glow, Kitty cursor enablement, and cursor definitions live in user_configs/yazelix_cursors.toml or an equivalent explicitly owned cursor sidecar. The sidecar should separate selection/settings from definitions: enabled_cursors = ["blaze", "orchid"] is the explicit source of truth for what can be selected and what random can draw from; [settings] owns global trail, trail_effect, mode_effect, glow, duration, and kitty_enable_cursor; [[cursor]] tables are definitions users can edit or add. For Kitty, keep the model binary: kitty_enable_cursor = true/false. Do not expose a separate Kitty fallback cursor selector in the first version. Keep effects/duration/glow global; do not add per-cursor effect overrides yet. Keep the schema minimal and avoid overlay/replace registry modes, random include filters, tags, per-entry enabled flags, or raw GLSL DSL. Preserve the existing simple selector UX through the sidecar: settings.trail = "random", named presets, and none continue to work. Validate strictly: enabled names must resolve exactly once, cursor names must be unique, families must be supported, colors must have exact counts and valid hex values, global setting values must be in their allowed sets, and duration must be in range. Start with data-driven generation for simple_dual and axis_gradient presets. Remove party from the supported set unless a real purpose appears. Keep neon as a curated_template/hardcoded shader in the first slice to avoid overfitting the registry around the most complex effect.
acceptance_criteria: Evaluate and implement a cursor sidecar model that removes Ghostty cursor settings from yazelix.toml while keeping useful Ghostty cursor presets data-driven where practical. Existing user intent from ghostty_trail_color, ghostty_trail_effect, ghostty_mode_effect, ghostty_trail_glow, ghostty_trail_duration, and Kitty cursor fallback behavior must have a clear transition path into the sidecar. Existing named values keep working via settings.trail, including random and none; party is removed or intentionally excluded unless a product purpose is found. Neon may remain a curated hardcoded preset with registry metadata for random behavior. Users can enable/disable cursors through enabled_cursors, edit/add definitions through [[cursor]] tables, set global effects/duration/glow through [settings], and toggle Kitty cursor fallback with kitty_enable_cursor; generated runtime shader output remains Yazelix-owned. Invalid sidecar data fails fast with actionable errors. Home Manager either gets an explicit sidecar ownership story or clearly documents that cursor customization is direct-config/user-owned for the first slice. Tests cover sidecar parsing, enabled_cursors resolution, missing/duplicate enabled names, named-preset compatibility, random drawing from enabled_cursors, global settings validation, Kitty enable/disable behavior, color/family validation, generated shader output, no-sidecar default behavior, and legacy yazelix.toml cursor-field handling.
notes:
2026-04-27 refinement from user: the intended design is to migrate existing Ghostty cursor presets to a data-driven registry so users can change and disable/enable them. This makes a sidecar appropriate because the cursor preset data is too large for yazelix_default.toml. The main config should stay small and selector-oriented; the sidecar should own the larger cursor dataset.
2026-04-27 refinement from user: party can be killed because it is too wild and has no clear purpose. Neon can stay as a hardcoded/curated preset for now, which keeps the first data-driven migration focused on simpler families instead of forcing every shader into the same schema.
2026-04-27 refinement from user: do not use registry mode = overlay/replace or random_include_tags. The sidecar should be a simple list of enabled cursors. Defaults ship with the existing useful cursors, and users add, modify, comment, or uncomment cursor entries directly.
2026-04-27 refinement from user: tags are unnecessary. Keep the cursor sidecar schema small and direct.
2026-04-27 refinement from user: the enabled list should be explicit, e.g. enabled_cursors = ["blaze", "orchid"]. Cursor tables define available presets; enabled_cursors controls selection/random.
2026-04-27 refinement from user: yazelix.toml can lose every cursor setting. Move trail selection, effects, duration, glow, Kitty fallback, and cursor definitions into the cursor sidecar, with effects kept global for simplicity.
2026-04-27 refinement from user: Kitty should be simpler: kitty_enable_cursor = true/false. Do not expose a Kitty fallback cursor selector in the first schema.
Implemented data-driven cursor sidecar: shipped yazelix_cursors_default.toml, bootstraps user_configs/yazelix_cursors.toml, moved Ghostty cursor settings out of yazelix.toml/Home Manager, removed party from supported runtime presets, kept neon as curated_template, added strict Rust validation, generated data-driven Ghostty shaders, and made Kitty cursor fallback a sidecar boolean. Verified with rust check, focused Rust tests, config-surface/upgrade/flake validators, fmt, Taplo checks for main TOML surfaces, and git diff --check.
close_reason: Completed

title: Replace Bash-heavy terminal wrappers in devenv.nix with Nushell-first helpers
description: Reduce remaining Bash-heavy terminal wrapper logic in devenv.nix only where moving it into shared Nushell helpers meaningfully shrinks duplication without obscuring terminal-specific launch behavior.
design: Treat this as a cleanup follow-up, not an abstraction contest. The goal is to centralize shared resolution logic, error messaging, and config-path handling in Nushell while leaving terminal-specific CLI flags explicit where they differ materially. Do not hide per-terminal launch contracts behind generic wrappers that become harder to reason about than the current code. Any refactor must preserve fail-fast startup, deterministic terminal config resolution, and the current Yazelix versus user mode semantics. Measure the change by whether the wrappers get simpler and the ownership boundary becomes easier to understand, not by whether every line of shell disappears.
acceptance_criteria: Shared wrapper logic that is truly common moves into Nushell, while terminal-specific launch behavior remains readable and correct. Add focused unit tests for the Nushell helpers that replace wrapper logic, especially config resolution, mode validation, and error paths. Add end-to-end wrapper smoke tests with detailed logs for at least Ghostty, WezTerm, Kitty, Alacritty, and Foot, showing the resolved config path, effective mode, and final terminal invocation shape. Verification must demonstrate that cleanup reduced duplication without changing supported behavior.
notes: Dropped as obsolete on v15: devenv.nix and the Bash-heavy terminal wrapper surface targeted by this bead no longer exist. Remaining devenv.nix mentions are historical/spec text, not an active wrapper implementation to refactor.
close_reason: Dropped as obsolete: v15 removed devenv.nix and the targeted Bash-heavy terminal wrapper implementation.

title: Adopt Nushell-style tutor flow for yzx tutor
description:
Follow up to the shipped `yzx tutor` MVP.

Keep this bead strictly about a richer tutor flow, not config onboarding. Config-first setup belongs under `yzx onboard`, not here.
design:
Adopt the shape of Nushell's built-in `tutor`: explicit subcommands like `begin`, `list`, and short guided steps instead of a single static overview. Keep the scope Yazelix-specific rather than copying Nushell literally. The goal is a small guided learning flow with mini quests that help users practice the managed workspace model.

Potential shape:
- `yzx tutor begin` starts the first Yazelix lesson
- `yzx tutor list` shows available lessons
- lessons are short, structured, and action-oriented
- mini quests ask the user to try concrete actions like `yzx keys`, `yzx menu`, `yzx cwd`, and Yazi-driven editor opens

Non-goal:
- guided `yazelix.toml` creation or interactive setup questionnaires; that belongs to `yzx onboard`
acceptance_criteria: The tutor surface can present multiple short lessons with explicit entrypoints instead of only a flat overview. At least one lesson includes a mini quest with a concrete Yazelix workflow to try. The flow stays distinct from `yzx help` \(command reference\), `yzx keys` \(keybinding discovery\), and `yzx onboard` \(config creation\).

title: Evaluate replacing yzx gc's du store-size probe with a modern Rust tool
description:
Investigate whether Yazelix should replace the current `du -sb /nix/store` size probe used by `yzx gc` with a modern Rust-based alternative such as `dust`, `dua`, or `gdu`.

Scope:
- identify which candidate tools are realistically available in the Yazelix environment or Nixpkgs
- verify whether any candidate provides a stable script-friendly single-path total size mode suitable for automation
- compare startup/runtime cost and output stability against `du -sb`
- avoid switching just for novelty; the replacement must be at least as robust for scripted GC summaries

Success means there is a clear decision: either adopt one concrete Rust tool for the probe with a clean contract, or document why `du -sb` should remain the implementation.
design: Judge replacement candidates by scriptability and robustness first, not by novelty or a Rust implementation. The current du probe may be ugly but acceptable if it is stable, available, and cheap enough. Any replacement must provide a clean single-path total-size contract, acceptable runtime, and predictable output that Yazelix can safely automate.
acceptance_criteria: There is a concrete keep or replace decision for the store-size probe. If replaced, one tool and invocation contract are chosen. If not, the bead records why the current du-based probe remains the better maintained option despite the appeal of modern tooling.
close_reason: Obsolete after deleting yzx gc from the v15 public surface.

title: Evaluate FlakeHub adoption for Yazelix
description: Evaluate whether FlakeHub would bring enough value to Yazelix to justify the additional platform coupling and paid features. Current evidence suggests the immediate upside is limited because Yazelix is still primarily a repo plus devenv plus yazelix.toml workflow, while the strongest future benefits would be binary cache ergonomics and distribution once Yazelix has a more package-first flake interface. Include current pricing, free/open-source eligibility, CI/cache use cases, and decision criteria for when adoption would become worthwhile.
design: Treat FlakeHub as a distribution and maintenance decision, not a generic ecosystem checkbox. Compare it against Yazelix's actual packaging maturity, expected install paths, binary-cache needs, CI ergonomics, open-source eligibility, and future flake-first direction. The decision should be time-aware: a no-for-now answer is valid if the product shape is not ready yet.
acceptance_criteria: There is an explicit adopt, defer, or reject decision on FlakeHub, with current value, costs, and future trigger conditions documented. If adoption is deferred, the bead records what would need to change in Yazelix's packaging model for FlakeHub to become worthwhile later.

title: Plan gradual Rust insertion across v15.x toward a Rust-forward v16 release
description: Create a long-term plan for introducing Rust into Yazelix only after v15.0 ships, and then only gradually during v15.x where it buys real safety, maintainability, or performance, while keeping the release identity honest: v15.0 remains the trimmed rebooted non-Rust release, and v16 is the release where Rust becomes a major visible architectural fact. The goal is not rewriting for aesthetics; it is getting a more explicit, testable, and maintainable system with clear behavior contracts and a cleaner boundary between deterministic core logic and the surviving shell/Nix glue.
design: Treat this as a current-v15 architecture-and-process initiative with explicit release framing. Do not make Rust a prerequisite for shipping v15.0 and do not market v15.0 as the Rust release. Rust can land incrementally behind later v15.x point releases when a slice is mature enough, but the roadmap should keep that work honest and narrow. The rewrite plan should define what belongs in a Rust core, what remains Nushell or Nix glue, and how feature behavior becomes spec-driven by default. Every user-visible feature should have a Markdown spec and tests that map to the spec's acceptance cases. The sequence should start with deterministic, reusable logic, not interactive shell glue.
acceptance_criteria: There is a concrete plan for post-v15.0 gradual Rust insertion during v15.x and for a more explicitly Rust-forward v16 release. The plan identifies the first migration slices, the surfaces that should remain Nushell or Nix, the parity strategy for each slice, and the support boundary between Rust and the remaining glue so work can proceed incrementally without confusing release identity or turning Rust into a prerequisite for the initial v15 release.
notes:
Updated 2026-04-13: release framing is now explicit. v15.x may gain Rust incrementally, but v16 is the release where Rust becomes a major visible architectural fact.
2026-04-19 deletion-discipline update:
- Every Rust bead must record temporary seams introduced, the exact Nushell or CI glue expected to disappear, and the earliest bead where deletion is required.
- A Rust slice that only adds helper code without shrinking any surviving Nushell owner seam is suspect and should not be treated as done unless it files an explicit deletion follow-up.
- Parity tests stay only while they defend migration risk or a permanent public contract. Transition-only parity coverage should be deleted once the replacement boundary is stable.
2026-04-26 successor note: the Rust insertion/migration plan is effectively complete enough that the next architecture risk is Rust bloat, stale bridge seams, and weak migration-era tests. Successor cleanup work is tracked under yazelix-9opk, which audits the Rust codebase for canonical form after migration.

title: Evaluate integrating sahaj-b/ghostty-cursor-shaders into Yazelix-managed cursor effects
description: Evaluate adding curated support for the MIT-licensed ghostty-cursor-shaders repo to Yazelix so Ghostty cursor effects remain fully Yazelix-managed instead of requiring users to clone and wire shader files manually. The goal is to extend the current Ghostty cursor-trail system with upstream trail and pulse/boom patterns while preserving Yazelix generation, docs, defaults, and random selection.
design: Current state: Yazelix already generates Ghostty shader config, vendors local shader files under configs/terminal_emulators/ghostty/shaders, and defaults terminal.cursor_trail to random. The upstream repo is a good fit because Ghostty supports multiple custom-shader lines and the repo is simple standalone GLSL under MIT. Important caveat: these upstream effects are not only color variants. They include trail shaders like cursor_warp, cursor_sweep, and cursor_tail, plus mode-change shaders like ripple_cursor, sonic_boom_cursor, rectangle_boom_cursor, and ripple_rectangle_cursor. That means this should be treated as a broader cursor-effects surface, not just more colors. Preferred direction: keep Yazelix as the builder and orchestrator, vendor or generate a curated subset of upstream shaders locally, and expose them through a structured selector that supports both existing color presets and new pattern effects. A robust design is to support an ordered list for Ghostty shader selection and composition, with backward-compatible string aliases for existing cursor_trail values. If random remains the default, define a curated random pool explicitly instead of sampling every shader, because pulse and boom effects are behaviorally different from trails and may need separate inclusion rules. Also account for Ghostty-specific caveats such as custom-shader-animation = always for some line-cursor effects, and avoid regressing Kitty fallback behavior that currently maps random to snow.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear implementation decision and scope for integrating ghostty-cursor-shaders into Yazelix. The chosen design keeps shader management inside Yazelix, documents the upstream source and license, and specifies whether the config surface stays terminal.cursor_trail, becomes truly list-capable, or is renamed to a broader cursor-effects concept. The plan identifies which upstream shaders are included, how random selection works, what Ghostty-only caveats must be emitted in generated config, and how existing cursor_trail users remain backward-compatible. Verification includes generating Ghostty config with a fixed preset, a composed or list-based preset if adopted, and the default random path.
close_reason: Implemented Ghostty cursor effect integration with vendored shaders, new Ghostty effect config, generated shader build, and collection/docs updates

title: Experiment: evaluate a Pixi-backed Yazelix branch
description: Evaluate whether Yazelix could support a separate Pixi-backed experiment branch without turning main into a multi-backend runtime. Focus on a narrow prototype: Helix, Yazi, Zellij, Nushell, and core CLI flows such as yzx env, yzx run, and launch/bootstrap. The goal is to determine whether Pixi is credible for a lower-support branch, what major gaps remain compared with the current devenv/Nix model, and whether the branch should exist at all.
design: Treat this as an exploration task, not a commitment to multi-backend support in main. Keep the scope narrow: prototype a separate Pixi-backed branch that covers the core Yazelix stack plus the command slice that still matters after the backend-contract and command-surface work lands. The task should identify what maps cleanly from the current runtime model to Pixi, what becomes unsupported or degraded, and whether the resulting branch would be honest enough to maintain as a lower-support option. Avoid solving generic backend abstraction unless the prototype proves the branch is worth pursuing first.
acceptance_criteria: There is a concrete recommendation on whether Yazelix should maintain a Pixi-backed experiment branch. The result includes: a validated package-availability check for the core toolchain; a clear list of current devenv/Nix assumptions that would need replacement; a small prototype or spike proving the core launch/env/run flow is either viable or not; and a documented capability matrix showing which features would work, degrade, or remain unsupported. Leave behind enough notes and logs that the branch decision is reproducible instead of intuition-driven.
close_reason: Not planned: Nix serves Yazelix well and we do not want to spend backlog space on a Pixi-backed experiment branch.

title: Proper agent command center IDE for teams of running agents
description: Create a real agent command-center surface for coordinating multiple running agents across one or more monitors, with fast visibility into agent state and the ability to pop open related tools.
design: Design this as a first-class operations surface rather than a pile of widgets. The core use cases are seeing which agents exist, whether they are idle or active, showing or hiding their panes, opening related terminals or tools, and exposing lightweight usage or health stats. Prefer a coherent workspace dashboard that can scale across monitors and degrade gracefully when some agent integrations are unavailable.
acceptance_criteria: Yazelix has a credible agent command-center direction with a concrete first slice that helps manage multiple running agents. The chosen surface can reveal agent state, toggle or focus the relevant workspace surfaces, and open related tools without feeling bolted on. Add unit tests for state and action routing plus end-to-end validation that logs the visible agents, derived status, and invoked workspace actions.

title: Prepare Steel-mode support for Helix
description: Prepare Yazelix to support Helix's emerging Steel-based plugin system once it is merged upstream or otherwise stable enough to justify first-class support.
design: Keep this future-facing but concrete. The bead covers three possible paths that should be evaluated explicitly: supporting the eventual upstream plugin system in stock Helix, allowing advanced users to point Yazelix at a Helix fork that already ships the Steel plugin runtime, or maintaining a Yazelix-managed fork if that becomes justified. Do not assume the right answer yet. First define the support model, packaging implications, runtime path implications, and how plugin installation and config would fit Yazelix without turning editor support into chaos. Options like pointing a derivation at the plugin author's fork or shipping a Yazelix-branded fork such as neohelix should be treated as tradeoffs to evaluate, not default answers.
acceptance_criteria: The result is a clear adoption plan for Steel-based Helix plugins, not a vague placeholder. It should spell out the supported path, unsupported path, and migration story if upstream Helix merges the system. Any implementation work must include unit tests for config parsing and runtime-path decisions and end-to-end smoke tests that launch the chosen Helix runtime with plugins enabled, with detailed logs showing runtime resolution and plugin load results.

title: Define curated LSP/formatter/linter defaults for every Yazelix-supported language pack
description: Define and document the default LSP, formatter, and linter for every Yazelix-supported language pack so each pack feels like a curated development environment rather than a loose bundle of packages.
design: Keep this opinionated and pack-first. For each supported language pack, decide the default LSP, formatter, and linter Yazelix stands behind, document the reasoning, and make sure the pack actually installs the chosen tools. Avoid turning this into an explosion of alternatives; the point is a clear supported default for each pack.
acceptance_criteria: Every supported language pack has a documented default LSP, formatter, and linter, and the pack implementation matches that documentation. Add unit tests for the pack-to-tool mapping, integration tests for config generation, and end-to-end fixture checks for a representative set of languages, with detailed logs of the resolved tools and launched language-service commands.
close_reason: Stale v14-era issue. v15 dropped language packs (yazelix_packs.toml, yzx packs) entirely. Yazelix no longer manages curated LSP/formatter/linter defaults — users bring their own editor tooling.

title: Experiment: use Nushell job freeze/unfreeze to reuse pane (no-sidebar)
description: Investigate whether Nushell job freeze/unfreeze can be used under the hood to let Helix and Yazi share the same pane in the no_sidebar layout instead of paying the full reopen cost every time.
design: This is an experiment, not a guaranteed feature. The target flow is the no_sidebar layout only: freeze one program, swap in the other, and unfreeze it later so the pane feels reused rather than recreated. Evaluate correctness first: terminal state, shell/editor cleanup, focus behavior, log noise, and what happens when a frozen process crashes or the pane is closed. If the technique is brittle, capture that clearly and reject it rather than forcing it into production.
acceptance_criteria: The bead ends with either a working prototype for no_sidebar or a written rejection based on concrete evidence. If implemented, add unit tests around the state machine that tracks frozen vs active processes, plus e2e coverage for repeated Helix/Yazi swaps, crash/recovery paths, and pane-close behavior with detailed timing and state-transition logs.
close_reason: Superseded by the v15 pane orchestrator architecture. The Rust plugin manages separate editor/sidebar panes with distinct identities and swap layout families handle visibility. Freeze/unfreeze pane sharing would fight the plugin's pane-discovery model and the reopen cost it aimed to avoid is no longer the bottleneck.

title: Yazi: open multiple selected files
description: Let Yazi open multiple selected files into the managed editor workflow instead of forcing one-at-a-time opens when a multi-file edit session is the natural next step.
design: The feature should respect the existing managed editor model rather than bypassing it. Decide how selections map into Helix or other supported editors, how pane reuse and focus behave, and what should happen when the current editor already has an active workspace context. Keep the first version predictable even if that means limiting which editors or selection counts are supported initially.
acceptance_criteria: Users can select multiple files in Yazi and open them through Yazelix in one action. Add unit tests for selection aggregation and editor-command construction helpers, integration tests around selection collection and editor-command construction, and end-to-end coverage for multi-file open behavior with detailed logs showing the selected paths, target pane, and resulting editor invocation.
notes:
Dependency decision for multi-file Yazi open: use no new Rust crates. Production crates stay unchanged; dev-only crates stay unchanged. Build selection aggregation, payload compatibility, and editor command construction in-house because this is small path/vector formatting logic. Rejected alternatives: shell-specific argument parsers or broader command-building crates, which would add packaging surface without buying meaningful safety. Packaging impact: none beyond rebuilding and syncing the existing pane-orchestrator wasm after source changes.
Implemented multi-file Yazi open by changing the bundled opener from legacy "" to Yazi's %s selected-file expansion, allowing yzx_control zellij open-editor to accept one or more paths, carrying file_paths through the pane-orchestrator open_file payload while preserving legacy file_path compatibility, and opening all selected files in managed Helix/Neovim panes or a new editor pane when the managed editor is missing. Verification: pane-orchestrator editor_open_contract tests, yzx_control multi-open workspace tests, yazi materialization test, yzx dev rust fmt --check, yzx dev rust check, cargo check for pane orchestrator, build_pane_orchestrator --sync, validate-pane-orchestrator-sync, validate-workspace-session-contract, validate-specs, validate-rust-test-traceability, validate-package-rust-test-purity, git diff --check.
close_reason: Completed: Yazi now passes selected files through %s to yzx_control, the control path accepts multiple target paths, the pane-orchestrator payload and editor command helper open all selected files, and focused tests plus wasm sync validators pass.

title: Agent usage widgets in the Zellij widget tray
description:
Expose agent usage information in the Zellij zjstatus bar so AI tooling feels like a first-class workspace surface.

v15 ships no packs or llm-agents input. The ccusage family of binaries (ccusage, ccusage-codex, ccusage-amp, ccusage-opencode) must be opt-in, sourced from numtide's llm-agents flake. Both standalone flake users and Home Manager users must be supported.
design:
Layered opt-in across both flake and Home Manager paths:

**1. Flake input (packages layer)**
Add llm-agents as a new flake input. Provide an opt-in package variant or overlay (e.g. packages.${system}.yazelix-with-agent-tools) so standalone flake users can get ccusage binaries in their runtime PATH. numtide's binary cache is already trusted in the build policy.

**2. Home Manager option (config layer)**
Expose a typed option (e.g. yazelix.agentUsagePrograms) defaulting to []. When non-empty, the HM module pulls the matching packages from the llm-agents input and adds them to the runtime — same underlying packages, HM-friendly surface with validation.

**3. Widget tray (zjstatus layer)**
Add new widget tray entries (e.g. "claude_usage", "codex_usage"). The zjstatus command slots shell out to a wrapper script that checks whether the binary is in PATH at runtime. If present, it runs the binary and formats output. If absent, it emits nothing — zjstatus renders empty, tray stays clean. Use a long interval (60-300s) since usage data changes slowly.

This means the widget tray config works regardless of whether the user opted in via flake or HM — it only needs the binary present in PATH.

**Seams to modify:**
1. flake.nix — add llm-agents input, wire into package variants
2. home_manager/module.nix — add agentUsagePrograms option
3. configs/zellij/layouts/fragments/zjstatus_tab_template.kdl — add command_* entries
4. nushell/scripts/utils/layout_generator.nu:28 — extend allowed list and match block
5. config_metadata/main_config_contract.toml:239 — extend allowed_values
6. New wrapper script or zjstatus_widget.nu handler for runtime detection and formatting
acceptance_criteria:
- llm-agents flake input is added and ccusage binaries are available in the opt-in package variant
- HM option (agentUsagePrograms or similar) wires the same binaries when non-empty, defaults to []
- Adding "claude_usage" to zellij.widget_tray shows a compact usage indicator when ccusage is in PATH
- When the binary is missing, the widget renders nothing (no errors, no placeholder)
- Same pattern works for ccusage-codex, ccusage-amp, ccusage-opencode
- Allowlist, contract, and layout generator updated consistently
- Existing widget tray entries unaffected
- Both nix run / nix profile install and Home Manager paths work
notes:
Dependency decision, 2026-04-28:
- Production Rust crates: none new. Use existing serde_json and std::process/path/env for the yzx_control agent-usage command.
- Dev-only Rust crates: none new. Existing cargo tests and Nix eval/build smoke are enough for this slice.
- Nix dependency: add the numtide/llm-agents.nix flake input because ccusage binaries are deliberately opt-in external packages, not part of the default Yazelix runtime.
- In-house logic: small provider-to-binary mapping, runtime PATH detection, compact JSON summary parsing/formatting, widget allowlist entries, package extra-runtime plumbing.
- Rejected alternatives: vendoring ccusage, making AI usage tools default, reviving pack-style dependency groups, shell wrappers for zjstatus, or making missing binaries render placeholder text.
- Packaging impact: default package remains unchanged; opt-in package/Home Manager selections add ccusage, ccusage-codex, ccusage-amp, and/or ccusage-opencode to the Yazelix runtime tool surface.
Implementation result, 2026-04-28:
- Added llm-agents flake input and opt-in #yazelix_agent_tools / #runtime_agent_tools outputs. Default #yazelix remains unchanged.
- Added Home Manager programs.yazelix.agent_usage_programs as a typed opt-in list for ccusage, ccusage-codex, ccusage-amp, and ccusage-opencode; selected binaries are included inside the Yazelix runtime package.
- Added zellij.widget_tray entries claude_usage, codex_usage, amp_usage, and opencode_usage across default config comments, contract allowlist, Home Manager description, onboarding, Rust render plan, and yazelix_bar.
- Added yzx_control zellij agent-usage <provider>, which detects the matching ccusage binary on PATH, runs blocks --active --json, formats a compact colored segment, and emits an empty line when the binary is missing or has no usable data.
- Added zjstatus command slots with format {stdout}, so missing binaries do not leave static labels in the tray.
- Verification: cargo fmt --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml --all -- --check; cargo test --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml -p yazelix_core; cargo test --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml -p yazelix_bar; yzx_repo_validator validate-contracts; nix eval .#packages.x86_64-linux.yazelix_agent_tools.name; Home Manager module eval with agent_usage_programs; nix build --print-out-paths --no-link .#runtime_agent_tools; flake show lists runtime_agent_tools/yazelix_agent_tools; runtime_agent_tools exposes ccusage binaries in toolbin and libexec.
close_reason: Implemented opt-in agent usage widgets and runtime packaging

title: Docs experience pass
description: Improve the docs experience so users can discover the right guide quickly and the written surface matches the current product instead of feeling like repo archaeology.
design: This is broader than fixing one page. Review navigation, entrypoints, installation flow, customization docs, troubleshooting, and how examples map to the current runtime model. The result should keep docs maintainable while making the first path through Yazelix much easier to follow.
acceptance_criteria: The docs have clearer entrypoints, more coherent structure, and fewer stale or confusing paths. Add unit tests for any generated docs-index or navigation helpers, end-to-end link and command-reference validation scripts for the main docs routes, and a logged checklist covering the examples and entrypoints that were verified.

title: Support a separate user-managed devenv file
description: Support a separate user-managed devenv file as a power-user override path without replacing yazelix.toml or the managed runtime model as the default story.
design: This should be a deliberate extension point, not a hole punched through the configuration model. Define what a user-managed devenv overlay can change, how it composes with Yazelix-managed files, and how support boundaries are documented so power users get flexibility without making the default runtime impossible to reason about.
acceptance_criteria: Power users can provide a separate devenv file through a documented, supported path, and the default yazelix.toml flow remains the primary story. Add unit tests for path-resolution and overlay-selection helpers, config and path-resolution integration tests, and end-to-end validation that the override file is discovered and composed correctly, with detailed logs of the final devenv inputs used.
close_reason: Stale v14-era issue. v15 dropped devenv entirely — no yazelix_packs.toml, no runtime-local devenv, no yzx packs. This feature request is no longer applicable.

title: Notification system
description: Design and add a notification system that surfaces important Yazelix events without turning the workspace into a noisy status carnival.
design: Do not start with implementation detail. First define the event model: which events deserve a notification, what channel they should use, when they should be silent, and how users can dismiss or tune them. The system should reinforce existing flows such as rebuilds, permission issues, and long-running actions instead of compensating for unclear product behavior with spam.
acceptance_criteria: Yazelix has a documented notification model and an implementation that surfaces the right events with restrained UX. Add unit tests for event routing and suppression rules plus end-to-end scripts that trigger representative events and log delivery channel, payload, and user-visible outcome.

title: Re-evaluate extracting Ghostty cursor presets to a separate repo
description: Revisit a separate Ghostty cursor preset repository only after the in-repo export proves the asset boundary and maintenance model.
design: Review demand, shader provenance, license notes, release/version policy, Nix/manual update instructions, and whether Yazelix can consume a separate repo without weakening runtime generation.
acceptance_criteria: Decision either keeps the in-repo export as the durable home or opens a concrete extraction plan with ownership, release, docs, validation, and Yazelix consumption boundaries.

title: Explore full deep Seahorse Misiurewicz Mandelbrot zoom
description: Research and prototype a future Mandelbrot screen path that targets a full deep Seahorse/Misiurewicz zoom: between-bulb valleys, tentacle/plant-like spiral structures, and a convincing self-similar return. This should not replace the current correct period-3 valley-crop loop unless it preserves seam continuity, bounded terminal CPU cost, deterministic rendering, and user-visible quality. Likely requires better coordinate selection, possibly higher iteration budgeting, and maybe an optional renderer path if f64/terminal resolution becomes the limiting factor.
notes:
2026-04-26 Rust dependency gate: Production crates: none. Dev-only crates: none. In-house/std logic: keep the existing CPU escape-time renderer and local Complex64 math. Rejected alternatives: Bevy/Fyrox/Ratatui/bigfloat for the shipped terminal runtime. Packaging impact: no new dependencies or Nix changes.

2026-04-26 implementation: mandelbrot now uses the Seahorse Valley Misiurewicz path directly. Removed the separate public mandelbrot_seahorse mode and deleted the old period-3 minibrot branch because the user rejected the boring first path and did not want fragmentation.

2026-04-26 continuity correction: the first Seahorse attempt looked better but failed spatial continuity. The fix keeps the canonical Misiurewicz center and repelling-cycle multiplier, uses a shallower integer derivative-power loop, starts from a tighter Seahorse viewport, and raises the bounded iteration floor to resolve filaments across the full loop. Added regression tests for sampled-frame detail, exact cycle-boundary repeat, penultimate-to-first visible continuity, monotonic zoom, and bounded iteration cost.
2026-04-26 second manual runtime test: 10-power Seahorse loop still looked static. Fix direction: use 121 repelling-cycle powers because its accumulated rotation is within 0.001 turns of a whole number while giving >9000x zoom, switch exterior rendering to repeated smooth contour bands with zoom phase compensation, raise bounded iterations to 800..2500, and add seam blending for the last 10 percent of the loop so the reset is visually continuous.
2026-04-26 final implementation pass: committed to a 121-power loop (>9000x zoom, <0.001 turn orientation error), smooth contour-band coloring with zoom phase compensation, 800..2500 bounded iteration budget, and deterministic seam blending over the final 10 percent. Focused tests now require material mid-loop visual difference, sampled-frame variation, exact loop-boundary repeat, penultimate-to-first continuity, orientation alignment, and bounded work.
2026-04-26 third manual runtime test: 121-power contour loop is still visually wrong: gray, sparse dot texture, high-frequency color noise, and repeated contour wallpaper. Next pass replaces contour wallpaper with distance-estimate boundary rendering, filled glyph density, and coarse color regions.
2026-04-26 final visual correction after screenshot: contour bands were too gray/sparse/noisy/repetitive. Replaced single-pass contour wallpaper with a two-pass distance-estimate renderer: compute boundary scores for the whole frame, normalize into local filled density bands, render only shade/block glyphs instead of dot glyphs, and use coarse 256-color neon bands for vibrant color without per-cell noise. Added tests defending no dotted texture, sufficient filled density, vibrant 256-color ANSI, mid-loop visual change, and seam continuity.
close_reason: Reworked Mandelbrot into filled distance-estimate neon rendering after runtime screenshot feedback

title: Extend shared Rust classification for doctor and status only where it deletes real Nushell reasoning
description: Now that `yazelix-vi8q` and `yazelix-4fi5` are complete, audit the remaining doctor/status logic and either move one genuinely shared typed classification seam behind existing `yzx_core` output or close this bead. The most likely surviving seam is generated-state classification: `yzx status` still summarizes repair need from `config-state.compute` alone, while the runtime-materialization path already owns richer generated-state decisions such as `status`, `reason`, missing managed artifacts, and the resolved managed layout path. This bead exists to delete any real remaining Nushell decision branch in that shared area, not to justify a broad doctor, status, or CLI rewrite.
design:
Keep doctor and status presentation, remediation prose, fix actions, row/table rendering, and command UX in Nushell.

Implementation approach:
1. Inventory surviving duplicated reasoning across doctor/status before changing anything.
2. Prefer reusing an existing Rust seam (`runtime-materialization.plan` first, `runtime-contract.evaluate` only if truly needed) instead of creating a new helper command family.
3. The preferred first candidate is `yzx status`: decide whether it should consume Rust-owned generated-state classification from the materialization plan instead of only `config_state.needs_refresh`.
4. If you need extra machine-readable status fields, make them additive and keep current human output broadly comparable.
5. Do not port install-artifact audits, plugin/session checks, live Zellij inspection, capability messaging, or text-heavy doctor rendering into Rust.
6. If the audit shows no meaningful shared seam remains after `vi8q` and `4fi5`, close this bead with a short note instead of forcing more Rust into the surface.
acceptance_criteria:
1) Either one real duplicated doctor/status reasoning seam is deleted via existing Rust helper output, or the bead is explicitly closed after documenting that no meaningful shared seam remains.
2) If implemented, any new shared classification comes from an existing `yzx_core` seam and Nushell no longer computes the same decision in parallel.
3) Doctor and status remain Nushell-owned presentation surfaces: prose, fix actions, JSON/human rendering, and command UX stay in Nushell.
4) Verification commands in the notes pass.
notes:
Concrete starting points:
- `nushell/scripts/utils/status_report.nu`
- `nushell/scripts/utils/environment_bootstrap.nu`
- `nushell/scripts/utils/doctor.nu`
- `nushell/scripts/utils/runtime_contract_checker.nu`
- `nushell/scripts/utils/generated_runtime_state.nu`
- `rust_core/yazelix_core/src/runtime_materialization.rs`
- `rust_core/yazelix_core/src/runtime_contract.rs`

Concrete questions to answer first:
- Does `yzx status` still have a real duplicated decision seam because it reports `generated_state_repair_needed` from `config_state.needs_refresh` while Rust already owns richer materialization status?
- Should status distinguish `refresh_required` vs `repair_missing_artifacts` vs `noop` using Rust-owned plan data?
- Are any remaining Nushell branches actually just presentation/mapping layers? If yes, keep them in Nushell.

Delete-first constraints:
- Reject additive Rust plus unchanged Nushell reasoning for the same classification.
- Keep `yzx status` and `yzx doctor` as Nushell-owned presentation surfaces.
- Preserve current doctor fix actions in Nushell.
- Avoid changing row labels / user-facing wording unless the seam you delete requires a small adjustment.
- If JSON output grows, keep it backward-compatible by adding fields rather than renaming existing ones.

Suggested minimal implementation if a seam is real:
- Route status summary generation through `compute_runtime_materialization_plan` or a narrow shared helper that consumes the same Rust plan.
- Keep `render_status_report` unchanged except for displaying any additive summary fields you intentionally expose.
- Add a focused regression proving status now reflects Rust-owned generated-state classification for a case that `config-state.compute` alone cannot describe well, such as missing managed artifacts with current hashes.

Suggested verification:
- `nix develop -c cargo test --manifest-path rust_core/Cargo.toml`
- `nix develop -c nu -c 'source nushell/scripts/dev/test_yzx_core_commands.nu; run_core_canonical_tests'`
- `nix develop -c nu -c 'source nushell/scripts/dev/test_yzx_doctor_commands.nu; run_doctor_canonical_tests'`
- `nix develop -c nu -c 'source nushell/scripts/dev/validate_specs.nu; main'`
- `git diff --check`
close_reason: yzx status now derives generated-state repair flags and materialization status/reason from runtime-materialization.plan (yzx_core). Added JSON fields generated_state_materialization_status and generated_state_materialization_reason. Core test harness sets YAZELIX_STATE_DIR for isolated fixtures. Regression: test_yzx_status_json_reports_materialization_repair_when_artifacts_missing. Verified: run_core_canonical_tests, cargo test rust_core, validate_specs, run_doctor_canonical_tests.

title: Evaluate a minimal schema-versioned Rust config migration core only if current diagnostics remain insufficient
description:
Past config churn caused real pain, but the current first-principles decision is not to reintroduce a broad automatic migration engine as a live v15 feature. The first cheaper fix is to make desktop-entry launch failures visible and keep the existing fail-fast config diagnostics and manual repair path honest. If that still proves insufficient after the launch UX work lands, then evaluate a much smaller migration approach.

The candidate scope is a Rust-first internal config core with an explicit schema_version in yazelix.toml and only deterministic structural migrations. This is an evaluation bead, not approval to rebuild the old Nushell migration subsystem.
design:
Keep the scope extremely small. Do not save per-user copies of the initial schema, do not log hashes for every user edit, and do not promise that every historical config shape will auto-repair forever. If revisited, prefer an internal monorepo Rust crate aligned with the surviving config core rather than a separate repo or product. Use existing seams such as the canonical config contract and config-state hashing, and consider small supporting crates like toml_edit only if they clearly reduce code.

Non-goals:
- reviving the broad v13/v14 migration rule engine
- a Nushell-first implementation that will immediately be rewritten
- automatic handling of ambiguous or lossy migrations
- inventing a second config product around migrations
acceptance_criteria:
1. There is a written recommendation to either drop this idea or proceed with a tiny Rust-first prototype.
2. Any approved prototype proves an explicit schema_version boundary and one deterministic migration on real Yazelix config data.
3. Ambiguous or lossy changes remain manual-only with clear diagnostics.
4. If the design cannot stay small and robust, the bead is closed rather than expanded into a large subsystem.
notes:
2026-04-19 Rust dependency gate: before any minimal migration-core prototype, record the crate-vs-in-house plan. Do not add toml_edit or migration-framework-like dependencies unless preserving user-authored TOML formatting becomes an explicit accepted requirement.
2026-04-19 guardrail:
- If this bead is ever revived, it must delete config-diagnostics duplication rather than layering a second migration engine beside the current Rust config core and Nushell error path.
- Any prototype must name the existing seams it replaces in config_parser.nu, config_state.nu, or related diagnostics, and the earliest point where transition-only migration parity code would be removed.
- If the prototype cannot stay smaller than the duplication it introduces, close the bead instead.
2026-04-19 roadmap update: keep this bead blocked on yazelix-vi8q so config.normalize becomes the canonical diagnostics source before any schema-versioned migration prototype is reconsidered.
close_reason: Current Rust-backed config diagnostics are sufficient for the live v15 contract: startup and doctor share one unsupported-config path, tests defend no-migration guidance, and there is no current backlog evidence that a schema-versioned migration core is justified. Closing instead of reviving migration machinery.
