title: Define the current Yazelix roadmap around the live v15 line, delete-first cleanup, gradual v15.x Rust insertion, and the v16 Rust release
description: Define the current roadmap for Yazelix now that v15 is the only supported line, config-migration support is being removed, and the release framing is clearer: v15.0 is the trimmed rebooted non-Rust release, later v15.x point releases may gain selective Rust slices where they pay for themselves, and v16 is the Rust-forward release. The roadmap should capture the main pillars that matter from here: tighter subsystem boundaries, deleting stale compatibility layers, top-to-bottom profiling, a clearer front door, selective post-v15.0 Rust insertion, and the longer path toward a much more Rust-heavy v16.
design: Treat this as the roadmap for the current live v15 line, not as a placeholder for a second product and not as a disguised all-at-once rewrite plan. Reuse the real existing lanes instead of creating duplicate implementation work: the runtime/helper trim work, the popup/menu and pane-orchestrator cleanup, the migration-layer deletion, the profiling bead, the website issues, and the Rust planning beads that now describe Rust as a post-v15.0 incremental path rather than something required to ship v15.0 itself. The useful output is a coherent framing for what Yazelix is now, what v15.0 must finish without depending on Rust, what v15.x may still improve incrementally, and what kind of architectural shift is intentionally reserved for v16.
acceptance_criteria: There is a durable roadmap skeleton for the live v15 Yazelix line and its path toward v16. It identifies the main pillars for the current product era, names the delete-first cleanup that still matters before and after v15.0, points at the active Rust/architecture/profiling/website lanes that feed the next stages, and keeps the release framing explicit: v15.0 ships as the trimmed non-Rust reboot, selective Rust slices may land later in v15.x, and v16 is the later Rust-forward release.
close_reason: all steps complete

title: Port launch/bootstrap state and backend adapter logic into Rust after the seam is extracted
description: After the backend adapter seam is clearer, and only after v15.0 has shipped, evaluate moving the launch/bootstrap state machine and backend-calling logic out of environment_bootstrap.nu, launch_state.nu, and related yzx env/run/launch/refresh helpers into a Rust core. The target is safer orchestration and clearer state transitions, not a generic backend framework. This is a candidate only for a later v15.x point release if the seam is truly stable; otherwise it belongs in the broader v16 Rust-forward push.
design: Do not start here and do not treat this as part of shipping v15.0. This is a later slice because it mixes process spawning, environment shaping, state reuse, and user-facing behavior. Only move it once the backend contract and command-surface audit have made the boundary explicit and after the earlier Rust slices have proven the integration strategy. A likely good outcome is a Rust core that owns the state machine and structured actions while Nushell stays as a thin CLI and host-integration layer. If the boundary still feels porous after the earlier work, defer this slice to v16 instead of forcing it into v15.x.
acceptance_criteria: There is a concrete migration plan for the launch/bootstrap/backend adapter path that respects the extracted backend seam and does not re-entangle product semantics. The plan identifies which Nushell modules become shims or are removed, how parity will be verified for env/run/refresh/launch behavior, why this slice is worth doing after the earlier ones, and whether it is realistically safe for a later v15.x point release or should wait for the broader v16 push.
notes:
Primary target modules: environment_bootstrap.nu, launch_state.nu, yzx/env.nu, yzx/run.nu, yzx/launch.nu, and any helper code that still encodes backend-specific state transitions.

Sequencing note after the 2026-04-05 architecture review:
- Treat `yazelix-m5rk.1` as the concrete precondition that makes the runtime state machine explicit before this slice moves into Rust.
2026-04-19 Rust dependency gate: before any launch/bootstrap Rust implementation, record the crate-vs-in-house plan. Process, shell, and terminal seams are high-risk; justify any framework or process-management crate before adding it.
2026-04-19 deletion ledger:
- Temporary seams expected in this slice: environment_bootstrap.nu, launch_state.nu, start_yazelix.nu, start_yazelix_inner.nu, launch_yazelix.nu, and the yzx env/run/launch helpers should not all remain full behavior owners after Rust state-machine adoption.
- The intended steady state is a thin Nushell CLI and host boundary over a Rust-owned launch state machine. If the slice would leave both sides owning the same state transitions, defer instead of merging additive glue.
- Must delete by close of kt5.4: at least one real Nu state-machine or decision layer, or file explicit follow-up beads per surviving duplicate orchestration seam.
- Permanent Nu ownership is acceptable for shell-specific argv/env handoff and host integration, not for duplicated launch state logic.
2026-04-19 start note: claimed for launch/bootstrap Rust migration planning. Rust dependency gate for kt5.4: keep the existing yzx_core production crate set only \(serde, serde_json, toml, sha2, thiserror, lexopt\); add no process, shell, async, terminal, PATH, or command-runner framework crates for the first slice. Keep current dev-only crates \(assert_cmd, pretty_assertions, tempfile\) for helper/fixture tests. Build in-house/std: launch/bootstrap state classification, canonical runtime-env planning, shell/terminal handoff plan records, env-var cleanup lists, and bridge request/response shaping. Rejected alternatives: clap for public CLI rewrite, tokio/async-process/process-control crates, which crate, shell-escape crates, terminal abstraction crates. Packaging impact: no new dependency closure or vendoring/hash change; any implementation must stay inside the private packaged libexec/yzx_core helper and leave Nushell/POSIX as the host/process execution boundary until a narrower deletion target is proven.
2026-04-19 planning completion:
- added spec docs/specs/launch_bootstrap_rust_migration.md to define the live v15 launch/bootstrap ownership map and the narrow Rust insertion plan
- current first Rust slice is `runtime-env.compute`; `runtime_env.nu` is the first Nushell decision layer slated to shrink into a bridge
- explicit first execution child: `yazelix-kt5.4.1`
- historical `devenv_backend`, `launch_state`, launch-profile reuse, and public `yzx refresh` remain out of scope for this plan
close_reason: Completed: concrete launch/bootstrap Rust migration plan captured in docs/specs/launch_bootstrap_rust_migration.md and first execution child yazelix-kt5.4.1 created

title: Decide which config generators should stay Nushell versus move to Rust
description: Evaluate the major generated-config surfaces such as yazi_config_merger.nu, zellij_config_merger.nu, terminal_configs.nu, and related template helpers to decide whether they are good Rust targets or should stay in Nushell. The goal is to avoid rewriting text-heavy, low-risk template glue just because v16 will be Rust-forward, while still identifying any generator slice that would genuinely benefit from stronger modeling. v15.0 should ship without Rust becoming the owner of these surfaces; any generator move would be a later v15.x or v16 decision.
design: Use the migration matrix to separate structured generator logic from simple template stitching. Favor keeping straightforward text/template assembly in Nushell unless there is repeated correctness pain, complex state reconciliation, or a clear typed model that would become substantially safer in Rust. Do not let this bead become a license to port all generators indiscriminately just because Rust is on the roadmap or because v16 is intended to be Rust-forward. The default should be to keep v15.0 and probably much of v15.x generator ownership in Nushell unless a narrower structured subcore proves worth extracting.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear keep-versus-port recommendation for the main config generators, with concrete reasons tied to defect history, complexity, and expected payoff. The output identifies any generator slice worth porting, explicitly names the ones that should remain Nushell, and states honestly whether any surviving Rust candidate belongs in a later v15.x point release or should wait for v16.
notes:
Likely keep-in-Nushell candidates today: zellij_config_merger.nu, yazi_config_merger.nu, and much of terminal_configs.nu unless a narrower structured core emerges that is worth extracting.

Sequencing note after the 2026-04-05 architecture review:
- `yazelix-jheg` is the likely non-Rust improvement path for `zellij_config_merger.nu`; if that typed render-pipeline work lands first, reevaluate whether any narrower structured subcore is still worth porting instead of assuming the whole generator should move.
2026-04-19 Rust dependency gate: before any generator-port Rust implementation, record the crate-vs-in-house plan. Template/text rendering should stay Nushell or in-house unless a crate clearly reduces real parser/renderer risk.
2026-04-19 deletion discipline:
- This bead is allowed to conclude that most generators stay Nushell. The goal is to avoid cargo-cult rewrites.
- If any generator slice is approved for Rust, the recommendation must also name the exact Nushell template or glue expected to disappear and the earliest deletion bead for it.
- Reject ports that only add a Rust planner while leaving the same text ownership and branching duplicated in Nushell.
- The default outcome remains deletion of unnecessary bridge ideas, not automatic generator porting.
2026-04-19 renderer ownership decision:
- Do not port Yazi/Zellij/terminal/Helix/shell initializer/layout/extern text renderers to Rust just to reduce Nushell LOC.
- Rust owns the structured decision layer already behind yzx_core: normalized config, rebuild-state hashing, refresh classification, expected artifact planning, and materialized-state recording.
- Nushell owns generated text/template output, plugin/layout copying, user override discovery, per-launch Ghostty random cursor rerolls, shader sync, shell initializers, and the fingerprinted Nushell extern bridge.
- runtime-materialization.apply should remain limited to materialized-state recording and expected-artifact verification unless a future renderer-specific bead explicitly moves one generated file family to Rust.
- Revisit only for repeated renderer correctness defects, need for a real parser/AST model, shared structured renderer logic that is cheaper in Rust, or a later v16 public-CLI ownership change.
- No follow-up Rust renderer port bead is recommended now; this bead resolves by locking the boundary in docs/specs/rust_migration_matrix.md.
close_reason: Completed: renderer/template ownership is locked in the Rust migration matrix; generated text/template surfaces stay Nushell for v15.x and Rust remains the structured config/state/materialization decision owner.

title: Narrow backend comparison issue into a Pixi-first exploration lane
description: GitHub issue #518 asked for a broad backend comparison across devenv, mise, brioche, pixi, and prefix. That framing mixed tools from different layers. The outcome is to close the broad issue and keep the real exploration scoped to a Pixi-first experiment branch instead of a generic multi-backend bakeoff.
close_reason: Closed in favor of the narrower Pixi-first evaluation lane tracked separately in yazelix-qow.

title: Postmortem: session-global Yazi sidebar state caused cross-tab cwd leaks
description: Symptom: yzx cwd appeared to change the cwd of other tabs, and the same class of issue was suspected around Yazi-driven path changes. Initial suspicion was a pane-orchestrator tab-isolation leak. Actual cause: read_active_sidebar_state in nushell/scripts/integrations/yazi.nu selected the most recently modified Yazi state file for the whole session, so sidebar sync could target another tab Yazi pane. Because every tab starts with Yazi, this produced a real cross-tab leak in normal usage. Fix: resolve the current tab sidebar_pane_id via debug_editor_state, then read that exact sidebar state file. Verified in a disposable two-tab Yazelix session.
design: Keep this as a regression postmortem rather than implementation work. The value is preserving the causal chain: session-global sidebar state looked convenient, but it violated tab locality and produced real cross-tab cwd leaks. Capture the invariant that any sidebar or path-sync state must be keyed to the current tab or pane identity rather than global recency.
acceptance_criteria: The bead clearly documents the regression cause, the violated tab-locality invariant, the final fix, and the guardrail future work should preserve. A maintainer touching sidebar state later can use this bead to avoid reintroducing session-global leaks.

title: Auto-assign new GitHub issues to the maintainer
description: Automatically assign new GitHub issues to the maintainer instead of leaving assignment as a manual follow-up step.
design: Treat this as repository workflow automation, not product runtime work. Decide whether GitHub Actions, issue templates, or another automation path is the right place to assign newly opened issues to the maintainer, and verify the permission model before committing to an approach. The solution should be reliable without creating noisy side effects on transferred, reopened, or bot-created issues unless that behavior is explicitly desired.
acceptance_criteria: New issues are assigned to the intended maintainer automatically, or the repo has a documented explanation of why that automation is not supportable. Include validation for the chosen workflow path, with logs or dry-run evidence showing the assignment behavior for representative issue-opening scenarios.

title: yzx wallpaper / screensaver
description: Explore whether Yazelix should ship a branded wallpaper and/or screensaver path instead of leaving the idea as a vague aesthetic request.
design: Keep this explicitly low-priority and opt-in. First decide what problem this would solve: branding, delight, showcase visuals, or an idle-state experience tied to Yazelix sessions. Distinguish between static wallpapers, lightweight animated assets, and an actual screensaver workflow, and avoid introducing platform-specific complexity unless the user value is real.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear decision on whether Yazelix should ship wallpapers, a screensaver path, both, or neither. If something is implemented, it is lightweight, optional, and documented with the intended use case and platform caveats. Leave behind validated assets or a documented rejection, plus any generation or preview commands needed to reproduce the result.

title: Support Emacs as a low-priority compatibility target
description: Keep Emacs support on the backlog mainly so Yazelix continues to cover the three big model editors, not because this is a current priority area.
design: This should stay aspirational for now. When revisited, verify the modes people actually use, especially Doom Emacs and similar real-world setups, and evaluate whether tools like chemacs2 help keep integration manageable. Do not let this expand into a broad editor-support project before higher-value workspace and packaging work is done.
acceptance_criteria: Yazelix has a documented path or verified compatibility story for mainstream Emacs setups when this eventually gets picked up. Until then, this should remain clearly marked as low-priority backlog work rather than active roadmap pressure.

title: Document and validate Zellij session sharing with Yazelix
description: Investigate how Zellij session sharing works in practice for Yazelix users, including whether Yazelix makes the process easier, harder, or just different compared with vanilla Zellij. The main sharing paths to verify are Zellij's built-in web client and ordinary attach/share flows for existing sessions.
design: Start as a docs-and-validation task grounded in the official Zellij sharing model. Zellij now has a built-in web server and web client that can be started with `zellij web` or through the share plugin, and authenticated with one-time login tokens. The default local URL scheme and token flow should be verified in a Yazelix session. Also verify ordinary multi-client session attach flows and session resurrection/share behavior. Then check how Yazelix interacts with these flows: shared Yazelix environments, shared devenv profiles, plugin/runtime expectations, persistent session naming, and whether the current keybinding model makes the share plugin discoverable enough. If Yazelix creates friction, consider small helper behavior or targeted docs from the Yazelix side to smooth it out.
acceptance_criteria: There is accurate documentation for sharing Zellij sessions with Yazelix, grounded in verified behavior rather than guesswork. Any Yazelix-specific helper or caveat is documented clearly. Leave behind a repeatable validation script or checklist with detailed logs covering the tested sharing path, authentication or web-client steps, and any plugin or session caveats that users need to know.
notes: Official Zellij sources to anchor this work: Web client tutorial, web client user guide, and the 0.43.0 release notes about browser sharing. The docs note that the web client is useful for remote access, demos, pair programming, and collaborative debugging. They also recommend HTTPS for anything beyond local-only use.

title: Update static preview assets (screenshots/gifs)
description: Refresh Yazelix's static preview assets so screenshots and GIFs reflect the current product instead of stale layouts, widgets, and onboarding states.
design: Treat this as a content-quality pass tied to the current runtime, not just regenerating everything blindly. Inventory where preview assets are used across README, docs, and the website, replace the stale ones, and prefer smaller, more focused captures that survive UI evolution better.
acceptance_criteria: The main docs and marketing surfaces show current Yazelix visuals, stale captures are removed, and the asset set is small enough to maintain. Include a logged capture checklist so the asset generation process is reproducible instead of ad hoc.
close_reason: Reduced repo preview assets to one maintained README screenshot, removed stale head demos, and documented the lightweight capture policy

title: Figure out how to install Yazelix in an existing app on Android
description: Figure out whether Yazelix can be installed inside an existing Android app environment and what the credible support story would look like if that path is worth pursuing.
design: This is an exploration bead, not a promise of a mobile app. Focus on realistic hosts such as Termux or other existing app shells that can run the needed Nix or terminal stack, and identify what would break first: rendering, keyboard input, terminal multiplexing, install size, or package assumptions. The result should distinguish technically possible from supportable.
acceptance_criteria: There is a concrete recommendation about whether Android-in-an-existing-app is viable, what the supported host would be, and what major caveats remain. Leave behind a documented validation path and detailed logs of the tested environment so the conclusion is reproducible.

title: Filter large GIFs from git history
description: Remove oversized GIF history from the repository in a deliberate way so the repo is lighter to clone without breaking the current docs and preview experience.
design: This is history hygiene, not just deleting files from HEAD. Identify which demo GIF blobs are disproportionately large, decide what should stay in git versus move to releases or external hosting, and plan the rewrite carefully so active docs, screenshots, and README references still work afterward. Document the migration and force-push implications before rewriting history.
acceptance_criteria: Large legacy GIF blobs are removed from git history, the current repo still has working preview assets, and contributors have a clear migration note if history rewriting is required. Include a verification script or logged before-and-after measurements for repo size, clone size, and asset reference integrity.
close_reason: Not worth the disruption. GIFs are already deleted from HEAD, not shipped in the flake package, and only affect full-clone size. History rewriting would force-push main and break every existing clone for ~54 MB of dead blobs. Shallow clones (the common user path) don't pay for them.

title: Split demo GIFs into smaller pieces
description: Split the large demo GIF story into several smaller focused demos so users can understand specific Yazelix workflows without one heavyweight clip doing all the work.
design: The goal is not just smaller files. Each demo should communicate one idea clearly: onboarding, Yazi plus Helix flow, workspace switching, and so on. Coordinate with the preview-asset and git-history cleanup beads so the final asset set is coherent and lightweight.
acceptance_criteria: The docs and website use smaller focused demo assets instead of a single oversized catch-all GIF. Asset file sizes, placement, and narrative purpose are documented in a capture checklist or logged plan so the set remains maintainable.
close_reason: Deferred to the website. GIFs in the repo bloat git history, the README already has a static screenshot at the right weight, and creating feature GIFs twice (README then website) is wasted effort. The asset creation work belongs to the website project.

title: Windows support: verify native and WSL paths and document caveats
description: Re-evaluate what level of Windows support Yazelix can credibly claim now that Zellij supports Windows, and document the caveats honestly instead of leaving the answer implicit.
design: Treat this as a support-boundary verification task, not a promise that native Windows is automatically first-class. Validate both realistic paths: native Windows, now that Zellij supports it, and WSL as an alternative/fallback path. Check the core flows that matter: install, launch, terminal integration, shell/path handling, clipboard and browser/file openers, fonts, file-manager interop, plugin/runtime caveats, and any assumptions in Yazelix that still depend on Unix-only behavior. The output should separate supported, works-with-caveats, and unsupported cases for each path.
acceptance_criteria: Users can tell whether Yazelix is a credible native-Windows option, a WSL-only option, or a mixed-support story with specific caveats. The docs include concrete caveats and recovery tips for both native Windows and WSL where relevant, and the validation process leaves behind a logged checklist or script so support can be re-verified later as Zellij and Yazelix evolve.

title: Benchmark: clean install using Nix (cold/warm)
description: Benchmark clean Yazelix installation and first-run behavior under Nix so the project can talk concretely about cold and warm setup costs instead of guessing.
design: Measure the path users actually care about: cold install, warm install, first launch, and any major cache effects. Capture the environment, cache state, hardware notes, and the exact commands used so the numbers remain meaningful instead of becoming one-off anecdotes. The README or docs update should present the results honestly rather than as marketing theater.
acceptance_criteria: Yazelix has reproducible cold and warm install benchmarks that can be referenced in docs. Add unit tests for any benchmark parsing or summarization helpers, end-to-end benchmark scripts or documented procedures with detailed logs for timing, cache state, and machine context, and update the relevant docs or README with the measured results.

title: Sidebar toggle/orchestration shipped in v13
description: Completed roadmap item. Deterministic sidebar toggle, editor/sidebar focus, and managed editor targeting are shipped in v13.
close_reason: Shipped in v13

title: Packaging: prepare and submit a nixpkgs package for Yazelix
description: Prepare Yazelix for nixpkgs submission once the runtime and packaging boundaries are clean enough that the remaining work is primarily packaging and review.
design: This is a distribution milestone, not a hidden architecture refactor. Define the package shape, wrapper behavior, runtime assets, and installation story expected by nixpkgs reviewers after the package-ready path model exists. Be explicit about Linux and macOS implications, update strategy, and what remains outside scope for a first nixpkgs submission.
acceptance_criteria: Yazelix reaches a state where a nixpkgs submission is mostly packaging work. Add unit tests for package metadata or wrapper-generation helpers where applicable, packaging smoke tests that build and run the wrapped entrypoints, and logged validation of install paths, runtime asset availability, and user-facing launch behavior.
notes: Upstream nixpkgs submission is no longer a planned project goal. Keep the completed contract and local package work as historical reference, but do not treat upstream submission as a downstream milestone for the current package-runtime-first simplification lane.

title: Evaluate a broader Rust/clap rewrite as a v16-or-later path after boundaries are proven
description: Keep the possibility of a broader Rust or clap rewrite on the table as a v16-or-later path, but only after the current v15.0 product, runtime model, and UX seams are proven well enough that a rewrite would clarify rather than scramble the system.
design: This remains intentionally late and skeptical. A broader Rust or clap rewrite should not be a reflexive response to complexity that really comes from fuzzy product semantics. Revisit it only after v15.0 has shipped as the trimmed non-Rust reboot, the runtime boundaries are proven, the Rust migration matrix exists, and the Rust/Nushell bridge strategy is explicit. The default assumption for v15.x should be incremental Rust helpers, not an immediate whole-CLI rewrite; v16 is the earliest release framing where a much broader Rust/clap move should become a visible product direction.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear justification document for any future broader Rust/clap rewrite path, including what problems it would solve, what parity must be preserved, whether it belongs in v16 or later, and what benchmarks or maintainability wins would justify the cost. Any eventual implementation must come with unit tests for rewritten helper logic, end-to-end parity comparison scripts against the current behavior, and detailed logs showing what changed and what remained equivalent.
notes:
2026-04-19 Rust dependency gate: before any broader Rust/clap rewrite work, record the crate-vs-in-house plan. In particular, justify clap or any public-CLI framework against the private-helper bridge path and the current Nushell UX owner contract.
2026-04-19 guardrail:
- A broader rewrite is only worth considering if earlier beads have already deleted substantial Nushell owner seams rather than accumulating permanent parity and glue layers.
- Any future v16 proposal must include a deletion budget: which Nushell modules disappear, which bridges vanish, and which parity tests are transition-only and can be removed.
- If the case for a broader rewrite depends on carrying the current helper and bridge stack forward indefinitely, that is evidence against doing it.
2026-04-19 planning start:
- using this bead as the next Rust-forward planning slice after kt5.2, kt5.3, kt5.4, and the launch/startup stop-condition audit
- goal: write the justification/deletion-budget document for any broader v16-or-later Rust CLI path instead of forcing more v15.x helper ports
- dependency gate for planning: default assumption remains no public CLI framework in v15.x; any v16 proposal must justify clap against keeping the current helper-backed private bridge and must spell out which Nushell owners disappear
2026-04-19 planning completion:
- added docs/specs/v16_rust_cli_rewrite_evaluation.md as the go/no-go and deletion-budget document for any broader v16 public Rust CLI path
- anchored the recommendation in the current public command families, bridge contract, launch/startup stop condition, v15 trimmed runtime contract, and cross-language ownership map
- conclusion: a broader rewrite is only justified if Rust becomes the single public command-tree owner for the control-plane families and deletes the public Nushell registry plus extern-bridge seams; clap-as-thin-shim is explicitly rejected
- added the spec to docs/specs/spec_inventory.md
- validated with nu nushell/scripts/dev/validate_specs.nu
close_reason: Completed: broader v16 Rust/clap path now has a justification and deletion-budget spec in docs/specs/v16_rust_cli_rewrite_evaluation.md with explicit go/no-go gates and scope boundaries

title: Packaging and distribution umbrella
description: Umbrella for the remaining packaging and distribution coordination work. Keep this small and planning-focused while concrete child issues track the real implementation work such as nixpkgs packaging and any packaging-surface consolidation follow-ons.
design: Keep this as the packaging and distribution coordination layer, not as another implementation issue. The children should capture the concrete work: Home Manager alignment, flake interface, and nixpkgs packaging. Use real blockers only where one packaging path truly depends on another.
acceptance_criteria: The packaging follow-on work is represented by concrete child beads with honest dependencies and clear deliverables. This umbrella stays small and planning-focused instead of accumulating implementation detail.
close_reason: Umbrella no longer needed; remaining public packaging work continues in #402 and follow-on tasks stay separate

title: Workspace UX and Yazi ergonomics
description: Umbrella for the workspace-model and Yazi ergonomics work that makes Yazelix feel more coherent and less fiddly in day-to-day use.
design: This cluster is about the lived workspace experience: keybindings, Yazi-to-editor transitions, pane cwd behavior, status surfaces, and other small seams that shape whether the environment feels magical or awkward. Keep the child beads concrete and user-facing, and avoid using this umbrella as a dumping ground for unrelated UI tweaks.
acceptance_criteria: The workspace-UX child beads cover the main friction points without duplicating each other, and their dependencies reflect real interaction-model prerequisites where those exist. The umbrella itself remains a coordination bead, not an execution target.
close_reason: Obsolete grouping container; replaced by labels and direct dependencies

title: Main roadmap sequence
description: Top-level roadmap track for the major product and packaging work that should shape Yazelix after the current stabilization pass.
design: This bead is a planning container, not a feature to implement directly. Its job is to keep the major streams visible without forcing fake blocker edges between unrelated work. The child beads under this epic should use real technical dependencies where they exist and otherwise remain parallelizable so Beads triage stays honest.
acceptance_criteria: The main roadmap children are well-scoped, their blockers reflect actual prerequisites rather than aesthetic ordering, and the graph remains usable for bv triage. Revisit this epic whenever major roadmap themes change rather than stuffing execution detail into the container itself.
close_reason: Obsolete grouping container; replaced by labels and direct dependencies

title: Always-on maintenance lane
description: Always-on maintenance lane for cross-cutting bug fixes, cleanup, and reliability improvements that should continue even while the larger roadmap moves forward.
design: This is the place for real ongoing maintenance work, not an excuse to bury urgent bugs under a generic label. Use it for issues that improve the product continuously without pretending they are the next big milestone. Keep the children concrete and user-impactful so the lane stays actionable.
acceptance_criteria: Maintenance-lane children are clearly described, independently actionable, and not hidden behind fake sequence blockers. The lane remains useful for triage and does not become a miscellaneous junk drawer.
close_reason: Obsolete grouping container; replaced by labels and direct dependencies
